What an honor! We had the great pleasure to welcome Capgemini CEO Aiman Ezzat to Germany! In addition to talking about our #researchanddevelopement programs, we also discussed about how we can help to bring the automated, sustainable mobility of the future safely and quickly to the road.

title: "Too slow, too sluggish, too cautious". Why German automotive suppliers are increasingly bringing their innovations to C
text:
"Too slow, too sluggish, too cautious". Why German automotive suppliers are increasingly bringing their innovations to Chinese carmakers first.

For many years there was an almost symbiotic relationship between German automakers and their suppliers. Innovations such as electronic fuel injection, ABS and ESP or airbag systems were often invented, but always industrialised and brought to success together.

In today's edition, the Handelsblatt  looks at how the elite of German automotive suppliers, under the impression of the challenges facing European and especially German manufacturers, are increasingly turning to producing the latest innovations with the up-and-coming Chinese brands.

It was a pleasure for me to contribute to this article with an analysis of the Chinese supplier industry. You will also find quotes from Arnd Franz (Mahle), Stefan Hartung (BOSCH), Harald Marquardt (Marquardt Group) and Stephan von Schuckmann (ZF),

Harald Marquardt, CEO of the group of the same name, says: "With a Chinese electric car costing around €100,000, you will hardly recognise that it is a non-European vehicle".

Would you like some examples?
ZF: The first steer-by-wire went to NIO, 800V power electronics can be found in several Chinese vehicles.

BOSCH: infotainment controller to Changan

Conti: break-by-wire to Leapmotor

Perhaps the best example of the new China strategy comes from ZF: Stephan von Schuckmann, member of the board and head of the driveline division, will in future manage his global unit from Shanghai. "The build quality is very high, the software expertise extraordinary. These are very, very good vehicles and they are on the road in a third of the time," says von Schuckmann.

Even though China itself is facing some major challenges and international cooperation with and through the People's Republic will be rewritten in the coming years, one thing is certain for German suppliers - as the Handelsblatt puts it: “they will not go down with the German automotive industry”.

The German manufacturers' electric offensive had a late start, and it is precisely the short-term effects that are preventing sales from growing to the desired extent. In markets such as Germany, they are even facing a decline in sales. They are trying to counteract this with innovative cooperation models in China (such as AUDI's acquisition of a SAIC electric platform to complement its own MEB platform).

While German carmakers are losing market share due to the strengthening of Chinese manufacturers, this development can be an opportunity for suppliers to reduce their dependence on domestic manufacturers.

Read more in the Handelsblatt print edition or online: Handelsblatt: Tempo der Deutschen „ein Unding“: Zulieferer wenden sich chinesischen Autoherstellern zu - https://lnkd.in/dtUjBdCi

Capgemini Engineering #innovation Capgemini Automotive #consulting #automotiveindustry #electricvehicles #china

title: "Intel CEO Pat Gelsinger is counting on Europe, of all places, to return to the top of the #semiconductorindustry", just
text:
"Intel CEO Pat Gelsinger is counting on Europe, of all places, to return to the top of the #semiconductorindustry", just reports Handelsblatt. It was a pleasure for me to contribute to this piece with some technological assessments.

* In the course of its IDM 2.0 Strategy (Integrated Device Manufacturing) - presented last year - Intel Corporation wants to become (again) one of the leading foundry services providers worldwide.

* In addition to new plants at the Ocotillo/Arizona site, an expansion of capacities in Europe is also planned.

* Intel wants to build two huge factories and several research and development sites in the EU with massive state support. More than 20 billion euros are to be invested for this purpose. 

* For example, new, technologically leading factories are to be built in Magdeburg and Italy. It also wants to expand in France, Spain and Poland with state support. In this way, an expected funding sum of around ten billion euros should come together. 

* This is a great opportunity for Europe, as it would be a chance to bring a factory for "2nm technology" (or now more nicely called technology of "the Ångström-era") to the continent. 

* Pat Gelsinger, an engineer and inveterate geek, as he calls himself, wants to make Intel the first chip producer in the world to use the new lithography systems for high-NA-EUV (Twinscan EXE:5x00-series, high numerical aperture) from the Dutch equipment manufacturer ASML in series production.

*  Due to the significantly improved resolution of these stepper machines, the finest chip structures under 3nm can be produced much more efficiently than is possible at all with today's equipment.

* The technology is planned to be used for the first time in the "Intel 18A" production process from the end of 2025. With the help of these technologies, Gelsinger wants to replace the Taiwanese contract manufacturer TSMC as the technology leader.

Read more about this topic in the Handelsblatt article online (link in the commentary) or in the print edition.

Capgemini #innovation Capgemini Engineering #semiconductors #technology 

Images: intel, ASML, Handelsblatt

title: The Silent Revolution of E-Motors: DER SPIEGEL sheds light on an often overlooked key component of electric vehicles
text:
🔌 The Silent Revolution of E-Motors: DER SPIEGEL sheds light on an often overlooked key component of electric vehicles

For years, the public discourse around electric vehicles has focused almost exclusively on the battery—its chemistry, energy density, raw materials, and charging speed. But quietly and steadily, a different core component is evolving at remarkable speed: the electric motor.

I recently had the privilege to contribute to a detailed feature in Der Spiegel that shines a long-overdue spotlight on how electrical drive systems are advancing. The article captures a wave of innovation happening across OEMs, startups, and research institutions that promises to make EVs more efficient, more affordable, and more sustainable—well beyond the battery pack.

As I noted in the article:

“In der öffentlichen Diskussion dreht sich beim Elektroauto fast alles um die Batterie. Dabei steckt auch in der E-Maschine noch enormes Potenzial, um Elektrofahrzeuge effizienter, leistungsstärker, leichter, umweltfreundlicher und günstiger zu machen.”

And it’s not just the e-motor in isolation:  Efficiency is a team sport.

It takes the entire propulsion chain—from battery and inverter to the electric motor, drive mechanics, tyres, and even the vehicle’s software and thermal management—to unlock true, system-level performance. Only when all these components are finely tuned and orchestrated can we reach the next level of energy efficiency and driving experience. And this end-to-end approach is where our teams at Capgemini shine.

The innovations emerging from Europe are impressive:

🔹 Mercedes-Benz is pioneering ultra-efficient motors with its “gesehnte Spulen” and double V-shaped magnets, achieving a 93% long-distance efficiency—positioning the upcoming CLA as the “one-liter car” of the electric era, as CEO Ola Källenius put it.

🔹 BMW (for the "Neue Klasse") is developing synchronous motors with no rare earths, and cutting energy loss by 40% and weight by 10%.

🔹 RWTH Aachen University (a cooperation partner of ours) and Hyperdrives are working on the industrialization of internally cooled copper hairpins—a breakthrough likened to a turbocharger for EVs as said by Till Augustin Backes.

🔹 AEM Advanced Electric Machines is eliminating both rare earths and copper, using aluminum windings for better recyclability and cost efficiency, as noted by CEO James Widmer.

🔹 DeepDrive is introducing a double-rotor radial flux motor, while YASA (a Mercedes subsidiary) is scaling axial flux technology to shrink motors dramatically.

👉 Read the full Der Spiegel feature here:
https://lnkd.in/duzghj-i

#ElectricVehicles #Innovation #Automotive #SustainableMobility 

Werner Ferreira da Silva Michael Müller Baligh Belgacem Christoph Weissinger Marcus Fiege Fabienne LEFEVER Jens Sulek Jochen Bechtold Capgemini Automotive

title: The battle for the crown: artificial intelligence permeates all areas of life and the chip giants are striving mightily
text:
The battle for the crown: artificial intelligence permeates all areas of life and the chip giants are striving mightily for leadership in terms of AI computing power. But especially when it comes to intelligent machines or edge computing applications, factors such as software support and performance efficiency are particularly important in addition to pure performance.

In the recently published MLPerf Inference V3 benchmarks, newcomers such as SiMa.ai  and NEUCHIPS  were among the big players in the industry.

Sima.ai, in particular, has attracted attention with excellent results. That's why Handelsblatt  brought in today's issue a portrait of the startup led by Krishna Rangasayee, an industry veteran.

The company has developed its own AI chip, which specifically targets performance-critical edge applications and is supposed to shine with high efficiency. A software stack has also been developed, which should significantly simplify the adoption and application of existing models.

How the manufacturers position themselves in this multi-billion market or where these chips are used in #UAV, #automateddriving  or #robotics, I had the pleasure to provide some industry analyses together with experts such as Karl Freund  / Cambrian and Kurt Keutzer  / University of California, Berkeley EECS. 

Read more in the article: Handelsblatt: Chip-Newcomer Sima übertrumpft Nvidia - https://lnkd.in/dRSW7zzE

As always, there are many ways to achieve a balance between in-device computing, edge computing via #5G, and classic data center computing. Our team of AI experts, #semiconductorindustry innovators and 5G gurus can help: Daniela Rittmeier , Shiv Tasker , Scott Runner , Fotis Karonis , Christoph Jungnickl , Loïc Hamon , Jonathan Nussbaumer

title: Is German Auto back in the race again?
text:
Is German Auto back in the race again?

The IAA MOBILITY 2025 officially opened today in Munich – inaugurated by VDA President Hildegard Müller, MP Dr. Markus Söder, OB Dieter Reiter and German Chancellor Friedrich Merz in a remarkably inspiring and positive way. What a start!

I was invited for a live interview on ARD  Tagesschau / Update Wirtschaft with Antje Erhard to share my perspective on:
• the atmosphere and momentum at this year’s IAA,
• the product premieres setting the tone,
• how Chinese products are being perceived,
• the current status of the Chinese automotive industry, and
• the challenges that lie ahead for the global mobility sector.

Yes, German manufacturers present great products and strong platforms today. Yet the challenge remains: to keep innovating, improve the cost base, mitigate trade risks, balance the transition from combustion to electric propulsion – and bring driving automation to the masses. This Herculean task cannot be solved by industry alone. It requires optimal support from politics. And today, we gladly heard this messages of support during the opening speeches. 

🎥 Watch the interview here: Update Wirtschaft vom 09.09.2025 https://lnkd.in/dQEhPvab

If you are at the IAA, come see me at the Capgemini booth in Hall 1b and let’s have a talk how to master those challenges successfully together!

 
Capgemini Automotive

title: AI just swapped the lab coat for the blue collar.
text:
AI just swapped the lab coat for the blue collar.
That was my line in this morning’s Tagesschau  interview, live from HANNOVER MESSE 2026.

Walking these halls, past robotic grippers, humanoids, edge-AI boxes running production workloads, it is hard to argue otherwise.

2026 is not the year of the next, bigger AI model. Although they are evolving at breathtaking speed. 2026 is the year the pilots stop and the scaling starts. On the shopfloor, in the field, inside the machine.

The numbers of our last research paper tell it: roughly seven out of ten industrial companies have at least one AI use case in production. Fewer than three out of ten have scaled it. That gap is where 2026 gets decided.

At the Capgemini booth we are making that tangible with our AI powered manufacturing solutions. Not with slide decks, with running systems. Agile enough to cut manufacturing time to days, not quarters. Efficient enough to show real P&L impact. Sovereign in substance, not in slogan: European data, European stacks, European choices.

None of it works solo. With Intel we run Physical AI on the edge. Heterogeneous compute, where milliseconds and watts decide. On the new Panther and Bartlett platforms. 

With Schwarz Digits we put a European, sovereign cloud underneath real industrial workloads. Not theoretical sovereignty. Operational sovereignty.

A good booth needs a good neighborhood. Around the corner at Bosch, a proper glimpse into what manufacturing is becoming. Great to see Stefan Hartung live in action this morning. German industry moves with conviction, it still sets the pace.

Day one started well and the tone is set. More to come on humanoids, edge AI, and what this Messe really says about where Europe stands.

Come find us — Hall 15, Booth F52.

👉 Tagesschau interview in the comments.

#HannoverMesse2026 #PhysicalAI #innovation #IndustrialAI #DigitalSovereignty #HMI26

title: The countdown is on for the world's most powerful technology event - CES 2025 in Las Vegas!
text:
The countdown is on for the world's most powerful technology event - CES 2025 in Las Vegas!

Once again, Capgemini  will be at the heart of it all, hosting our Executive Lounge at the Venetian. It's a place where bold ideas, transformative technologies and industry-defining discussions converge. We're bringing thought-provoking concepts and practical benefits to multiple industries - and I'm proud to be a part of it.

One of my personal highlights is always the Software Defined Mobility corner. This year, we're tackling one of the industry's most pressing challenges: how to make the Software Defined Vehicle (SDV) fast (think "China speed" for our German friends) and affordable. Our full SDV stack demonstrator, developed in collaboration with partners like SODA.Auto and Apex.AI  will be on display, showing what's possible when ambition meets execution. My colleagues Mike Welsh and Michael Tenschert  will be happy to show you more in person. And we are also showing our take on virtual, cloud-native development at the Amazon Web Services (AWS) booth where my colleague Steffen Krause will be showing our tools and services. 

But that's not all. We'll also be demonstrating:
🚗 Highly Automated Driving - Accelerate safety and speed to market with our data-centric AD validation platform, led by my colleague Oussama Ben Moussa.

🎥 Next-Level Passenger Experience - See how GenAI-powered intelligence can revolutionize in-car entertainment. Our entertainment system, brought to life with the help of my colleague João Neiva and a host of local artificial intelligence assistants (hello, Liquid AI ).

To add to the excitement, we'll be hosting panel discussions led by our automotive leader, Laurence Noël , where industry experts and thought leaders will discuss the future of mobility, the role of software in the automotive world, and how to achieve speed, affordability, and sustainability in this critical transition.

But what makes me particularly excited this year? The news and research we'll be sharing on the #semiconductor industry. This space is moving fast and I can't wait to share Capgemini's insights and perspectives with our partners, clients and industry leaders.

If you're in Vegas for CES, join us. Let's connect, learn, and shape the future of mobility and beyond together.

👉 Learn more and book your visit to the Capgemini Executive Lounge at CES 2025: https://lnkd.in/dgcgS-Hv

title: Automotive Grade Open-Source for SDV at #CES2026: On behalf of Capgemini, I had the distinct pleasure - together with my
text:
Automotive Grade Open-Source for SDV at #CES2026: On behalf of Capgemini, I had the distinct pleasure - together with my esteemed colleague Laurence Noël - of signing the Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) for the expansion of the Automotive-Grade Open Source Software Ecosystem. 

Seeing the coalition between the Eclipse Software Defined Vehicle (SDV) working group and the German Association of the Automotive Industry (VDA) grow to 32 strong is a clear mandate from the industry. It demonstrates that OEMs, suppliers, and tech partners are united in recognizing the value of shared, non-differentiating software foundations built in the open.

As my industry colleague Martin Schleicher summarised it perfectly:
"The ambition is clear and critical:
✔️ Reduce redundant engineering
✔️ Accelerate time to market
✔️ Improve interoperability
✔️ Create certifiable, long-term maintainable components
The real impact will come from disciplined execution:
➡️ Stable APIs and transparent governance
➡️ Consistent upstreaming
➡️ Integration into real vehicle programs
➡️ Viable business models that balance innovation with cost efficiency"

The recent release of S-CORE v0.5 is the evidence that this initiative is literally "turning wheels".

A massive thank you to all industry members for their trust and collaboration. A special acknowledgment to Marcus Bollig and Martin Schleicher from the VDA, as well as Ansgar Lindwedel (he/him) and Mike Milinkovich from the Eclipse Foundation for their tireless leadership in driving this initiative forward.

I am proud to see Capgemini standing alongside these industry leaders:
42dot, Accenture, AVL, Coretura, Cummins, ECARX, Elektrobit, Infineon, LEAR, LG Electronics, Michelin, MOBIS, QNX, Qualcomm, Red Hat, Schaeffler, Traton, Stellantis, T-Systems, and Useblocks joining founding participants Aumovio, BMW, Bosch, ETAS, Hella, Mercedes-Benz, Qorix, Valeo, Vector, Volkswagen, and ZF.

Press release available here: https://lnkd.in/dR55CrE3

#Automotive #SDV #OpenSource #Innovation #SoftwareDefinedVehicle

title: Recap of Day 1 at the IZB 2024 and Outlook for the Coming Days
text:
Recap of Day 1 at the IZB 2024 and Outlook for the Coming Days

Day 1 at the IZB in Wolfsburg started with great momentum! The opening speeches from Ministerpräsident Stephan-Peter Weil, Volkswagen ’s board members Dirk Große-Loheide and Kai Grünitz as well as Thomas Krause of Wolfsburg AG set the tone, highlighting the critical themes driving our industry: transformation, speed, efficiency, and innovation. It’s clear that the role of suppliers is evolving rapidly, and the industry must adapt to meet these new demands.

Capgemini has made a strong impression at the event with a series of exciting innovations, including:

* AI across the value chain: from #AugmentedEngineering to our virtual AI[wo]man, ELLEM. -> much loved by the public and the press!

* Augmented Reality in design and manufacturing processes.

* Blueprints for the software-defined factory, co-developed with Bosch, alongside groundbreaking concepts for highly efficient battery cell manufacturing with Siemens.

* Virtualization technologies to accelerate the development of the software-defined vehicle (based on Amazon Web Services (AWS) technologies). 

* Next-gen IVI developments for an enhanced passenger experience (featuring our Android expertise, and NXP Semiconductors , Qualcomm technologies). 

* Application life cycle management solutions for future software-defined vehicles (think Codebeamer, a PTC Technology)

In addition, there were excellent panel discussions featuring Volkswagen and industry leaders on what will shape our future—and, more importantly, how we can get there quickly.

As always, the IZB provides a wonderful opportunity to connect with clients, partners, press and friends in the industry. 

I’m looking forward to the next few days here in beautiful Wolfsburg and I hope to see you at our booth in Halle 3! The most interesting place!

And a big thanks to our CG organizing team who made everything happen!!!

title: Excited to be at the Eclipse Software Defined Vehicle AUTOMOTIVE OPEN SOURCE SUMMIT 2024, where we are diving deep into
text:
Excited to be at the Eclipse Software Defined Vehicle AUTOMOTIVE OPEN SOURCE SUMMIT 2024, where we are diving deep into how Open Source Software (OSS) is shaping the future of mobility. 

Today’s discussions are a treasure trove of insights into the evolving landscape of automotive-grade software. From the status of OSS in our industry to the strides made by the Eclipse SDV working group, it’s clear that OSS is unlocking unprecedented innovation, resilience, and efficiency.

Key highlights from my perspective:

- Sarah Novotny  sharing invaluable lessons from successful OSS ventures like Kubernetes.

- Max Lemke discussing European initiatives for open-source and software-defined mobility.

- Johan Linåker  challenging us to rethink collaboration and competition.

- An engaging panel on "Cost vs. Efficiency vs. Talent Shortage vs. New Business Models - What Are the Drivers for Utilizing OSS" with Dr. Michael Dinkel / Luxoft , Dr. Matthias Traub of VECTOR Informatik  , Isaac Trefz of Elektrobit , Daniel Lueddecke of HARMAN International  and myself, excellently moderated by Michael Plagge 

- Another insightful panel on "Roles and Responsibilities in an OSS-Based Middleware Supply Chain" by Chris Salzmann of BMW Group , Stephen Walli of Microsoft , Christian Müller of ZF Group , Sven Kappel of ETAS   and Francis Chow of Red Hat 

- The exciting announcement from the Core Stack team Ansgar Lindwedel (he/him) 

- Again a great panel on the non-differentiating parts of the SDV stack with Martin Schleicher  of Continental , Hannes Fuchs of AVL , Markus Blonn of IAV GmbH , Thomas Fleischmann of CARIAD and Holger Grandy of BMW Group , excellently moderated by Sara Gallian 

- The opportunity to meet so many bright minds from across the industry!

The summit showcases how OSS is steadily advancing from off-board areas to in-vehicle infotainment systems and even into safety-critical domains. 

But, we need to admit, that we all need to work on the velocity of change in order to leverage the advantages of OSS. 

A big thank you to Mike Milinkovich , Michael Plagge, Sara Gallian , Paulina Gueorguieva  and the entire team for orchestrating this excellent event. 

I encourage everyone to join the Eclipse SDV working group and contribute to the industry’s progress.

Eclipse automotive projects: https://lnkd.in/drs-PHhY

Capgemini Automotive Capgemini Engineering 

For more information on Automotive FOSS Services by Capgemini reach out to me or my colleague Daniel Leuchter. Happy to help!

#AutomotiveOSS #OpenSource #FutureOfMobility #Innovation #EclipseSDV

title: As we conclude the “Car IT Symposium,” today’s sessions left us not just with insights but with actionable pathways towa
text:
As we conclude the “Car IT Symposium,” today’s sessions left us not just with insights but with actionable pathways towards a future where software-defined vehicles (SDVs) redefine our relationship with mobility.

The day started with an enlightening talk by Peter Mertens , seasoned CTO/veteran in the automotive sector. He navigated through the challenges the auto industry faces in its shift towards becoming software-centric companies, yet optimistically highlighted that there’s light at the end of the tunnel. His perspective set a tone of resilience and hope for what lies ahead.

Cybersecurity emerged as a central theme, underlining its critical role in making the software-defined vehicle a reality. Today’s discussions went beyond identifying challenges to actively exploring strategies to win the cybersecurity race, ensuring that our vehicles are not just smart but also secure.

I had the honor of opening the SDV section with a pitch on what propels the SDV forward—the opportunity presented by customer-specific chips for automotive. This segment provided a unique lens on the technical and strategic innovations on hardware and software driving our industry - and how Capgemini can help to make your custom silicon happen!

The day was further enriched by fantastic presentations from leading entities like VAIVA , Continental , VECTOR Informatik , KPIT , Apex.AI , Red Hat , Luxoft and Momenta . Their collective insights on development methodologies and future chip-to-cloud architectures were revelatory. The consensus? Collaboration and open platforms are indispensable for our journey forward.

Product overviews by Huawei on automated driving and Hesai Technology (LiDAR )added depth to our understanding of the current landscape and the tools at our disposal to shape the future of automotive technology.

A panel discussion I participated in alongside Markus Oertel , Jan Becker , Giuseppe Serio  and Martin Schleicher  on the future of the SDV was particularly memorable. It was a profound exchange of visions for a future where technology, safety, and user experience converge seamlessly.

Wrapping up, a heartfelt thank you to the CAR Center Automotive Research team for orchestrating an event that has undoubtedly propelled us forward in our quest to redefine mobility. The symposium has been a beacon of knowledge, innovation, and collaboration.

As we move forward, it’s clear that the road to the software-defined vehicle is paved with challenges. Yet, the discussions, insights, and camaraderie of the past two days reinforce my belief in our collective ability to overcome them, driving towards a future marked by innovation, security, and an unwavering focus on the user experience. Here’s to shaping the future, together. 

Dirk Wollschläger , Prof. Dr. Helena Wisbert , Beatrix C. Keim

text:

I just stepped off a live interview with ARD / Tagesschau24 ("Update Wirtschaft"), reporting directly from Las Vegas on the major shifts we are witnessing at CES 2026.

Here is what is defining the conversation on the ground: 
🚀 AI becomes "close" and personal: Moving from cloud-dependence to powerful On-Device AI, and a core component of most products and services.
💻 The AI PC Era: We are entering a new hardware cycle driven by local processing power. Intel/AMD/Qualcomm in the limelight.
👓 XR Focus: Moving away from abstract "Metaverse" concepts toward tangible utility in Smart Glasses, driven by new display technologies and energy efficient processor platforms. 
🤖 Consumer Robotics: Becoming ready for everyday adoption. Your vacuum robot is getting arms and legs!
🏥 Smart Health: AI is learning to create significantly more accurate diagnoses, utilising new sensor systems.

We also took a deep dive into the current state of Automated Driving—discussing where the industry really stands amidst the rapid hardware evolutions.

title: "Dangerous Gaps in Europe's Chip Production." Even if the semiconductor crisis is not currently dominating the headlines
text:
"Dangerous Gaps in Europe's Chip Production." Even if the semiconductor crisis is not currently dominating the headlines: the issue of European high-tech sovereignty is more pressing than ever. 

In an article of the Handelsblatt, I was allowed to assess the situation together with experts like Alan Priestley from Gartner or Sascha Bütterling from Supplyframe and which strategies Europe has to build up comprehensive chip competence on the continent.

Here's my take on the situation:

* With the help of the Chips Act, the European Commission plans to double Europe's share of global chip production to 20 percent by the end of the decade. And that's with an increasing absolute volume worldwide.

* It is not enough to concentrate on so-called front-end plants (fabrication from a blank wafer to a completed wafer) worth billions, such as Intel Corporation wants to build in Magdeburg. Even though this accounts for the largest share of value creation, almost 40%. Europe must take care of the entire value chain, from raw materials and tools to design and manufacturing. Today, the continent is a real power only in the areas of intellectual property for chip design/architecture and semiconductor manufacturing machinery (with ASML being an epicenter of innovation and global importance for the industry).

* Even if there are initiatives in design and fabrication, the area of assembly, testing and packaging (ATP) is gaining in importance. Due to the strong global splitting of value chains in #semiconductors, many chips are "real world travelers" and have often visited 3 continents (or more) on their way from raw silicon to integration. Taiwan, China, Korea dominate the ATP market (together more than 50%) and also Malaysia and Indonesia are popular locations for such production steps.

* Infineon Technologies, world leader in power semiconductors as well as extremely strong in the #automotiveindustry, has just opened a new backend site in Hungary. And this at attractive manufacturing costs as Alexander Gorski, head of Infineon's backend manufacturing confirms in the article.

* Even if the location of further work steps in Europe means a tour de force, it is of highest importance for the preservation of the competitiveness of whole industries, as also Siegfried Russwurm,  president BDI - Bundesverband der Deutschen Industrie e.V.  , often emphasizes.

* Labor shortages in Europe can also be addressed by giving greater consideration to near shore countries such as Tunisia or Morocco in the industrial strategy.

* Especially in times of strong industrial policy, be it in China or the USA, Europe must find its own strategy and implement it coherently.



Capgemini #consulting Capgemini Engineering #innovation no more #chipshortage

title: SDV: From Hype to Realization
text:
SDV: From Hype to Realization

At the AEK Automobil-Elektronik Kongress in Ludwigsburg, the journey from visionary concepts to tangible products in the realm of Software Defined Vehicles (SDV) is being intensely discussed. This premier event on the future of vehicle electronics/software is setting the stage for transformative insights and industry-defining conversations.

The congress kicked off this morning with an inspiring speech by Chairman Ricky Hudi , setting a high bar for the subsequent sessions. Chris Salzmann  from BMW Group followed with a compelling presentation on Software Centered Boardnet Development, providing a deep dive into BMW’s strategic shift towards open-source solutions and new roles like Tech Stack Maintainer and Distributor, essential for future software development ecosystems. His insights on unlocking efficiencies and enhancing performance were particularly noteworthy.

Next, Christoph Hartung  from Bosch  delivered an intense session on mastering the revolutionary transformation of SDV. He highlighted the industry’s current status and the critical need for modular hardware ( e.g. #Chiplets) and advanced software, emphasizing the importance of culture and leadership in navigating this complexity.

Katrin Matthes from Ampere (Renault Group ) concluded the opening sessions with an enlightening overview of the E/E and Software Strategy behind Renault’s acclaimed electronics and infotainment platform.

In just the first few hours, AEK has already reaffirmed its status as the premier venue for cutting-edge discussions and insights in our field. AI is, unsurprisingly, a significant topic of conversation here as well.

My colleague Steffen Krause  and I are here representing Capgemini eager to engage in meaningful exchanges with industry peers and friends. Looking forward to an exciting and insightful two days in Ludwigsburg.

title: Software Defines Mobility — and at today’s Automobil-Elektronik Kongress (#AEK_live ) in Ludwigsburg, we witnessed a maj
text:
🚗 Software Defines Mobility — and at today’s Automobil-Elektronik Kongress (#AEK_live ) in Ludwigsburg, we witnessed a major leap into that software-driven future.

Industry leaders, united under the umbrella of the German Association of the Automotive Industry (VDA) and in collaboration with the Eclipse Software Defined Vehicle group, have signed a Memorandum of Understanding to create a shared, automotive-grade open-source software ecosystem. This is a historic step for the industry — one that embraces collaboration to tackle complexity, accelerate innovation, and ensure functional safety and security across vehicle lifecycles.

💡 The initiative, named S-CORE, is hosted by the Eclipse SDV Working Group and aims to:
•	Establish a common, certifiable middleware software stack,
•	Enable open collaboration across OEMs, suppliers, and tech players,
•	Accelerate time-to-market for software-defined vehicles,
•	Align with standards like AUTOSAR and COVESA.

📍 A Minimum Viable Product (MVP) is expected later this year. First SOPs based on the open-source stack are targeted by 2030 — but the momentum is real now.

I’m proud to see Capgemini actively contributing to this transformation as a member of the Eclipse ecosystem, supporting the S-CORE idea with the engineering excellence and commitment required to shape the future of mobility.

More on the announcement here 👉 https://lnkd.in/dDJCSqru

And details here: https://lnkd.in/dAMeCRZ2

Let’s go!

Kudos to the drivers of these ideas:

Chris Salzmann Markus Rettstatt Holger Grandy and many more!

text:
𝗗𝗲𝗯𝘂𝗻𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗠𝘆𝘁𝗵: 𝗔𝗿𝗲 𝗚𝗲𝗿𝗺𝗮𝗻 𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗮𝗸𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗚𝗶𝘃𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗨𝗽 𝗼𝗻 𝗘𝗹𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗰 𝗩𝗲𝗵𝗶𝗰𝗹𝗲𝘀?

A recent rumor circulating in #China claims that German car manufacturers like Mercedes-Benz are abandoning electric vehicles (EVs) due to strong Chinese competition. But fear not, this video by Deutsche Welle sets the record straight! And explains much more details about EV’s and the recent discussions around this topic!

While there are undeniable challenges to EV adoption in Europe, including factors like cost of electricity, government subsidies, and charging infrastructure, German automakers remain firmly committed to electric mobility.

In interviews featuring industry leaders like Mercedes CEO Ola Källenius, Audi Boss Gernot Döllner, VW Chairman Thomas Schäfer, car expert Prof. Dudenhöfer and me, presented by Jun Yan, the video explores the current state of EVs in Germany. 

Despite the challenges, German brands are unwavering in their commitment. Mercedes-Benz has pledged to achieve a 50% sales ratio of electric and hybrid vehicles by the end of this decade, with an ultimate goal of carbon neutrality. This commitment is echoed by other German brands like Volkswagen and Audi, signaling a clear understanding that electrification is the future of the industry.

Sure, there may be adjustments to the pace of electrification depending on the market, but one thing is certain: German carmakers are not giving up on EVs. Advancements in battery technology, like Sodium-Ion or further down the road solid state batteries, new design and manufacturing approaches as well as growing economies of scale promise to make EVs much more affordable and convenient, paving the way for a future primarily powered by electricity.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗳𝘂𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗺𝗼𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝗻 𝗺𝗮𝗻𝘆 𝘂𝘀𝗲 𝗰𝗮𝘀𝗲𝘀 𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗰, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗚𝗲𝗿𝗺𝗮𝗻 𝗮𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗮𝗸𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝗯𝗲 𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗶𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗿𝗮𝗰𝗲.

title: Speed, Capability, and the Future of European Defense
text:
🇪🇺 Speed, Capability, and the Future of European Defense
"Wrecking ball politics." That is the stark term used by the Munich Security Report 2026 to describe our current geopolitical reality.

This year at the Munich Security Conference, the stakes are higher than ever.

I had the great honor of moderating a panel discussion titled "Code over Steel - why human augmentation is the key to Europe's defense readiness", under the patronage of Cyril Garcia, to address how we meet this challenge. 

We debated the  architecture of our 2030 forces - specifically how to balance operational speed and high-end capability against the reality of scarce human resources.

The dialogue between excellencies, military leaders, industry veterans, and start-up disruptors made one thing clear: The future of defense is software-defined. And much more.

To understand how technology is reshaping global security, I invite you to read Capgemini’s new study, "Code over Steel." It provides a critical look at the intersection of active military needs and software innovation.

1. Defense readiness hinges on software-defined, human augmented capability
2. Europe faces a readiness bottleneck: talent, legacy systems, complexity
3. Armed forces agree: Human-Machine Teaming (HMT) must scale
4. Europe has a knowing-doing gap in tech adoption
5. Interoperability is the catalyst for speed, scale and resilience
6. Defense industry must undergo a dual transformation
7. Europe's production surge will take 2-3 years, we are at risk missing 2030 deadlines
8. Human augmentation is central to detterance in the 2030 battlespace
9. Achieving readiness requires immediate, coordinated action
10. Four imperatives are core: Unified digital backbone, cross-national cooperation, integrated and user-oriented development, continuous learning 

🔗 Link to study in the comments.

A big thank you to our distinguished Defense experts Andreas Conradi, Dr. Benjamin Schulte, Christopher Gaube, Thibaud Frossard, Philippe KOFFI, Bruno Bouf for the excellent program.

#MSC2026 #Defense #TechAndInnovation #CodeOverSteel #innovation

title: What an incredible day at the Car Symposium! Yesterday, the forum truly lived up to its tradition as the premier venue f
text:
What an incredible day at the Car Symposium! Yesterday, the forum truly lived up to its tradition as the premier venue for shaping the future of mobility.

🔬 We gained invaluable insights from industry leaders like Sigrid de Vries (DG of European Automobile Manufacturers'​ Association (ACEA), Ola Kaellenius (CEO of Mercedes-Benz AG), Gunnar Kilian (Member of the Board Volkswagen), and Jörg Grotendorst (SVP R&D Magna International). The discussions were robust, centering around current market dynamics and sustainable mobility—an electrifying future indeed!

💚 #Sustainability was at the forefront, highlighted for instance by Sandra Tostar of Volvo Cars, who gave a fascinating presentation on the use of sustainable materials in vehicles. The commitment to a greener future was palpable throughout the day.

👩‍💻 Equally compelling were the sessions on the software-defined future of vehicles. Joachim Franz / Microsoft discussed "Transforming the Automotive Value Chain with GenAI," while Thomas Dannemann of Qualcomm explored "GenAI & Evolution of Next-Gen In-Vehicle Experience." Tobias J. Nadjib / Volkswagen Infotainment GmbH delved into the "Coding Mindset," and Jan Becker / Apex.AI shared insights on "How to Speed Up with Software Platforms?".

📢 I had the privilege of speaking on "Revving Up the Revolution: Custom Silicon in Automotive, Learnings from Tech Players in the USA and China," exploring how custom silicon and chiplet technology is set to revolutionize our industry. Beside technology itself, we took some sidesteps to the aspects like resilience and ownership, as well as on global semicon supply.

The day culminated in a highly engaging panel discussion where we shed light on accelerating the digital transformation of the automotive industry—making it faster, better, and more efficient. 

🚘 And yes, the car's exhibited have also been of high interest. Next to vehicles from Mercedes-Benz, Volkswagen, Skoda, Volvo, Nio, Ford (to name a few) als Chery / OMODA UK automobiles could be examined. 

Thank you to all the participants and organizers for a truly inspiring event!

你好, IAA MOBILITY! In a few weeks, Chinese manufacturers will take the stage at the largest mobility trade fair to present their products.

I am currently in China not only to visit customers but also to take a closer look at the Chinese manufacturers and their go to market strategies.

Even if everyone in Europe is talking about BYD, Nio, Xpeng and Co: a look at the (for many Westener still unknown) manufacturers like LiAuto, DongFeng Voyah, HUAWEI Seres and many others is worthwhile! The future lab China offers interesting views!

We have been saying it for years now: apart from the fact that many local manufacturers have made enormous progress in terms of design, construction, manufacturing quality and appearance: especially the infotainment systems and associated software make many Western systems look dated. After all, there are still some high-quality components from German suppliers under the hood.

Just as HUAWEI's Experience Stores are unmistakably inspired by the great role-model from Cupertino, the Chinese EV pioneers have taken a very close look at how to create a positive customer experience in retail - and in my view, they have definitely overtaken certain Western brands in terms of customer experience.

title: Accelerating Autonomous Driving Validation with AI & Automation – Our Latest Research at Tech.AD Berlin
text:
Accelerating Autonomous Driving Validation with AI & Automation – Our Latest Research at Tech.AD Berlin

The road to highly automated driving is still long, with validation remaining one of the biggest hurdles—technically, financially, and organizationally. Despite billions invested, current validation processes extend development cycles significantly, slowing down time-to-market. But what if we could change that?

At Tech.AD Europe, my colleague Thomas Häberle will present the abstract/paper we’ve written together on how AI and automation can revolutionize the validation process, accelerating the path to deployment. Our approach—developed in collaboration with leading partners and research programs—demonstrates how we can dramatically reduce costs and time-to-market while ensuring safer, more efficient automated driving systems.

Key breakthroughs from our research:
🕵️‍♂️ Ground Truth Acceleration – Leveraging real-world data, virtual scenarios, and AI-generated datasets to optimize training quality and coverage.
🤖 Automated Model Optimization – Insights from KI-Wissen & GAIA-X4KI power AI-driven scenario generation and automated identification of AI weaknesses.
📈 Data-driven Ecosystem & Marketplace – Built on an open toolchain and scalable validation platform, enabling cross-company economies of scale.
🔃 Looping Validation & Model Training – Seamless access to high-quality data for faster, cost-effective optimization of autonomous systems.

This work is the result of our ongoing collaboration in multiple research programs, including NXTAIM, KI-Wissen, and GAIA-X4KI—pushing the boundaries of secure, trustworthy AI for autonomous mobility.

While I’ll be in Cambridge, MA, I wish Thomas and the entire Tech.AD audience a fantastic conference! Looking forward to the discussions and new insights emerging from the event.

If you are there, stop by at our booth and get the latest insights from Thomas, Sherif (Noureldin) Hussein, Özgür Dincel and Heiko Jung !

Big thank you also to our tireless ADAS/AD champs Oussama Ben Moussa Ralf E. Nikolaus Sebastian Pretscher Alexander Schnelle Thomas Reicher Reicher and many others! 

Werner Ferreira da Silva Jochen Bechtold

title: Sunday Reading: "the market for used e-cars is slow to take off. What's the reason?" . This question was posed by "auto
text:
Sunday Reading: "the market for used e-cars is slow to take off. What's the reason?" . This question was posed by "auto motor und sport“,the traditional German trade magazine, in its current issue.  

The price development of used vehicles has resembled a rollercoaster ride in recent years, as Martin Weiss of DAT Group describes in the article. For example, the residual values of electric vehicles had plummeted by July 2021 and, despite recovery, are still up to 10 percentage points behind ICE vehicles in 2023.

When buying a used vehicle, customers are primarily interested in the health of the battery, charging time and range.

In addition, many second-hand buyers are not yet sufficiently informed about the topic of electromobility - and makes customers hesitate. 

This also manifests itself in the longer standing times of electric models at the dealership. In this respect, the ambitious asking prices of many sellers are simply no longer feasible at present.

Together with my esteemed colleague Christian Hummel  I was able to discuss the following topics in an interview for the article:

* Development of the e-vehicle market
* Aging of batteries and rapid technical progress
* Price pressure for used electric automobiles 
* Second Life for batteries and circular economy considerations 

Read more on this in the current issue of AMS and find interesting reports about:
* Technology highlights of the all electric Porsche Taycan (cc Steffen) and an efficiency test of the Porsche InnoDrive system (cc Ralf )
* insightful tests of the BMW i5 M60 and the Fisker Inc Ocean
* update of the Volkswagen Touareg. (Cc Luciano , Werner) 
* Kia EV6 GT vs. Tesla Model Y Performance
* Battery Cell manufacturing (cc Michael, Marcus, Rupert, Christoph, Tobias)

title: First Day at Shanghai Auto 2025 – More Than Kawaii!
text:
First Day at Shanghai Auto 2025 – More Than Kawaii!

The Shanghai Auto Show opened today with its press day – and it’s nothing short of a revelation. A vibrant blend of innovation, competition, and design excellence. One of the more unexpected highlights: a furry, kawaii-style concept car from IM Auto that brought smiles and smartphones out in equal measure. But behind the cuteness lies a serious message – China is redefining the pace and face of automotive innovation.

Here are some key takeaways:
* Global meets local: The sheer number of brands and models – from both Chinese and international manufacturers – underscores the strategic importance of this market.

* “In China for China” bears fruit: German OEMs’ local development strategies are clearly paying off. Some of the new models developed here are not only highly competitive – they’re in some aspects better than what’s currently available in Germany and increasingly “crying for export.”

* China’s EV momentum: While Europe is rediscovering hybrids, China is firmly focused on full electrification – with compelling battery and drivetrain solutions.

* No corners cut: Interior quality, cockpit software, and mobile integration are executed to a very high standard. A clear reminder to Western OEMs: perceived product quality must not fall victim to extreme cost pressure.

* German presence strong, but challenged: The big German brands made a good impression with promising products– but they’re facing a benchmark that’s being set here, not back home.

* Fierce local competition = speed: The intensity of competition among Chinese OEMs is driving rapid innovation. But it raises the question: how long can this breakneck pace be maintained before consolidation begins?

* AI and automation rising: From intelligent assistants to driving automation, AI continues to shape user experience and differentiation. Made in China.

* Tier-1s in transformation: German suppliers are well visible – but Chinese specialists in cockpit and ADAS are surging in capabilities.

* Small EVs, big buzz: Vehicles like the Firefly show that small can be stylish, clever, and desirable. And affordable. Coming soon to Europe.

* Fast charging reaches new heights: 500+ km in 10 minutes is no longer a moonshot – it’s a commercial roadmap.

Tomorrow, I’m excited to join the China Car Symposium for a conversation on “How to Decode China Speed.” This is more than a slogan – it’s a new global reality.

See you there!

#ShanghaiAutoShow #ChinaSpeed Capgemini Automotive  #AutomotiveInnovation #EV #AI #SDV

title: The energy at #EmbeddedWorld is incredible today! It’s not just about what you showcase at your own booth—it’s about wha
text:
The energy at #EmbeddedWorld is incredible today! It’s not just about what you showcase at your own booth—it’s about what you create together with your partners. The real power lies in the ecosystem. Here are some key highlights from today:

🤖 AI at the Edge is a defining topic this year.
👩‍🔬 STMicroelectronics is making edge AI more accessible to developers—great to see their advancements in action. I also liked their new solutions for ultra-low power applications (see the speech of our “Ultra (Low Power) Man Herman Roebbers on how to design systems that run on a single charge years-not just days)
🔋 SiMa.ai  is pushing the boundaries of ultra-efficient processing (think a multi-modal AI model running with a handful of Watts consumption), aligning perfectly with Capgemini’s mission to bring AI to the edge—both with AI-powered development tools (visit Silvio Filipe at our booth!) and smart architectures.

💡 Infineon Technologies has a true highlight: RISC-V International for automotive.
With open architectures gaining traction across industries, this could be a game changer for Europe and China, where RISC-V is thriving. If you want to dive deeper, see my colleague Hugo Martins!

🚗 Of course, no electronics show without automotive!
🤝 Michael Plagge and Eclipse Software Defined Vehicle—great to see the latest from this important initiative, where we’re proud to contribute (ask Sherif (Noureldin) Hussein  for more).
🏎️  SODA.Auto is exhibiting at NXP Semiconductors booth—watch the legendary Gidi Geller demonstrate how intelligent features make SDVs more comfortable. And if you want insights into our joint SDV stack, talk to our Mike Welsh.
☁️ And, of course, if I talk to Gidi about connected vehicles, our partner Amazon Web Services (AWS) and Mr Cloud Auto Swen Postels is not far! 
📺 Qualcomm’s latest embedded platforms—one iteration is at our booth, where Tiago Rodrigues  is showcasing Capgemini’s SDV infotainment stack, built with Unity -based visualization.

🔬 Beyond systems, chip technology is making waves!
🥼 Our booth neighbors and innovation partner Fraunhofer-Gesellschaft  are showcasing cutting-edge Chiplets—speak with Francois Babin for details about our Chiplet technology.
⚠️ Sustainability and efficiency in semiconductor manufacturing are key—Dieter Rathei from DR YIELD is at our booth, sharing insights on perfect-yield fabs.

💡 And finally—a nod to future architectures:
Great to see Prof. Dirk Slama  and the digital.auto  initiative driving progress on zonal architectures.

So much more to explore, and the energy is electrifying! If you’re here, let’s connect—see you at #EmbeddedWorld!

title: A "reality check" for the industry. With these words, Ricky Hudi   opened the 27th Automotive Electronics Congress in Lu
text:
A "reality check" for the industry. With these words, Ricky Hudi   opened the 27th Automotive Electronics Congress in Ludwigsburg today. The industry has been talking about "visions" of the #Software Defined Vehicle for a long time. But as an #automotiveindustry, we are now entering a crucial phase. Now is the time to turn ideas into reality. Otherwise, new players will create facts for us.

Today's lecture programme has just been opened by Ms Bui Kim Thuy, Deputy CEO of VINFAST . Remarkable insights into the structure of the Vinfast vehicle programme and its approaches! 

Throughout the day we will hear interesting speeches from industry leaders like Maria Anhalt  (Elektrobit), Wendy Curcuri Bauer  (AWS), Shalini Palmer  (Analog Devices), Jutta Schneider  (Mercedes) as well as Jochen Hanebeck  (Infineon), Lars Reger  (NXP), Dr. Ulrich Herfeld  (Audi) and Dr. Christoph Grote  (BMW).

Capgemini is represented at this - dare we say it - legendary Automobil-Elektronik Kongress  with the themes of automated driving, function development and assurance, and infotainment/connected vehicle ecosystem. As part of our #intelligentindustry strategy, we at Capgemini see ourselves as comprehensively positioned to support our partners. 

As part of the current programme, we have equipped our autonomous vehicle for instance with some components from our partners Red Hat , Heex Technologies  and NVIDIA  among others.

With our infotainment showcase, we are demonstrating Android-based solutions for in-car entertainment screens and instrument clusters based on NXP Semiconductors  and Qualcomm  chipsets.

title: The #chipshortage is far from being over. Millions of #vehicles can't be manufactured this year and it will affect the #
text:
The #chipshortage is far from being over. Millions of #vehicles can't be manufactured this year and it will affect the #automotiveindustry also in 2022. The ARD #tagesschau, Germany's prime #news show, has investigated the #situation and what #OEM's need to do now in order to #mitigaterisk of the #semiconductor #crisis and to ensure a steady #production. I had the pleasure to #contribute some #insightsanddata to this topic. 

In addition to improving the planning processes, the introduction of corresponding warning systems, the creation of strategic buffers in the supply chain, as well as an adjustment of product development and #engineering processes to define application-specific elements at a late stage are of course obvious. As a "first aid", plans for retrofitting already produced vehicles are also necessary. Fundamentally, however, is that car manufacturers, driven by the trends "#electrification" and "software-defined vehicle", have to deal with their #supplychains much more comprehensively. In some cases, this also extends as far as to control  the sustainable purchase of raw materials.

Check at https://www.tagesschau.de/ for the recording of this interesting report.

If you are interested how the supply side (the #chipdesigners, the #fabs, etc) can better deal with the #semiconductorshortage, then reach out to my colleagues Scott Runner and Yatin Trivedi. 

From adapting #chipdesign, to migrating #process nodes, to selecting an alternative fab, every choice brings solutions and risks - and they can help you to make the right decisions.  Check out: https://lnkd.in/e2ZdZfG

title: "China is outpacing us!" Chinese carmakers are pushing their way into global markets with a vengeance - and they seem to
text:
"China is outpacing us!" Chinese carmakers are pushing their way into global markets with a vengeance - and they seem to be leaving their German competitors behind. In its latest issue, WirtschaftsWoche asks the provocative question: can the decline of Germany's leading industry still be halted?

In a cover story well worth reading, WiWo investigates what is behind the rise of Chinese manufacturers, where the cost advantages lie and what could still hinder the "march through".

It was a pleasure to work with experts such as Stefan Bratzel, Prof. Dr. / Center of Automotive Management (CAM) , Fabian Brandt / Oliver Wyman, and Prof. Dr. Benedikt Maier / Institut für Automobilwirtschaft (IfA)  to contribute to this article.

Here are some facts from the article:
* China has been building large new e-car factories for the past three years. 
* China has overtaken Germany and Japan as the world's largest exporter of cars. 
* China has overtaken Germany, the most innovative car nation, in patents. 
* And according to an analysis by the Swiss bank UBS, China is shaking up the industry. 

The result: China is building cars that are unrivalled in price - and often unrivalled in quality. The industry is facing "tectonic shifts". By 2030, China's carmakers could take more than 20 percentage points of global market share from established manufacturers such as VW, Mercedes and BMW - around €500 billion in sales.

"What if Germany stopped making cars?" asked The Economist some time ago.

Wiwo sums up: "The industry accounts for just under five per cent of gross domestic product and employs around 770,000 people in Germany. Volkswagen, Mercedes-Benz and BMW have a global turnover of around 600 billion euros and produce 13 million cars a year in 230 plants. They benefit from around 900 automotive suppliers; the three largest - Bosch, Continental and ZF - alone contribute 120 billion euros in sales. Without the automotive industry, the country's innovative capacity would also be weak. Companies in the sector plan to invest more than 220 billion euros between 2022 and 2026, mainly in electromobility, battery technology and digitalisation". 

A fate similar to that of Grundig, Telefunken and AEG is inconceivable. 

For more facts and details on the Chinese manufacturers' product initiaves, European and German manufacturers' plans to achieve cost advantages - especially in the battery sector - and the challenges of winning the hearts of discerning European customers, click links below.

Spoiler alert: the European car industry has not given up!

title: The energy on Day 2 of the Car IT Symposium in Ingolstadt is fantastic! The automotive industry has poured massive capit
text:
💡The energy on Day 2 of the Car IT Symposium in Ingolstadt is fantastic! The automotive industry has poured massive capital into the Software-Defined Vehicle (SDV) vision, and today’s sessions made one thing clear: we are officially moving past the “first era”. 

Three major highlights from this morning:
1️⃣ Florian Kraft (CEO, Renault Germany) on how AI is transforming car manufacturing and the customer experience. Renault is making huge waves right now—the leap in digital experience with vehicles like the Renault 5 is phenomenal.

2️⃣ Werner Koestler (Executive Officer, Pioneer) delivered a compelling look at AI-Enhanced User Experience, redefining how intelligent systems will interact with our senses in the cabin of the future.

3️⃣ The centerpiece of the morning: Markus Rettstatt (VP SDV, Mercedes-Benz Tech Innovation) on "How to build a software-defined company." Markus has been a long-time companion and a steadfast ally for open source, joint development, and industry-shifting initiatives like Eclipse Software Defined Vehicle. He is truly one of the forward thinkers in our space, and his session today was an absolute masterclass in navigating the tension between startup speed and enterprise reality.

A few critical takeaways from his presentation on why architecture is the master key:
👉Curing the "Mechanical Hangover": We cannot build digital products using the physical divide-and-conquer methods of the assembly line. That just creates isolated silos and "big bang" integrations that push feature round-trip times from months to years.
👉Structure > Headcount: The most convenient, but wrong, answer to speed is "we need more developers." Without proper structure, more teams simply cause an exponential explosion in integration complexity.
👉Architecture is Management: If software is the product, architecture dictates the organization (Conway's Law). It is not just an IT artifact; it is the decision-making logic and the risk model. A company only truly becomes software-defined when "architecture beats politics."
👉The "Fast & Right" Matrix: Differentiation doesn't come from speed alone. It comes from establishing an architectural backbone (End-to-End teams, a single mainline, daily backmerges) that allows an organization to be "Fast & Right"—a position that is rare and highly defensible.

Ultimately, the winners of the SDV era won't necessarily be the fastest out of the gate. They will be the ones who stay in control at speed!

Looking forward to discussing these architectural shifts with industry peers this afternoon. To “Make It Real”!

Christoph Aretz Michael Tenschert Holger Cermann Heiko Jung Elisabeth Gill Tobias Meyer Waldemar Schäfer  Claudia Hytrek  

#CarITSymposium #SDV #ArtificialIntelligence #Automotive #Semiconductors #EnterpriseArchitecture #OpenSource #EclipseSDV #TechLeadership

title: "Daimler Truck AG invests in Apex.AI" The startup for scalable, safety-certified software for vehicles and smart machine
text:
"Daimler Truck AG invests in Apex.AI" The startup for scalable, safety-certified software for vehicles and smart machines has thus gained another prominent strategic investor. In today's issue of Handelsblatt, I had the pleasure of contributing to an article, together with Stefan Bratzel, Prof. Dr., that answers the questions: 
* What's behind it? 
* Who are the competitors? 
* How great are the market opportunities for open source-based vehicle software such as that offered by Apex.AI? 

Jan Becker, the CEO and co-founder of Apex.AI, is not only a long-standing expert in autonomous driving but has also been involved in the highly successful Robotic Operating System project (ROS) since the very beginning. With the open source #ros, probably the world's most successful platform for robotic systems (as well as for development of automated vehicles, like in some of the development vehicles of Capgemini Engineering) was created. 
Note: Jan Becker was speaking yesterday at the CAR Center Automotive Research IT-Symposium in Stuttgart-Zuffenhausen -> Great speech and great event from Prof. Dr. Ferdinand Dudenhoeffer and Dirk Wollschläger.

Apex.AI builds on the #ros2 software, and has extended it with automotive relevant features like real-time capability (RT execution, RT data logging, deterministic execution), support for a wide range of automotive HW, etc. and was able to achieve #iso26262 ASIL D certification for the software stack.

By using open-source based SW like the Apex.OS, automotive clients could avoid dependencies (and license costs/revenue sharing) on/with providers of proprietary SW providers (mostly the new tech players in the #automotiveindustry). Also, one major goal of Apex.OS was a high level of abstraction from the basic hardware, thus allowing even greater freedom and flexibility.

For further insights on this disruptive topic, feel free to read the article in today's Handelsblatt and make sure to leave your thoughts on #opensource in automotive and industry in the comments. 

Links below.

title: The commercial vehicle and transport sector is the backbone of our economy - whether it's buses that transport many of u
text:
🚚 The commercial vehicle and transport sector is the backbone of our economy - whether it's buses that transport many of us to work every morning, vans that deliver parcels or heavy-duty trucks that enable the transportation of goods across the continent. Starting next week, IAA TRANSPORTATION in Hanover will focus on the various transformations that the industry is currently going through and the enormous potential that lies within.

 

⚡Decarbonization in the commercial vehicle and off-highway sector is without alternative in the medium term, but is more challenging than in the passenger car sector due to the requirements of range, charging times and payload capacity. E-fuels and biofuels are often seen as promising alternatives for operating existing fleets in a CO2-neutral manner. Fuel cell vehicles are another potential alternative that is already operational. In terms of efficiency and effectiveness, however, the battery-electric drive is unbeaten - there will be no way around it in the medium term.

 

💻Software-defined vehicles (SDVs) are on everyone's lips, but they are not an end in themselves! Rather, SDVs enable cost optimizations as well as more sustainable mobility - and, above all, they allow OEMs to implement new customer functions quickly and over the vehicle's lifetime. For manufacturers, this offers the opportunity to develop and implement new service-oriented business models. Unleashing the potential of generative AI, OEMs will be able to take the customer experience to the next level, also in the commercial vehicle sector.

 

I am already looking forward to discussing these and other topics next week in Hanover. Let's meet!

 Eric Dalla Vecchia Werner Ferreira da Silva Sebastian Tschödrich Christoph Aretz Dr. Philipp M. Haaf 

#IAA24 #Trucks #GenAI #Innovation #Transformation #Sustainability Capgemini Automotive Capgemini Engineering

title: Live from the Car IT Symposium in Ingolstadt! 🚗 What a fantastic event so far. The overarching theme is palpable: “From
text:
Live from the Car IT Symposium in Ingolstadt! 🚗 What a fantastic event so far. The overarching theme is palpable: “From buzzword to imperative: AI reinvents automotive.” 

It’s fascinating to witness the shift in our industry reflected right in the agenda. What started as an IT forum has truly evolved into a fully tech-driven event spanning the complete vehicle stack.

A few key highlights from today’s sessions:
👉The Full Stack: We saw the entire ecosystem represented, bridging the gap from chip architectures with Andreas Hennig (Qualcomm) and Stephan Hönle (SiMa.ai ) to cloud solutions with Salvatore Romeo (IBM).

👉The Driver Experience: I especially enjoyed the keynote from Dr. Céline Laurent-Winter (BMW) on the Connected Digital Vehicle—paving the way for a safer, smarter, and more intuitive driving experience.

👉The Value Chain: Patricia Rennert (CARIAD) perfectly outlined how this transformation means AI is shaping not just the end product, but the complete value chain. And how to create the perfect developer environment, that enables to “move the needle”.

Beyond the insights, this symposium is a phenomenal forum to connect with clients, partners, and friendly competitors. Great catching up with Christian Hort (T-Systems) and Christof Horn (Accenture). While we run our own races in business, we are absolutely united in our core goal: to “make it real” when it comes to AI for our clients.

A massive thank you to the organizers who made this possible, industry veteran Dirk Wollschläger   and my fellow China expert Beatrix C. Keim, for curating such a stellar agenda. And a special shoutout to gold sponsor and local hero, Ole Harms, for supporting this fantastic gathering.

At Capgemini, we are incredibly well-set to drive this transformation. We bring comprehensive know-how—combining deep industry understanding with a full-stack mastery that spans from the chip to the edge device, and straight up to the cloud and user devices.

The change we are seeing doesn't stop at automotive; it’s accelerating across all sectors. If you want to experience what that looks like, come see us at the upcoming Hannover Messe to witness impactful AI in action—both physical and virtual!

Cc: Dr. Nico Wolf Michael Müller Sascha Kaus Heiko Jung Dr. Jochen Thäder Hans Hagen Didjurgeit

title: Yesterday at the Automotive RISC-V Ecosystem Summit hosted by Infineon Technologies in Munich, we saw a clear signal: Op
text:
🚗🧠 Yesterday at the Automotive RISC-V Ecosystem Summit hosted by Infineon Technologies in Munich, we saw a clear signal: Open architectures are on the rise — and they’re here to stay.

The event delivered on all fronts — from strategic insights to deep technical dialogue. A personal highlight: Infineon CEO Jochen Hanebeck opened with a strong message on why architectural choice matters — resilience, cost control, IP boundaries — and how Infineon is building on the success of AURIX to shape the future with RISC-V.

We discussed what it truly takes to bring RISC-V to a safety-critical domain like automotive:
•	Robust tooling & certification paths
•	Long-term software and IP support
•	Migration paths from existing legacy codebases
•	And the role of ecosystems and industry collaboration

It was energizing to reconnect with colleagues like Martin Schleicher, advocating German Association of the Automotive Industry (VDA)’s new open source initiative, joined by Michael Plagge  from the Eclipse Software Defined Vehicle group,  and to see long-time partners in action: Green Hills Software (Arnaud Van den Bossche ), VECTOR Informatik (bravo Dr. Matthias Traub ‘s team), Eva S.  (Quintauris ) — to name just a few.

I stand firmly with colleague Thomas Fleischmann  (Accenture): embracing RISC-V doesn’t mean starting from scratch. The tech base exists (Codasip, Synopsys Inc), design and validation expertise is available (Capgemini Engineering & others), top players like Infineon have a clear strategy, and manufacturing can be secured — in Europe or via trusted global fabs. And, most important maybe, also software engineers will feel quickly right at home on the RISC-V platform to create new applications or port their existing code. 

👉 We need this architectural diversity — not only for automotive, but across industries. The time to accelerate is now.

Thanks again to the Infineon team and Andrea Gallo of RISC-V International  for creating a high-caliber platform for the community. Let’s keep pushing. Europe needs choice. The world needs open innovation.

Francois Babin , Dr. Nico Wolf , Pascal Gouédo , Nicolas Rousseau , Werner Ferreira da Silva , Jochen Bechtold , Philipp Prott , Christoph Jungnickl , Giles Guidicelli 

#RISC-V #Automotive #OpenSource #Semiconductors #Innovation

title: Dispatch from Shenzhen – where the future is already in motion
text:
🇨🇳 Dispatch from Shenzhen – where the future is already in motion

🚀Autonomous robots ride the metro to deliver goods, fleets of unmanned vehicles handle over 100,000 orders daily, robotaxi services operate across the city with millions of autonomous test kilometers, and drones have already completed 900,000+ cargo flights since 2023. 

🤖 Shenzhen is not only the Silicon Valley of the East. It has become a living lab for autonomous logistics, mobility, and the low-altitude economy.

🤝For Europe, the message is clear: integration of European OEMs and industry players into this ecosystem is not optional – it’s a must. I kindly recommend you to have a look on the LinkedIn posts of Mathias Pillin and Christoph Hartung of Bosch  on their views regarding this topic. 

🏎️ And for engineering service providers, the role is evolving fast, as I discussed earlier and see in our daily business. This was also underlined in a recent post by fellow industry colleague Joerg Astalosch of IAV GmbH. 

📞 Even with all the talk about de-risking and market segregation, staying connected is the only way to shape standards, build trust, and co-create solutions. 

🗣️By maintaining dialogue – through research exchanges, industrial partnerships and participation in standards bodies – Europe can both benefit from and help shape the autonomous revolution that is unfolding at scale in China and the world.  

👥 In times of fragmentation, openness and collaboration become competitive advantages.

👉 If you’d like to discuss innovation and strategies to win in automotive – in China or Europe – let’s meet at the upcoming IAA MOBILITY  in Munich at the Capgemini booth in Hall 1b or later at The Autonomous event (from grandmaster Ricky Hudi and his team) in Vienna.  See you there!

title: Live from ELIV 2024! 🌍
text:
🌍 Live from ELIV 2024! 🌍

What an insightful and inspiring day at the #ELIV2024 conference, where the future of vehicle software and electronics is taking shape. The key themes here are truly “what’s next?” in the industry.

A few personal highlights from the first few hours of the conference today:

🔹 AI is everywhere! From development systems to enhancing mobility experiences. I’m especially proud that the paper I had the honor co-authoring with my colleagues Andy Vickers , David Hughes  and Mark Roberts  —“Harnessing Generative AI for Automotive Excellence”—will be presented by Andy today. Our focus? Trustable, dependable, and explainable hybrid AI for critical automotive applications. Also, a standout presentation by Cerence Inc.  ’s Holger Scholl  on AI-powered voice interaction with vehicles—fascinating!

🔹 Semiconductors are shaping tomorrow’s E/E architectures. Fathi El-Dwaik  from BMW Group  gave an excellent talk, reminding me of my recent presentations, for example at the CarIT Congress, —lots of alignment in our thoughts!

🔹 Open source is gaining tremendous momentum in the automotive industry. I am looking forward to the discussions around how we can harness this power effectively.

🔹 Automated driving is another key focus. I’m eagerly awaiting the afternoon discussions on digital homologation and the findings of the joint study led by Asimi Elmar Frickenstein, Uwe Michael  and Alois Knoll  together with Technical University of Munich  and Capgemini Invent . Well done my colleagues Sherif, Dr.-Eng. Hatem and Steffen and thank you for your contributions!

🔹 Speed and agility in the industry were addressed insightfully by Angela Wang  from Neusoft  —always great perspectives on how we could and should move faster.

🔹 Finally, a special mention to Dr. Rolf Zoeller  for his excellent moderation and showmanship—it’s a pleasure to be part of such a well-curated event! Kudos to the whole ELIV - Electronics in Vehicles team!

The future of mobility is right here. 🚗💡

See you in Bonn and maybe also today at our AUGMENTED ENGINEERING lunch !!! Just drop by in the Mariott Hotel across the conference hall and join us for great AI discussions and some nice food!

title: "DeepSeek: A System Shock in the AI Landscape"
text:
"DeepSeek: A System Shock in the AI Landscape"
The global headlines are full of alarmist takes: "China Will Win the AI War" or "Wake-Up Call for the West." Yet behind the hype and hyperbole, there’s a deeper story that demands a nuanced view.

Yesterday, I had the privilege to speak with the NZZ Neue Zürcher Zeitung  about DeepSeek, its groundbreaking advancements, and what they mean for the global (AI) industry.

DeepSeek’s latest model, developed in record time and with remarkable cost efficiency, is reshaping the competitive landscape. Its emergence highlights not just technological prowess but a shift in the global dynamics of innovation, resource optimization, and market leadership.

Key points we explored in the article:
* What sets DeepSeek apart? An efficient approach to AI development with results that rival Western models like GPT-4 while using significantly fewer resources.

* What does it mean for the industry? A potential rethinking of R&D strategies, partnerships, and global competition.

* The strength of open-source innovation: How openness and collaboration could act as a counterbalance to closed proprietary ecosystems.

More importantly, this growing competition in the AI model market is a positive development, especially for Europe. It inspires hope that the race is far from over. With less resource-hungry large language models (LLMs), we can witness a democratization of technology—opening new opportunities for innovation and accessibility. This is what progress for mankind should look like. And what we implement for our customers every day, like with #augmentedengineering.

This is more than just a story about a single company—it’s a story about how innovation ecosystems are evolving, and what that means for businesses, policymakers, and consumers worldwide.

If you're navigating the challenges and opportunities of the AI revolution, this is an important read: https://lnkd.in/d5ByARAh or in the NZZ print edition today.

Capgemini Engineering #innovation Werner Ferreira da Silva Andy Vickers Jochen Bechtold #ai Keith Williams

title: ️Volkswagen's strategic investment in Rivian: a transformative move for the industry?
text:
⚡️Volkswagen's strategic investment in Rivian: a transformative move for the industry?

Volkswagen's announcement of its collaboration with Rivian  has sent ripples through the industry. The venerable Automobil Industrie magazine has delved into the topic with an article, in which I had the pleasure of being quoted, discussing Volkswagen's strategic investment of up to $5 billion in Rivian, the innovative US-based electric vehicle startup.

Here are some insights from the piece:

This collaboration has the potential to be a real game changer for both companies. Volkswagen's investment includes the purchase of $1 billion in convertible notes and plans to form a joint venture with Rivian. The partnership focuses on #software, control units and board net, allowing VW to leverage Rivian's advanced E/E (electrical/electronic) architecture (zonal-oriented hardware/comprehensive software stack).

RJ Scaringe, CEO of Rivian, emphasised the importance of this partnership, not only because of the cash injection, but also because of the potential to leverage economies of scale for Rivian's technology. 

To illustrate the continued evolution of their technology, the latest model update of their R1 vehicle range has reduced the complexity and number of control units from 17 in the first generation to just 7 in the second. This streamlined approach is in line with the future of automotive design, which emphasises efficiency and scalability.

As I highlighted in the article, forming regional partnerships is a strategic move in today's rapidly evolving automotive landscape. Traditional manufacturers face significant challenges in transforming their engineering organisations and integrating new, complex technologies into existing platforms. This collaboration provides a viable solution, giving Volkswagen access to cutting-edge technology while accelerating development timelines.

Pedro Pacheco, an automotive analyst at Gartner,  also highlighted the value of this deal for VW, describing it as "a real bargain" given VW's significant investment in platform development.

Moreover, Volkswagen's strategic collaborations extend beyond Rivian. In China, VW and its software subsidiary Cariad are working closely with XPENG  and SAIC to leverage local expertise and accelerate the development of next-generation E/E architectures. 

These partnerships (VW/Rivian/XPENG or Stellantis/Leapmotor) not only demonstrate the large automakers commitment to innovation, but also highlight the importance of strategic alliances in navigating the complexities of the automotive industry's digital transformation towards software-defined-mobility.

For more insights, read the full article here: https://lnkd.in/dkSx69dq
