title: If you want to understand what will drive the next decade of industry, start with what is on display at embedded world E
text:
If you want to understand what will drive the next decade of industry, start with what is on display at embedded world Exhibition&Conference  World in Nuremberg.

Walking through the fair, one thing becomes clear very quickly: this is where the future stops being abstract.

You can see it, touch it, discuss it. In chips. In sensors. In display technologies. In electronics development. In embedded intelligence at the edge. And in the growing convergence of hardware, software, AI, and systems engineering.

I am proud to be here with my fantastic colleagues of the Capgemini team, showcasing what end-to-end capability in semiconductors, electronics and embedded systems really means in practice.

We are bringing to life a broad range of topics that matter deeply for our clients and for Europe’s industrial future:

🏆Silicon engineering for the capable and sovereign chips of tomorrow.

👐RISC-V showcases and the power of open ecosystems. A big shout-out to Andrea Gallo and the RISC-V International team for driving this movement-don’t miss their presentations here!
 
🦾Physical AI for smart and autonomous systems together with our partner Intel .

🔐Security and quantum-safe cryptography solutions for a world that is becoming more connected and more exposed at the same time.

🤖Embedded systems excellence for advanced applications such as automotive, together with strong ecosystem partners like Qualcomm.  

💡And, across the full lifecycle, AI along the toolchain and engineering process to help clients move with more speed, quality, and confidence.

⛓️‍💥One topic that also comes up again and again in conversations here is supply chain resilience. Rightly so. Because technological strength is not only about innovation. It is also about robustness, sovereignty, and the ability to deliver. And we showcase solutions to achieve this at our booth, too! Say hello to Capgemini Invent ! 

What I particularly like about the atmosphere this year is that the mood is positive, but not naive. There is energy, curiosity, and real interest. Less theory. More engineering reality. Just: make it real!

That is exactly why Embedded World matters.

I am very much looking forward to the many conversations ahead here in Nuremberg and to seeing clients, partners, colleagues, and friends over the next days.

title: From Passenger Cars to Tanks? Why “German Auto” Thinks It’s Time to Look Beyond Industry Borders
text:
From Passenger Cars to Tanks? Why “German Auto” Thinks It’s Time to Look Beyond Industry Borders

The challenges facing automotive suppliers are immense – volatile demand, structural transformation towards electrification, the uphill race toward software-defined vehicles, new competitors from China, geopolitical hurdles, and shrinking margins. In this environment, the industry is searching for orientation and stability. And, somewhat surprisingly, it’s increasingly finding both in an old, but newly energized sector: Defense.

As WirtschaftsWoche compellingly reports, more and more automotive suppliers are venturing into defense – not out of opportunism, but necessity, and with impressive technological depth. According to the report, ZF Group , IAV GmbH , AUTFORCE Automations GmbH  or HIRSCH Engineering Solutions , all of them are leveraging core competencies that are just as critical in military systems as in civilian vehicles – from high-precision drivetrains to robust control units.

I fully agree with my valued colleague Joerg Astalosch , CEO of IAV GmbH (and fellow industry veteran): entering the defense sector is not an easy step, but it’s a logical one for engineering-driven companies seeking a new strategic anchor. The regulatory environment is demanding, timelines are long – but the potential is real.

At Capgemini Engineering, we understand this shift well. While we are often known for automotive engineering excellence, our DNA spans far beyond – from #semiconductors to #aerospace and #defense. We operate across industries, transferring know-how, processes, and safety expertise wherever it’s needed. At scale.

A concrete example for successful cross industry collaboration: our Canadian subsidiary, Microsys Technologies Inc. (I have the pleasure of leading it since nearly a decade), is not only a proven partner in global vehicle safety – it’s also an active supplier to the ordnance systems industry. Our iTANK systems (initially conceived for automotive safety applications) meanwhile support safe and efficient testing and qualification of components for large calibers. In a time where security, sovereignty, and freedom are being redefined, we see this work as a meaningful contribution.

And yes – it is a good feeling to know that my colleagues across Europe, Canada and other countries bring their ingenuity to the table every day, helping our clients develop systems for land, sea, air, and space – systems that keep our continent safe, drive technological progress, and create lasting value for our economy and society.

Defense is not a retreat into the past – it is a strategic leap forward for those ready to chart new territory.

Links to further information and the interesting article in the comments.

At Capgemini we are proud to be a true citizen of the semiconductor industry. From silicon engineering - from ideation to tape-out - our capabilities span the entire spectrum. Whether it's analog and power semiconductors, mixed-signal design, or developing solutions at the most advanced node technology for System-on-Chip (SoC) or complete chiplet designs, we are deeply involved in driving the next generation of semiconductor innovation.

But Capgemini is about much more than silicon. This is where our expertise in electronics engineering, embedded software and industry-specific domains truly shines. We enable companies in sectors such as automotive, aerospace, life sciences and consumer goods to innovate faster and smarter. 

From AI-driven software solutions for autonomous vehicles to digital manufacturing optimization and smart healthcare devices, we deliver transformative solutions that shape the future of technology.

By leveraging our strengths in AI, cloud, and IoT, we help our customers achieve new levels of efficiency, sustainability, and scalability. title: ZF, Brose and Co. Seeking New Partners: German Automotive Suppliers Are Fighting for Their China Business - reports the
text:
🚗 ZF, Brose and Co. Seeking New Partners: German Automotive Suppliers Are Fighting for Their China Business - reports the WirtschaftsWoche. 

For decades, German engineering has enjoyed a privileged position in China — admired for its precision, quality, and innovation. Many automotive suppliers established strong local footprints (I had the chance to be a part of this) and fueled China’s rapid ascent as the world’s largest car market. But times are changing: Western OEMs are losing ground, and Chinese carmakers now expect partners who can move at their pace, speak their language, and co-develop from day one.

Today, success in China requires more than a local office — it requires local DNA. Development-to-SOP timelines for parts shrink from 30 months to 6–10. Sequential development is obsolete. Instead, it’s “Everything, Everywhere, All at Once.”

📌 Industry voices featured in the article:

Peter Fintl, Capgemini: “Chinese carmakers like BYD have their own supplier subsidiaries. This deep integration of certain players makes the competition even tougher."

Kersten Tom Janik, WITTE Automotive: “Chinese customers expect a response within 24 hours — otherwise you’re out.”

Markus Heyn, Bosch Mobility: “In China, we can suddenly develop solutions we couldn’t offer before.”

Stefan Krug, CEO Brose Group: “Chinese OEMs increasingly turn to local suppliers — that’s a major challenge for us.”

Stefan Hartung, CEO Bosch: “Bosch is also one of the innovation leaders in the fiercely contested Chinese market". Over 65% of new mobility orders in 2024 came from software and electrified powertrains.

Roger Dr. Busch, MAHLE: “The innovation speed in China is tremendous. We see further growth potential for ourselves particularly in the areas of electrification and thermal management.”

Klaus Rosenfeld, CEO Schaeffler: “Even Chinese OEMs can’t do everything" There’s still a role for high-tech specialists.

Dr. Juergen Simon, Berylls by AlixPartners: “Chinese suppliers are no longer just growing with domestic OEMs — they’re expanding globally.”

📊 Market signals:
100+ Chinese carmakers are active today.
23+ million passenger vehicles sold annually.
CATL 37.2bn€, HASCO 22bn€ revenues in 2023
Up to 80% of free cash flow for some German suppliers comes from China.

🧭 My take:
Germany’s automotive suppliers have been among the most successful in China — but past success is no guarantee for future relevance. What’s needed now is speed, autonomy, and ecosystem integration. Those who embed themselves as genuine local players — with Chinese pace and decision culture — still have a strong future. 

For more information on how to gain "China Speed" reach out to Yan Chu and me!

🔗 Full article here (in German): https://lnkd.in/dEmKyAnB or in the current print edition.

Werner Ferreira da Silva Jochen Bechtold Christian Hummel Dr. Rainer Mehl

title: 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐜𝐚𝐫 𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐬 𝐨𝐟 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐡 𝐠𝐢𝐚𝐧𝐭𝐬
text:
🚗 🤖 🤯 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐜𝐚𝐫 𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐬 𝐨𝐟 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐡 𝐠𝐢𝐚𝐧𝐭𝐬

In a recent feature in Automobilwoche, I had the privilege of sharing insights together with industry leaders on the tumultuous journey of tech giants in the automotive sector, the preliminary end of Apple's vehicle dreams and the perseverance of Chinese tech companies in the field.

The saga of Apple's Project Titan underscores a key lesson: the path to revolutionising the automotive industry is fraught with challenges far beyond the realm of traditional tech ventures. Despite a decade of effort and (some say) a staggering $10 billion investment, the project's demise serves as a sober reminder of the intricate web of technical, safety and regulatory hurdles that define this sector. "The expertise of our BMW development team has grown over decades. It doesn't happen overnight. And you can't just copy it," says Frank Weber, Member of the Board of Management for Development, in the article.

But the spirit of innovation burns bright, with companies such as Japan's Sony and Chinese giants Huawei and Xiaomi boldly stepping into the spotlight. Sony's collaboration with Honda, powered by Germany's Elektrobit, highlights an interesting detail of the collaboration between tech players and automotive experts. While traditional OEMs approach suppliers with comprehensive specification documents, new players involve partners much earlier in the process. Maria Anhalt, CEO of Elektrobit, describes the approach in such partnerships: "We start with a vision, an idea or an ambition of the new OEMs." 

Xiaomi's ambitious entry with its SU7 electric sedan exemplifies the relentless pursuit of automotive excellence. Lei Jun, Xiaomi Technology's CEO, asserts that "believing in this industry means it's never too late to enter", encapsulating the strategic optimism that drives tech companies' continued interest in automotive ventures.

The journey of tech companies into automotive manufacturing is not just about bringing new vehicles to market. It's a bold quest to redefine mobility, informed by the lessons of past pioneers and the limitless possibilities of tomorrow. As we stand at this crossroads, the question remains: How can technology companies use their expertise to contribute to the automotive landscape without necessarily building cars?

The answer lies in collaboration, innovation and a deep respect for the intricate dance of automotive engineering. As we move forward, let us embrace the challenges and opportunities that lie ahead, and drive towards a future where technology players and the automotive industry come together to redefine the essence of mobility.

To read more about Google's plans for automotive software, Foxconn's next steps in becoming an automaker and more, please visit https://lnkd.in/dfa4mvCZ.

title: The automotive ecosystem is changing rapidly. Pacesetter Capgemini Engineering jumps to #2 in the #ATZ Automotive Engine
text:
The automotive ecosystem is changing rapidly. Pacesetter Capgemini Engineering jumps to #2 in the #ATZ Automotive Engineering Partner Ranking 2022!

I'm holding the print edition of #ATZextra in my hands right now. Although the results were published digitally a few days ago, it's nice to read it "in black and white": Capgemini Engineering has moved up to second place in the ranking. 

* This shows that the company's focus on Software Defined Vehicle, Connectivity, Driving Automation, Sustainable Propulsion Systems and Consulting was absolutely right.

* Whereas last year IAV GmbH, Bertrandt Group, EDAG Group and Capgemini Engineering were within a 10% margin, the field has now thinned out. Capgemini Engineering was able to pull clear of the market leader, AVL List GmbH (with it's powerful test systems and simulation software business), to within a few percentage points.

* In general, it can be said that the effects of the Corona crisis last year still have left their mark on the balance sheets of some market participants. 

* The trend towards takeovers ("IT consultants go engineering", or the acquisition of competencies) also continues unabated. -> Modis bought AKKA, Cognizant took over ESG Mobility, Accenture goes with Umlaut, Ferchau cooperates with M-Plan, etc.

In any case, congratulations to the entire Capgemini Automotive team for this great achievement, for setting trends and the tireless commitment for our clients! 

Especially to our global Automotive leaders Markus Winkler and Alexandre Audoin as well as to our Engineering Leaders William Rozé, Olivier EMORINE, Werner Ferreira da Silva (just to name a few) and all the other great Auto colleagues in Germany like Dennis B., Ralf Blessmann, Luciano Bottoni, Giles Guidicelli, Christoph Jungnickl, Dr. Rainer Mehl, Sven Potthof, and many more colleagues at home and abroad.

For a detailed look at the industry and all the facts/figures read the full article here at Springer Professional:
https://lnkd.in/dECkzpVV

text:

380,000 m². 1,451 vehicles. 181 world premieres. Nearly 1M visitors. But the real story is the technology shift happening right now.

title: AI and the German Mittelstand: A Blueprint for the Future?🇩🇪
text:
🚀 AI and the German Mittelstand: A Blueprint for the Future?🇩🇪

I'm pleased to announce that I had the opportunity contributing with some semiconductor topics to an insightful article in Handelsblatt as part of their series on #AI in the German "Mittelstand". This time, the spotlight is on industry leader Trumpf.

📢 At the start of the article, Prof. Sepp Hochreiter, a leading figure in AI and the father of the LSTM neural network architecture, who now advises Trumpf, was cited urging the Mittelstand not to fail in their AI efforts - “Vermasselt es nicht!” - in order to keep their leading edge.

🔭 Trumpf is in my eyes a perfect example of this journey. Its transformation from a sheet metal fabricator to a leader in industrial laser and semiconductor manufacturing technology is remarkable. Now, Trumpf is on a mission to become the leading provider and user of AI solutions in its industry within the next five years.

⚡️ Trumpf's collaboration with Silicon Valley's SiMa.ai (see images of Krishna Rangasayee , Harald (Harry) Kroeger of Sima and Richard Bannmueller of TRUMPF ) is particularly noteworthy. Together, they're developing cutting-edge AI applications that promises to significantly enhance laser applications in manufacturing, for instance in the production of FC and B electric vehicles. This strategic partnership highlights how a strong focus on software, coupled with innovative hardware such as dedicated AI chips for edge application, is the way forward - an insight I'm proud to have shared in the article.

🚙 The impact of these initiatives is already being felt according to the article. At Mercedes-Benz AG  for example, Trumpf's AI-powered cloud monitoring has significantly reduced the risk of unplanned machine downtime by 80%, saving millions annually - a tangible example to the transformative potential of AI when used correctly.

📈 If you want to know more about how AI can help to transform industries and how to enhance development processes with augmented engineering: my brilliant colleagues like Daniela Rittmeier and Andy Vickers can help you!

title: Beyond survival: is the European industry bold enough to reinvent the future of business?
text:
Beyond survival: is the European industry bold enough to reinvent the future of business?

In a landscape marked by rising costs, geopolitical challenges, evolving consumer demands, and the urgent march towards sustainability and net-zero, businesses are navigating a period of unprecedented transformation. The demands of today require not just an eye on the future but a fundamental reimagining of operations, products, and services to elevate performance across all dimensions.

This week, I had the distinct pleasure of collaborating with esteemed colleagues Alexandre Audoin , Michaela Dr. Schaefer  and Daniela Rittmeier on this challenging topics. Together, we engaged with clients to address these multifaceted challenges, providing insights on optimizing cost structures in product engineering while simultaneously enhancing product performance and operational agility.

A highlight of our discussions this week was exploring the tangible benefits of AI integration across the engineering value chain. With Daniela Rittmaier, we delved into how AI can revolutionize product development—speeding up processes, elevating product safety, and empowering scientists and engineers to reach new levels of productivity. 

These interactions underscored for me the transformative power of Capgemini - integrating excellence in strategy consulting, information technology, and engineering.

title: „The next level has begun“. Automakers are developing new ambitions for automated driving functions. Will it be the busi
text:
„The next level has begun“. Automakers are developing new ambitions for automated driving functions. Will it be the business of the future after all? In today's issue, the leading industry magazine Automobilwoche has a special focus on the topic of automated driving and the strategies of manufacturers, suppliers and chip providers.

* "In Germany, we want to be able to follow a driver ahead on the highway as an intermediate step from the end of 2024 up to about 90 km/h highly automated (Level 3, Highway)." , says Georges Massing , Vice President Automated Driving at Mercedes-Benz AG 

* BMW plans to introduce a Level 3 offering in the current seven-series (G70) by the end of the year "The path to the introduction of Level 3 is clearly marked and testing is underway," confirms Nicolai Martin, Head of Development Automated Driving at BMW Group. And a new L2+ feature that allows hands free driving will be available this summer in the all new 5 series. 

* "The classic suppliers, on the other hand, are coming under even more pressure. But something has also changed in development itself. Modern software is no longer developed in black boxes. The software user, i.e. the manufacturer, must have the entire software in source code," says Apex.AI  founder Jan Becker 

It was a pleasure for me to contribute to this article with some industry analyses. 

In today's issue of AW, you will also find highly interesting reports about the reorganization at Bosch towards software, services and semiconductors, as well as the latest updates from Nvidia and Qualcomm.

Read more here: www.Automobilwoche.de

title: ️🚗 “Xiaomi – the better Apple?” Here’s what the Handelsblatt article didn’t tell you…
text:
🍏➡️🚗 “Xiaomi – the better Apple?” Here’s what the Handelsblatt article didn’t tell you…

First of all, thank you to Handelsblatt and Martin Benninghoff for featuring me with industry insights in their excellent article on Xiaomi’s entry into the automotive arena. The piece rightly underlines how a tech giant is reshaping the race towards electrified and automated mobility.

Yet there’s even more to the story. Beyond speed of execution and daring strategy, Xiaomi’s cars have more areas you should have a look at:

🛠️ Engineering in the details – innovative chassis, electronics, and bodywork; excellent finishing, fit and trim. An area where western OEM's often took compromises in the past-or did not allow radical innovation like in body structures.

🤓 Smart cockpit innovation – the lower windscreen activation, celebrated by BMW as “Panoramic iDrive,” was pioneered by Xiaomi. It is interesting to see what different OEM's are making out of the same projection technology.

🚘 Portfolio shaping – the SU7 Ultra targets the sporty sedan market, while the YU7 comes in the ever-popular SUV shape. Its lines clearly interpret the Ferrari Purosangue – an homage rather than a copy.

📲 Ecosystem integration without lock-in – Beatrix C. Keim Keim notes, the Xiaomi ecosystem could feel like a lock-in. But in practice, the Apple integration works seamlessly – so customers can enjoy the Xiaomi universe without being forced into it.

💺 Interior comfort – from lay-flat seats in the YU7 to options for physical buttons if touchscreens aren’t your preference.

👫 Customer experience – young, motivated, friendly and competent sales staff redefining how cars are introduced to new audiences.

🔋And on batteries: some Western observers see the lack of an in-house cell factory as a strategic risk. I dare to say it matters less than many think. Xiaomi demonstrates excellence in battery pack design – from ultra-fast charging to dynamic energy delivery and efficient packaging. And if there is one Chinese company viewed benevolently in Zhongnanhai, then ensuring cell supply will not be an insurmountable issue.

Professor Edward Tse highlights Lei Jun’s “phenomenal ability to recognize opportunities and build capabilities.” I couldn’t agree more — Xiaomi’s automotive play embodies exactly this Chinese entrepreneurial DNA.

The journey from “Porsche-challenger” headlines to real credibility in Europe will not be easy. But with every detail executed well – from safety standards to user experience – Xiaomi is already changing perceptions of what “Made in China” means.

👉 Xiaomi's slogan: “Innovation for Everyone.” What do you think: will European customers embrace this new challenger?

title: Vertical integration matters. New component strategies at IAA MOBILITY - and why you should care.
text:
Vertical integration matters. New component strategies at IAA MOBILITY - and why you should care.

I was pleased to be asked by EDISON to comment on the new component strategies presented at the IAA - like from Volkswagen Group. 

My view:
“A deeper vertical integration ensures that, in the age of electromobility, one’s own electric models stand out from the competition.”

This reflects a broader shift we are seeing in the industry. For decades, in-house component plants were part of the natural value chain of large OEMs. Later, many of these units were outsourced under the premise of focusing on the “core competence” of car building – with suppliers stepping in, delivering cost efficiency and innovation.

Today, the transformation of our industry demands a redefinition of what core competence means. In an era shaped by sustainability, electrification, and software-defined mobility, the decisive factors are no longer direct injection or dual-clutch gearboxes, but bits and watt-hours.

Volkswagen’s decision to re-emphasize vertical integration – spanning batteries, motors, and power electronics – reflects this paradigm shift. Especially in battery cell development and production, OEMs have realized that cost, charging performance, energy density, and lifetime directly define the customer experience. Building these capabilities in-house is not just logical – it is increasingly indispensable.

Europe has learned that establishing a competitive battery value chain is more expensive and complex than anticipated, with many projects delayed or failed. Those manufacturers who had the stamina to pursue their own cell programs are now in a strong position to secure a long-term advantage.

The same applies to e-drives and power electronics: higher efficiency, improved performance, and greater range can set vehicles apart – provided cost-efficient production can be achieved. Vertical integration thus becomes a way not only to create differentiating products, but also to safeguard employment across the manufacturing footprint. 

👉 Read the full article here: https://lnkd.in/dhrhfMKG

If you are interested in gaining competitive advantage, accelerating your development and optimizing cost - visit us at the Capgemini Automotive booth at IAA in Hall 1b! See you there!

title: What to expect from Shanghai Auto Show 2025: new vehicle concepts, interior innovations, a reality check for automated d
text:
What to expect from Shanghai Auto Show 2025: new vehicle concepts, interior innovations, a reality check for automated driving, non-lithium cell chemistry, ultra-fast charging  for the rest of us, a “comeback” from western OEM’s and much more….

As the doors soon open to this year’s Shanghai Auto Show, we’re once again reminded that “The Year of the Snake” is not just a symbol of transformation—as Burkhard Riering of Automobilwoche recently noted—but of the speed and scale of innovation in mobility.

The coming days promise an impressive showcase of what’s next:
* A little love for the van – more on high-end people movers like the Li Auto Mega and ZEEKR极氪 009 who set new standards in comfort and luxury, signaling a broader trend that’s now catching the attention of German OEMs as well.

* Interior innovation redefined – Chinese OEMs are leading with bold ideas in design, functionality, and quality. As one German executive once said: “Da scheppert nix.”

* Small is Big – Keep an eye on NIO  ’s Firefly, a compact urban EV under 4m with Horizon Robotics  -powered ADAS—set to challenge the Mini and Smart models.

* Battery breakthroughs – From sodium-ion in series production (CATL - Energy Storage Solution Provider) to 10C+ charging perspectives and 5C+ fast charging entering broad adoption—electrification is accelerating on all fronts.

* Driving automation, rethought – A more grounded, safety-oriented approach is emerging in China’s new regulatory framework—avoiding overpromising (and misleading drivers) while still pushing boundaries. 

* Software takes center stage – Not just in features, but in how it’s built. Li Auto’s open-sourcing of its OS could mark a new chapter in collaborative software development. See also what Markus Rettstatt has recently published in analysing what this means for global initiatives like Eclipse Software Defined Vehicle . 

* A Western comeback – BMW’s Neue Klasse, Mercedes’ next-gen CLA, and Volkswagen’s “In China for China” strategy demonstrate strong ambitions to reassert leadership in the world’s largest car market.

* AI in the vehicle – From next-gen infotainment to driver assistance, AI continues to reshape the in-car experience. Notably, Huawei’s H901C AI chip has just entered client delivery.

And finally, the overarching question: What can we learn from “China speed”? What are the key enablers—and how can we collaborate across ecosystems to accelerate innovation responsibly?

I look forward to exploring the topics of engineering acceleration and collaboration in depth at the China Car Symposium, where I’ll join Yan Chu (Capgemini ) and Lijun(Jerry) Zhang of Horizon Robotics in a fireside chat exploring those questions. See https://lnkd.in/ddxzKnYF

Exciting days ahead in Shanghai. Let’s stay curious!

Laurence Noël Dr. Rainer Mehl  Jens Sulek Werner Ferreira da Silva Luciano Bottoni Prof. Dr. Ferdinand Dudenhoeffer

title: Happening now: Driving the Future Symposium
text:
Happening now: Driving the Future Symposium

It’s a great pleasure and honor to be part of this year’s Driving the Future Symposium (DTF) of the Technical University of Munich, Chair of Robotics, Artificial Intelligence and Real-Time System (TUM AIR). A truly unique platform where industry, academia, and policy come together to shape tomorrow’s industries.

The symposium focuses on Architectures & #HPC, #Automation & #AI in Mobility, AI & #Software Engineering, and Testing & Simulation, giving participants access to over 50 scientifically curated research abstracts and posters. It’s an inspiring deep dive into the technologies driving the next generation of mobility and intelligent systems.

In my presentation, I had the opportunity to share insights on the “pulse of the industry”, based on our latest Capgemini Research Institute study “The Software-Driven Mobility Era: Beyond Vehicles.”

A highlight was the welcome address of Dorothee Bär, Federal Minister Bundesministerium für Bildung und Forschung, Technology and Space. A most promising outlook on the research agenda for Germany! 

The discussions here are exceptional — from Prof. Alois Knoll’s vision for future systems, Michael Paulweber (AVL) presenting the Federate Project, and Prof. Juergen Becker (Karlsruhe Institute of Technology (KIT)) and Knut Hufeld (Infineon Technologies) exploring new architectures for in-car computing, to thought-provoking contributions from Maria Anhalt (Elektrobit ), Jochen Langheim (ALFRATEC, longtime STM legend), Marcus Bollig and Martin Schleicher (German Association of the Automotive Industry (VDA)), Michael Plagge  (Eclipse Software Defined Vehicle), Prof. Lutz Eckstein (President VDI, RWTH Aachen University), and Prof. Dr. Ferdinand Dudenhoeffer, who provided a sharp view on the global market and China’s influence. Highly interesting was also the presentation of Shan Jiang (Huawei ) on the highly interesting ADS4 automated driving system. Just to name a few! 

It is the living proof that real progress happens when science and industry meet. When we connect ideas, technologies, and people to move mobility forward.

📘 The Capgemini study “The Software-Driven Mobility Era: Beyond Vehicles” is available for download via the link in the comments as well as further information on the event. 

Thanks to Marie-Luise Neitz and the TUM AIR team for the flawless execution.

title: How can Europe keep up with the global competition for the battery cell of the future? Is the race already lost or do we
text:
🔋 How can Europe keep up with the global competition for the battery cell of the future? Is the race already lost or do we still have aces up our sleeves? 🔋

In the race to a sustainable, electrified society, battery technology is at the heart of the transformation - not only for mobility, but also for Europe's energy transition.

I'm delighted to share an insightful report written by my talented colleagues at Capgemini Engineering , PEM RWTH Aachen University  and Fraunhofer Research Institution for Battery Cell Production FFB. The report, published in #ATZ magazine,  highlights breakthrough approaches to accelerate battery cell development and ensure Europe's competitiveness in the global marketplace.

Key findings and achievements include:
✅ 50% faster time-to-market for battery cell technologies through advanced virtual methods.
✅ Addressing Europe's (up to) 80% cost disadvantage on a per-cell basis compared to Asian manufacturers.
✅ Integrate an end-to-end tool chain, ensuring seamless data transfer across development phases.
✅ Use early thermal and electrochemical simulations to reduce expensive physical prototypes and production delays.

The report also highlights how a robust framework combining digital tools and simulations can enable Europe to compete effectively, adapt to shorter innovation cycles and prioritize sustainability criteria.

Battery technology is the backbone of electrification, and this work shows how Europe can lead through innovation and excellence. 🌍

Congratulations to the authors Dr. Christoph Weissinger, Benedikt Späth , Ruiyan Li and Jens Radtke for their valuable contribution to this critical topic! Their expertise is shaping the future of sustainable mobility and electrification. 🚗⚡

But wait, there is more! The ATZ magazine is always a treasure trove of insights. Beside numerous technical articles on battery technology and propulsion, in this issue you also find a great commentary from Dr. Alexander Heintzel, chief editor, about “Einfach mal machen” - how to do electric mobility right. title: The Future of Semiconductor Manufacturing in Germany: A Critical Juncture
text:
The Future of Semiconductor Manufacturing in Germany: A Critical Juncture

📰 I’m proud to have contributed to the ongoing discussion around semiconductor manufacturing in Germany, as featured in a recent DER SPIEGEL article. The piece highlights the significant challenges and opportunities surrounding the investments by major players like Intel Corporation and TSMC in our country.

🤨 Intel's ambitious plan to invest €30 billion in Magdeburg is both a bold vision and a potential risk, with many questioning whether the economic and strategic returns justify the substantial public subsidies involved. As Clemens Fuest, President of the ifo Institute – Leibniz Institute for Economic Research, points out, these investments are “fragwürdig” (questionable) from his perspective assuming that the production facilities will operate without significant R&D activities in Germany. I already hear the outcry of semiconductor experts about this statement...

📢 However, from my perspective, the strategic importance of these investments cannot be overstated. It is much more than a reaction to the semiconductor shortage during the COVID-19 pandemic. As I mentioned in the article, “Industrial policy has taken on new significance in a world where geopolitical risks are rising.” The ability to produce advanced semiconductors in Europe is crucial, not only for our economy but also for our technological sovereignty. 

🦾 The growing role of artificial intelligence (AI) is amplifying the need for cutting-edge chips. The modern semiconductors planned for production in Magdeburg could power the next generation of AI applications, from autonomous vehicles to sophisticated industrial machinery. “When Intel announced its factory in Magdeburg, ChatGPT didn’t even exist,” I noted in the article, highlighting the pace of change.

🚥 It's also critical that Germany establishes and follows a clear national strategy for its role in the global semiconductor industry. As Jan-Peter Kleinhans  from”the think tank interface (formerly SNV)  emphasized, “Germany must decide what role it wants to play in the global semiconductor ecosystem, aligning our national goals with the broader European and global context.”

⚡️ Germany has the potential to enhance its position in the semicon industry—delivering “Spitzentechnologie made in Germany.”

For further insights into the local aspects of these developments in the region of Silicon Saxony , I recommend reading the insightful posts of Frank Bösenberg.

🔎 I invite you to read the full article in “Der Spiegel” to explore these issues further either in this weeks print edition or online here: https://lnkd.in/dEfGemMz

title: How to accelerate automotive transformation? The answer starts with partnership.
text:
How to accelerate automotive transformation? The answer starts with partnership.

Transformation is a top priority for the vast majority of the well-established OEMs, especially in Europe. But what is the best way to approach and succeed in transformation?

There is no simple or proven answer – every organization is different and is forging its own path to transformation.

But one thing is clear – many of the topics that we bundle conveniently under the umbrella of transformation, such as re-architecting and re-designing vehicles for the electric, software-defined age, are simply too big (or risky) to tackle alone.

To minimize the risk and accelerate the process, partnership is key.

This is my view, and I’m glad to see it shared in an insightful new white paper from our friends at Bosch : “Accelerating automotive transformation through partnerships”

Covering a variety of topics, related to the major changes taking place in the automotive landscape, it encourages a focus on four key priorities:

1️⃣ Think local, win global
Moving from one-size-fits-all strategies to market-specific solutions, developed on the basis of customer needs.

2️⃣ The essential shift for SDV
It’s about more than just software: everybody needs to embrace fundamental operational and cultural shifts to meet the demands of SDV development.

3️⃣ No AI, no future: 
AI will be the defining factor when it comes to cost-efficiency, speed, and developing the crucial competitive edge.

4️⃣ Partnerships are key: transformation is a journey, and collaboration within a growing ecosystem is the key to reaching new horizons.

It’s a great read for automotive professionals and enthusiasts alike and one that I’m sure will provide valuable guidance and insight to players right across the automotive and mobility ecosystem as they embark or seek to accelerate their own transformation journeys.

You can download it here: 
https://lnkd.in/dEtzEacn

title: electronica Celebrates 60 Years & SEMICON Europa Shines Once Again! 🎉
text:
🎉 electronica Celebrates 60 Years & SEMICON Europa Shines Once Again! 🎉
 
This past week, I had the pleasure of attending two of the most impactful events in the electronics and semiconductor industries on behalf of Capgemini : electronica and SEMICON Europa 2024. Here’s my recap of the key highlights:
 
📅 60 Years of electronica 
From its humble beginnings in 1964 to becoming one of the world’s most significant trade fairs for electronics, electronica has come a long way. This year's event was no exception—Munich was the place to be, with all halls fully booked and the atmosphere buzzing with energy. It truly was a treasure trove of everything electronics, bringing together innovators, visionaries, and experts from around the globe.
 
🎤 SEMI Europe: A Packed Agenda of Insights 
As always, SEMICON Europa offered more than one could ever cover in just a few days, necessitating some careful cherry-picking. I was particularly drawn to tracks focused on: 
• Silicon Ecosystem & Business 
• Advanced Packaging 
• Fab Management 
These sessions delivered deep dives into the industry's hottest topics, and the quality of speakers was simply outstanding.
 
🔍 Personal Highlights:
 
1️⃣ #AI Everywhere 
AI is no longer a niche technology—it’s permeating every aspect of electronics. From novel chip design approaches to new AI-computing specific components making data centers more power efficient, to System-on-Chips (SoCs) incorporating AI accelerators for on-device computing. These advancements are enabling AI at the edge, reducing power consumption, cutting data traffic, enhancing privacy, and opening new use cases. The potential is enormous!
 
2️⃣ Sustainability in Semiconductors 
A standout moment for me was a thought-provoking presentation by Philippe Quinio of STMicroelectronics, on sustainability across the entire semiconductor lifecycle. It’s clear that the industry is prioritizing green practices more than ever before.
 
3️⃣ Data-Driven Fab Optimization 
A particularly insightful presentation was given by Sarah Fischbach of Bosch Semiconductor Manufacturing Dresden. It was fascinating to see how in-house research is driving advancements in yield management. It left me thinking about how this aligns with market-leading yield software solutions from players like DR YIELD.

4️⃣ Chiplets Stealing the Show 
Chiplets were a hot topic this year. Notable presentations like "Meeting the Demands of Future Computing with Chiplets and Advanced Packaging" by James C. Sexton of IBM and Bart Plackle of imec  captured everyone’s attention. Of course, it was a privilege to join the panel discussion with Dr. Andreas Aal,  Dr. Heike Riel and Prof. Dr. Albert Heuberger to share insights on this game-changing technology.

title: Semiconductors are not just the backbone of digital transformation—they are the strategic lever for Europe’s sovereignty
text:
🌍 Semiconductors are not just the backbone of digital transformation—they are the strategic lever for Europe’s sovereignty, competitiveness, and resilience.

Today I had the pleasure of attending the Bayerische Halbleiterkongress in Munich, together with my colleagues Dr. Christoph Jungnickl and Dr. Nico Wolf. The place for the key players from government, industry and academia!

🔹 It was reassuring to hear clear, committed messages from political leaders such as Dr. Markus Wittmann, Prof. Dr. Angelika Niebler and Dr. Beate Baron: the semiconductor sector is of national and European interest, and long-term support will continue to be a cornerstone of industrial policy.

🔹 Laith Altimime  from SEMI Europe reminded us powerfully: semiconductors are indispensable to modern economies—and to virtually every innovation frontier.

🔹 The afternoon brought a wealth of technological insight:
	•	Michael Heckmeier (Siltronic AG) explored advances in materials science.
	•	Dr. Kurt Stadlthanner (Siemens Healthineers) gave an eye-opening talk on Cadmium Telluride for medical imaging.
	•	A special highlight: Prof. Albert Heuberger  (Fraunhofer IIS) presented the new APECS Pilot Line for advanced packaging and heterogeneous integration—positioned at the heart of the Research Fab Microelectronics.
I fondly recall our last panel discussion together—alongside Dr. Dr. Heike Riel  of IBM Research—on chiplet technology and the future of modular semiconductors. His vision remains as sharp and relevant as ever.
	•	Prof. Jörg Schulze  (Fraunhofer IISB ) laid out the path for industrialization of Ultra-Wide Bandgap materials such as Aluminium Nitride and Gallium Oxide—materials that may well define the next era of power electronics.

👏 A heartfelt thank you to the Bayerisches Staatsministerium für Wirtschaft, Landesentwicklung und Energie and the Bavarian Chip Alliance for orchestrating such a forward-looking event. 

📣 A special mention goes to Staatsminister Hubert Aiwanger  whose keynote was both memorable and engaging—and who made time to actively listen to voices from the industry.

🔗 Program link: https://lnkd.in/djUzM2nH

More info on Capgemini Semiconductor Services: https://lnkd.in/d7snWGRJ

#Semiconductors #EuropeanSovereignty #Chiplets #PowerElectronics Capgemini Engineering  #Innovation

title: BYD’s “God’s Eye” ADAS/AD System—A Game Changer for the Industry?
text:
BYD’s “God’s Eye” ADAS/AD System—A Game Changer for the Industry?

I recently spoke with Automobilwoche  about BYD’s latest announcement, discussing its impact on the automotive ecosystem and the evolving technology roadmap in China. 

BYD has sent a shockwave through the industry by declaring that its “God’s Eye” ADAS/automated driving system will become standard across nearly all of its vehicle lineup—including entry-level models. Even the budget-friendly Seagull, priced below €10,000 (in China), will feature a version of this technology.

This move is not just about democratizing autonomous driving—it’s a bold step toward reducing reliance on Western suppliers and accelerating China’s self-sufficiency in automotive technology. 

Several key factors make this particularly significant:

↕️ Vertical Integration & Localized Supply Chains – BYD has long championed in-house development, from batteries to semiconductors. Extending this to ADAS and automated driving tech reduces dependence on Western Tier-1 suppliers.

🧑‍💻 Software-Defined Competitive Edge – While hardware is crucial, software determines the real battle in automation. BYD’s move suggests a China-first approach in perception, decision-making, and AI-driven driver assistance/interaction—pushing Western algorithms and platforms further out of the equation.

🏅 Cost Leadership at Scale – Bringing a (from the hardware perspective) strong ADAS also to sub-€10,000 vehicles is a strategic cost and volume play. By leveraging China’s domestic semiconductor and AI ecosystem, BYD is outpricing and outscaling competitors, potentially forcing a rethink of premium ADAS pricing models globally.

💪 A Shift in Industry Power Dynamics – China is no longer just a fast follower but an innovation leader in ADAS and automation for volume models. Instead of adopting foreign technologies, Chinese OEMs are setting new industry benchmarks—challenging the dominance of Western tech players. This is well in line with China’s overarching technology strategies, like “Made in China 2025” which I have talked about earlier. 

European and Western automakers must soon rethink their premium pricing strategies for higher ADAS features, as customers will start expecting these capabilities as standard. 

Yet, the race is far from over—while BYD’s hardware push is impressive, the real battleground remains software. High automation levels and safe, intuitive user experiences still require advanced AI integration, an area where the competition remains fierce.

What’s your take—does BYD’s strategy mark a new Tesla moment for the industry, or is this just the next step in an ongoing race?

Find the article here: https://lnkd.in/dA-nRsY8 "God's Eye": Wie BYD den Wettbewerb ums autonome Fahren aufwirbelt 

#ADAS #AutonomousDriving  #Innovation #SoftwareDefinedVehicle

title: The future of AI will not live in the cloud alone. It will move, sense, decide, and act in the real world.
text:
The future of AI will not live in the cloud alone. It will move, sense, decide, and act in the real world.

That was very much the spirit of a great conversation today at embedded world Exhibition&Conference, where my colleague Kevin Cloutier and I spoke with John Healy of Intel about the rise of physical and embodied AI and what it will mean for industry.

What made the exchange particularly valuable was the quality of the discussion. We spoke with the perspective of a trusted technology advisor, not just about products, but about the bigger picture: how physical AI will shape industrial systems, how it will drive the next generation of embedded architectures, what matters in the boardroom when making technology decisions, and what engineering teams need to master at the edge in AI and robotics.

A few things stood out clearly. As AI moves into machines, robots, and industrial environments, the challenge is no longer only about models. It becomes about system design, latency, determinism, power efficiency, software integration, safety, and the strength of the ecosystem behind it.

That is why leaders who want to shape tomorrow need to look beyond the beaten path. They need to evaluate open ecosystems carefully and understand which platforms can truly support the next generation of intelligent systems.

In this context, Intel’s latest portfolio direction around embedded, edge AI, and robotics deserves close attention, including the strong momentum around its newest processor platforms, Panther Lake and Bartlett Lake, now coming into focus here at embedded world.

Curious to see the latest Intel embedded processors in action? Visit our robotics demo at the Capgemini booth 4-508.

A video from the discussion will be released soon.

Stay tuned.

And big thanks to all the colleagues for their magic to make it happen!

title: "Own chips or the new strategy of the car industry", reports Handelsblatt today. Tesla was the pioneer, Volkswagen AG, G
text:
"Own chips or the new strategy of the car industry", reports Handelsblatt today. Tesla was the pioneer, Volkswagen AG, General Motors, BMW Group, Mercedes-Benz AG and now Ford Motor Company are following suit. More and more companies are networking and ordering directly from producers of #semiconductors. And are thus also becoming competitors of their previous suppliers.
 
I had the great pleasure of making some assessments of the developments in the #automotiveindustry for the article.
 
* Tesla uses own silicon for FSD (Fab Samsung Electronics) slightly modified Ryzen CPU's and standard Radeon GPU 's from AMD in their latest computing platform.
* BMW has announced a partnership with Qualcomm 
* Daimler relies on NVIDIA 
* Volkswagen / CARIAD has made computing platforms a priority and development goal
* GM just announced a partnership with Wolfspeed  for #SiC #powerelectronics components and wants to develop a unified processor family to significantly reduce complexity and cost.
* Ford has reached an agreement with semiconductor manufacturer GlobalFoundries (GF) to source chips directly and develop its own semiconductors in the future.
* Until now, the automotive industry has generally used semiconductors from manufacturers such as Renesas Electronics,  STMicroelectronics, Infineon Technologies, Texas Instruments, NXP Semiconductors or Bosch, which develop and manufacture components specially tailored to the high demands of the automotive industry, including extensive software and hardware platforms.
 
For more details on what this means for the industry, which talents and partnerships they need, for instance with ip-providers like Arm or chip design-houses and semiconductor/electronic service providers, please read the article here online https://lnkd.in/eKwfp8jt or in today's print edition.

title: Day 2 at the China Auto Show in Beijing: technology, emotion, and a little bit of fun.
text:
🚗 Day 2 at the China Auto Show in Beijing: technology, emotion, and a little bit of fun.

After sharing some facts and figures from the show yesterday, today it is more about impressions from the floor.

First highlight for me personally: seeing the Peugeot Sport 9x8 FIA World Endurance Championship car here in Beijing. What a beautiful machine. Thank you, Alain Favey and team Peugeot! Even better: Capgemini is a sponsor of the team. As someone who loves engineering, racing, and technology, this genuinely made me proud. Thank you, Laurence Noël for making this possible! 

Go, Team Peugeot Sport! 🏁

And of course, no auto show visit would be complete without my now almost annual picture of a “fluffy car”. This year: My Little Pony themed. Automotive marketing can be serious, but sometimes it should also make people smile.

Beyond the emotional highlights, a few things really stood out:

1. Battery and charging technology is moving from promise to proof.
BYD did not only show its latest battery technology as a static exhibit. They demonstrated live charging at -35°C, from 20% to 97% in 12 minutes. That is impressive, especially because cold-weather charging remains one of the real-world pain points for EV drivers.

2. Design confidence is rising fast.
Denza showed a beautiful open-roof sports car. The message is clear: Chinese brands are no longer only competing on cost, software, or battery performance. They are increasingly competing on emotion, desirability, and brand experience.

3. There is no Chinese auto show anymore without robots.
The XPENG robot was a good reminder that the automotive world is increasingly connected to the broader field of physical AI. Vehicles, robots, smart factories, autonomy, simulation, sensors, and AI are becoming part of the same technology conversation. What a great continuation of the Hannover Messe a few days ago and Capgemini’s robotics excellence at the show (Kudos Kevin Cloutier, Alexandre Embry !) 

My verdict from Day 2:
China’s automotive industry is not only moving fast. It is also becoming more confident, more emotional, and more integrated across mobility, energy, software, and robotics.

And yes, it still has room for a pink fluffy pony car.

Dr. Rainer Mehl Luz G. Mauch Mikhail Bykov Rino Ariganello Dr. Michael Dinkel Mike Welsh

title: Europe’s Electronics Backbone at Risk – But Not Beyond Repair
text:
🇪🇺 Europe’s Electronics Backbone at Risk – But Not Beyond Repair

Few components are as foundational to modern technology as printed circuit boards (PCBs) — the quiet enablers behind every moving vehicle, medical device, and defense platform. Yet as Handelsblatt highlights, the PCB industry in Germany is approaching a tipping point.

🏭 From 561 local producers in 2000, fewer than a third remain. Even Würth Elektronik Group, a stalwart of the sector, is closing its Schopfheim site. Europe’s market share in PCB manufacturing has fallen from 20% to 2%. In contrast, China dominates — with cost advantages, state support, and global reach.

Why this matters:

PCBs are more than components. They are strategic assets at the core of Europe’s industrial and societal resilience. Without them, our sovereignty in sectors like mobility, aerospace, defense, and healthcare is compromised.

What industry leaders are saying:
* Daniel Klein (Würth): “It hurts to shut down the factory – but it was too large for today’s market.”
* Clemens Otte (ZVEI e. V.): “What’s in the coalition agreement must now be implemented consistently.”
* Thomas Michels (ILFA GmbH): “Chasing the cheapest source is no longer sustainable. We need a mindset shift in boardrooms.”

My contribution:
“We must not abandon this industry. But greater independence from Asia will cost money. Coordinated EU-wide action is key to forming a powerful and resilient European electronics ecosystem.”

But let’s be clear:
👉 This is not a call for closed borders or isolationism.
👉 It is a call for an intelligent rebalancing — aligning global scale and efficiency with regional resilience for components that matter most to Europe’s autonomy and stability.


What’s needed:
* Strategic Procurement: European industries must treat PCBs as strategic infrastructure, not commodity spend.
* Policy Reinforcement: Support must extend beyond semiconductors to include the broader electronics value chain.
* Ecosystem Coordination: A stronger, integrated European industrial base through collaboration, innovation, and shared ambition.

At Capgemini, we’ve long advocated for a re-industrialized Europe - digital, sustainable, and resilient.
👉 Capgemini POV: Reindustrializing Europe https://lnkd.in/dQePMyFE
Resilient supply chains as a foundation for global competitiveness
👉 Capgemini Research Institute / Expert Perspectives: Supply Chain Resilience https://lnkd.in/dH2GFKcX

If semiconductors are the brain of modern systems, then PCBs are the nervous system. It’s time to ensure both are secure, smart — and sovereign.

Let’s move forward!

Link to Handelsblatt article: https://lnkd.in/dXbW343j 

Werner Ferreira da Silva Jochen Bechtold Christoph Jungnickl Phil Davies Jörg Junghanns
author: Peter Fintl

title: At #CES2025: AI and Software-Defined Everything—Will We Have Enough Chips to Power the Future?
text:
At #CES2025: AI and Software-Defined Everything—Will We Have Enough Chips to Power the Future?

Last week at CES, the epicenter of innovation, it became once again clear that semiconductors are the critical enabler of the next wave of technological transformation. From AI-driven solutions to software-defined vehicles, these tiny chips are the backbone of our digital-first world.

Against this backdrop, we had the honor of unveiling Capgemini’s latest semiconductor study. It was my pleasure to discuss the insights from this research work with the Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung (FAZ). The report sheds light on the challenges the semiconductor industry faces as demand for these critical components continues to skyrocket.

📊 Key Insights from the Study across hundreds of industry executives from around the globe:
•	Global semiconductor demand is expected to grow 29% by 2026, driven by AI and 5G, far outpacing current production growth.
•	Generative AI alone is fueling a 15% surge in demand for high-performance AI chips in the next 12 months.
•	Geopolitical tensions, reliance on dominant players like TSMC (60% market share), and limited access to resources create supply chain vulnerabilities. Three in five downstream companies already express doubts about supply adequacy over the next two years.

🏗️ Investment Shifts:
In response, manufacturers are rethinking their strategies:
•	74% plan to increase investments in the U.S., supported by the CHIPS Act.
•	59% will expand operations in the EU, incentivized by the EU Chips Act.

The question remains: Can the semiconductor industry overcome these challenges fast enough to power a world increasingly defined by AI and software?

🔗 Strategic Imperatives for the Future:
To ensure resilience and sustainability, the industry must:
1.	Collaborate globally, embracing ecosystem thinking to navigate uncertainty.
2.	Invest in innovation, from 3D chip stacking to silicon photonics.
3.	Reimagine supply chains, prioritizing regionalization and diversification to mitigate geopolitical risks.

At Capgemini, we are committed to helping our clients address these pressing challenges. Through cutting-edge research, strategic partnerships, and innovative solutions, we aim to drive sustainable growth.

and the study https://lnkd.in/dWaxkP6N).

title: "Billions for the material of the future": semiconductor giants invest heavily in silicon carbide (SiC) technology for #
text:
"Billions for the material of the future": semiconductor giants invest heavily in silicon carbide (SiC) technology for #electricvehicles. Handelsblatt reported on the background to this investment initiative by the major players STMicroelectronics , Infineon Technologies , Wolfspeed  and Bosch . I was allowed to contribute with an industry analysis. 

The SiC market leader ST (according to its own information about 50% market share), for example, plans to double its production capacity for SiC by 2024, including the start of production of 200mm wafers. 

Competitor Wolfspeed has built its own 200mm Fab in Mohawk Valley, NY, for entering into this production technology.  This will enable players with significant economies of scale over current 160mm lines. 

Bosch has also recognized SiC as a key technology, as SiC-based components enable between 7 and 12% more range in electric vehicles compared to purely silicon-based components. Higher switching frequencies / lower switching losses and increased thermal conductivity of SiC components are the enablers for more efficient designs. 

For instance, the drive module of the NIO ET7, pictured below, is an example for compact, high power density, efficiency design. To illustrate this, SiC-based inverters reach over 65kW/kg inverter as where standard Si based IGBT designs are around 25 to 40kW/kg. Lower switching losses also mean that the thermal management system can be designed smaller and lighter. Also the BorgWarner SiC inverter module will soon be found in German vehicles. 

For this reason, manufacturers of base materials such as ROHM Co., Ltd.  / SiCrystal GmbH  are also expanding their capacities accordingly. 

Overall, according to Yole Développement , the SiC market will grow to over USD 6 billion in 2027 (30% YOY). 

Further information can be found in the Handelsblatt print edition. 

For more information on how #semiconductor #technology can be mastered by the #automotiveindustry reach out to us. My colleague Shiv Tasker and I are happy to discuss this with you. 

Picture credit: Handelsblatt, Nio, BorgWarner

title: The manager magazin describes Dr. Dirk Abendroth, CEO of CUSTOMCELLS® , as the "master of super batteries". In a portrai
text:
The manager magazin describes Dr. Dirk Abendroth, CEO of CUSTOMCELLS® , as the "master of super batteries". In a portrait of the former Continental and BMW Group manager that is well worth reading, it is explained how he wants to turn a specialist for cell prototypes and small series into a manufacturer of high-performance cells for vehicles ("Cellforce" with Porsche, around 15,000 vehicles per year with 1.3GWh) and electric aircraft, as well as continuing to build battery factories with up to 40GWh.

Customcells can draw on enormous knowledge. The founders, Leopold König and Torge Thönnessen, come from the sphere of the renowned Fraunhofer Institute for Silicon Technology ISIT  and started the company in 2012 as a spin-off.

A leading German player in the hotly contested global battery market? Customcells' course is not without risk. The established battery giants such as CATL, BYD, LG and Samsung are disproportionately stronger financially; the major vehicle manufacturers are also pushing into the business themselves. Volkswagen, for example, wants to bring the supply of battery cells into its own value chain through its subsidiary PowerCo with up to eight 40-GWh factories, following the example of Tesla. And how much the auto industry will demand the supposed supercells from Customcells remains to be seen.

For further details I recommend you to have a look at the article here (m+): https://lnkd.in/dr2fv2rT

It was a great pleasure to contribute to this article with some industry analysis.

Capgemini #innovation Capgemini Engineering #consulting #batterytechnology #automotiveindustry #electricvehicles

title: Leading the Charge in Automotive Electrification 🌍
text:
🚗🔌 Leading the Charge in Automotive Electrification 🌍

In my recent interview with Automobilwoche , I discussed the significant challenges and opportunities in the automotive industry's transition to electric vehicles. Western manufacturers face fluctuating demand, the dual need for ICE/plug-in and BEV technologies, intense cost pressures and growing competition from new entrants, particularly from China.

To be successful as an industry, we need to focus on:

	1.	Better collaboration: Cooperation between manufacturers is currently rather weak, hampered by different ideas on product designs, technical requirements and cost-effective manufacturing. This is particularly problematic for entry-level BEVs in Europe, which are currently largely absent from manufacturers’ portfolios.

	2.	Cost efficiency: There are big savings (and efficiencies of scale) to be made in smart engineering, software, batteries and optimised manufacturing processes and locations.

Stellantis is setting an interesting example with its joint venture with Leapmotor, bringing Chinese technology to Europe and producing new vehicles locally. This approach promises to save time, lower costs and reduce risk.

We need to adopt such collaborative strategies (for software e.g. Eclipse Software Defined Vehicle) , streamline our processes and form strategic partnerships to lead in the future of mobility.

Read more in my full interview with Automobilwoche here: https://lnkd.in/dsKWKAMf

Also noteworthy from the current print edition of Automobilwoche: 

* the report on the Stellantis / Leapmotor cooperation
* news on the evolving battery landscape in Europe, especially on the new “PreFab” and “FFB”, the research factory for battery cells (shout out to Achim Kampker , Heiner Heimes  ,  Michael Müller , Marcus Fiege , Christoph Weissinger )
* a commentary from Burkhard Riering titled “Wanting too much too quickly” about the current woes of the established industry: “Change with a sense of proportion and an understanding of what customers really want is what counts now. In the coming decades, the majority of new registrations will be electric cars with a long range, high performance and fast charging times. You have to be well prepared. But to make money today, everything has to be part of the portfolio.”

I encourage you to have a look inside the edition!

title: "After the chip crisis is before the crisis" - lessons learned or forgotten?
text:
"After the chip crisis is before the crisis" - lessons learned or forgotten?

I'm honored to be featured in the venerable Handelsblatt alongside leading voices such as Kurt Sievers (NXP Semiconductors) and Jochen Hanebeck (Infineon Technologies) discussing the disruptions caused by #semiconductor shortages during the pandemic and the risk of history repeating itself.

🔎 Key points from the article:

During the pandemic, production lines around the world ground to a halt due to shortages of critical components. A recent study by the ZVEI e. V. German Electrical and Electronic Manufacturers' Association illustrates the economic impact of such crises: the German automotive industry alone has lost €99 billion in sales between 2021 and 2023. In addition to lost sales, companies faced rising procurement costs, costly redesigns of electronic systems, and significant investments in supply chain task forces to manage the crisis.

Now, leading semiconductor manufacturers are warning of new shortages on the horizon:

- Short-term order risks: Kurt Sievers (NXP) highlights how some parts of the supply chain continue to engage in tactical, last-minute purchasing despite the long lead times required for chip production (12-20 weeks).

- Lessons unlearned: Jochen Hanebeck (Infineon) points out that some stakeholders seem to have forgotten the hard lessons of the pandemic, risking a repeat of supply chain disruptions.

- Investment gaps: With semiconductor demand currently weak, manufacturers are cutting back on investment in new production facilities - decisions that could lead to capacity shortages if #automotive demand recovers quickly. Could Chinese chips be an alternative? 

💪 Resilience through collaboration:

Efforts to build resilience are underway. Companies such as Bosch have taken proactive steps, including building safety stocks and leveraging own chip production. Automakers are also engaged in long-term demand planning. However, many suppliers under financial pressure are unable to maintain large inventories, commit to advance payments or co-invest in capacity.

In my remarks, I emphasized that risks such as natural disasters/infrastructure  (water scarcities in Taiwan, fires in Japan, blackouts in the U.S., we've had it all), geopolitical conflicts (export bans on machinery and materials), and trade barriers (tariffs on components) remain underappreciated and could exacerbate supply chain vulnerabilities.

📈 The way forward:

The urgent need for improved demand planning, long-term sourcing agreements, and greater collaboration between OEMs, suppliers, and semiconductor manufacturers remain. Strategic partnerships can secure supply chains and drive innovation in this complex, high-stakes industry.

As we look to 2025, these challenges will be in focus, including at CES in Las Vegas, where the new Capgemini Research Institute report "Semiconductor Trends Shaping the World" will be launched next week.

Read more and see the links in the comments!

title: "We don't want to be the cheapest, we want to be the best".  Chinese carmaker BYD has carefully planned its tiger's leap
text:
"We don't want to be the cheapest, we want to be the best".  Chinese carmaker BYD has carefully planned its tiger's leap into Europe, launching six cars to gain a substantial foothold. But the Chinese don't stop there; they want to be among the top five most successful car manufacturers in Europe. The German News magazine “Stern” reports in detail on the background, the group's efforts in research and development and its search for the right channel partners to succeed in Europe.

It was a pleasure for me to contribute to this article with my analysis on technology and industry.

Some key facts from the article:
📈 The goals of BYD's president and founder, Wang Chuanfu (pictured below right), are big: To overtake Toyota as the world's largest automaker by 2025. 

🇪🇺The window for success in Europe is two years," says Brian Young of BYD Europe.

🚗 Key vehicles will be the HAN e-sedan (with innovative LFP battery technology) and the new version of the TANG electric SUV (to be seen at IAA MOBILITY in Munich in a few weeks).

💼 In addition to the already available Atto 3 model, which will compete with the VW ID.4 models, entry-level offerings are also expected. With the BYD Dolphin and BYD Seagull, the Chinese electric specialist has suitable models in its portfolio which, once adapted to European requirements, could provide serious competition for the future entry-level models of European manufacturers. Think of the e-mobility segment around €20,000 or even less.

⚡️ BYD also has two highlights in its model pipeline for the new Yangwang luxury brand: the U8, a competitor to the Land Rover Defender, is due to be launched this year. The U9 hypercar, which is due to be launched next year at the latest, will also cater to the speed crowd. Yangwang's vehicles are based on a technology platform that features 4 independent electric motors (enabling torque vectoring, tank turns and other gag’s) and an innovative chassis control system that (according to announcements) rivals - and in some respects surpasses - the best the industry has to offer.  With German designer Wolfgang Egger at the helm, the models have been designed with international tastes in mind.

💡 BYD spent USD 3 billion on research last year. 

💴 Last year, BYD sold 1.86 million electric vehicles, an increase of 208.6 per cent. The upward trend continues. In the first half of this year, more than 1.25 million units have been sold, an increase of 95.78 per cent over the same period last year. Outside China, there were 74,300 vehicles, which is more than the total for 2022. "And the second half of the year will be even better," said Li Yunfei, Division General Manager BYD in the article.

Read more in the Stern article here: https://lnkd.in/d4FHc-EX

title: "Five alarm signals for the (German) car industry" - the leading industry newspaper Automobilwoche sheds light on the si
text:
"Five alarm signals for the (German) car industry" - the leading industry newspaper Automobilwoche sheds light on the situation of German car manufacturers - because despite a good portfolio of #electricvehicles, they hardly play a role on a global scale so far. It was a great pleasure to be able to assess the situation with industry experts such as Stefan Bratzel, Prof. Dr. from Center of Automotive Management (CAM), Friederike Piper from Transport & Environment (T&E) Germany and Felix Kuhnert from PwC.

The main challenges are in the areas of:
1) Shortage of chips and other parts
Due to delivery problems with #semiconductors and other components, as well as production planning errors, nowhere near as many cars can reach customers as planned. 

2) Lack of software expertise
While German manufacturers were the undisputed technology leader in the combustion engine, this is no longer a foregone conclusion in the new world, to say the least.

3) Strong competition in China
If you look at the sales lists for electric cars, only VW's ID.4 is in the top 20; otherwise, Chinese brands dominate here alongside Tesla. This also applies to the luxury segment, which used to be dominated almost exclusively by Audi, BMW and Mercedes.

4) Strong competition from China
With NIO, BYD and Great Wall Motor Deutschland GmbH, after Aiways or Polestar, further competitors are ready to gain a foothold in Europe. And the chances are good after German manufacturers make room in the lower segments.

5) Shrinking manufacturing in Europe
The shift to electromobility is also affecting production in Europe. This is because many models, including those from German manufacturers, are migrating to China. Today, automotive manufacturing plays a crucial role in job creation in the European Union, providing direct and indirect employment for 3.4 million people. This equals to 11.5% of EU employment in manufacturing, according to Eurostat.

For more information and interesting facts and figures, please read the article on the Automobilwoche portal now. Link in the comments.

title: Navigating the Future: Insights from Forrester Predictions 2025 🌟
text:

This week, I had the pleasure of attending Forrester's Predictions 2025 session in Munich—a fantastic program that explored the key trends and technologies shaping the future. 

Events like these are invaluable in helping business leaders navigate the sea of change, plot the right course, and select the right partners for a successful journey.

Two key topics stood out for me:
🔹 AI at Work – Across the Value Chain
 The discussions on AI—its impact, evolving role, and practical applications—resonated deeply. This aligns closely with Capgemini’s AI and GenAI strategy, emphasizing safe, secure, meaningful, and effective AI adoption for real business impact. From GenAI-driven ideation and design to augmented engineering and innovative CRM solutions, we are already seeing these trends materialize in our projects. If I just think about the huge leaps in advancing the software development life-cycle through agentic AI systems, that my colleagues Jiani Zhang and her teams achieve, they demonstrate  clearly the tangible impact!

🔹 Robotics Beyond the Factory Floor
 Robotics today is much more than an industrial one-armed assistant. From robotic process automation (RPA) to context-aware collaborative systems, robotics is transforming retail, manufacturing, and soon, perhaps, healthcare and homes. Looking at our own research, some of you may recall our collaborative, team-transporting swarm robots #Tetrabot, developed in partnership with Airbus. They exemplify how innovation in processes, mechanics, and AI-driven intelligence leads to new cyber-physical systems, redefining efficiency and value creation.

All in all, Forrester delivered a compelling event, touching on critical, high-impact trends while bringing together a fascinating audience. Looking forward to continuing these discussions—how are you thinking about AI and robotics in your organizations? 🚀

title: The vibrant Shanghai Auto Show 2025 – I took the liberty to capture some impressions in a short video below.
text:
The vibrant Shanghai Auto Show 2025 – I took the liberty to capture some impressions in a short video below.

The atmosphere? Electric. The competition? Fierce. What stood out is how competition truly benefits the consumer. China remains the most competitive vehicle market globally – and it shows. Innovation cycles are fast, and the value customers get for their money is impressive. Many models are on par with global benchmarks – and in some areas, clearly ahead of what’s currently offered in Europe or the US. Is the current market model in China sustainable? Certainly not. So consolidation is coming. But “to wait it out” does not work for the established players. They need to up their game-and we see they try.

European OEMs were present with strong innovations, but an interesting observation: While Chinese brands proudly highlight features like active suspension systems, their European counterparts – even those with top-tier technologies (looking at you, Active Ride) – often shy away from tech communication in favor of abstract “luxury” narratives. And at the same time making visible compromises on interior quality... We had discussed this yesterday in a thread here with Christoph Seyerlein , Alexander Bloch , etc.

Time will tell if this storytelling strategy resonates. Until then – enjoy the short video and let me know your thoughts.

title: CES 2025 in today's FAZ: Gary Shapiro on America's Future, Peter Fintl on AI's Role in Industry
text:
🚀 CES 2025 in today's FAZ: Gary Shapiro on America's Future, Peter Fintl on AI's Role in Industry

Today, the Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung (FAZ) published a full-page article on #CES2025, featuring an insightful interview with Gary Shapiro, President and CEO of the Consumer Technology Association (CTA). Shapiro reflected on the expected impact of the change in US administration and the similarities between Donald Trump, Elon Musk and Steve Jobs. Sounds interesting!

An overarching theme at CES this year will again be Artificial Intelligence (#AI) - its potential, challenges, and impact. In my own discussion with the FAZ, I explored key industry trends, with a particular focus on AI. I noted:
"Over the past year, expectations around AI have skyrocketed. Now the industry is under pressure to deliver. These technologies have immense potential, but they are not automatic success stories. In addition to advancing the capabilities of AI systems, we need to demonstrate viable business models." - and business value for industries!

This will be a longer post, so buckle up!

Gary Shapiro's Key Insights:
- Optimism about the new U.S. administration: Shapiro anticipates a more business-friendly environment under the new administration, with policies prioritizing areas such as autonomous driving and a balanced approach to energy policy to address the growing energy needs of AI.

- Trade and immigration challenges: He expressed concerns about tariffs, trade wars, and inflation, but emphasized the importance of innovation-friendly immigration policies to attract top global talent, a critical factor in the growth of the tech industry.

- Industry collaboration across the political aisle: Shapiro noted the growing bipartisan support for technology, which he sees as a positive shift for both the industry and democracy, ensuring that the sector is no longer tied to a single political party.

- Visionary leadership and controversy: He highlighted the involvement of leaders like Elon Musk, praising their brilliance and transformative vision while acknowledging potential conflicts of interest as a necessary consideration for their roles in government.

Read more in the comments section about my view on some of the challenges for AI in 2025, outlook on chips as well as highlights from the Capgemini Executive program at the CES!
