title: Are We Ready to Navigate the Future? Transforming Challenges into Opportunities in the Automotive Supply Chain
text:
Are We Ready to Navigate the Future? Transforming Challenges into Opportunities in the Automotive Supply Chain 

🔎In the latest insightful analysis by Handelsblatt  the German automotive supplier industry, renowned for its global leadership, faces unprecedented challenges. Despite boasting giants like ZF, Continental, and Bosch with revenues soaring well above 40 billion euros, the stark reality of diminishing profit margins cannot be overlooked. With average profit margins dwindling to 4-5%, and companies like Continental grappling with a mere 1.9% in their auto division, the industry is at a pivotal crossroads. "The general situation in the supplier industry is tense, competition is very intense," explained Schaeffler CEO Klaus Rosenfeld when presenting the annual figures on Tuesday.

📈The findings from Roland Berger  and the German IKB Deutsche Industriebank AG  bank underscore a trend of deteriorating earnings since 2019, a stark contrast to the pre-COVID era of near double-digit margins. As Continental  CEO Nikolai Setzer optimistically claims, “In terms of innovation and technology, we lead. Our strength in innovation pays off,” the underlying challenges suggest a more complex scenario. The cessation of investments in future-critical technologies, such as battery cell manufacturing and Lidar for automated driving, hints at potentially missed opportunities in harnessing innovation for sustainable growth for the German industry.

🤖The pivot towards tech giants like Nvidia and Qualcomm by automakers, bypassing traditional suppliers for critical components of automotive operating systems and automated driving, marks a significant shift. This evolution emphasizes the urgent need for reinvention within the supplier industry towards software & services, as I pointed out in the article.

⚡️This transition towards software and high-margin technologies by new market entrants like Nvidia, Qualcomm, and CATL is not just a wake-up call but a clear indicator of the transformative power of innovation and strategic foresight. The automotive industry stands at the brink of a new era where adaptability, collaborative innovation, and strategic partnerships will define the next generation of market leaders. This is an opportunity we shall not miss.

💡As industry experts, we must champion a culture of continuous innovation and strategic adaptation to remain competitive. The journey ahead is challenging but ripe with opportunities for those ready to embrace change and innovate relentlessly.

Read more about this in the article "Suppliers caught in the technology trap" in the current print edition of of the Handelsblatt or online: https://lnkd.in/d3PXntmc 

Let’s discuss how we can collectively steer our industry towards a sustainable and innovative future. #innovation #automotiveindustry #futuremobility #technology Capgemini Automotive

title: Dissatisfied with the Tesla Investor Day? Then you should take a closer look again! Because for the semiconductor indust
text:
Dissatisfied with the Tesla Investor Day? Then you should take a closer look again! Because for the semiconductor industry there was quite substantial information in it! The Handelsblatt has called its comprehensive analysis on this with the headline "Tesla boss Musk scares chip manufacturers". It was a pleasure for me to support here with assessments on the industry.

What did Tesla announce?

* Tesla plans to produce about 20 million vehicles p.a. in the future.

* The next generation of drive units should not only set standards in terms of efficiency (again). With planned all-in costs of around USD 1000, the power density shall be significantly increased (50% reduction footprint) and at the same time the SiC requirement is to be reduced by 75%.

* The share of Tesla-owned ECUs is to be increased from currently around 61% (Model Y) to 85% (Cybertruck) and 100% for the "next gen vehicle".

* Today, Tesla produces about 1.8M vehicles per year. This means a semiconductor demand of about 700k wafer (12" equivalent), about 0.5% of the worldwide capacity.

* If Tesla succeeds in producing about 20M cars per year, this would correspond to a demand of about 8M 12" wafers (less than 5% share of an estimated future worldwide capacity of 200M wafers p.a.).

How these announcements have affected (and maybe will in the future) the semiconductor manufacturers, especially in the SiC area, in the short term can be found today in the Handelsblatt in print and online.

https://lnkd.in/ebptUgrX

title: AMD to acquire Silo AI: Challenging Nvidia in the AI chip race. 🤖
text:
🛒 AMD to acquire Silo AI: Challenging Nvidia in the AI chip race. 🤖 
🔎 Insights from industry experts in the Handelsblatt

AMD is shaking up the AI chip market with its $665 million acquisition of Silo AI, a leading European AI company. With this strategic move, AMD CEO Lisa Su aims to overtake current market leader Nvidia. But can she succeed?

It was an honor to join other industry experts in Handelsblatt's in-depth analysis of AMD's latest acquisition.

Silo AI's expertise in training language models on AMD hardware could be a game changer, potentially making AMD chips and software solutions more competitive in the AI market.

Some key statements from the article:
- Lisa Su (AMD CEO): "We want to be the leading company in AI."
- Alan Priestley (Gartner analyst): Sees AMD as the only serious competitor to Nvidia in the AI chip market.
- Guido Appenzeller, Partner at Andreessen Horowitz: Emphasizes the importance of software optimization in addition to chip performance, stating that Nvidia's software advantage gives them a significant advantage.
- Peter Sarlin (CEO of Silo AI): Claims that Silo AI's software makes AMD chips "absolutely competitive" with Nvidia.
- Peter Fintl (Capgemini): I was excited to share my perspective on the industry's growing adoption of chiplets.
- Behnam Tabrizi (Stanford University lecturer): Considers AMD's transformation under Lisa Su a "masterpiece" and a prime example of a successful corporate turnaround.
- Mark Papermaster (AMD CTO): Emphasizes AMD's focus on core products and the development of innovative chip architectures.
- Satya Nadella (CEO of Microsoft): Recently unveiled new laptops with dedicated AI chips, but none of them use AMD technology.
- Blayne Curtis (Jefferies analyst): Points out that government regulation is a major risk for the chip industry (specifically on advanced AI chips), especially with rising tensions between the U.S. and China.

Despite these challenges, Su's track record in transforming AMD and the company's focus on innovation and new chip architectures utilizing chiplets offer a good outlook for AMD's ambitious goals.

I invite you to read the full Handelsblatt article for a deeper dive into this exciting development in the AI chip landscape: https://lnkd.in/d7xmN94y

title: After Mechanisation Comes Digitalisation: The New Race in #Agritech
text:
🚜 After Mechanisation Comes Digitalisation: The New Race in #Agritech
Honored to be featured by Automobil Industrie in a piece on "Automobiltechnik trifft Landwirtschaft". The article makes a point I strongly agree with: agricultural machinery is turning into a software, electronics, and data business, not “just” heavy equipment.

Yes, the cycle is tough right now. After the 2021–2023 boom, the market is correcting. In Germany alone, land-machinery and tractor revenues fell from €15.4bn (2023) to €11.1bn (2024).

But structurally, the direction is clear: after mechanisation comes digitalisation. 

Why this matters for traditional automotive suppliers: this is a rare adjacency where your core strengths already fit, E/E architectures, sensors, embedded software, functional safety thinking, validation, cybersecurity, OTA, but applied to a high-value domain with different constraints and plenty of white space. In other words: not copy-paste from automotive, but a very attractive collaboration field.

What’s changing fastest:

📈  Efficiency under constraints: more yield with fewer inputs (soil, water, fertiliser, energy) means precision and uptime become decisive

🦾  Powertrains: drop-in fuels (HVO/RME), efficiency upgrades, and electrification where duty cycles fit (often helped by farm PV)

🤖  ADAS → autonomy/teleoperation: technically feasible, but road-legal machines bring validation expectations closer to automotive reality

🧠  AI that pays for itself: monitoring, optimisation, and camera-based analytics already work in day-to-day operations, with strong examples from CLAAS

🦹  Cybersecurity becomes a board topic: connected ecosystems raise the bar, and industry standards are catching up fast

A pragmatic reminder I liked from Dr. Arne Bohl (CLAAS): humans remain part of the system. The goal is not “remove the operator”, but augment them and make outcomes repeatable at scale.

Question: Where do you see the biggest unlock first in agritech, autonomy, powertrain transition, or the data layer around “farm operations”?

👇 Full article link in the first comment.

Marco Schendzielorz Christoph Jungnickl Konstantin Hendrik Kirsch

title: "The 1000-kilometre mania: Does the BEV manufacturers' arms race for the longest range make sense? Or are they making a
text:
"The 1000-kilometre mania: Does the BEV manufacturers' arms race for the longest range make sense? Or are they making a mockery of a key promise of e-mobility? Germany's leading news magazine DER SPIEGEL took a closer look at the manufacturers' race for the 1000 km battery. 

Together with Prof. Prof. Dr. Maximilian Fichtner, Managing Director & Head of the Solid State Chemistry Research Group at the Helmholtz Institute Ulm (HIU), I had the pleasure of contributing some assessments to the article:

* Based on CATL's "Qilin" battery technology, the Geely Group will introduce its luxury model Zeekr 001 with a range of 1032 km (on test cycle) and a capacity of 140 kWh.

* A number of carmakers - including Nio, Kia, General Motors and Mercedes-Benz - have announced electric vehicles with an expected range of 1000km. Recently, Toyota also announced its intention to launch such an electric vehicle by 2026.

* However, this arms race means that the electric SUVs that are already popular with consumers are getting bigger and heavier to accommodate the huge batteries of today's technology. And the demand for precious resources is growing at the same rate.

* Range anxiety is still an issue for many customers. And high electric range figures on the sticker attract buyers.

* For instance, the Lucid Motors Air offers up to 883km range (per WLTP) with a 118kWh net battery - and a highly efficient vehicle design. The Fisker Inc Ocean with the CATL-supplied "Hyper-Battery" of 118kWh offers a WLTP range of 707km.

* The only manufacturer not currently aiming for 1000km is - surprise - Tesla. Instead, Tesla focused early on the issue of fast-charging capability and now claims to operate the largest fast-charging network in the world. In addition, the cars are streamlined and highly optimised, so they use relatively little energy to drive.

* New battery technology will be introduced over the next few years. Up to 30% more capacity is expected in the same space. 

* The key is to reverse the spiral. Smaller, lighter, reasonable ranges with excellent fast-charging capabilities should be the goal. And certainly developments such as HiNa's sodium-ion cells for the upcoming Sehol vehicles or the next generation of BYD's blade cells will also significantly reduce costs by eliminating critical materials.

Then e-mobility could better fulfil its promise of greater sustainability.

Read more about this in the report from Der Spiegel: https://lnkd.in/dtHgR4C4 (paywall)

title: “Open Source: China’s quiet accelerator of innovation.”
text:
“Open Source: China’s quiet accelerator of innovation.”
China’s AI stars—DeepSeek, MoonshotAI, and others—are forging a distinctive path. Unlike OpenAI or Google, which keep their top models tightly controlled, many Chinese firms are embracing open-weight models—allowing developers to adapt, fine-tune, or build upon them, even if the training data remains closed. This approach lowers entry barriers, fuels innovation, and builds communities, helping compensate for China’s chip limitations. And it is already causing reactions by the American market leaders - making some of their models more open!

But this strategy isn’t confined to AI—it extends across entire industries:

🚘  Automotive (Li Auto Halo OS): In late April 2025, Li Auto open‑sourced its in‑vehicle operating system—Halo OS—making core modules like vehicle control, intelligent driving OS, communication middleware, and virtualization platform freely available. This move -if the technology gets properly adopted- could to save the automotive industry billions of RMB annually avoiding duplicated R&D, dramatically reduce chip adaptation cycles down to a month, and even challenge dominant closed systems like AUTOSAR. See the analysis of Markus Rettstatt on this topic, too.

🤖  Robotics (Unitree): The company has open‑sourced key algorithms, hardware designs, and even full-body motion datasets enabling humanoid models like H1/-2 and G1 to move more naturally—or even dance. This openness has empowered widespread experimentation, helped lower prices, and positioned Unitree at the forefront of global robotics, with hardware accessibility driving what many call a “humanoid robotics boom.”  

💻  And of course in Semiconductores: for processors and SoC's, adopting the open RISC-V International standard is paramount these days.   

In my conversation with WirtschaftsWoche’s Jörn Petring, I drew the following analogy: just as DIN standards once enabled Germany’s industrial scale and reach global influence, China now leverages open standards in AI, automotive systems, and robotics to ensure compatibility, amplify reach, and shape global norms.

🤔  What are the implications?

1️⃣  Openness isn’t just a software ethos—it’s a leverage tool. Counteracting hardware limitations by inviting global collaboration can accelerate adoption and innovation. 

2️⃣  Open models and OS can create vibrant developer communities—where innovation isn’t bogged down by proprietary bottlenecks.

3️⃣  Shaping interoperable norms—rather than just selling products—can pay long-term influence dividends.

4️⃣  Where hardware supply is constrained, software—and communities—can tilt the balance. Europe and the U.S. should recognize this interplay and consider openness as both a competitive and constructive response.

🔗  For more insight on China's AI strategy follow the link to the article, featuring insights from Dr. Thomas Thiele (Arthur D. Little) and myself: https://lnkd.in/ewRp47jc

Andy Vickers Werner Ferreira da Silva

title: 𝐓𝐡𝐢𝐬 𝐢𝐬 𝐡𝐨𝐰 𝐆𝐞𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐧 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐢𝐞𝐬 𝐛𝐞𝐧𝐞𝐟𝐢𝐭 𝐟𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐩 𝐬𝐮𝐛𝐬𝐢𝐝𝐢𝐞𝐬 - the construct
text:
𝐓𝐡𝐢𝐬 𝐢𝐬 𝐡𝐨𝐰 𝐆𝐞𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐧 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐚𝐧𝐢𝐞𝐬 𝐛𝐞𝐧𝐞𝐟𝐢𝐭 𝐟𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐩 𝐬𝐮𝐛𝐬𝐢𝐝𝐢𝐞𝐬 - the construction of new factories by Intel, TSMC and Wolfspeed is being supported with billions of taxpayers' euros. 

In response to supply chain bottlenecks and to increase overall resilience, governments around the world are trying to attract semiconductor manufacturing capacity to their shores. The German government wants to give Intel of the US ten billion euros alone to build new factories. Taiwan's TSMC is to receive five billion. 

But following a ruling by the Federal Constitutional Court, the German government is now short of 60 billion euros. Even without a budget crisis, high subsidies for foreign companies are highly controversial. 

Economists such as Ifo chief Clemens Fuest have called for the money to be spent on research, while others want to promote industry but not specific sectors, similar to the mechanism of the US Inflation Reduction Act.

Economists have also often argued that such "mega-fabs" only take jobs away from local companies - and do not bring much else.

In an article today, the Handelsblatt newspaper has highlighted how German companies will also benefit from government support - and significantly so. Among the beneficiaries are companies that have so far hardly been associated with chips in the public perception. 

The semiconductor value chain today is highly globalised. Suppliers do not necessarily have to be located at the production site. But it helps if they are - and if they can keep up with international competition. And in many areas German suppliers are represented with competitive products. 

Intel plans to invest a total of around 30 billion euros in Magdeburg to create a full ecosystem around the fab. The Handelsblatt today presents a selection of companies that could benefit from this: 

Merck Group: One of the largest suppliers of materials to the chip industry

Heraeus: Glass components, chemicals and wires for semiconductor production

AIXTRON SE: Machines (not only) for gallium nitride (GaN) and silicon carbide (SiC) semiconductors

But back to the point of positive effects on supporting the semicon industry:
For me, the Silicon Saxony is a shining example of a vibrant semiconductor ecosystem with a positive impact on the economy. As you can see on the map (from the Silicon Saxony team around Frank Bösenberg), Germany today is not only a network of fabs, but also excellent know-how in research, development, suppliers and service providers.

I am convinced, as the HB quotes me: "For the domestic high-tech industry, the planned major projects are a real economic driver in the long term".

Read more in today's Handelsblatt in print or online: https://lnkd.in/eRDJap8u

title: Electric shock from China? Is the German automotive industry losing ground?
text:
Electric shock from China? Is the German automotive industry losing ground?

This week brought several headlines that particularly affected the German auto industry. I had the pleasure talking about this with Kate Katharina Ferguson on the Deutsche Welle  Business program. 

Here are the most important talking points from our conversation: 

* At the Shanghai Auto Show, the Chinese auto industry shone with a flurry of new launches of all-electric vehicles. From small cars starting at the equivalent of 10,000 euros (from GAC, for example) to mid-range (see Zeekr X available in China from the equivalent of 26,000 euros) to upper and luxury class (Nio ES6/ET7). 

The premiere of the Volkswagen ID.7, the electric successor to the Passat/Magotan, was almost lost in the shuffle. Undeservedly, one might say, because this is a competitive and technically high-quality vehicle which will certainly be well received worldwide.

Overall, the "traditional" car manufacturers have very well felt the "electric blow". Speed of change is practically everything here. 

* BYD overtook VW in China for the first time and became the market leader. Volkswagen was practically the first (well, actually the second manufacturer) to venture into China in the early 1980s. It was a spot-on and groundbreaking decision by VW's then (and recently deceased) chief executive Carl H. Hahn. VW was the undisputed market leader for decades and earned brilliantly. From my point of view, it was also because VW always made modern technology quickly available in China (TSI, DKG, etc) and thus was able to keep the products of other joint ventures at bay. This technical advantage - as well as the quality - which can be experienced by the customer, must now be rescued or resurrected in the electric age. 

A further riding on the combustion engine as well as dreams of quickly available eFuels (for passenger cars) and thus an eternal life of the piston engines will only have a detrimental effect here.  

* Many models from German manufacturers do not qualify for the Tax Credit of the US Inflation Reduction Act. Buyers of electric vehicles for which a) batteries are predominantly assembled in the USA and b) raw materials come from the USA or certain countries will receive a tax credit of up to USD 7,500. The Volkswagen ID.4 for instance will benefit from these measures due to its fairly high American added value. Other manufacturers will now try hard to make their products/factory footprint "fit" to get at least a part of the subsidy. 

* Is there now a race for government subsidies? Now regions are scrambling for future technologies - and also want to become strategically resilient: yes, absolutely. But in addition to direct government subsidies (as exemplified by Chips Acts in the U.S., EU and Japan), reasonable and consistent framework conditions for industry (energy prices) are also needed. 

More on Dw.com

title: Automobilwoche Konferenz today: #sustainability in #automotive needs a holistic approach
text:
Automobilwoche Konferenz today: #sustainability in #automotive needs a holistic approach
 
At the Automobilwoche Konferenz, happening now in Motorworld Group Munich, the frontrunners of the #automotiveindustry are coming together to discuss how to tackle the challenges of a changing industry in the race for becoming carbon neutral. As always, Burkhard Riering and all the colleagues from the AW have prepared an insightful event. 
 
There is no question: Sustainability is already a priority for the leading automakers. The path towards #electricvehicles has been taken - but that can’t be enough. 

Like Hildegard Müller (German Association of the Automotive Industry (VDA)),  Roberto Rossetti (BMW Group ), Thomas Ulbrich (Volkswagen AG ) or  Karsten Schnake (ŠKODA AUTO a.s. ) said at the conference: the industry needs a new holistic approach that addresses the whole automotive product lifecycle and much more.
 
👌 There are many innovations around battery technology which is crucial for the success of electric vehicles. Progress here can put an end to a century of fossil-fuel dependence. A sustainable strategy for batteries is must for the automotive industry. Talk to us to find the right strategy for your organization. 
 
♻ OEMs must embrace the #circulareconomy  to minimize their footprint, especially in raw materials, keep vehicles and parts usable as long as possible (repair, maintain, reuse), and recycle them into new product life cycles at the end of their usage. To start this, they need not only a product life cycle assessment of all scopes (1-3) a product has. Real change can be reached by a sustainable product design and a new, holistic view on manufacturing and business model / processes. My colleagues Dr. Dorothea Pohlmann , Lukas Birn and their teams know how!
 
💪 I am proud that we are also working on these important challenges of the automotive industry with the leading OEMs and TIERs. We can make a real change here – To #getthefutureyouwant.

Thank you Werner Ferreira da Silva , Markus Winkler for your support (not only at the meetings today) to push this topic!

title: The 29th International Automobil-Elektronik Kongress (AEK) is just around the corner, and I’m genuinely looking forward
text:
The 29th International Automobil-Elektronik Kongress (AEK) is just around the corner, and I’m genuinely looking forward to this annual "class reunion" of the industry.

AEK is "the" forum to learn on the key developments — from software-defined architectures and automated driving to semiconductor strategy and global platform harmonization.

Several sessions stand out for me this year:
🔹 Magnus Östberg / Mercedes-Benz AG on "MB.OS – Setting Standards from Chip to Cloud" and of course Stephan Durach (BMW Group) on the new BMW Panoramic iDrive. We can't wait to see the products on the road soon!

🔹 I’m particularly interested in the session by Dr. Matthias Traub (VECTOR Informatik), who will outline a collaborative SDV platform initiative with QNX and TTTech Auto. A pre-integrated, ASIL-D and ISO 21434-compliant software stack has the potential to drastically reduce development time and risk — and help OEMs focus on differentiating features rather than base integration.

🔹 The panel on global E/E architectures, moderated by Ricky Hudi, with Maria Anhalt (Elektrobit) and Dr. Christoph Grote (BMW) promises deep insight into the strategic and operational implications of scaling software and electronics across platforms and regions.

🔹 The discussion on next-generation compute architectures with the great Jyotika Athavale (Synopsys Inc, and IEEE legend), Bart Plackleé (imec), Sebastien Clamagirand (NXP Semiconductors), and Andrea Gallo (RISC-V International) will no doubt shed light on how open architectures like RISC-V and chiplet-based designs are reshaping the performance, cost, and sovereignty equation in automotive electronics.

🔹 I’m also very much looking forward to the keynote from Dr. Liming Chen, President of Horizon Robotics, on “A Chinese Perspective to Address Global Challenges: Innovation and Development in Intelligent Driving.” I recently had the pleasure of sharing a stage with Horizon Robotics at the China Car Symposium — their vision and execution speed are impressive. This will be a must-watch session.

🔹 Equally exciting are the upcoming presentations from CARIAD and Bosch on automated driving — and the much-anticipated joint announcement on open standards in collaboration with the German Association of the Automotive Industry (VDA). 

At Capgemini, we actively contribute to many of these themes. We support our clients in bringing automated driving technologies to market faster and safer, applying new engineering methodologies and leveraging our global engineering network. With one of the largest silicon engineering teams in the services industry, we operate at the intersection of hardware, embedded systems, and vehicle software — turning complexity into opportunity.

See you in Ludwigsburg! Agenda here: https://lnkd.in/d_s3nFXR

title: As we close out 2024, I was thrilled to contribute to the South China Morning Post SCMP’s recent piece on “Automotive op
text:
As we close out 2024, I was thrilled to contribute to the South China Morning Post SCMP’s recent piece on “Automotive operating systems on a roll as drivers seek smartphone-style in-car features,” alongside Magnus Östberg and Toralf Trautmann. The article dives into the fascinating evolution of automotive technology, with a nod to the iconic Mercedes-Benz AG  W140 and its groundbreaking CAN bus—still impressive by today’s standards. A car that truly lived up to its reputation as “the best car in the world.”

Let us stick with vehicle topics a little longer. Reflecting on the year, 2024 was nothing short of transformative for the automotive industry. Looking ahead, 2025 promises even more seismic shifts (from my point of view):

•	China’s EV tipping point: It’s likely that EV sales in China will outpace ICE vehicles for the first time. This raises the question—where will excess production capacity go? The answer: exports. Prepare for heightened competition.

•	The EV comeback in Europe: With battery prices dropping to remarkable levels (NMC 80 USD/kWh, LFP 50 USD/kWh, even a kind of buyer market) and OEMs driven to avoid fleet emission penalties, EVs will take center stage.

•	The software-defined vehicle revolution: This shift continues to redefine what mobility means in a digital age. And the challenge remains how to achieve it efficiently with real consumer relevance. 

All in all, 2025 is shaping up to be another pivotal year. To dive deeper into these trends and hear insights from top industry leaders, I invite you to join us at CES in a few days. It’s the perfect start to the year, and I’m particularly excited to hear perspectives from experts like Laurence Noël  and her esteemed guests.

Wishing you all a healthy, happy, and successful 2025. See you in the business!

Werner Ferreira da Silva Jochen Bechtold Giles Guidicelli Christoph Jungnickl Mike Welsh Steffen Krause João Neiva Rodrigo Maia Keith Williams 

Links in the comments!

title: India – the world’s most underestimated automotive market
text:
🇮🇳 India – the world’s most underestimated automotive market
This week, WirtschaftsWoche published an article by Thomas Stölzel on the rise of India’s automotive industry — and I was pleased to share my thoughts on why this market deserves far more attention in Europe.

Too often, when we speak about India, it’s through the lens of supply — engineering, IT services, software talent. But we rarely talk about India as a market of demand. And that’s where the real momentum is building. Especially in the automotive sector.

India is now the third-largest automotive market globally — and the fastest-growing. Vehicle production jumped from 3.4 million in 2019 to 5 million in 2024, with 10 million projected by 2030.
With just 57 cars per 1,000 people, the headroom for growth is enormous. In Germany, there are 630 cars per 1,000 inhabitants, in the United States 850, and in China 230.

Manufacturers from Asia are already moving fast:

Maruti Suzuki India Limited is doubling capacity with new plants near Delhi and Ahmedabad.
Hyundai Motor Company / Kia Worldwide are expanding, developing EVs by Indians, for India.
Toyota Motor Corporation is investing around €2 billion in a new hybrid and EV factory.

For European OEMs, this shift is more than an opportunity. It is a strategic necessity. As competition in China intensifies, with local champions advancing rapidly in software, electrification, and affordability, India represents a rare counterbalance: a vast, growing market that is open to international players and geopolitically roughly aligned with Europe. Building presence there isn’t just about sales. It can be seen as chance for resilience, diversification, and long-term relevance in a multipolar automotive world.

As I mentioned in the article:
“Where can the auto industry still grow today? There’s India – and perhaps Vietnam.”

🔗 Read the full article about the status, challenges and potential future of the subcontinent's automotive market here: https://lnkd.in/dbuDxa8d
📰 Also in this week’s print edition of the WiWo.

Discussion:
➡️ Can Europe afford to treat India mainly as a supplier — when the next major demand wave is already taking shape there?

Laurence Noël Christian Hummel Dr. Rainer Mehl Jens Sulek Christoph Jungnickl Jayashree Ravichandran Balaji N P Somesha Somegowda Capgemini Automotive

title: DeepSeek, BYD & the Tech Power Shift: The Battle for (AI) Compute Supremacy
text:
DeepSeek, BYD & the Tech Power Shift: The Battle for (AI) Compute Supremacy

You saw the news: DeepSeek AI shakes up the AI world. BYD aims to democratize automated driving. China sets the pace. So see what’s behind and what’s next!

➡️ Which chips will fuel China’s AI and autonomous future?

For now, Nvidia still dominates the Chinese AI space—DeepSeek’s training and inference pipelines remain heavily optimized for CUDA and PTX. But the U.S. chip sanctions have accelerated China’s AI semiconductor push, creating both a challenge and an opportunity.

Since February 1, 16 Chinese AI chip firms have announced compatibility with DeepSeek. While they are still catching up to Nvidia’s H100 and A100 GPUs, they are gaining traction in specific market segments. Here are 4:

🔹 Huawei  Ascend – China’s leading AI compute alternative. Already supporting DeepSeek in cloud offerings, the 910B is the most capable domestic AI chip, though still behind Nvidia in efficiency and ecosystem maturity.

🔹 Moore Threads Technology Co. Ltd.  – general-purpose AI GPUs. The company is actively working on full-stack domestic AI compute solutions to break free from foreign reliance.

🔹 HYGON  – Rooted in a JV with AMD, now fully focused on developing domestic processors. It is playing a crucial role in China’s AI chip independence.

🔹 MetaX, Enflame, Tsingmicro, and Jingjia Micro – Smaller but significant contenders, working on GPU’s and inference-focused AI accelerators to optimize workloads that don’t require Nvidia’s cutting-edge GPUs.

Despite their momentum, Chinese AI chipmakers face key obstacles:
❌ Performance Gap – Design, manufacturing, software-hardware optimization.
❌ Ecosystem Maturity – Nvidia’s developer tools, CUDA, AI models, and training frameworks are more refined resulting in efficiency and adoptability.

However, China’s strategy is clear:

1. Shift from Training to Inference – Training massive LLMs like DeepSeek still require Nvidia’s tech, but inference—where AI is actually deployed—is moving to local AI chips.

2. Build a Domestic AI Compute Ecosystem – Huawei’s CANN (Compute Architecture for Neural Networks) is emerging as an alternative to CUDA.

3. Localize AI Model Development – DeepSeek’s open-source model allows China to tune and optimize LLMs for local chips.

4. Push for Full-Stack Integration – Companies like BYD Electronics are integrating domestic AI chips into autonomous driving systems (think Horizon Robotics), ensuring China’s industry is not reliant on Western silicon.

Predictions:

➡️ Nvidia will stay relevant for large-scale AI training
➡️ Domestic AI chips will take over inference, edge computing, and automotive AI
➡️ RISC-V will compete with ARM/x86 architectures

More on BYD/ADAS next week…

For “semi-accurate” info see our study: https://lnkd.in/dtYiYgKi

Or consult our silicon engineering services for your own AI and Compute HW designs!

title: Day Two of CES 2025: Insights, Inspiration, and Collaboration for the Mobility of Tomorrow
text:
Day Two of CES 2025: Insights, Inspiration, and Collaboration for the Mobility of Tomorrow 

Today, the “Automotive Leaders Forum” in the Capgemini Executive Lounge at CES brought together a dynamic mix of global experts and partners to explore the most pressing topics in mobility and automotive innovation. Through insightful sessions, we tackled the challenges and opportunities shaping the future of our industry, from geopolitical shifts to the rise of software-defined vehicles.

Here are the highlights from today’s sessions:

1️⃣ Shifting Gear: Automotive Transformation Amid Geopolitical Turmoil
This session explored the seismic changes impacting the global automotive and mobility sector, the influence of geopolitical developments, and predictions for the future.
Speakers: Paul Thomas, John Moavenzadeh, Jessica Robinson, Laurence Noël 

2️⃣ Chips at the Center of the Automotive Industry
With the shift to electric, software-defined vehicles, semiconductors have become critical to differentiation and resilience. This panel examined how organizations can design, source, and leverage chips effectively.
Speakers: Thomas Boehm , Michael Budde , Subramani (Subi) Kengeri , Sanjiv Agarwal 

Please see also our latest study: „The semiconductor industry in the AI era: innovating for tomorrow’s demands” here  https://lnkd.in/dtYiYgKi 

3️⃣ Autonomous Freight: Driving Safety, Sustainability, and Data
While autonomous passenger vehicles often take center stage, autonomous freight holds the potential to reshape economies, address labor shortages, and transform business models.
Speakers: Peter Vaughan Schmidt, Robert Oh, John Bozzella, Daniel Ives, Praveen Cherian

4️⃣ Innovation, Regulation & Adoption in Autonomous Mobility
What does it take to achieve autonomous mobility? This panel delved into the technological, psychological, and regulatory dimensions needed to transform how we move from A to B.
Speakers: Ozgur Tohumcu, Lars Kunze, John Bozzella, David Liu, Andreas Wendel, Rodrigo Maia, Praveen Cherian 

5️⃣ Driving the Future: Building the Software-Defined Vehicle of Tomorrow
With the software-driven transformation of vehicles in focus, this session explored innovations like GenAI and virtualization, offering strategies to accelerate the industry’s transformation.
Speakers: Vishnu Jayamohan,  Ryan Rummer, Craig Turner,  Eric Cesa,  Jayashree Ravichandran

title: "Turning the tide through AI - the next industrial revolution for the automotive supplier landscape?"
text:
"Turning the tide through AI - the next industrial revolution for the automotive supplier landscape?"  
 
The automotive industry is at a critical crossroads. The push for sustainable mobility, increasing competition from technology-driven entrants, and rapidly evolving consumer expectations are reshaping the landscape. Artificial Intelligence (#AI) is now emerging as a catalyst to address these challenges.  
 
I was honored to contribute to a recent Automobilwoche feature alongside industry leaders, discussing how AI is revolutionizing our industry. 
 
For the automotive supplier industry in particular, which is under intense pressure to innovate, it is "the order of the day" to get a handle on these promising tools for increasing efficiency.
 
Here are some key takeaways:  
 
💡 Jen-Hsun Huang, CEO of NVIDIA:  
"The next industrial revolution has begun. AI will bring significant productivity gains to nearly every industry, enabling companies to be more cost and energy efficient while expanding revenue opportunities.".   
 
📈 Adoption of generative AI is skyrocketing. According to a Capgemini study, the percentage of automotive companies using generative AI in pilot projects will jump from 32% in 2023 to 81% in 2024.  
 
🌍 Holger Klein, CEO of ZF Group, shared:  
"AI enables us to accelerate validation processes by a factor of ten, reducing annotation times from 12 to 2 months.".  
ZF is also working with Infineon Technologies to develop AI algorithms to accelerate variant development of vehicle software (the "EEmotion" project). 
 
🤝 Continental partnered with Google Cloud to integrate generative AI into vehicles to deliver personalized, voice-controlled experiences. 
 
💡 Tanja Rueckert, Chief Digital Officer at Bosch:
"Generative AI will transform industries, much like the invention of the computer." Bosch partnered with Microsoft to train AI on massive datasets to improve automated driving systems. Bosch has also trained 30,000 employees through its internal AI programs since 2019.  
 
- Webasto Group is using neural network-driven validation systems to improve the quality and stability of its high-voltage heaters for electric vehicles.  
 
While the opportunities are immense, I highlighted a key point in the article:  
"As fast as AI is advancing and as exciting as its potential is, we must prioritize safety and ethics in its development and application."  
 
Bringing the benefits of (Gen)AI to industries and use cases that demand precision, accuracy and reliability is one of the goals of Capgemini's "Augmented Engineering" paradigm. Through our Hybrid Intelligence approach, we enable the use of AI in areas such as critical systems engineering, life sciences research or aerospace operations. 
 
Read the article here https://lnkd.in/eeywRs8 or in the print edition.

title: Reflecting on a Convergence of Innovation: Bosch Connected World Summit 2024
text:
Reflecting on a Convergence of Innovation: Bosch Connected World Summit 2024

Today marked the conclusion of the #BCW24 in Berlin, an eminent gathering spotlighting the forefront of AI, Digital Transformation, and IoT. Representing Capgemini, I had the privilege to join my colleagues in welcoming many visitors at our booth. Discussions centered around transformative migration pathways towards software-defined manufacturing, acceleration of SDV development through virtual methodologies and vECUs, and leveraging custom chips poised to propel the AI revolution across cloud, edge, and in-vehicle applications.

Among the plethora of insightful presentations, notable highlights included Markus Heyn  of Bosch  on the future of mobility, Gill Pratt  of Toyota Research Institute  unraveling the truth about AI, and Jan-Maarten de Vries of Bridgestone Mobility Solutions  underscoring fleet management's significance alongside a collaboration with BOSCH for commercial vehicle applications. The discourse on AI's role in revolutionizing product development by Nina Alexandra Burdensky  of Microsoft  and Michele Del Mondo  of PTC  was particularly compelling, alongside the impactful "SDV Software Factory" panel featuring luminaries from Hyundai MOBIs Pradeep Kumar Kondabattini ,  Amazon Web Services (AWS) Bill Vass , ETAS  Dr. Thomas C. Irawan (俞 礽 褔) Dr. Detlef Zerfowski, BOSCH Smruti Guttal  and more.

A discussion not to be missed was "Moving at China Speed", offering unique insights from Hui Zhang  of NIO ,  Andrew Hart  of SBD Automotive , and Philipp Ibele   of Bosch. This was complemented by the unveiling of a groundbreaking collaboration between BOSCH and WeRide, setting new benchmarks in ADAS development speed. Very nice presentation by Mathias Pillin BOSCH and Tony Han of WeRide.ai l

The day's zenith was the thought-provoking dialogues with OpenAI's Sam Kwong, Tanja Rueckert  and the visionary exchange between Elon Musk and BOSCH's Stefan Hartung  and Markus Heyn. These conversations underscored the event's significance as a nexus for thought leadership and collaboration.

The Bosch Connected World Summit has once again affirmed its stature as a place for innovation, offering a glimpse into a future shaped by AI, digital transformation, and IoT. As we reflect on these two days of rich discussions and presentations, it's evident that the path forward is paved with collaborative efforts, cutting-edge technology, and an unwavering commitment to innovation.

Thank you to the BOSCH team for the event and to the Capgemini team for the commitment! Clemens von Braunmuehl Dr. Nico Wolf Jochen Bechtold Frank Lubnau Yvonne Veyhelmann Werner Ferreira da Silva Dr. Christoph Stich Helmut Hegger Tammo Schwindt  and many more!

title: "𝐅𝐮𝐥𝐥 𝐜𝐮𝐫𝐫𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐚𝐡𝐞𝐚𝐝 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐞𝐥𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐫𝐨𝐦𝐨𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲." 𝐇𝐨𝐰 𝐦𝐮𝐜𝐡 𝐜𝐨𝐮𝐫𝐚𝐠𝐞 𝐜𝐚𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞
text:
"𝐅𝐮𝐥𝐥 𝐜𝐮𝐫𝐫𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐚𝐡𝐞𝐚𝐝 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐞𝐥𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐫𝐨𝐦𝐨𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲." 𝐇𝐨𝐰 𝐦𝐮𝐜𝐡 𝐜𝐨𝐮𝐫𝐚𝐠𝐞 𝐜𝐚𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐆𝐞𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐧 𝐚𝐮𝐭𝐨𝐦𝐨𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐲 𝐚𝐟𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐝? Hardly a day goes by without German manufacturers being criticised for their electric strategies. "Too late", "half-hearted" or "worse than Tesla and the Chinese" are just some of the terms often used by critics. But is this objectively true, and is the fastest possible transition to all-electric really "without alternative"?

In an interesting and readable article published in Automobilwoche, Michael Gerster recently examined Porsche's electric strategy regarding the rapid and complete electrification of the important Macan model range. In doing so, he raised important questions and also looked beyond the national borders to see whether customers in key international markets would go along with the "all-in electric" approach.

It was a pleasure to be able to contribute to this article with some market assessments. In particular, I believe that German industry can do "electric" - and also that this topic should not obscure our view of other disciplines where customer tastes are changing and competition is also becoming tougher.

You can read more about this in Automobilwoche here: https://lnkd.in/d74UyVkB

A lot has happened in terms of customer acceptance of electric mobility, especially in Europe. Many studies show that the group of company car drivers in Germany, which is enormously important for sales, is on the whole quite satisfied with their electric cars. 

One of the classic "business limousines" is the BMW 5 Series. It has just been relaunched and is now available in an all-electric version, the i5, which has impressed in a test by the renowned news magazine DER SPIEGEL. As mentioned in the article, for which I was happy to contribute to, I believe that such strong products will make it easy for many company car drivers to say goodbye to the combustion engine.

Read more here: https://lnkd.in/dw8pmcEG

The topic of "moving away from the combustion engine" is always a hot one. I look forward to hearing your views in the comments!

title: BMW Group joins forces with Qualcomm / Arriver to advance #autonomousdriving. Today, Handelsblatt reports on the partner
text:
BMW Group joins forces with Qualcomm / Arriver to advance #autonomousdriving. Today, Handelsblatt reports on the partnership between the two companies and how a software platform for autonomous driving is to be created in the next few years. I had the pleasure of contributing market assessments to the article.

In the article, Nicolai Martin, head of automated driving at BMW, emphasizes that the collaboration with Qualcomm is not a classic customer-supplier relationship, but a true cooperation.

Based on BMW's experience and technology, with the help of Arriver (Computer Vision, Drive Policy and Driver Assistance assets; from the Veoneer acquisition environment) and Qualcomm (semiconductor / SoC know-how), a leading platform is to be created, which is also open to other manufacturers. According to BMW, around 1,200 people worldwide work in the partnership between these three companies. 

This means that Qualcomm is now replacing Intel/Mobileye as BMW's partner for automated driving.

Although the automotive share of Qualcomm's sales is currently only around 3%, the group is showing great determination in building up its future business under the leadership of Nakul Duggal.

But how does the customer base look like for this? While deep integration from silicon, middleware, and function development is the goal in this partnership, some competitors are choosing other paths.

In my view, the strategy of Bosch is worth mentioning, as Mathias Pillin, head of the Cross-domain Computing / autonomous driving division at the world's largest automotive supplier Bosch, says in the article: "At the end of the day, it will be a question of who supplies the best software modules at the best cost. In any case, our software runs on all chips."

The approach of Apex.AI is also interesting. Under CEO and co-founder Jan Becker, work is underway to develop a ROS2-based "open" operating system with maximum hardware abstraction and ISO26262 ASIL-D certification. In any case an exciting approach!

For more information, see the article (link in comment).

What is your opinion? Will deeply integrated, vertical solutions win the race for the #software defined vehicle of the future, or will #opensource prevail in this area as well? I'm eager to hear your comments!

 Capgemini #innovation Capgemini Engineering #automotiveindustry Capgemini Automotive

title: Is Robotics finally having its "iPhone moment"? 🤖📱
text:
Is Robotics finally having its "iPhone moment"? 🤖📱
For decades, the success of industrial robotics relied entirely on predictability. We engineered mechanically brilliant, high-precision systems, but their capabilities were fundamentally capped by deterministic software and rigid control loops. They excelled in perfectly structured environments—executing highly repetitive, pre-programmed trajectories—but remained inherently inflexible, unable to adapt the moment an unexpected variable was introduced on the shop floor.

🛑 That era of highly constrained automation is coming to an abrupt end.

We are standing at a massive technological inflection point driven by a convergence of deep tech enablers. The shift from classical "pixel-processing" to true Embodied AI is being unlocked by next-generation Silicon Engineering. Specifically, the integration of high-performance NPUs and heterogeneous compute architectures at the extreme edge.

🔝 When you combine this raw compute power with multimodal sensor fusion and Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, robots finally gain "semantic understanding." (Take a look at the screen in the photos attached. You can see this real-time transition from understanding the environment to autonomous decision-making and action). They no longer just execute predefined paths; they comprehend unstructured "brownfield" environments and dynamically adapt.

I recently sat down with it-daily.net | IT-Verlag GmbH to discuss why the next few years in robotics will mirror the explosive trajectory of the EV market a decade ago. As I highlight in the interview, our problem in Europe is rarely a lack of invention; it's scaling. Perfectionism in the lab is no longer a virtue. Waiting for the technology to fully mature is arguably the riskiest strategy you can adopt today.

🔗 Read the full interview here: https://lnkd.in/d989i72Q

You can see this architectural shift in real-time this week at #MWC26. We are demonstrating how humanoid robotics and advanced edge compute are fundamentally changing the game. If you are in Barcelona, stop by the Capgemini booth (Hall 2, Booth 2K21) to see these systems in action.

But understanding the "why" is only half the battle; we also need to master the "how." The real magic happens under the hood—where semiconductor innovation, robust embedded software, and AI-driven engineering collide. Or better: undergo a fusion process.

That is exactly what we will be unpacking next week at embedded world Exhibition&Conference in Nuremberg. 

Let's discuss how to architect the secure, scalable, and software-defined robotic systems of tomorrow.

📍 Meet me and the Capgemini team at #ew26: Hall 4, Booth 4-508.

I look forward to the deep-dive discussions!

Alexandre Embry Rodrigo Maia Kevin Cloutier Ramon Antelo Claudia Hytrek Dr. Nico Wolf Luz G. Mauch Christoph Jungnickl Ralf Bus Shamik Mishra

title: Hot off the press: In today’s Automobilwoche, several industry voices — among them Hildegard Müller (German Association
text:
🔥 Hot off the press: In today’s Automobilwoche, several industry voices — among them Hildegard Müller (German Association of the Automotive Industry (VDA)), Sigrid de Vries (European Automobile Manufacturers'​ Association (ACEA)), Dr. Klaus Schmitz (Arthur D. Little) and myself — take a close look at the renewed semiconductor crisis of the automotive industry.

⚠️ The warning signs are unmistakable: if no alternative supply routes are found, certain semiconductor inventories could start running dry in November. 

🏭  Production slowdowns and temporary shutdowns may once again become a very real risk.

This latest disruption is not about high-end computing chips, but the humble, high-volume semiconductors (Diodes, Transistors, MOSFET's) that underpin virtually every vehicle function — from powertrain control to safety systems.
🌏 What makes this crisis different is its geopolitical dimension.

As I put it in the interview:
“Der Fall Nexperia zeigt auf drastische Weise, wie die Konzentration von Wertschöpfung in einer bestimmten Region neue Abhängigkeiten schafft. Selbst günstigste Elektronik-Komponenten können so zu einem Druckmittel werden.”

❎  When the world’s most basic components become tools of leverage, Europe’s industrial model finds itself at a crossroads.

👉 In the immediate term (we are talking days and weeks), the situation might only be defused through diplomatic pragmatism — possibly via face-saving arrangements that allow limited exports to continue, for instance a "local sale in China" to Chinese subsidiaries of European OEMs, who get a temporary export license to deliver "excess raw materials" to their parent companies. Such deals could buy time, but they won’t solve the underlying issue.

As my fellow industry colleague Klaus Schmitz rightly notes, “Re-Regionalisierung und Lokalisierung sind gefragt.” 

🇪🇺 Europe will need to strengthen its own semiconductor backbone, also think about diversifying packaging and testing capacity for simple components, and build genuine transparency across supply chains.

⚡ At Capgemini, we’re supporting our clients in precisely this transformation — combining technology foresight, digital engineering, and supply-chain resilience to make mobility more robust, sustainable, and future-proof.

📰 The full article:
“Kein Weiter-so bei Chips & Co.” by Michael Knauer, in today’s Automobilwoche print edition. https://lnkd.in/dGb49iM4

Shout out to my colleagues Francois Babin Christoph Jungnickl Christian Hummel Roshan Batheri Dr. Rainer Mehl Dr. Nico Wolf for their domain expertise!

title: "Fierce competition, production problems, stock drops 65%, a CEO with many side jobs" - what's really going on at Tesla?
text:
"Fierce competition, production problems, stock drops 65%, a CEO with many side jobs" - what's really going on at Tesla? Deutsche Welle Business America tried to look behind the scenes. It was a pleasure for me to talk with Daniel Winter of DWTV and to contribute some industry analysis.

An excerpt of Tesla's challenges in the past year:
* An increasing number of product recalls
* Accident investigations by NHTSA related to the use of the Autopilot system
* Debatable progress as well as safety concerns around Tesla's Full Self Driving software
* Public discussions related to Elon Musk's (erratic) management style, to some of his personal tweets and opinions, the increased politicization ("partisan") (at least in the U.S.) of the products, and Musk's distraction by his other activities - specifically his purchase of Twitter.

What often gets lost in the shuffle, however, is the list of milestones Tesla has achieved in the past year.

* Production ramp-up in Austin and Grünheide (albeit with difficulties)
* Entered 4680 cell manufacturing (and preparing for major product launch in 2023)
* Keeping Tesla Shanghai factory somehow productive under the strict COVID regulations in China
* Delivery of face-lift Model S/X at scale
* first Tesla SEMI commercial vehicles handed over to customers (as a Christmas present, so to speak)
all that under the global economic conditions such as: Ukraine crisis, inflation, supply-chain disruptions, etc.

After all, a delivery record of 1.31M vehicles in 2022 was achieved. In 2021 it was still 936k vehicles. Even though they missed the self-imposed target of 50% growth (they have done ~40%), it is still a significant success for the now nearly 100,000 Tesla employees.

If you want to learn more about the content drivers of Tesla's strategy (and its stock market valuation) such as driving automation, robotaxis, monetization of the Tesla fleet as Virtual power Plants, etc as well as the new appointment of Tom Zhu as "Number 2" on the Tesla bridge - watch the video of the show here:  https://p.dw.com/p/4Lhtg

title: NVIDIA is doing to the industrial and automotive worlds what Android did to the smartphone market. And its competitors a
text:
NVIDIA is doing to the industrial and automotive worlds what Android did to the smartphone market. And its competitors are running out of time. 🧠🚗🏭

In the latest Handelsblatt "Insight: Innovation" report by Philipp Alvares de Souza Soares and Joachim Hofer, I had the privilege of sharing my thoughts on the trillion-dollar question that keeps the tech world busy right now: Can competitors like Google break Nvidia’s ~90% dominance in the AI chip market, and why are customers actively searching for alternatives?

We are now playing a multi-dimensional chess game: NVIDIA’s competitors are not standing still, and attacks are coming from three major flanks.

💻 The Software Revolt: Tech giants like Intel, Google, Qualcomm, Broadcom, Samsung and Arm have formed the UXL (Unified Acceleration)Foundation. Their goal? To build open-source software that breaks the "CUDA monopoly" and allows AI code to run on any chip. 

⚡ Hyperscaler Custom Silicon: Google (TPUs), Amazon (Trainium), and Microsoft are designing their own highly specialized chips. They cannot match Nvidia’s raw power for general tasks, but they offer extreme energy efficiency and lower costs for specific AI workloads—a key factor for modern data centers.
 
🌍 The China Factor: Driven by export controls and a decisive industrial strategy, China is pushing hard for domestic alternatives, focusing heavily on matching Western silicon in inference capabilities.

So, what is Nvidia doing to defend its moat? Instead of just defending its silicon, Nvidia is aggressively embedding itself into the software of key industries: 
🏭 The Industrial Metaverse: Expanding deep into the industrial sector with partners like Siemens, who aim to build the "operating system for industry."
 
🚗 Automotive Dominance: Cementing its standing position in automated driving with a "premium" model, far beyond its existing deep integration with OEMs like Mercedes-Benz.

My Verdict: Do the challengers have a chance? In the short term, hyperscalers will successfully carve out territory in AI inference where cost and energy efficiency are king. 

However, for the heavy lifting of AI training, Nvidia’s lead is secure. The challengers need to realize that Nvidia has a 15-year head start. Just as Android became the undisputed standard in automotive infotainment, Nvidia has built a hardware-and-software combination so compelling—and sticky—that it remains the de facto baseline for the future of AI.

For more details, grab the weekend print edition of Handelsblatt, or read the full analysis online here: https://lnkd.in/draTqcvR

Share your thoughts on: will open-source alliances truly disrupt Nvidia’s AI dominance, or will its ecosystem remain unchallenged?

Dr. Nico Wolf Andy Vickers Christoph Jungnickl Francois Babin

title: ️ When “simple” components bring an entire industry to a halt
text:
⚠️ When “simple” components bring an entire industry to a halt
The German automotive industry is once again facing an uncomfortable truth: even the smallest electronic parts can bring production to a standstill.

What initially sounded like a minor supply issue - a single supplier of components was facing disruptions -  has now reached German Association of the Automotive Industry (VDA) and the German federal government. I was recently asked to share my expert perspective — featured in Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung , Süddeutsche Zeitung, Handelsblatt  and others (see comments).

At first glance, the components in question (diodes, power electronics, logic chips) seem like mass-produced commodities. Cheap, standardised, always available.

But in reality, they are highly specialised, safety-critical elements, developed and certified specifically for automotive use. And that’s where the challenge begins.

Even if alternatives exist, they can’t simply be swapped in. Each part must be qualified for reliability, lifetime and safety — a process that can take sometimes 12 to 18 months. Even minor deviations in form or performance can trigger redesigns and costly revalidation.

And although Europe continues to strengthen its semiconductor footprint, we must acknowledge:
Semiconductor manufacturing remains a global system.
Raw materials, packaging and testing still rely heavily on Asia. A single disruption there — and production lines here fall silent.

As Wolfgang Weber, Chairman of the Executive Board of the Zentralverband Elektrotechnik‑und Elektronikindustrie (ZVEI e. V. ), recently put it: “However, one problem lies in the necessary qualification of the replacement components – so we cannot give the all-clear yet.”
  
And as VDA President Hildegard Müller also warned:
“The situation could soon lead to significant production constraints, and potentially even stoppages.”  

Some may ask: Has the industry learned nothing from the last chip crisis?
I believe it has. This time, automotive manufacturers were informed early, reacted fast, and initiated mitigation measures immediately. The response has been far more coordinated and transparent.

Still, the core message remains:
Resilience isn’t built through local production alone — it demands strategic collaboration, foresight and shared responsibility across the entire semiconductor value chain. And also accepting the fact that increased resilience comes often hand in hand with increased cost.

And a special shout-out to all my Capgemini colleagues in Supply Chain, Silicon/Electronics Engineering who work tirelessly behind the scenes to keep the “spice flow” in electronics, automotive and other industries. 🌍⚙️

👉 What’s your view? Has the industry become more resilient — or are we still too dependent on fragile global links?

Christian Hummel  Francois Babin Christoph Jungnickl  Dr. Rainer Mehl Jochen Bechtold Michael Schulte  

#Automotive #Semiconductors #SupplyChain #Innovation #Chips #Electronics

title: AI: Navigating the "Great Crisis of Meaning" and a look at a promising innovator
text:
🌐 AI: Navigating the "Great Crisis of Meaning" and a look at a promising innovator

This weekend, Handelsblatt provides an insightful analysis of the current state of AI, revealing a challenging yet promising landscape. The article, "The Great Crisis of Meaning", highlights the growing scepticism in the markets towards the AI giants and the inevitable hurdles of unmet expectations. However, as history and the Gartner Hype Cycle have shown, this phase is merely a precursor to the next wave of innovation - one in which those who adapt will thrive. 

In it's "startup-check" column, the Handelsblatt reports on Recogni, a start-up that is poised to challenge Nvidia's dominance. Initially focused on automated driving, Recogni has strategically pivoted towards data centres, targeting highly efficient AI inference. As Gartner analyst Alan Priestley aptly noted, "There is no money in training AI models. Running AI is the market." 

Despite fierce competition and significant capital challenges, Recogni's ambition to become a major player in the AI chip industry is backed by its technology suite to accelerate model development, transformation and deployment. The management team, led by CEO Marc Bolitho and founders R K Anand and Gilles Backhus, claim that their highly energy-efficient solutions also position them well for edge applications. Think connected, automated vehicles, autonomous robotic systems or LLMs at the edge (see  CARIAD's Peter Schlicht's insightful article on integrating large language models on wheels here: https://lnkd.in/dbAUCksJ, see the buzz about #ELLEM the AI[wo]man in various posts from Daniela Rittmeier or have a look at another highly interesting startup SiMa.ai here https://lnkd.in/dXbczeCX).

As global tech giants and nimble start-ups alike strive to break free from their dependence on the market (and many would say technology) leader, opportunities abound for those who dare to disrupt the status quo. 

For businesses and innovators, the message is clear: adapt your strategies, embrace the evolution of AI, and position yourself for the opportunities that lie ahead.

🚀 The future of AI is unfolding - don't just watch from the sidelines, be a part of it.

On a personal note, it was a pleasure to contribute to this article in the Handelsblatt and engage in such forward-thinking discussions. Let's continue to explore these disruptive changes together.

title: "Two factories in two weeks" - right after the opening of the Gigafactory Grünheide, Tesla will officially launch the Au
text:
"Two factories in two weeks" - right after the opening of the Gigafactory Grünheide, Tesla will officially launch the Austin plant in a few days. In today's issue, Handelsblatt highlights the #gigafactory 5 facility in Texas in an extensive article. I am very pleased that I was allowed to contribute to this piece. 

* in less than 2 years Tesla has invested around 1.1bn USD in Austin and created a factory building more than 1.2 km long and 400 m wide. 

* In the final stage, not only the models Y, 3, Cybertruck and Semi will roll off the assembly lines, but also a new type of battery cell production shall be started. 

* Tesla has delivered about 930,000 vehicles in 2021. This means that the current production facilities in Fremont and Shanghai are almost fully utilized despite all efficiency programs. 

* Tesla plans to deliver 1.4 million vehicles in 2022. Without a quick and smooth production ramp-up in Berlin-Brandenburg and Austin, this will be difficult to achieve. 

* Austin has become a magnet for startups and technology companies. 

* Many best-paying jobs have been created in the city. This has led to real estate prices for houses, for example, rising by 40% in the past year. And thus have become too expensive for many locals. 

* Tesla is now bringing much needed jobs for skilled workers to the area. When completed, up to 20,000 jobs are planned at the Austin Gigafactory. 

* There have also been discussions in Austin around the issue of water supply for production (especially battery cells) and the overall environmental footprint of the plant. 

You can find the full article in today's issue of Handelsblatt.

title: China Is Rewriting the Rules of Global Automotive — Are We Evolving Fast Enough? Read the interview for more.
text:
China Is Rewriting the Rules of Global Automotive — Are We Evolving Fast Enough? Read the interview for more. 

⏱ 280,000 reservations in one hour.

That’s what Xiaomi’s YU7 achieved at launch. A tech company — without automotive legacy — is now outperforming many traditional OEMs in both demand generation and product-market fit. The YU7 combines consumer electronics-grade UX, software-oriented architectures, solid mechanical innovations and a cost structure that stuns.

Now contrast this with the situation of many Western brands in China:

🇩🇪 German OEMs are losing share in their most important market.
Sales are soft. Margins are down. In some cases, premium brands are resorting to deep discounts, self-registrations, or rental channels to keep volume flowing. The former “profit engine” is sputtering — and with it, the cashflow to fund the global shift to electrification and digital platforms.

In my recent interview with firmenauto magazine, I had the opportunity to explore what this seismic shift means for European OEMs, fleet operators, and the future of mobility. Spoiler: the challenge is bigger than pricing — it’s systemic.

🚘 The “world car” is dead.
Global platform standardization once promised economies of scale — but in today’s market, localization wins. The strategy “in China – for China” is not a fallback, it’s a competitive necessity. Consumer preferences, tech ecosystems, and regulatory environments now diverge faster than product cycles can adjust.

⚙️ Germany’s engineering strength must become ambidextrous.
Yes, precision matters. But it must coexist with agility. The benchmark is no longer internal combustion refinement — it’s rapid digital product development, integration of AI-driven UX, and seamless OTA update capability.

💸 Fleet and B2B markets are the next battleground.
Chinese EVs haven’t yet stormed European fleet markets — but they will. Product maturity is converging. Cost structures are already superior. Service and support models are scaling. Procurement strategies must become more data-driven, value-focused, and open to emerging players.

🔧 Price pressure is not a temporary anomaly — it’s the new operating system.
Margin recovery can’t rely on legacy brand premiums alone. It requires leaner organizations, faster decision loops, and a willingness to sunset products that no longer serve the strategic core.

✅ The upside?
Real competition is a forcing function for bold moves:

- Rethinking portfolio complexity
- Accelerating innovation-to-market
- Investing in what customers truly value — not what we’ve always done

Thank you to firmenauto and Nicole Holzer for this timely and important dialogue. We are entering a defining decade for mobility — and the clock is ticking faster than ever.

👉 Full interview (German): https://lnkd.in/dt6rJE5y

title: If you want to learn more about what is behind the new "go-to China" strategy of many automotive players, then I have an
text:
If you want to learn more about what is behind the new "go-to China" strategy of many automotive players, then I have an interesting article for you. 

"Volkswagen decouples its China business" writes the Süddeutsche Zeitung today in a captivating headline. In what I consider to be a very informative article, the SZ not only reports how 
* Volkswagen built its new plant in Hefei/Anhui in 18 months (as main owner, with JAC as junior partner, one of 30 plants in China), but also 
* the plans for the brand new Technical Development Centre there (3000 engineers by the end of 2024), and 
* the state of battery production at partner Gotion. 

The article is worth reading not only because it provides direct access to VW China boss Ralf Brandstätter's thoughts and ambitious plans, but also because it sheds light on the current and future challenges - but also opportunities - for foreign carmakers in China. After all, expected vehicle sales of 28 million units (passenger cars) by 2030 speak for themselves! 

My colleague Dr. Rainer Mehl and I had the opportunity and pleasure to contribute to this article with analyses of the Chinese automotive market and the industrial value chain.

A few more thoughts:
It is important that companies (still) see China as an opportunity. We need to get away from seeing the country as a "technological one-way street" where "we provide technology" - for a fee. 

Just as companies come to Germany or Europe with their development centres to benefit from local know-how, we would do well to benefit from China's vibrant and innovative technology ecosystem. 

Today, the slogan for expanding the value chain in the Middle Kingdom is often "from china for china"; tomorrow, it could be "from china for the world" for foreign companies. 

Only by seizing these opportunities early will it be possible to take advantage of the huge market in China, while reaping the benefits for locations outside the country. And improve the resilience of businesses even in demanding times like ours now.

This is not just about hardware - China is on the verge of becoming the world's largest exporter of automobiles, making it extremely interesting for suppliers - but also about software - the powerful ecosystems and platforms as well as the advanced app ideas are a source of inspiration.

Read more in the article here (paywall):
https://lnkd.in/dMP93RFB

title: From crisis to catharsis: The collaborative way forward for the automotive industry - or the "Schulterschluss von Wolfsb
text:
From crisis to catharsis: The collaborative way forward for the automotive industry - or the "Schulterschluss von Wolfsburg" at IZB

📢 It was an honor to be featured in the Wolfsburger Nachrichten discussing the new dynamic between Volkswagen Group and its suppliers at the recent Internationale Zuliefererbörse (IZB).

🎢 The automotive industry is undergoing a fundamental transformation - one that requires accelerated collaboration, innovation and a commitment to sustainability. And - as Kai Grünitz, board member Research and Development at Volkswagen, mentioned: Speed!

🏎️ As I pointed out in the article, this is not a crisis; it's a catharsis. A period of necessary change where rapid innovation, fueled by artificial intelligence, digitization, and cloud-based knowledge networks are essential. Companies like Capgemini are at the forefront, bridging the gap between technological opportunities, innovative ideas and large-scale industrial manufacturing to enable faster time to market.

‼️ The IZB 2024 received significant attention from the Volkswagen Board of Management, who conducted a "Messerundgang" to personally inspect the latest innovations from suppliers. 

👩‍🔬 This high-level engagement underscores Volkswagen's commitment to understanding and integrating new technologies and solutions that will shape the future of mobility. It also underscores the importance of strategic partnerships in addressing the industry's most pressing challenges.

Thomas Krause, Spokesman of the Management Board of Wolfsburg AG, emphasized that "IZB 2024 has shown that the supplier industry is ready to shape this transformation with innovative solutions.” He also emphasized the importance of global collaboration across the supply chain: "We must work together to set the standards for the future.

Dirk Große-Loheide, Member of the Board of Managment at Volkswagen Brand and Head of Procurement, emphasized Volkswagen's commitment to partnership: "Only together can we implement solutions with the necessary speed to sustainably strengthen the automotive sector." This renewed approach is evident in Volkswagen's expanded Group Hubs, which facilitated in-depth discussions on critical industry challenges, ensuring that progress doesn't stop at the show.

The discussions and innovations presented this year will shape the automotive landscape. I look forward to seeing these ideas evolve and take shape in the years to come. Together, we can set the standards for a robust and innovative mobility future.

Thank you Wolfsburger Nachrichten for critical, necessary and constructive reporting!

Find the full article here: https://lnkd.in/dy4NgAJ2

title: "99% Market Share - Is the AI Boom Built on a Fragile Foundation?"
text:
⚡ "99% Market Share - Is the AI Boom Built on a Fragile Foundation?"

As part of the ongoing discussion about Taiwan's key role in the global semiconductor industry and the related geopolitical tensions, today's Handelsblatt cover story highlighted some important aspects that could impact our industry and the future of #AI technologies. Given the importance of the debate, particularly around subsidies for the chip industry in Germany and Europe, I am delighted to have been able to contribute to this article.

Taiwan is undoubtedly at the centre of the global technology supply chain. Jensen Huang, CEO of NVIDIA, recently reiterated the technology industry's critical dependence on Taiwan: "Taiwan is the epicentre of the global technology supply chain", and warned of the risks associated with this centralisation. This dependence is becoming increasingly risky, especially in light of rising tensions between China and the US. According to TSMC executive Kevin Zhang, the group now produces 99 per cent of the world's AI accelerators. 

"The global AI boom stands on a fragile foundation as long as the world is so heavily reliant on Nvidia and TSMC," warns Juergen Matthes, lead economist at the German Economic Institute. 

But Taiwan also plays a key role beyond the much-cited "advanced nodes", i.e. high-end technologies. Its position as a supplier of machinery and materials, as a chip designer, and in packaging/testing is also significant.

Faced with this uncertainty, it is not only the West that is stepping up its efforts to build its own capabilities. Just this week, the Chinese government announced an impressive €44 billion increase in funding for its own chip industry to become less dependent on Western technologies.

Ian Bremmer, US strategist and head of geopolitical consultancy Eurasia Group, comments: "A war over Taiwan could lead to an economic rupture between the US and China and cause serious problems for Nvidia." 

Bloomberg estimates that global economic output would fall by five per cent if China completely sealed off Taiwan. This estimate illustrates the potential impact of a conflict on the global economy as a whole.

It is clear that the high concentration of semiconductor production in Taiwan, where, according to Dr.-Ing. Gunther Kegel, president of the German industry association ZVEI e. V., "40 per cent of all semiconductors worldwide pass through a TSMC factory at some point during their production", poses an enormous challenge. 

As advocates of sustainable and resilient technology supply chains that promote diversification and technological independence, these developments are a wake-up call for us all. We need to strengthen the resilience of our supply chains and avoid over-reliance on individual actors.

I look forward to your thoughts and discussions. Link in comments!

Jonathan Nussbaumer Dr. Mourad Aberbour Loïc Hamon Christoph Jungnickl Frank Bösenberg Keith Williams Werner Ferreira da Silva

title: "Engineering and IT are growing even closer together. Data and software are the foundation for customer-centric products
text:
"Engineering and IT are growing even closer together. Data and software are the foundation for customer-centric products," writes Mario Zillmann of Lünendonk & Hossenfelder GmbH  in the lead article of the "Engineering Service Providers 2022" issue of Automobil Industrie. 

I can only agree with this. Capgemini recognized this trend early on and created a "melodious triad" of strategy, IT and engineering expertise. And was without a doubt a trendsetter in the market to optimally support automotive customers in their transformation to software, services and brand, experience. 

Capgemini was represented in this issue with two contributions:

* Sustainability must become an integral part of every corporate strategy. Werner Ferreira da Silva takes up the cudgels for sustainability and circular economy. In his contribution, which is well worth reading, he also warns of the many negative effects that can be expected for companies that do not embrace the topic in a progressive manner. 

* How must OEMs position themselves in the future? This is a question that I have explored in another article. In the public discussion, it is often reduced to the new trend topic "car.os". But there is much more to it. In addition to strategic decisions to occupy new fields in the value chain of tomorrow, these also require profound adjustments in the organization ("partnerships for software and semiconductors at eye level") and in the processes. In the hitherto strictly hierarchical automotive industry with a defined pecking order, this can certainly be classified as a revolution. I would also like to refer you to a highly regarded study by our company ("Next destination software"), which impressively shows how tough the race for the podium positions will be here. 

Clearly, there is also a deep technological aspect to all that. Transversal technologies like AI, EDGE, 5G and Cloud permeate all industries and products/services. This is why this week at the HANNOVER MESSE we preserved at our booth, among many other great showcases, our autonomous driving engineering activities (“safe, trusted AI”), jointly with my buddy Ralf, to illustrate how we master those technologies to help our clients building the #intelligentindustry across sectors. 

All in all, an interesting issue! Take a look at the paper edition or visit https://lnkd.in/d9Ja2dqJ (should be available as e-paper soon)


If you are interested in the topics, I invite you to visit my colleagues Ralf Blessmann  ("Automotive IT Transformation"), Dr. Rainer Mehl  ("Data as the new sunshine"), Markus Winkler (“Global Automotive”) and Christian Hummel  ("Strategies for the mobility industry") on LinkedIn. It’s always worth reading their thoughts!

title: ️🚀 From road to runway – can automotive know-how unlock value far beyond the car? Or is it "too good to be true" ?
text:
🚗➡️🚀 From road to runway – can automotive know-how unlock value far beyond the car? Or is it "too good to be true" ?

In our contribution to the Top Company Guide 2025, Werner Ferreira da Silva  and I explore why more and more automotive players are looking beyond their traditional domain.

Margins are under pressure. Complexity is rising. And many of the tools that made this industry world-class — modular thinking, validation discipline, cost engineering, system integration — are now highly relevant in adjacent sectors.

But here’s the catch: they don’t just transfer. They need to be translated. That’s where we see enormous potential — not just to consult, but to bridge worlds. From telematics to medtech, from embedded software to smart factories, we share how this crossover can work - when done right!
