title: Electric mobility in the midst of a crisis? Do decreasing growth figures, unenthusiastic consumers and constant criticis
text:
Electric mobility in the midst of a crisis? Do decreasing growth figures, unenthusiastic consumers and constant criticism from combustion engine supporters merely serve as a sidenote on the journey towards "all-electric mobility"? Automobilwoche has explored this topic, and more, in their special issue "Generation E" released today.

With an engaging range of subjects, the editorial team around Burkhard Riering has successfully provided an informative overview of the current state of e-mobility.

Some noteworthy articles from this issue:

🇨🇳 "Hue and Cry in China": Price wars and the economic crisis are causing the first local newcomers to go bankrupt. Nevertheless, China remains the lead market for e-mobility. It was a pleasure for me to join Dr. Nils Schaupensteiner of MHP – A Porsche Company  in the article to provide a deeper understanding of the situation, analysing the evolving needs of Chinese customers, the challenges domestic and foreign brands are facing, and the opportunities for Western manufacturers to partake in technical collaborations with Chinese OEM‘s.

⏰"Farewell to Just in Time": In response to supply chain disruptions, the automotive industry has adapted by focusing on regional supply, higher stock levels, and new collaborative approaches with suppliers, such as in the semiconductor sector. As noted by my colleague Alexandre Audoin in the report: "The proportion of offshore supplies has decreased by 22% in the past two years." title: Robotaxis in China: The Quiet Revolution
text:
🚖 Robotaxis in China: The Quiet Revolution

🌎While our focus in Europe often tends to be on developments in the US, particularly the robotaxi fleets in San Francisco, an equally impressive but quieter revolution is taking place in China. 

📰A recent article in the leading German newspaper Sueddeutsche Zeitung delves deep into the subject, shedding light on how Chinese tech giants are rapidly advancing in the autonomous vehicle space.

It was a real pleasure for me to contribute some insights to this article. 

❔Did you know that Baidu, Inc. Apollo alone operates over 400 robotaxis in Wuhan? 

🇨🇳The Chinese government has already issued 16,000 licenses for automated vehicles, allowing them to drive on more than 32,000 km of roads. 

🌟These numbers aren't just statistics; they signal a shift in how we think about mobility and the role of technology in shaping our cities.

☝️But it's not just about technology (which, as the article notes, still has room for improvement). It's also about the broader social and labor implications of such advances-a crucial discussion that cannot be ignored.

👀For those interested in understanding the global landscape of autonomous vehicles, this article is a must-read. 

🕵️‍♀️Is the future of mobility closer-and more global-than we think or is it just an illusion with a lot of unpleasant side effects?

title: Is AI the key to future (automotive) semiconductors? At the forefront of automotive innovation at AEK Congress!
text:
🚗🔧 Is AI the key to future (automotive) semiconductors? At the forefront of automotive innovation at AEK Congress! 

The second day of the Automobil-Elektronik Kongress   #AEK_live kicked off with a bang. Nakul Duggal  from Qualcomm  and Ray Cornyn  of NXP Semiconductors  highlighted the latest advancements in #AI and their critical role in meeting increasing computing demands at the edge (in the vehicle). Their call for tighter industry collaboration resonated strongly with all attendees.

🔍 Highlights that stood out for me included:

Bart Plackle  from imec  delivered an insightful presentation on the transformative changes within the automotive industry and the technology stack of vehicles. His emphasis on #chiplet technology, already prevalent in servers and consumer devices, underscores a trend the automotive sector must embrace in order to meet the ambitious goals. For further insights on chiplet technology, please kindly check out the contributions from my esteemed Capgemini  colleagues Dr. Mourad Aberbour  and Loïc Hamon  as well as an article in Handelsblatt to which I contributed: Handelsblatt: Das Lego-Prinzip revolutioniert die Chipfertigung  - https://lnkd.in/dkw9wA58

Peter Schiefer  from Infineon Technologies  showcased the pivotal role of energy distribution and management in future vehicles, focusing on decentralized systems and fault-tolerant architectures and the interesting innovations like all-silicon, ultra-fast circuit protection.

🌟 However, my personal highlight was the speech by Aart de Geus , Executive Chair and Founder of Synopsys Inc . Suitable for a true industry legend, Aart took us on a thrilling journey through the future of the semiconductor industry, emphasizing key inflection points: AI in chip design and optimization, digital twins, advanced simulation methods, 3D stacking of chip dies, and the imperative to reduce energy consumption and CO2 emissions. Perfect!

Looking forward to this afternoon's sessions with Alexander Kocher of Quintauris  on RISC-V, Angela Wang  of Neusoft  on the Chinese software ecosystem, and Joachim Mathes / Valeo  on #SDV and #sustainability.

👏 Hats off to Ricky Hudi , the AEK team and scientific advisory board members for a stellar program!

title: Greetings from a buzzing #CES2026 in Las Vegas! 🇺🇸✨
text:
Greetings from a buzzing #CES2026 in Las Vegas! 🇺🇸✨
The overarching theme this year is unmistakable: Physical AI. We are moving beyond digital chatbots to machines that perceive, reason, and act in the real world.

NVIDIA’s keynote was the talk of the town. Beyond announcing the faster "Vera Rubin" chips, Jensen Huang unveiled ambitious plans to have NVIDIA-powered Robotaxis on the road by 2027, with technology for private cars following shortly after.

I had the pleasure of contributing to a Handelsblatt article on this exact topic (see attached). My take? Germany created a world-leading legal framework for automated driving very early on. Technically, our manufacturers and suppliers are still impressive. However, we are currently struggling to scale real-world use cases quickly enough. We need to turn this technical excellence into viable business models fast, or we risk leaving the value creation to players like NVIDIA, Waymo, or Chinese competitors.

Speaking of real-world application: I stopped by the Zoox booth today with esteemed VDI President Univ.-Prof. Dr.-Ing. Lutz Eckstein. Seeing their latest steering-wheel-free vehicle up close in Las Vegas (also in operation on the streets of the city) really drives home how fast the competition is moving.

Link to the article (with much more details): https://lnkd.in/dWjM3gUz

How do you see the current position of European OEMs in the race for autonomy?

#CES2026 #PhysicalAI #AutonomousDriving #NVIDIA #Automotive #Zoox #Handelsblatt #Innovation

title: A New Frontier in the Automotive Industry! 🌏
text:
🌏 A New Frontier in the Automotive Industry! 🌏
Spoiler: it is not the upcoming CES! 

I am delighted to share my recent feature in an interesting special report by DW Rev magazine on Deutsche Welle‘s  global channel. The focus? The burgeoning automotive market in India - a landscape brimming with potential and transformation.

In recent years, China has dominated the automotive industry headlines with its swift growth and shift towards “new energy vehicles“ - and the growing relevance of Chinese manufacturers on the global market. 

However, it’s time to turn our attention to what could be the next big disruptor in the global automotive sphere: India.

This special report sheds light on the  opportunities unfolding in the Indian automotive market, particularly for the German automotive industry. It was an honor to be interviewed alongside industry luminaries such as Thomas Schmall  of Volkswagen, Shashank Srivastava  of Maruti Suzuki,  Hormazd Sorabjee (Editor Autocar India) esteemed researcher Stefan Bratzel, Prof. Dr. and experts like Peter Mock (The International Council on Clean Transportation ), Angela Mans (German Association of the Automotive Industry (VDA) , Bernd Lange (MEP) We delved into the nuances of this market and explored how (not only) German innovation could play a significant role in this promising business venture. 

🚗💡 This is more than just a story about cars; it’s about the intersection of culture, innovation, and global economic shifts.

🇨🇳 It also sheds some light on the relationship between the powers China and India.  

🎥 Watch the full report here https://lnkd.in/dM_PWUxb and discover why India might just be the next big thing in the automotive world. This is underlined by the recent rumors that Tesla might officially announce their plans for a plant in India within this month.

title: "Software will conquer every company," says our CEO Aiman Ezzat today in an interview with Handelsblatt
text:
"Software will conquer every company," says our CEO Aiman Ezzat today in an interview with Handelsblatt  

In addition to the brilliant earnings figures for 2021, the following points were important in the article from my point of view:

* "#cloudcomputing and #dataanalytics are currently the main topics among CEOs" - in all industries.

* In 2021, Capgemini increased its revenue by 15 percent year-on-year to a record 18.2 billion euros, while net profit rose by 21 percent to 1.2 billion euros.

* Strategic shift from IT specialist to technology company works (Altran successfully became the heart of Capgemini Engineering).

* #automotive  engineering topics in Germany for Capgemini include e-mobility, autonomous driving and infotainment.

* Cooperation with Bosch in the area of digital factory.

* "You can clearly see that the Germans are making great leaps in digital transformation," says Aiman Ezzat. "Companies must not slow down their investments under any circumstances if they want to survive in global competition with Asian challengers and American tech corporations."

* Growth of 8 to 10% expected for 2022. 

* Last year, the number of employees rose by 20 percent to 325,000, and is expected to increase further this year. In Germany alone, Capgemini plans to hire 2800 new employees.

* Beside that, I think it is important to mention how powerful Capgemini adresses pressing needs of the industry and society in areas like #sustainabilty / #circulareconomy as well as in #pharmaceuticals / #healthcare. 

Great news and I am proud to be part of that exciting journey driving #digitalization, #automation  and networking / #connectivity  across industries.

title: 𝐀 𝐬𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐲 𝐨𝐟 𝐁𝐨𝐬𝐜𝐡 𝐬𝐮𝐩𝐩𝐥𝐲𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐤𝐞𝐲 𝐒𝐈𝐂 𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐩𝐬 𝐭𝐨 𝐁𝐘𝐃, 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐨
text:
🚗🔌 𝐀 𝐬𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐲 𝐨𝐟 𝐁𝐨𝐬𝐜𝐡 𝐬𝐮𝐩𝐩𝐥𝐲𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐤𝐞𝐲 𝐒𝐈𝐂 𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐩𝐬 𝐭𝐨 𝐁𝐘𝐃, 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐨𝐟 𝐂𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐚'𝐬 𝐞𝐯𝐨𝐥𝐯𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐬𝐞𝐦𝐢𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐨𝐫 𝐥𝐚𝐧𝐝𝐬𝐜𝐚𝐩𝐞 🌟 

In today's Handelsblatt article, Bosch was highlighted for its advances in the design and manufacture of silicon carbide (SiC) chips, critical components for energy-efficient power electronics. The piece underlines Bosch's technological prowess, but also its strategic decision to supply these crucial components beyond its own needs, serving big names from China such as BYD and Great Wall Motors, as Michael Budde, President Mobility Electronics Bosch confirms.

It is certainly good news that European companies such as Infineon Technologies, STMicroelectronics, BOSCH or American leaders such as Wolfspeed or onsemi are defending their leading role in this expanding field. Infineon CEO Jochen Hanebeck expects the SiC market to be worth seven billion euros by 2030. Around half of this will be accounted for by the automotive industry. 

🚀 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐫𝐢𝐬𝐞 𝐨𝐟 𝐂𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐬𝐞 𝐒𝐢𝐂 𝐦𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐢𝐚𝐥 𝐬𝐮𝐩𝐩𝐥𝐢𝐞𝐫𝐬

But times are changing. In the article, I was allowed to shed some light on the Chinese semiconductor market together with analysts from Jefferies. SiC materials experts from the People's Republic, such as SICC, TankeBlue and Sanan Optoelectronics, are beginning to rise. They are now challenging established players with the help of substantial government support (land, taxes, energy, etc.). These companies have also secured long-term contracts for the supply of SiC substrates (wafers from SICC, pictured below left) with industry stalwarts such as Infineon and STMicroelectronics. In substrate supply, some scenarios show for Chinese suppliers a leap from a modest 5% global market share to an impressive 50% in the coming years. Strong price competition for Chinese SIC substrates is expected to begin this year. Existing heavyweights in SiC substrates such as Wolfspeed, II-VI, ROHM Co., Ltd., SK siltron css and others will take a good look. Also, Chinese companies like UNT United Nova, are planning to begin the manufcturing of complete SIC modules (e.g. with NIO, image upper left)

🔍𝐀 𝐛𝐚𝐥𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞𝐝 𝐯𝐢𝐞𝐰 𝐨𝐟 𝐜𝐨𝐥𝐥𝐚𝐛𝐨𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐞𝐭𝐢𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧

As the lines between collaboration and competition become blurred, the importance of strategic partnerships is growing. Handelsblatt reports on Bosch's approach, which balances open market offerings with in-house innovation to manage this complex dynamic. "It is essential to give the units the freedom to compete in the market," was one of my quotes on this topic, and I believe it underscores the strategic imperative to remain competitive amidst these shifting sands.

For more details on the strategy of BOSCH and the overall industry dynamic, see the article in today's Handelsblatt.

title: A new player is entering the #automotiveindustry. "With a lot of money, political support and tremendous production know
text:
A new player is entering the #automotiveindustry. "With a lot of money, political support and tremendous production know-how - but without many years of automotive expertise.",  this is how the plans of Taiwanese electronics giant Hon Hai Precision - better known as Foxconn - are often described by automotive press. In an interview for the German news magazine "Stern", we looked at the state of the industry, what opportunities but also what challenges await new entrants to the mobility sector. Likewise, what the entry of a heavyweight like Foxconn (around $215 billion in annual sales) from another industry into the mobility industry means for established market players.

What is interesting here is not just the ambitious growth targets of the company. For example, around 5 percent of the global market for e-vehicles is to be captured by 2025. This would represent sales of around 31 billion U.S. dollars.

Unlike some other newcomers, the Group wants its "Mobility in Harmony Initiative" to cover the entire value chain from engineering, technology platforms and components through to production. Additionally, they are constantly extending their partner ecosystem. For instance a couple of weeks ago, they announced to collaborate with their long standing business partner nVidia for automated driving. 

As such, they would also like to become a partner for traditional manufacturers. This could be seen during the last Hon Hai Tech Day, where Chairman Terry Gou presented the latest vehicle developments.

You can read more about this in the article here:  https://lnkd.in/d27U7gdt

#innovation #electricvehicles #automateddriving

title: “Expensive Bits, Scarce Bytes – Why memory will become a luxury good in 2026”💡, titles the WirtschaftsWoche. I had the
text:
“Expensive Bits, Scarce Bytes – Why memory will become a luxury good in 2026”💡, titles the WirtschaftsWoche. I had the pleasure being included in a great article by Thomas M. Kuhn  about the dynamics we are currently seeing in the market for electronic components.
  
We are no longer just looking at a standard market cycle; we are in the middle of a structural shift. As I noted in the feature, the root cause is clear: "Fueled by massive investments in AI data centers, the demand for memory chips has surged enormously."  
Because hyperscalers are essentially sweeping the memory and hard drive markets empty, this AI rush is indirectly driving up costs for everyday consumer electronics, from PCs and smartphones to gaming consoles. 
 
A few key takeaways from the current dynamics:
• Market Concentration: A staggering 93% of the PC memory market is dominated by just three players: Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron.  
• Geopolitical Shifts: This extreme consolidation, combined with global supply constraints, is creating a massive window of opportunity for new Chinese entrants to emerge stronger and capture market share.
• Unprecedented Ripple Effects: Industry leaders are sounding the alarm. Jeff Clarke (COO, Dell) stated that such rapid cost increases have never happened before. HP's CEO, Enrique Lores, warned of lower profits after memory module prices surged over 200% in just weeks, while Khein Seng Pua (CEO, Phison) projects these shortages could persist until 2030.

However, the story goes beyond memory. We are also seeing price increases across further components of the semiconductor value chain due to scarcities, ranging from substrate materials and passive components right through to power electronics. More on this - and how it will be a chance for new suppliers from China, Taiwan and maybe later India - in future posts!

Link to the article in the comments!

Christoph Jungnickl Dr. Nico Wolf

title: Second life for EV batteries: promising, but still far from simple.
text:
🪫Second life for EV batteries: promising, but still far from simple.
A new heise online report by Matilda Jordanova-Duda offers one of the clearest snapshots of where the second-life battery market really stands today. 

I was glad to contribute, and even more glad to see how many expert voices came together — from BVES Energy Storage Systems Association, Voltfang, AUDI AG, EnBW Energie Baden-Württemberg AG, encore | DB and Fraunhofer-Gesellschaft institutes.

A few themes stand out:

🔎 True second-life batteries are still rare.
As Simon Steffgen (BVES) notes, EV batteries last longer than expected. That means very few real returns and a temporary dependence on production surpluses — what David Oudsandji (Voltfang) calls “second chance” modules.  
It’s a reality check for everyone planning circular battery flows.

⚡ The technical progress is real.
Start-ups and utilities are pushing the frontier:
Voltfang with deep module testing;
Audi and EnBW with stationary systems that run on the original vehicle BMS;
Fraunhofer ISE with mixed-chemistry control strategies.  
These are the hidden engineering steps that make second life safe and reliable.

☯️ Hybrid systems are the next chapter.
EnBW’s sodium-ion / lithium-ion combos show how different chemistries can complement each other: dynamic response from the EV cells, long-term stability from sodium.  
A glimpse of future grid architectures.

💹 Economics remain the toughest barrier.
Used batteries aren’t automatically cheaper. Testing and certification add cost, while new LFP cells drop in price and offer excellent stability. In the article, I highlight details of LFP chemistry that shift the economics for stationary storage.  

🔋 Design choices matter.
Today’s “body-integrated” packs improve vehicles but complicate reuse. Several experts raise this tension. My view: we shouldn’t weaken first-life performance for second life — but we need balanced design strategies supported by initiatives like BATTERY2LIFE Project.  

🧭 The industry is between pilots and scale.
Qualification, liability, health monitoring and optimal economic strategies — all still evolving. As I put it in the piece: the system needs time to settle before true industrialisation is possible.  

Across all perspectives, one message is shared:
👉 Second life won’t solve everything, but it is a building block for resilience, resource efficiency and a more sovereign European battery ecosystem.

Thank you to heise online, Matilda Jordanova-Duda, and all experts featured for helping move the discussion into the real world — where engineering, economics and system thinking meet. Capgemini #battery #innovation expertise is at your service: Pierre Bagnon, Michael Müller, Jens Radtke, Christoph Weissinger, Marcus Fiege

Find the article (incl. sources) here: https://lnkd.in/dMeQU6YZ

Curious to hear how others in the field see this evolving.
author: Peter Fintl

title: From Promise to Purpose: Scaling Industrial AI with Impact
text:
From Promise to Purpose: Scaling Industrial AI with Impact

Today I’m attending the Siemens AI with Purpose Summit 2025 in Munich — a high-impact event that builds on the energy of this year’s AI-centric HANNOVER MESSE. Together with my Capgemini colleague Dr. Andy Vickers we are exploring how to turn AI into a true force for industrial transformation.

Andy delivered a standout session on “Scaling AI in Engineering”, cutting through the noise with clarity and precision. His use of vivid metaphors and deep engineering insights brought one message home: to move AI beyond the buzz, we need cross-disciplinary collaboration that bridges research, operations, and systems integration. To achieve true hybrid intelligence.

This theme was echoed powerfully by Peter Koerte, CTO of Siemens, who outlined Europe’s opportunity in Industrial AI — but also its responsibility. For AI to play a critical role in industry, it must meet specific demands: accuracy, trust, explainability, and reliability. These aren’t just technical checkboxes. They are foundational to making AI viable in high-stakes, real-world applications.

I see Capgemini and Siemens working closely to meet this challenge — combining domain expertise, data fluency, and system-level thinking to unlock the next wave of value creation in industry.

At Capgemini, we are already applying AI at scale across key sectors:
🚗 In automotive, we accelerate E/E development and validate autonomous systems using data-centric, AI-driven toolchains.

✈️ In aerospace, we enhance system reliability with prediction technologies from design phase to in-use and maintenance.

💊 In life sciences, we apply AI to optimize clinical trials and drug development pipelines.

📡 In telecom, we help design, simulate, and manage next-gen network infrastructure using AI-powered engineering platforms.

These are not pilots — they are real-world implementations delivering measurable outcomes.

And yes — we also had the pleasure of meeting the award-winning Siemens Industrial Copilot. Today, it can already assist with programming your trusty S7 PLC. But even more exciting is what lies ahead: we look forward to the day it can seamlessly interact with solutions from Capgemini and others — evolving into a truly indispensable engineer’s assistant for the industrial world.

A sincere thank you to Siemens for hosting such a timely and engaging summit, for the insightful exhibition, and for curating such a high-caliber, diverse audience. It’s a privilege to be part of this conversation — where purpose meets precision, and ambition meets execution.

Some snaps from Andy’s speech in the comments.

title: “Ingenuity, Innovation and Common Sense”. In a remarkable speech at today's Car Symposium, Carlos Tavares, CEO Stellanti
text:
“Ingenuity, Innovation and Common Sense”. In a remarkable speech at today's Car Symposium, Carlos Tavares, CEO Stellantis , once again confirmed his reputation not only as a legendary car manager but also as "Mr. Straight Talk”.

Some key points:
* Mobility is a basic need
* Decarbonization, while not an individual need, is a global imperative
* Global objectives and local implementation to address both
* respect for the (automotive) industry and it's efforts for transformation
* next year, 50% of Stellantis portfolio is electrified, but where is the green energy and the charging infrastructure? This needs a joint effort!
* “parliaments can’t overrule physics”
* what will be the best way to compete with Chinese manufacturers? 40% cost disadvantage?
* it is a fact that Chinese consumers perceive local brands as superior in the field of EV
* and on the overall change of the industry: “We are ready for the fight” - and Stellantis is guided by “ingenuity, innovation and common sense” 

Most refreshing to listening to such clear and concise ideas from one of the industry legends. 

And: we note that #autonomousdriving and #connectedmobility has not been mentioned 

Stellantis not only brought it's extensive model portfolio to the symposium (electric, hybrid, #hydrogen) but also it's hypercar for LeMans. So proud that Capgemini  is a partner in this exciting Motorsport program (spot the logo on the door).

Again, Prof. Dr. Ferdinand Dudenhoeffer , Beatrix C. Frisch , Prof. Dr. Helena Wisbert and Dirk Wollschläger , an amazing symposium with a fantastic speaker line up! And your new focus on international participants from GEELY and other Chinese companies is much appreciated. No progress without dialogue!

I am happy to be here with colleagues like Ralf Blessmann , Christian Hummel , Arnim Buch , Julia Müller  to meet many clients and partners to talk about transformation of the industry, digitization, 🇨🇳 and sustainability. And if you are in Bochum at the symposium: take time and visit the Capgemini booth: it is more than worthwhile!

title: Recharged in 5 Minutes—Inside BYD’s Ultra-Fast Charging Innovation: Technology, Infrastructure, User Experience
text:
Recharged in 5 Minutes—Inside BYD’s Ultra-Fast Charging Innovation: Technology, Infrastructure, User Experience

This week I had the privilege of spending time at MIT in Cambridge. I remain deeply impressed by the extensive collaborations Capgemini maintains within the Massachusetts Institute of Technology ecosystem, ranging from research at MIT Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (CSAIL) to innovative start-ups such as Liquid AI . (Thank you Anne-Laure and Keith Williams!)

However, innovation extends far beyond software alone; it encompasses advanced materials, sophisticated engineering, and impactful practical applications.

In this context, this week I also spoke with Jan Rosenow  of Automobil Industrie  and »kfz-betrieb«  regarding BYD’s announcement of ultra-fast charging technology (1,000 kW, 10C, Super-E-platform). 

Key takeaways from our discussion include:

⚡️ BYD’s introduction of a 1 MW charging solution represents a significant technological advancement, yet aligns with anticipated progress in Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP) cell technologies. Such cells offer extremely high charging rates without compromising cycle stability, lifespan, or safety, enabled by sophisticated thermal and charging management systems.

🔋 While Western manufacturers have traditionally favored Nickel-Manganese-Cobalt (NMC) technologies due to superior energy density and performance in colder climates, the competition for future cell chemistry dominance remains wide open. NMC cells still hold considerable potential through enhancements in anode (silicon), cathode (high nickel), and electrolyte innovations (semi-solid state).

🚗 Ultra-fast charging is a compelling answer to practical and perceptual barriers associated with electric mobility (range anxiety, charging times).

🚚 Importantly, the proven effectiveness (and need) of megawatt charging in sectors like heavy-duty transport underscores the feasibility of extending this technology into passenger vehicles. 

💵 Convenience and efficiency are decisive factors for customers, making selective(!) investments in high-power charging infrastructure highly relevant.

⚠️ BYD’s progress on 1,500V SiC power modules will heat up competition in this field, pressuring the Euro-semiconductor players. 

Knowledge nugget: Capgemini is deeply engaged in battery research and system simulation. For example, through our research cooperation with the Technical University of Munich, colleagues Christoph Weissinger, Jens Radtke and others from our Munich-based battery lab are developing advanced methods to significantly enhance predictions of battery cell behavior throughout the lifecycle. Check out their papers!

More details here: https://lnkd.in/d5_z2XS8

Werner Ferreira da Silva Jochen Bechtold Michael Müller

title: "Internal combustion engine vehicles are becoming more and more expensive" - this is what the leading industry newspaper
text:
"Internal combustion engine vehicles are becoming more and more expensive" - this is what the leading industry newspaper Automobilwoche highlights in its current issue. But this is not about the much-discussed entry-level mobility in vehicle classes A and B - which should remain affordable even after de-carbonization. As an exclusive evaluation by DAT shows, prices for combustion vehicles have risen much more sharply than those of comparable #electricvehicles, especially in the D segment.

* Naturally, higher design costs due to legal requirements and generally higher costs are driving up prices - but that's not the real culprit

* However, manufacturers are also trying to "motivate" customers towards electromobility by offering price parity in the long term - because without sufficient sales of electric models, the fleet consumption targets cannot be met. This is also a most welcome opportunity to optimize margins on the established ICE technology

* As electromobility continues to ramp up, however, cost structures will continue to improve significantly, for example due to economies of scale and new cell chemistries

* This means that better manufacturer margins on BEV's are highly likely

It has been a pleasure to contribute to this article with industry assessments on #semiconductors  and #batteries.

Find more details through the link in the comments!

title: "There is life after China." - Is #India the new #China for German Luxury Auto? 🇮🇳🚗. Here are the facts. 📈
text:
"There is life after China." - Is #India the new #China for German Luxury Auto? 🇮🇳🚗. Here are the facts. 📈 

The global automotive landscape is in constant flux. India is now the world's fastest-growing major automotive market, driven by strong economic growth and a rapidly expanding affluent class. For German manufacturers, this means a market that could - in a few years - compensate for the loss of the previously lucrative Chinese business. Provided, that is, that the companies do not approach it half-heartedly.

While the volume segment in India is firmly dominated by established players like Maruti-Suzuki (40% market share), a massive window of opportunity is opening in the premium and luxury sector. 

I recently discussed this "Crossroads" moment with Thomas Stölzel of WirtschaftsWoche. 

The data is compelling: 
🔹 India now has nearly 900,000 millionaire families (doubled since 2021). 
🔹 The aspirational middle class is ready for an upgrade—moving from entry-level mobility to premium brands. 
🔹 Geopolitical factors are currently shielding the market from Chinese EV disruptors, giving European OEMs a unique, perhaps final, head start.

👉 But here is the hard truth: We cannot win this market with a "business as usual" approach. As I mentioned in the article, we must clearly define our playing fields. The days of relying solely on Completely Knocked Down (CKD) kits are numbered due to tariff structures and rising competition.
Local value creation is key. As my co-expert in the article, Ramnath Eswaravadivoo from MarketsandMarkets™, rightly points out: Speed is everything. While German OEMs often route decisions through headquarters in Europe, competitors like Hyundai and Suzuki have empowered leadership on the ground in India.

To capture this growth, we need to combine German engineering excellence with local operational agility.

The demand is there. The question is: Are we bold enough to localize fully? Strategy, Engineering, Manufacturing?

📖 Read the full analysis in WirtschaftsWoche here: https://lnkd.in/d-q8qmHW

What is your take on the automotive opportunities in India?

Somesha Somegowda Balaji N P Rajesh Kosalram Vignesh Natarajan Jayashree Ravichandran Christoph Jungnickl Dr. Rainer Mehl Christian Hummel

title: % of OEMs plan it, but only 14% have scaled it: The SDV Reality Check.
text:
83% of OEMs plan it, but only 14% have scaled it: The SDV Reality Check.

I recently explored this gap in the 70th-anniversary jubilee edition of Automobil Industrie.

We are witnessing a fundamental change: digital architecture is replacing mechanics as the primary value driver. By 2035, up to 60% of OEM revenues could come from software and digital services.

But the reality check is harsh:
❌ 83% of companies plan a unified platform.
✅ Only 14% have a software-driven use case in global rollout.
The difference between the two? A shift in operating model, not just code.

In the article, I outline the 4 pillars to close this gap:
1️⃣ Monetize the Lifecycle: Beyond the point of sale.
2️⃣ Build "Software Factories": Industrialize CI/CD to cut costs by 20%.
3️⃣ Secure Sovereignty: Control your OS and AI baselines.
4️⃣ AI Beyond ADAS: Using AI to build the car (like virtual calibration).

Europe remains a powerhouse in engineering and safety. But in 2025, "decision logistics" is the new competitive advantage.

Links to the sources in the comments.

Discussion:
Do you see "Software Factories" as a realistic goal for traditional OEMs in the next 5 years, or will tech partnerships become the dominant model?

title: "TSMC sends warning signal to global economy". In yesterday's 4Q22 earnings call, the Taiwanese chip giant held out the
text:
"TSMC sends warning signal to global economy". In yesterday's 4Q22 earnings call, the Taiwanese chip giant held out the prospect of a decline in sales for the next few quarters. Handelsblatt investigated the situation - I was allowed to contribute with industry analysis. 

Here are the 2023 highlights from TSMC's perspective:
* Due in part to declining global demand for smartphones and notebooks, the company expects revenue to decline 14.2% in 1H23 compared to the same period last year. Gross margin and operating margin are expected to be 53.5-55.5% and 41.5-43.5%, respectively.

* This means a lower utilization of the 7/6nm process nodes. At the same time TSMC sees great customer interest in the N3/N3E processes. The share of 3nm nodes in wafer revenue 2023 is expected to be between 4 and 6%. 

* A word about the share of technologies in 4Q22: 
Advanced technologies, defined below 7nm, accounted for 54% of the overall wafer sales.

* Demand is expected to increase in mid-23 - after supply chain inventories are emptied again and end-customer demand is expected to pick up.

* Overall, the company expects sales to increase slightly in 23, but this assumes that TSMC manages to further expand its position in a generally slightly shrinking semiconductor market (consensus among analysts is for now a decline between 3 and 4%, with some risk coming, for example, from the further development of the Chinese market.

* The CAPEX target is expected to be US$32 billion and US$36 billion for 2023, of which 70% is for advanced process technologies. To put that in perspective: this is more than twice the annual sales of Infineon, the largest European #semiconductor  manufacturer.

* For the first time, TSMC has officially confirmed its intention to build a fab specifically for automotive semiconductors in Europe. Germany certainly has good cards here - 
and the Silicon Saxony area could for sure be a competitive location. As always, the location decision depends on customer feedback, the existing ecosystem, and the financial (and thus also political) framework conditions. TSMC CEO C.C. Wei said in the call, "28-nm and below overseas capacity could be 20% or more of our total 28 and below capacities in 5 years or more time."

* Likewise, TSMC is already considering a second fab in Japan. As things stand, the new line in Kumamoto, Japan (12-16 nm / 22-28 nm) is expected to go into production as planned in 2024. 

* Everyone is talking about the need to balance supply chains and governments around the world are trying to build semiconductor capacity in their own spheres of influence. TSMC has now put a price tag on it: so the cost of the fab in Arizona, USA, is said to be 3-4 times higher than a comparable fab in Taiwan.

For more details - and how the semicon industry is a pre-indicator for the global economy - check out the article in the Handelsblatt of today.

title: Last week was a global tech fest: Auto China 2024 in Beijing, on the future of mobility, and HANNOVER MESSE on the futur
text:
Last week was a global tech fest: Auto China 2024 in Beijing, on the future of mobility, and HANNOVER MESSE on the future of industry. What do they have in common?

The future they describe is largely based on high-performance semiconductors. But how do we keep pushing the limits of chip performance and efficiency? That's where chiplet technology comes in. 

📰 So it's my pleasure to present an article, published in the venerable German business newspaper Handelsblatt, that discusses this technology and provides interesting insights into technology dynamics as well as expert voices from the industry.

Key points from the article:

⚡️ Chiplets are revolutionising semiconductor manufacturing: By enabling the assembly of individual chip components into larger systems on a chip, chiplets offer greater flexibility, cost efficiency and performance optimisation.

🔭 Chiplets are an important future of semiconductors: Major chip houses, including AMD, Intel, Apple and Nvidia, are embracing chiplet technology because of its many benefits.

🤖 Chiplets are paving the way for advances in AI and other high-performance applications: The ability to combine different chip components with different performance levels makes chiplets ideal for demanding applications such as artificial intelligence and autonomous driving.

💹 Chiplets are democratising chip design: Startups and companies outside the traditional semiconductor industry (think automotive and manufacturing for instance) can now design and manufacture their own SoCs using Chiplets, reducing barriers to entry and fostering innovation.

Statements from other experts featured in the article:

* Peter Fintl, Capgemini  "The trend in the SoC industry is towards chiplets".

* Michael Schiffer, Fraunhofer IZM Institute for Reliability and Microintegration (IZM) in Berlin: "The interfaces between the chiplets are crucial for the supply chain.

* Ondrej Burkacky, McKinsey & Company:  "Many companies in the industry expect the AI boom to further increase sales to $1.1 to $1.2 trillion by 2030. That would be $100 billion to $200 billion more than previously forecast."

* Sam Naffziger, industry legend and AMD fellow:  "We have a cost-optimised, highly flexible platform that is completely unattainable with a traditional SoC design."

* Erik Jung , Fraunhofer IZM researcher: "Standards are enormously important to unlock the full potential of Chiplets.

I encourage you to read the full article in todays print edition of Handelsblatt to gain a deeper understanding of the Chiplet revolution and its impact on the semiconductor industry.

Jonathan Nussbaumer Dr. Mourad Aberbour Shiv Tasker Christoph Jungnickl Loïc Hamon Keith Williams Werner Ferreira da Silva Jochen Bechtold Alexandre Audoin 

Capgemini Engineering #innovation #semiconductors Capgemini High Tech  Capgemini Automotive
author: Peter Fintl

title: "More advanced #semiconductors from Europe for the #automotiveindustry?" By using highly efficient silicon carbide power
text:
"More advanced #semiconductors from Europe for the #automotiveindustry?" By using highly efficient silicon carbide power semiconductors, e-vehicles achieve significantly higher ranges and better driving and charging performance. Wolfspeed, one of the technology leaders in this field, who has just attracted attention by opening a new 200mm fab for #SiC semiconductors in Marcy/Utica, NY, USA, is now exploring possibilities for a production site in Europe, according to it's CEO Gregg Lowe in an Handelsblatt article today. 

This is good news, because the demand for SiC components in the industry will increase significantly in the next few years and bring good business to the big players like Wolfspeed, Infineon Technologies, STMicroelectronics, onsemi or ROHM Co., Ltd..

One of the main reasons for the decision to locate a fab in Europe is, in addition to the classic location factors, the availability (and amount) of government subsidies. 

Even if the discussion about the location of new chip factories mainly focuses on plants for logic chips of the latest generation (5nm to 2nm): For increased resilience in the semiconductor sector, Europe also needs capabilities on the continent to master power semiconductors, "mature" technology nodes and also assembly, packaging and testing. But that is not all.

For example, the US CHIPS Act has made it possible for a DRAM fab to be built in the USA again after 20 years (Micron Technology, in Idaho).

There is still a lot to do for the European Commission to achieve Europe's ambitious - and truly necessary -  goals in the field of semiconductors!

It was a pleasure to contribute to the Handelsblatt article today with some industry insights.

Check out the link in the comments.

Capgemini #innovation Capgemini Engineering #consulting Capgemini Automotive

title: The global AI chip race is entering a new phase. And the latest developments coming out of China underline how fast the
text:
The global AI chip race is entering a new phase. And the latest developments coming out of China underline how fast the landscape is shifting.
I had the chance to contribute my perspective to a detailed analysis by the executive briefing service China.Table, which took a close look at China’s decision to restrict foreign AI-chips in state-funded data centers.

A few signals stand out:

1. China is pushing hard for technological independence.
New guidelines prohibit foreign AI accelerators in subsidized data centers. Projects still under construction must remove Nvidia and AMD hardware. This isn’t a ad hoc regulation – it’s a response to years of frustration and a industrial roadmap to compete with US chips.

2. Export controls are accelerating what they were meant to slow.
As I noted in the article: China has little interest in fundamentally constrained chips. And the idea that broad technology embargoes can stop their rise has become unrealistic. Separation on both sides speeds up independence on both sides.

3. Nvidia’s near-term challenge is real.
The company once held roughly 95 percent of China’s AI accelerator market. Today the share is close to zero. US export bans hurt, and China’s countermeasures do the rest.

4. Domestic players are catching up. Not by matching performance, but by scaling.
Huawei, Cambricon, Biren and others push new generations at high tempo. Performance gaps remain, but Beijing can close them through volume. With cheap, subsidized energy and massive data-center build-outs, quantity becomes a strategic tool.

5. Global consequences are likely.
Countries unable to source the latest US chips may look to Chinese alternatives. Meanwhile the US plans to keep its most advanced processors, such as the successor to the Blackwell line, strictly at home.

6. Even Nvidia’s CEO issued a clear warning.
Jensen Huang recently said: “China will win the AI race.”
He later softened the statement, but the message stands: isolating yourself from half the world risks losing both customers and innovation momentum.

What does this mean for the industry?
We are entering a period where not only technology, but also policy, energy economics and national strategy shape global AI capabilities. The race is no longer just about faster chips. It’s about access, alignment and scale.

Thank you to Table.Briefings / Jörn Petring  for the invitation and for the thoughtful conversation.

And one note from my professional perspective:
Capgemini supports clients across the semiconductor value chain with strategy, supply-chain resilience, and silicon engineering. We help shape next-generation chip architectures, strengthen sourcing and ecosystem structures, and support the development of critical manufacturing equipment.
Our goal is clear: to give our clients an innovation edge, resilience, and technological sovereignty in an electrified and AI-driven world.

Links in the comments. 

Christoph Jungnickl Dr. Nico Wolf Francois Babin

title: Sunday reading (after a relaxing week's vacation) of Automobilwoche. Entry-level electric vehicles, automated driving, p
text:
Sunday reading (after a relaxing week's vacation) of Automobilwoche. Entry-level electric vehicles, automated driving, perspectives for the European battery cell industry - a few notable highlights of the past week from my point of view:

🚙 While others are still discussing affordable electric mobility, Carlos Tavares and the Stellantis Group are making a big splash by presenting the Citroën  ë-C3 at the unrivaled low price of 23,300 euros (in Germany). Based on the CMP Smart Car Platform (already running in India, with a 29kWh battery from SVOLT), it will roll off the production line for Europe with a 44kWh lithium iron phosphate battery at the Group's Trnava plant in Slovakia. It will be exciting to see how established and new players deal with this challenge - and whether customers will actually follow the old slogan "reduce to the max" and choose the sensible path to electric mobility for many applications.

🤖 The Automobilwoche special "Autonomous Driving" not only reported on the effects of the Cruise/San Francisco incident and the corresponding temporary halt to unmanned operation. Among other things, the necessary and useful sensor technology - LIDAR - and the withdrawal of German industrial players from this key technology were also reported. It was a pleasure to be able to contribute to this with industry analyses. Even if it is not nice for the German optronics experts - but there are still enough areas in automated driving where the German industry can - and will - shine.

🔋 A report on sodium-ion technology caught the eye of readers interested in batteries: the Fraunhofer Research Institution for Battery Cell Production FFB, together with the Fraunhofer IPA  Institute for Production Technology, the PEM RWTH Aachen University chair and the   Fraunhofer Institute for Systems and Innovation Research ISI, has published a status report on sodium-ion technology. Even if from today's perspective the proven Li-ion cell is often superior in technical terms, the Na-ion cell scores with strengths in areas that are becoming increasingly important: Costs, supply chain reailies and sustainability. On the way to technological sovereignty, this technology will become enormously important for Europe. Even if some research work is still required, these cells can be easily manufactured as a drop-in solution in existing and future lithium-ion cell factories. A must-read report from Dr. Florian Degen and colleagues -> https://lnkd.in/dQ7NC_zg

If you want to know more about efficient battery cell production feel free to reach out to my colleagues Michael Müller and Christoph Weissinger - they have the insights. 

Here's to a good start to the new week!

Capgemini Engineering #Consulting Capgemini Automotive #innovation #automotiveindustry #electricvehicles

title: The IAA TRANSPORTATION is casting its shadow ahead. Starting Monday, the comprehensive platform for the transport of goo
text:
The IAA TRANSPORTATION is casting its shadow ahead. Starting Monday, the comprehensive platform for the transport of goods and people, last mile solutions, infrastructure and the open discourse around climate-neutral and digital logistics will start in Hanover. 

Capgemini will of course also be there and we are looking forward to seeing clients and partners again, to exciting discussions and new innovations around sustainable and automated mobility. 

In keeping with the theme of sustainable commercial vehicles, DER SPIEGEL just reported on the use of #hydrogen and tested the new Opel Vivaro Hydrogen. Based on the proven platform of the Vivaro electric, Opel says this vehicle offers the benefits of local zero emissions with a range of up to 400km (WLTP) and hydrogen refueling of up to 4.4kg (at 700bar) in just 3 minutes. 

Its all-electric engine (rated 100 kW) is powered by a 45 kW hydrogen fuel cell and features a 10.5 kWh buffer battery. This propulsion system could be accommodated in a space-saving way, so that, as in other models, up to 6.1 m3 loading volume and 1,000 kg payload could be achieved.

It was a pleasure for me to be able to contribute here with assessments on the subject of hydrogen, the beneficial use of this important energy carrier as well as the competition with battery-electric drives (such as MAN Truck & Bus SE successfully demonstrates with it's eTGM vehicle). Link to the article in the comments.

I am looking forward to see you at IAA Transportation with colleagues like Ralf Blessmann, Dr. Rainer Mehl, Sebastian Tschödrich, Markus S., Klaus Feldmann and many others to talk sustainable and automated commercial vehicles - and more! Stay tuned for our posts in the next day's!

title: Unlocking the Full Potential of Vehicles: AI Beyond the Wheel
text:
🚗💡 Unlocking the Full Potential of Vehicles: AI Beyond the Wheel

In a recent conversation with the esteemed industry magazine firmenauto  I had the privilege of sharing insights into the transformative world of artificial intelligence within the automotive sector. This interview sheds light on how, for instance, intelligent chat functions and assistants are not just redefining the driving experience but are opening up groundbreaking opportunities for corporate fleets.

Historically, AI was synonymous with automated driving. However, today’s discussions are dominated by advanced language models like OpenAI ChatGPT, Google Bard/Gemini, and Amazon’s new Alexa, showcasing a significant shift in the technology’s application and impact.

The automotive industry’s commitment to integrating AI extends to the introduction of AI-powered chat assistants directly into vehicles, marking a pivotal evolution in how we interact with our cars. What began as a pioneering beta-test by Mercedes-Benz last year has set a trend that was quickly embraced by other leading manufacturers. At the recent CES in Las Vegas, both Volkswagen and BMW announced their own implementations, signaling a robust industry-wide move towards making vehicles not just means of transportation, but intelligent companions on the road.

But, again, what tangible benefits does the AI-powered car offer to businesses and their fleets?

title: "Better than Tesla? Surprisingly competitive? Unbeatably low cost?" the headlines about Chinese manufacturers' electric
text:
"Better than Tesla? Surprisingly competitive? Unbeatably low cost?" the headlines about Chinese manufacturers' electric models entering the European market are overflowing.
Announcements such as BYD's intention to become the market leader in Europe are causing additional excitement.

Struggling with overcapacity in the home market, Chinese brands are poised to be successful on the world market. It is very likely that in 2023 China will become the world’s largest car exporter, overtaking the long-time champion Japan.

In a report, the MIT Technology Review Germany investigated the areas in which Chinese manufacturers have already caught up.

It was a pleasure for me to shed light on some aspects here together with experts from UBS.

* Speed of development
* Exterior and interior design
* Vehicle safety
* Body quality
* Manufacturing Quality and Material Appearance
* Cost advantages and vertical integration

Overall, Chinese manufacturers have made considerable progress in many of the above aspects - especially for their higher end models and trim levels - and are thus well placed to compete in the global market.

However, there are many other aspects that need attention.

Vital product features such as digital experience, fast-charging capability, efficiency, sales and service network and, above all, realistic pricing are further important topics. And there is definitely still potential here.

Read more in the article from Technology Review.

Capgemini Engineering #consulting Capgemini Automotive #innovation #automotiveindustry

title: Affordable electric mobility for all? Western manufacturers are currently focusing on premium rather than sustainable en
text:
Affordable electric mobility for all? Western manufacturers are currently focusing on premium rather than sustainable entry-level mobility. I had a chance to have a chat with Edison magazine about the opportunities for Chinese manufacturers. 

Some key takeaways: 
* Chinese suppliers are likely filling the gap in the market with affordable electric vehicles. 

* Cars available on the Chinese market, like the BYD Seagull (for the equivalent of €10,400), the Wuling Bin Guo EV (from €8,000) or the Ora R1 (around €8,900), could be a real challenge for the traditional players from Europe or Japan. And they remain so when you add in transport costs, customs duties and taxes to bring them to European markets. And even when one considers slight adaptations for export markets.

* Some of the Chinese cars have nothing to fear from comparison with controller-optimised Western models. The strength of the Chinese is simply their ability to scale up. This is likely to be advantageous for a small car offensive in other markets as well

* In past decades, Western manufacturers have relied heavily on China as a sales market - and also as a production base for the local market. In recent years, however, China has also become a popular destination for vehicle exports. Some electric models from German manufacturers roll off production lines in China for global export.

* Last year, for example, China overtook Germany in the ranking of the world's largest exporter of cars. With around 3.1 million vehicles, the People's Republic is now the new number two behind Japan.

VW is planning to launch an all-electric small car, the ID.2, in 2025 with a target price of under €25,000, with a target of under €20,000. 

However, new electric cars should ideally be priced around €15,000 to match the purchasing power of most entry-level German buyers. 

Chinese carmakers have an advantage in this market due to their experience in developing low-cost products and optimised manufacturing processes. China is also a leader in battery technology, such as CATL's sodium-ion cells, which could cut battery costs by around 40% and reduce dependence on critical raw materials. 

Western - and especially Japanese - manufacturers need to rise to this challenge and accelerate their efforts to add affordable, sustainable electric mobility to their portfolios. 

This can only be achieved through new approaches to technology, engineering, manufacturing and industry collaboration, both for battery cells and for complete vehicles. It will not be enough to simply reduce the quality of the vehicle interior! And hoping for trade barriers or ideas about cutting ties with the important Chinese market will (hopefully) stay an illusion as such measures will not solve any problem. 

Read more here: https://lnkd.in/dsXQGH6U

title: The headlines are screaming "crisis" for the German auto industry—and if you just look at the profit margins in this cli
text:
The headlines are screaming "crisis" for the German auto industry—and if you just look at the profit margins in this clip, it's easy to see why.

In an excellent new report by Deutsche Welle's Kassandra Sundt, the central question is laid bare: Will German Auto finally get its act together in 2026?

The segment highlights the "perfect storm" of challenges currently hitting the sector. DW's Steven Beardsley rightfully points to the massive shift in China—the days of easily dominating the world's largest car market are behind us as agile, domestic EV makers rewrite the rules of innovation and speed. 

Furthermore, as Volkswagen CEO Oliver Blume notes in the report, the era of navigating around US trade barriers is coming to an end.

I had the opportunity to share my perspective on these shifts in the report. But while it’s easy to focus purely on the pain of double-digit tariffs and supply chain localization, I think the most important story is what is happening quietly behind the scenes.

The industry is not just cutting costs to survive; it is executing a structural reset to fund the future.

1. Margin is the Fuel for Innovation: The rigorous cost management we are seeing in Europe right now has a specific purpose. Improving the earning situation is exactly what gives these companies the financial firepower to create better products and leap ahead of the ever-increasing competition. 

2. The Demand is Still There: When the industry gets it right, the consumer responds. If you look at the hard evidence in the sales statistics for the newest generation of electrified models from traditional manufacturers, the order books are full. Some are sold out for nearly a year.

The industry is navigating a brutal restructuring, yes. But consumers still fundamentally trust these legacy brands when they deliver the right product. 

We will soon see exactly how this renewed focus plays out on the global stage. I am incredibly looking forward to the Auto China show in Beijing this April, where we will get a real-time look at the impact German Auto is bringing to the world's largest car market!

Finally, beyond the supply chains and margins, this transformation is fundamentally a leadership challenge. Management changes and structural shifts have become the new normal across many automotive companies. If you are interested in what this implies—the specific kind of leadership required now, or how to navigate these shifts if you are personally affected—I highly recommend following Dr. Wolfgang K. Eckelt's insightful posts.

Link to the full video in the comments!

Are we looking at a temporary dip, or a permanent shift in global auto dominance? I’d love to hear your thoughts below. 👇

Laurence Noël Jens Sulek Dr. Rainer Mehl Luciano Bottoni Helmut Hegger Igor Bieda Christoph Jungnickl

#AutomotiveIndustry #GermanAuto #EVs #SupplyChain #AutoChina2026 #innovation #leadership
author: Peter Fintl

title: China Auto 2.0: if you read China’s new Five-Year Plan only as an EV story, you are missing the bigger point.
text:
China Auto 2.0: if you read China’s new Five-Year Plan only as an EV story, you are missing the bigger point.

I was pleased to be featured in the latest China.Table briefing, with my dear industry colleague Beatrix C. Keim, on what the new Chinese 15th Five-Year Plan ("Shiwuwu" 十五五) means for the automotive industry.

My core takeaway: China is no longer treating the car as a standalone product. It is increasingly treating it as part of a much larger technology, industrial, and mobility system.

That shift matters.

The first phase focused on scale, electrification, and industrial buildup. The next phase is broader: intelligent, connected new energy vehicles; AI across development and production; smarter factories; tighter links among vehicles, infrastructure, data, and traffic management; and a stronger push from pure volume to quality, efficiency, and profitability.

This becomes tangible very quickly.

Take automated driving. China initially pursued a high degree of experimentation. Now the field is becoming more regulated while moving further into everyday use. As discussed in the article, robotaxis in cities such as Shenzhen are no longer a distant future scenario. That combination of experimentation, deployment, and tightening governance is worth watching closely.

Take manufacturing. The plan’s emphasis on AI, robotics, and embodied intelligence is not abstract. It points to factories that are more automated, more data-driven, and more tightly linked to the wider industrial ecosystem. That will shape speed, cost, resilience, and ultimately competitiveness.

And take market structure. The article rightly highlights the message that destructive price wars and weak-margin growth should give way to higher-quality development. For the automotive sector, that likely means stronger pressure for consolidation, profitability, and industrial discipline. And a continued push for the globalization of Chinese players.

For Europe, this is not just a story of threats. It is also a learning story.

China is increasingly becoming a real-world laboratory for hyperautomation, industrial AI, and ecosystem-based mobility. And when Chinese manufacturers expand into Europe, they are unlikely to bring only vehicles. They will also bring suppliers, production logic, and execution speed. That will increase pressure, but it may also force the kind of improvement our industry urgently needs.

As Beatrix rightly notes in the piece, this is no longer only about the individual car. It is about an integrated transport system. And that is exactly why this plan deserves close attention.

Many thanks to Jörn Petring and the team at Table Media Group, Inc. for covering this important topic.

The full article is well worth reading for anyone trying to understand what Europe’s automotive industry should prepare for next. Link in the comments.

#China #Automotive #Mobility #AI #Manufacturing #IndustrialPolicy #SoftwareDefinedVehicle

title: "The hydrogen bubble", is the current headline of the WirtschaftsWoche: The transition to green industrial production is
text:
"The hydrogen bubble", is the current headline of the WirtschaftsWoche: The transition to green industrial production is stalling. Projects are unprofitable, the first companies are testing the exit. Is the dream of the hydrogen economy bursting?

I'm pleased to have contributed to Wirtschaftswoche's recent cover story, "I Had a Green Dream", where I shared insights on the evolving role of hydrogen, particularly in passenger cars and its broader applications for transportation.

As highlighted in the article, hydrogen remains central to global efforts to decarbonise industries.

🏭 Hydrogen in industry: Sectors such as steel are actively exploring hydrogen as a way to reduce emissions. For example, Thyssenkrupp's €3 billion Green Steel project is seen as a flagship for this transition, although rising costs and infrastructure delays have raised questions. The steel industry will need 1.8 million tonnes of hydrogen per year by 2035, highlighting the scale of the task ahead. 

🚙 Passenger cars and hydrogen: In the automotive sector, hydrogen fuel cell vehicles, once considered a major future technology, have struggled to gain traction. As I mentioned in the article, it is highly unlikely that hydrogen passenger cars will play a significant role in the market. The main reason for this is that there will be no surplus of cheap 'green hydrogen' in the foreseeable future. It will remain a scarce commodity that will have to be used very selectively in areas where hydrogen is irreplaceable (e.g. steel, chemicals, high-temperature applications, etc.). Even the dream of converting surplus green electricity (which, it is argued, is "free") into hydrogen costs money and makes the resulting gas "not cheap". Similarly, the distribution of hydrogen and the possible construction of a refuelling infrastructure is an enormous expense. The bottom line is that it is simply not worth "burning" hydrogen in the mobility sector on a large scale. The money is better and more effectively invested in other decarbonisation measures. And all this in the face of competitive, in many cases cheaper-to-manufacture and run battery-electric vehicles that offer unbeatable efficiency. 

🔎 Decarbonisation and the road ahead: Despite the current hurdles, hydrogen remains critical to the decarbonisation of sectors that are difficult to electrify. Green hydrogen, although six to seven times more expensive than natural gas today, is seen as essential to meeting long-term climate goals. Germany's target of 10GW of electrolysis capacity by 2030 (according to “Nationaler Wasserstoffrat”) is an important step, although only 3% of planned projects have secured investment to date. Urgent action is required to put projects on track.

👉 Read more here for interesting details: https://lnkd.in/dsuf_pTi

#Hydrogen #Decarbonisation #Sustainability #AutomotiveIndustry #EnergyTransition #FutureMobility

title: Laser pulses lasting femtoseconds. That is less time than it takes light to cross the width of a human hair. And still t
text:
Laser pulses lasting femtoseconds. That is less time than it takes light to cross the width of a human hair. And still they permanently change how aircraft aluminum behaves, replacing the toxic chemical baths that have defined aerospace surface treatment for decades.

You follow me mostly for #automotive, #AI, and #semiconductors. But in Capgemini's Technology & Innovation group, we operate across a much wider canvas. At our recent kick-off in Paris, Rodrigo Maia hosted a panel at which I delivered a speech on materials science. One of the most consequential yet undervalued fields in modern engineering. Ed Conway's fantastic book "Material World" frames it well: the materials beneath our feet decide the fate of industries-and societies. 

The infographic below shows where our research agenda sits.

Today's paper by my colleague Dr. Selim COSKUN and his team is a direct example of what that agenda produces. Applied to Aluminum 6061, the alloy backbone of certified aircraft structures, 500-fs laser pulses restructure metal surfaces at micro- and nanoscale. No chemicals. No hazardous waste. No coating contamination. 

The ISO 14040/14044-compliant life cycle assessment described in the paper shows greenhouse gas emissions up to 40× lower than legacy wet-chemical processes. Peer-reviewed, industrially relevant, and a direct technical challenge to established aerospace manufacturing practice. That's how I like it!

Congratulations to my dear friends and colleagues Dr. Selim Coskun, Dr. Luis Prado, Anne-Laure CADENE, Ramon Antelo at Capgemini Engineering and to Jake Carter, Kyle Ito, Minok Park, and Vassilia Zorba at the University of California, Berkeley, Laser Technologies Group at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory. Strong work, and a strong foundation to build on.

Read the paper here:
👉 https://lnkd.in/exywgdE3

Thank you also to my team and colleagues supporting that research: Alexander Sorgenicht, Torsten Persson, Philipp Prott, Keith Williams, Nicolas Rousseau

#MaterialsScience #SustainableAviation #LaserProcessing #AdvancedManufacturing #AerospaceEngineering #CleanTech

text:
"𝐄𝐮𝐫𝐨𝐩𝐞'𝐬 𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐮𝐟𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞𝐫𝐬 𝐡𝐚𝐯𝐞 𝐬𝐨𝐦𝐞 𝐠𝐚𝐩𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐢𝐫 𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐭𝐟𝐨𝐥𝐢𝐨"
Many are asking: When will electric mobility finally become affordable for everyone? In an interview with Automobil Produktion magazine, we discussed why the price parity between electric vehicles and internal combustion engines is getting closer, but is by no means the end of the story. 

We have also been talking about how the Chinese car manufacturers are making a big splash in their competitive home market with new entry-level electric models. 

Next week's Auto Shanghai show will not only be used by Volkswagen, for example, to present the new ID.7 (the "electric Passat") in China. 

JAC Group will also unveil its new electric brand Yiwei (钇为) and the Yiwei 3, a attractive hatchback with a 300-500km Chinese test cycle range (with LFE cells). All priced between RMB 100,000 and RMB 150,000 (€13,000 to €19,000). 

The direct competitor is the Wuling Bingo, produced by the joint venture of SAIC-GM and Wuling. It has an appealing design and a range of up to 333 km (according to Chinese - optimistic - figures) and is offered from 59,800 RMB (7,880 USD).

Read Timo Gilgen's article to find out how decisive pressure from #China is in the small car segment, which technology alternatives we see for cheaper batteries and whether a societal discussion is enough or if specific subsidies are needed.

title: More and more governments around the world have adopted roadmaps for phasing out the internal combustion engine in passe
text:
More and more governments around the world have adopted roadmaps for phasing out the internal combustion engine in passenger vehicles. Battery electric propulsion is the most efficient solution for many applications. Still, a major challenge here is battery technology. 

To this end, Prof. Dr. Ferdinand Dudenhoeffer  of CAR Center Automotive Research  today called the industry to Braunschweig for Battery Day 2022. 

In front of a completely sold out hall, OEM's, battery manufacturers, raw material suppliers, equipment manufacturers and experts will discuss the essential questions of battery technology.

* Battery requirements worldwide due to accelerated combustion engine phase-out (FREYR Battery  says up to 9GWh/year are required by 2030).

* Ways to balance cost, energy density, fast charging capability, lifetime, raw material needs, circularity/sustainability, etc. (e.g. Contemporary Amperex Technology Co., Limited new Qilin Battery)

* Building production networks (great presentations by PowerCo SE , Svolt Energy Technology Co., Ltd.   , Farasis Energy - Stefan Bergold ,  CALB Co., Ltd.  Gotion Inc. , etc)

* highly efficient cell production through consistent use of digital manufacturing technologies (Capgemini , by  my esteemed colleague Pierre Bagnon of Capgemini Invent )

* new machines and equipment for cell production

* value chain until recycling

All in all a highly exciting event to discuss the main challenges of the industry and to meet a lot of (old) colleagues and customers.

We are looking forward to many good talks today!

And reach out to use for latest insights on cell manufacturing excellence. 

Dr. Rainer Mehl , Ralf Blessmann , Sebastian Zeeb

title: “Playoff-Season in China’s Auto Industry” – Why Only (Two) Handful Will Remain 🚗
text:
🏅  “Playoff-Season in China’s Auto Industry” – Why Only (Two) Handful Will Remain 🚗

While I’m currently again traveling across China meeting clients and partners, I had the pleasure of speaking with the great Andreas Wehner, who is reporting for the venerable trade magazines »kfz-betrieb« and Automobil Industrie. Our discussion was about the already ongoing consolidation wave looming over China’s hypercompetitive automotive landscape.

With over 100 local brands, falling domestic profit margins, and the rising need for export successes, the Chinese auto sector is entering a phase of reckoning. In the interview, I share why I believe only a low double-digit number of OEMs (at best) will ultimately prevail — and what that means for Western players, partnerships, and the global balance of automotive power.

The automotive industry is one of the large engines for jobs - not only in China. And it was one of the few sectors recently adding much needed jobs in the Middle Kingdom. A least painful consolidation of the industry is a challenging task. Directions set by the central government on national level for industry policy are facing targets on provincial level that aim to ensure sufficient availability of jobs and maintain social peace. Thus, making it challenging to predict the pace of the "clean-up" going forward. 

Main talking points as summarized excellently by Andreas here: https://lnkd.in/dbxKDK2j
1️⃣ No fast crash
2️⃣ Many brands will vanish
3️⃣ Surprise winners in global export: solid vehicles beat premium ambitions
4️⃣ Local management decides over success in Europe

👉 Read the full interview here:
https://lnkd.in/davTScSN

Let me know your thoughts — and if you’re also in China, I’d be glad to connect.

Capgemini Automotive - Shout out to Yan Chu and the great colleagues from the Auto (China) team!

Luciano Bottoni Cellia Yang Christian Meininger Christian Hummel Laurence Noël Jens Sulek

title: ️ eFuels: A lifeline for the combustion engine or a step backwards for innovation?
text:
⛽️ eFuels: A lifeline for the combustion engine or a step backwards for innovation?

I recently had the opportunity to discuss the highly controversial issue of eFuels with Claus-Peter Köth, editor-in-chief of Automobil Industrie. As Europe reignites the debate on the future of the internal combustion engine (“Aus vom Verbrenner-Aus?”) and mulls the promise of a green future for mobility using eFuels, one can't help but wonder: Are we moving towards a real solution for decarbonising the vast fleet of existing vehicles, or are we getting lost in a mirage?

🇨🇳 In China, "new energy vehicles" (NEV) have recently surged to almost 50% of sales. A portfolio of competitive electric vehicles and a capable infrastructure have been the enablers to a strong growth of pure-electric cars in NEV sales. I believe this is a clear indication of where the future mainstream is heading. 

🔎 Let's be clear: the idea of continuing with our familiar vehicles, infrastructure and mobility patterns is indeed tempting. And the argument for decarbonising the millions of existing vehicles on the road sounds too good to be true.  But the reality is that efuels (and biofuels, see the HVO100 initiative happening now in Germany) are far from the miracle solution they are often made out to be. 

⚡️ The genuine global lack of green electricity (around 60% of world’s electricity today is generated from Coal, Gas and Oil), an energy-intensive production process (ca 27kWh to turn CO2 in a Liter of eFuel, Prof. Dr. Maximilian Fichtner) and ICE’s high inefficiency compared to battery electric vehicles (BEVs) raise critical questions about their viability as a mainstream alternative. 

🔎 Please see the interview here to further details: https://lnkd.in/dDzhkWFF

📝 In short, from my point of view, the widespread use of eFuels is not economically or environmentally viable. 
 
🇪🇺 The European automotive industry needs to look beyond short-term solutions. Instead of debating whether to delay the 2035 phase-out of the internal combustion engine, we should be asking: How can we ensure that Europe remains a global leader in innovation? How can we develop and produce competitive, affordable EV’s that meet consumer needs?

💪 China is already leading the way. Europe must follow suit by strengthening its entire EV value chain - from raw materials to battery production - to regain our position of strength. (Achim Kampker, Heiner Heimes,  PEM RWTH Aachen University)  

📈 The internal combustion engine (ICE) has brought prosperity and progress to society for decades. We should be grateful for this and for the many talented people who have made it possible. The ICE will have a role to play in the future - but a much smaller one.

🧭 The choice is clear: embrace the future or risk being left behind.

Capgemini Engineering #innovation Capgemini Automotive #technology

title: Auto China 2026 is weeks away. Beijing will once again be a showcase of what Chinese OEMs can do — and how fast they can
text:
Auto China 2026 is weeks away. Beijing will once again be a showcase of what Chinese OEMs can do — and how fast they can do it. Automobil Produktion asked me the right question at the right moment: Can the German auto industry keep up with China Speed?

My answer: Yes. But not by pretending the last ten years didn't happen — and not without making some deliberate, uncomfortable choices.

We discussed in the article
* The velocity gap
* The German response
* Stakes of the Transition

So what needs to happen? In parallel, not sequentially.

First: get ruthlessly clear on Core vs. Context. OEMs are already pulling this lever — BMW with Neue Klasse, Volkswagen with platform/system/application separation. The logic is right. Software, E/E architecture, safety validation, driving dynamics — that's Core. Own it, protect it, invest in it. Everything else is Context: important, but not where your identity lives. The global development network needs to be redesigned around this distinction. Not every site does everything. The best Chinese work gets done in China — with Chinese speed and Chinese market proximity. The best systems integration work gets done where that depth exists. Stop pretending one-size-fits-all global R&D still works.

Second: stop tolerating the POC flood. The industry has spent three years running AI pilots. Impressive demos, good press releases, negligible production impact. That era needs to end. AI's real value in automotive engineering isn't in the showcase — it's in the grind: augmenting software engineers writing embedded code, hardware engineers running simulation loops, systems engineers managing the integration complexity that grows exponentially with every new domain. That's where hours get recovered at scale. The shift from AI as hype to AI as engineering infrastructure is the actual productivity lever — and most organizations haven't pulled it yet.

Third: defend the differentiation that Chinese OEMs are actively trying to buy. The headhunting offers are reportedly doubling salaries to acquire German systems integration expertise. That's not flattery — that's a signal. The ability to tune a chassis, validate safety-critical systems, and make complex interdependencies work reliably is still a genuine competitive asset. It needs to be treated as one: invested in, concentrated, and not assumed to be self-sustaining.

Auto China 2026 will be impressive. Visit, come home, make the hard calls, and move faster.

👉 Full article (in German) with all the facts and figures: Automobil Produktion, April 2026, by Wolfgang Gomoll in the comments

📍 Attending Auto China in Beijing, April 24 – May 3? I'd love to connect on the show floor. Drop me a message — always up for a conversation about where this industry is actually heading.

Dr. Rainer Mehl Sebastian Tschödrich Luz G. Mauch Hans Hagen Didjurgeit Laurence Noël Dr Pascal Feillard Luciano Bottoni
