title: Play-off Season for German OEMs in China – Can We Turn the Game Around?
text:
🏁 Play-off Season for German OEMs in China – Can We Turn the Game Around?

The Chinese automotive market is entering a decisive phase — and so are Germany’s carmakers. I was pleased to share my perspective in an excellent WirtschaftsWoche article by Tobias Gürtler and Jörn Petring, which takes a clear-eyed look at where we stand.  https://lnkd.in/eTUBtG-6 or in the current print edition.

The reality for "German Auto" is sobering: market share losses are accelerating. In EVs, Volkswagen holds just 2%, BMW 0.9%, and Mercedes 0.5%. Porsche’s China sales across all vehicles (incl. ICE/(P)HEV) are down 28% this year.

The competitive edge lies elsewhere:
Chinese brands like Xiaomi, AITO (with its promising new M8 SUV blending luxury with top tech like the HUAWEI Automated Driving System), and SAIC-owned IM Motors (whose IM 5 and IM 6 models will soon arrive in Europe under the roof of MG, the SAIC platform is also the basis for the much talked about "ring-less" Audi) are delivering more desirable designs, stronger specs, and richer digital experiences — often at a fraction of the price.

As Beatrix C. Keim (CAR) noted, Volkswagen’s ID lineup has “simply failed” in China. My esteemed colleague Prof. Dr. Ferdinand Dudenhoeffer warns the window for recovery is narrow. I do see a lot of potential in the upcoming German platforms.

In my view, all points in the article are relevant. Germany had a golden run in China — it helped motorize the Middle Kingdom. But in the decisive revolutions — sustainable battery-electric propulsion, software-driven architectures, and compelling UX — we were either too early (“Mega City Vehicle”) or too late-and did too little. Now, catching up is an uphill climb.

The Xiaomi SU7 and YU7 are just one example of the shockwave. Innovation for everyone is their motto — and they deliver on it. Why pay more for a German badge if a local alternative offers better performance, luxury, and tech at a lower price?

If we respond by cutting specs to save costs while Chinese OEMs add quality and features, the gap will widen. And if our marketing shifts to treating cars as fashion accessories — as I recently saw in Shanghai with a German sports car brand at a cosmetics pop-up event (interesting, Christoph Seyerlein) — we can really leave Nürburgring records to Chinese players.

The Chinese government’s move to end ruinous price wars may create space for consolidation — a potential opening for those who survive the “play-off season.” But success will demand:
uncompromising product competitiveness,
design and UX that truly delight Chinese customers,
and execution at speed.

Germany can still win in China—but the clock is ticking.

Check the promising new German models yourself! Visit IAA MOBILITY in Munich. See you there. 🚗

Jens Sulek Alexandre Audoin Christian Hummel Giles Guidicelli Werner Ferreira da Silva Jochen Bechtold Laurence Noël
author: Peter Fintl

title: Affordable Electric Mobility: A Delicate Balancing Act 💵 🔋 🚗
text:
Affordable Electric Mobility: A Delicate Balancing Act 💵 🔋 🚗
Volkswagen’s unveiling of the ID. Every1, a €20,000 concept for an entry-level EV, marks a significant moment in the industry's pursuit of mass-market electrification. DER SPIEGEL explored this critical segment—and I had the pleasure of contributing insights on the challenges and opportunities for the industry at hand.

The fundamental question remains: Can entry-level EVs be both affordable and good? 

The answer lies in rethinking the entire automotive value chain:
💶  Affordability is key—EV adoption will only scale if electric mobility is inclusive, reaching beyond premium segments. Cost remains the top barrier for many consumers, according to a recent YouGov study in Germany.

🏅  Cost vs. User Expectations—A truly competitive entry-level EV must offer a real-world range of at least 250 km, practical charging speeds, and an uncompromised ownership experience. Stripping down features excessively to cut costs is not a viable strategy.

🔎  More than a sourcing challenge—Winning this race is not just about battery costs and component procurement. OEMs must fundamentally rethink their development processes and organizational structures to optimize cost efficiency. Global engineering resources, streamlined development cycles, and deep supplier integration will define success.

💡  Software & architecture matter—A modern, efficient electronic and software architecture is as crucial as the physical platform. Smart digital capabilities (including intelligent integration of user devices) and continuous software updates will enhance product longevity and value.

Volkswagen is taking a bold step—but the competition is fierce, with challengers from China and beyond already offering ultra-low-cost EVs. The race for accessible, compelling electric mobility is accelerating.

Who will emerge as the leader in making EVs for the many, not just the few? 

Read the full Der Spiegel article here: https://lnkd.in/dWQnPEYF

How do you see the future of affordable electric mobility?

title: Expenditure on #research and #development fell significantly last year; especially in the #automotiveindustry. Neverthel
text:
Expenditure on #research and #development fell significantly last year; especially in the #automotiveindustry. Nevertheless, this can be an opportunity for development service providers. In a conversation with Automobil Industrie, I had the pleasure to shed light on the industry's situation and outlined opportunities in this market.

* According to the latest research study by the Stifterverband, (commissioned by the Bundesministerium für Bildung und Forschung), German companies invested in 2020 around 71 billion euros in their own research, just over six percent less than before.

* Particular obstacles to investment in R and D in 2020: Declines in turnover, interrupted supply chains or lockdown regulations. 

* While overall #automotive, #mechanicalengineering and #plantmachinery have significantly reduced their #RnD expenditures, sectors such as #informationtechnologies, #telecommunications as well as #pharmaceuticalsciences and #servicesector have seen an increase.

* But the same applies to the automotive industry: in the medium term, the outlook for development service providers is absolutely positive. Even in the current crisis, topics such as #digitalization  and #electrification have developed very positively. 

* In the future, we see great demand from industry for partners who can combine know-how in the areas of #electronicsengineering , #software and digital #productdevelopment.

* There is an increasing willingness of the big players to enter into real partnerships with service providers.

Read more here: https://lnkd.in/e8pAT7Xf

title: Is the AI boom ignoring a $176 billion risk? "The Big Short" investor legend thinks so. And Handelsblatt talked with the
text:
📉 Is the AI boom ignoring a $176 billion risk? "The Big Short" investor legend thinks so. And Handelsblatt talked with the experts.

📈 We are witnessing an unprecedented race in AI infrastructure. But as companies invest billions in GPUs from Nvidia and AMD, a critical accounting question arises: How fast do these assets actually lose their value?

I was happy to contribute my perspective to the latest Handelsblatt deep dive on this topic.

The Core Debate: While Tech giants depreciate their AI hardware over 5-6 years, hedge fund manager Michael Burry (famous from "The Big Short") argues this is delusional. He suggests a 2-3 year lifespan is more realistic. If he is right, we are looking at massive future write-offs.

My Take: As I shared in the article, the issue isn't technical durability. As Sven Rzepka from Fraunhofer ENAS correctly points out, these chips can physically survive huge stress for over a decade.

⏩ The real "value killer" is technological progress.

🚀 Innovation cycles are accelerating.

Newer generations (like Nvidia’s upcoming Rubin) offer drastically better energy efficiency and performance.

Older chips don't break, but they become economically less attractive to operate.

However, I remain optimistic: Given the massive hunger for compute, even "older" capacity will find its use case in the medium term.

Other expert voices in the piece:
Gil Luria (D.A. Davidson Companies) warns that long depreciation artificially inflates current margins.
Jay Goldberg (Seaport Research Partners) notes that software demands often retire chips long before their physical end of life.

Huge thanks to the editorial team Philipp Alvares de Souza Soares, Felix Holtermann, and Joachim Hofer for putting together this nuanced analysis of the semiconductor market risks.

👇 Read the full analysis in the paper edition, link to the online article in the comments.
 
Questions around AI, Hot Chips and Custom Silicon? Give us a message:
Christoph Jungnickl, Dr. Andy Vickers, Dr. Nico Wolf

title: The Terminator is not coming – but intelligent machines are.
text:
The Terminator is not coming – but intelligent machines are.

This year’s HANNOVER MESSE  places Artificial Intelligence and Robotics at the center of industrial transformation. And rightly so: we are witnessing a profound shift from traditional automation to autonomous, adaptive, and collaborative robotics—with humanoid robots emerging as both a technological and symbolic frontier.

In a feature by the Süddeutsche Zeitung, I shared my perspective on why this wave of innovation matters:

“Fabriken, in denen überwiegend Roboter arbeiten, halte ich nicht bloß für unvermeidlich, sondern sogar für erstrebenswert. Nur so, mit Hochautomatisierung, könne Europa den gewohnten Lebensstandard halten.”

Robots are no longer confined to cages—they are evolving into context-aware collaborators. As AI, sensors, and energy-efficient compute converge, robots like Boston Dynamics’ Atlas are becoming eerily fluid in motion. But it’s not just about mobility—it’s about understanding.

“Sie [humanoide Roboter] müssen ein Verständnis für ihre Umwelt entwickeln”, I emphasized. Only then can they move from lifting boxes to performing complex assembly tasks on the factory floor.

According to Horváth, humanoid robots could take over up to 50% of manual tasks in manufacturing by 2030. Yet this leap comes with big questions:
	•	Who pays into the social system?
	•	Do we need a “robot tax”?
	•	And how do we ensure Europe keeps pace?

As Dr. Dietmar Ley (Chairman Robotics+Automation at VDMA, and CEO of Basler AG) rightly warns:

“Europa darf hier nicht den Anschluss verlieren. Wir brauchen ein koordiniertes Vorgehen, um humanoide Roboter aus dem Labor in die wettbewerbsfähige Skalierung zu bringen.”

Investments in the US and China are massive—€128 billion in China alone over five years. And while BMW and Mercedes are piloting humanoids with US startups like Figure AI and Apptronic, Europe still struggles to scale despite world-class research.

This is why Hannover Messe is the place to be.
It’s where the future of AI, robotics, and manufacturing intersect—and where the big conversations must happen.

I’ll be at the Capgemini booth, Hall 15 | F52 all week. Let’s connect and explore how we can turn industrial intelligence into industrial advantage.

Robots & exoskeleton: NEURA Robotics , Unitree Robotics , Booster Robotics , igus GmbH , Ottobock 

Michael Schulte Jochen Bechtold Frank Lubnau Tammo Schwindt Borja Tinao Perez-Miravete Werner Ferreira da Silva

title: 𝐂𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐚'𝐬 𝐆𝐫𝐞𝐞𝐧 𝐌𝐨𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲: 𝐔𝐧𝐯𝐞𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐑𝐞𝐚𝐥 𝐒𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐠𝐲
text:
🌱 𝐂𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐚'𝐬 𝐆𝐫𝐞𝐞𝐧 𝐌𝐨𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲: 𝐔𝐧𝐯𝐞𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐑𝐞𝐚𝐥 𝐒𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐠𝐲
Amidst the whirlwind of discussions about China's recent moves in the automotive powertrain realm, it's time we clear the air and correct some widespread misconceptions. Having engaged in meaningful dialogues with "Die Zeit" and being a part of the larger narrative on the future of transportation, I've noticed a glaring gap between perception ("China is shifting focus back to internal combustion engines") and reality regarding China's automotive ambitions.

🔋 𝐂𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐚'𝐬 𝐄𝐥𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐫𝐢𝐜 𝐃𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐦 𝐑𝐞𝐦𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐬 𝐒𝐭𝐫𝐨𝐧𝐠
Let's get one thing straight: China's dedication to electric mobility hasn't wavered. It will be the main mode of propulsion for the mega-cities in China as well as being an export hit for many export markets. European car-makers can and shall not relax upon those new competitors on their home-turf. Contrary to the belief that there's a shift back to the old ways of internal combustion engines, China's recent "Road-map for the Automobile Industry 1.0" simply broadens the horizon. This isn't a step back but a leap forward, embracing a dual-path strategy that champions e-mobility alongside the exploration of hydrogen and alternative fuels.

🌏 𝐀 𝐏𝐫𝐚𝐠𝐦𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐜 𝐀𝐩𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐚𝐜𝐡 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐚 𝐒𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐚𝐛𝐥𝐞 𝐅𝐮𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞
Together with experts like Dr. Jan Burgard of Berylls, we've shed light on this balanced strategy, revealing China's pragmatic approach to ensuring its automotive sector leads in clean energy transportation. By expanding into hydrogen and alternative fuels, China is gearing up for scenarios where electric vehicles might not be the perfect fit. And for applications where "molecules" are a better energy carrier than "electrons".
This strategic diversity mirrors the complexity of global mobility challenges, showing a commitment to net-zero emissions without putting all eggs in one basket.

🚀 𝐖𝐡𝐲 𝐓𝐡𝐢𝐬 𝐌𝐚𝐭𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐬 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐆𝐥𝐨𝐛𝐚𝐥 𝐒𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐋𝐞𝐚𝐝𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐡𝐢𝐩
China's nuanced strategy is a clarion call for innovation, flexibility, and the acknowledgment that the path to sustainability is multifaceted. It encourages us to prepare for a future where electric mobility takes center stage, yet it's supported by an ensemble of sustainable solutions.
As we navigate this journey, let's draw inspiration from China's roadmap, thinking expansively and innovating with responsibility. The goal? A sustainable future where electric mobility shines, supported by a spectrum of clean energy solutions.

Join the conversation on how we can embrace diverse paths to achieve our sustainability ambitions. 

Find the article in the current print edition of DIE ZEIT or online here: https://lnkd.in/dnb9FdYv

title: The sustainable #mobility of the future also needs new vehicle concepts. The renowned manager magazin used the virtual l
text:
The sustainable #mobility of the future also needs new vehicle concepts. The renowned manager magazin used the virtual launch event of the new #Microlino as an opportunity to highlight developments in this segment. It was a pleasure for me to contribute to this piece with an industry analysis.

* The Microlino was already presented at the Geneva Motor Show in 2016. After some teething problems, a version 2.0 is now set to roll out onto the streets this year.

* With much improved features (unibody construction, up to 14kWh battery, up to 320km range and speeds up to 90km/h) the Micro Mobility Systems AG believes with their class L7e vehicle to have a winner in the stable.

* However, existing competitors products in class L6e, like the succcesful Citroën Ami or it's sibling the Opel Rocks-e, their accessibility as well as their much more aggressive price point will pose a challenge for new entrants. Even tough Micro Mobility Systems claims that the Microlino is "a class above".

* Additionally, announced products, like the Renault Group EZ-1 and its integration in the Mobilize sharing service, will be strong contenders.

* A strong influence on the success of this vehicle class will be legislation and subsidy policy. The EU needs to decide how to standardize access to those vehicles for persons <16, how to set useful speed limits, etc. across all member states. Additionally, governments need to unify subsidies for "full-size" electric vehicles and L6e/L7e vehicles. The current policies are a disadvantage for those smaller and lighter vehicles.

However, in order to make de-carbonisation happen we also need sustainable, efficient, small and inclusive mobility offers - like those micro/mini mobiles.

Read more here:  https://lnkd.in/gfayCm3y (Paywall)

title: Auto Shanghai 2025: China’s Momentum. Global Impact. Shared Opportunity. The global automotive industry is converging on
text:
Auto Shanghai 2025: China’s Momentum. Global Impact. Shared Opportunity. The global automotive industry is converging on Shanghai Auto show next week—a showcase of velocity, vision, and integration.

China’s automotive landscape has evolved into a true innovation engine. From battery-electric and serial hybrid vehicles, to full-stack software integration, to AI-driven automated driving, the pace is not only fast—it’s structurally transformative.

Here’s what’s shaping the narrative:
👩‍💻Software-defined vehicles are no longer siloed machines. They are embedded into the digital lifestyle—connected, contextual, and constantly learning.

🦾Chinese OEMs are becoming tech companies in their own right—owning their stacks from chips to batteries to cloud, and now branching out into fields like robotics.

📖Open-source-powered software platforms and cross-OEM collaborations are accelerating innovation cycles—suggesting a more modular, agile, and collaborative future.

🚖In automated driving, Chinese players are not just experimenting—they’re deploying at scale. Full AI stacks and sovereign technology standards are emerging across the nation.

Yet amid this transformation, the global interplay remains strong:
🌎Many of the most advanced systems in Chinese vehicles are built on European and Western technology foundations.

🇪🇺European Tier-1 suppliers are crucial innovation partners—especially in areas like chassis, electronics, safety.

🆕And this year, Western OEMs are showing up with purpose—presenting new vehicle platforms, cutting-edge E/E architectures, and powertrain excellence designed to compete, inspire, and scale.

Why does this matter for Europe?
📰Because what’s introduced in Shanghai today will shape international markets tomorrow—be it via direct exports, partnerships, or a global shift in cost-performance expectations.

📈In 2024, China sold over 31 million vehicles, including 12.8 million NEVs—a 35% YoY surge. Without China’s scale, its policy-driven ecosystem, and its relentless innovation momentum, affordable electric mobility at global scale would remain out of reach.

📣I’m proud to join the China Car Symposium hosted by Prof. Dr. Ferdinand Dudenhoeffer  where I’ll moderate with my colleague Yan Chu Capgemini’s panel on
“Engineering Acceleration & Collaboration” exploring how to translate China speed into global opportunity through collaboration, capability, and co-innovation. My colleagues  Dr. Rainer Mehl and Hoi Tran will also be on stage talking with great guests like Stephan Durach of BMW Group (we all look forward to news from Neue Klasse!). Other speakers highlights: Ralf Brandstätter (VW) , Holger Klein (ZF) and Prof. Helmut List (AVL). 

Let’s connect in Shanghai—the epicenter of tomorrow’s mobility.

Check the event: https://lnkd.in/ddxzKnYF 24.04.25

Laurence Noël Luciano Bottoni Werner Christian

title: "Change of strategy in software", reads the front page of today's Handelsblatt. Together with experts such as Dr. Christ
text:
"Change of strategy in software", reads the front page of today's Handelsblatt. Together with experts such as Dr. Christoph Grote of BMW Group, Markus Baum of Roland Berger and Thomas Ingenlath CEO of Polestar, I was able to shed some light on how car companies are restructuring their projects for vehicle operating systems and forging mutually beneficial alliances with tech players.

💡 "The car industry is just realising that you don't just have to come to a forced arrangement with tech corporations like Apple or Google, but that you can even do so voluntarily (and in a beneficial way)." is my opinion.

⚡ Underestimated effort, lack of know-how, escalating costs: many automotive companies that wanted to develop their own operating systems for their vehicles and bring added value in-house (which in the past was often at suppliers) are now having to rethink their strategies.

🚗 Especially the lack of scaling is a real challenge for (premium) manufacturers with less than 2.5 million cars per year. 

⛅ At the same time, the tech players have significantly improved their offers. Not only because they want to keep the industry happy as eager users of comprehensive cloud services. They have also recognised that the use of user data needs to be better regulated collectively. 

💴 This also means that new economic models between the car and tech industries are suddenly becoming acceptable. 

🛠 What remains, however, is the challenge for the traditional TIER-1 suppliers. The long-standing innovation drivers of the industry must now rethink their offers in order to remain relevant in the long run.

More on this in today's Handelsblatt print edition or online: https://lnkd.in/dU7VJ3sc

title: Backwards out of the "vision-only" impasse? The Handelsblatt today analyzed Tesla 's decision to rely on a front radar a
text:
Backwards out of the "vision-only" impasse? The Handelsblatt today analyzed Tesla 's decision to rely on a front radar again in connection with the new hardware 4 generation. It was a pleasure for me to contribute with analyses together with Dr. Klaus Schmitz Arthur D. Little  and Sam Abuelsamid Guidehouse .

The reasons for using a modern 4D radar (can detect distance, speed, direction and height of objects) are obvious. It is a useful complement to camera sensors and can provide essential information in critical situations (dynamic/low lighting, fog, obscured objects, high speed, etc) to make driving decisions safer.

Leading edge radar systems, such as the Continental  ARS540, detect the environment with such a high quality that a reliably detection of underridable elevated objects, non overridable ground obstacles, complex/dense traffic, debris/potholes, road boundaries and landmarks are possible.  

It is remarkable that Tesla itself put development effort into its own radar system. A task that OEM’s usually put in the hands of Tier-1’s. The processors are based on proven technology from Xilinx - similar to what is used in other systems. However, the software part is maybe much more important to watch. In order to achieve maximum functionality, highly complex algorithms are required to develop a coherent image despite multipath reflections in the signal stream. 

More about this topic and the implications for Teslas Autopilot today in the Handelsblatt print edition or online:  https://lnkd.in/dqR_edTj

title: Is the Future of European EVs Being Sold Off? A (Deep) Dive into Sino-Western collaboration
text:
🚗🔋 Is the Future of European EVs Being Sold Off? A (Deep) Dive into Sino-Western collaboration

Recent trends in Germany have sparked discussions about the future of EVs. A decline in sales of electric vehicles meets concerns about the influx of Chinese manufacturers into the European market.

📉 According to Andreas Wehner, "New e-car registrations in Germany fell massively again in July. According to KBA statistics (https://lnkd.in/dJgieYJs), 36.8 per cent fewer BEVs were newly registered in July than a year earlier, at just under 31,000 units. title: A field full of opportunity.
text:
🌾 A field full of opportunity.
Agricultural machinery remains a market with strong prospects. Global demand for food is rising fast, while resources like soil, water, and fertilizers are becoming scarce. That tension fuels innovation and opens new paths for suppliers, engineers, and technology providers.

In Automobil Industrie’s special edition on Agricultural Engineering (right for the upcoming AGRITECHNICA fair in Hanover), I spoke about how automotive know-how in powertrains, electronics, and software-defined systems can directly accelerate this transformation.
As I said: “Higher yields with fewer resources can only be achieved through innovative, resource-efficient technologies.”

The numbers underline it:
🌍 The agricultural machinery market is growing 6–7% (CAGR) per year.
⚙️ Battery-electric drives up to 250 kW / 800 V are already being shown at Agritechnica 2025.
💧 The €18.9 million “AI Real Laboratory Agriculture” project explores how AI and robotics can make farming more sustainable and precise.

A big thanks to Claus-Peter Köth, Thomas Günnel, Hartmut Hammer and their editorial team for another outstanding issue, and to Dr. Tobias Ehrhard, Managing Director of the VDMA Agricultural Machinery Association, for his insights on the powertrains of the future— where sustainable fuels like HVO and RME meet advanced hybridisation.

Our article delves into the transformative potential of #AI. No industry can afford to ignore this potential, not only for end-user benefits but also for its profound impact on product architecture, engineering, and manufacturing.

🚀 AI, Shaping Our Future: We are witnessing the dawn of another industrial and social revolution. We already see remarkable improvements in machine translation and large language models, enabling new content creation for sales, marketing, and service sectors.

🤖 The Future of Robotics: AI-enhanced perception and new collaboration methods with humans will redefine machine capabilities. Social-cognitive learning will replace complex programming, applying to both traditional and humanoid robots.

title: "Billions in investment: Europe and the USA working on the chip turnaround", writes the Merkur, a Munich-based newspaper
text:
"Billions in investment: Europe and the USA working on the chip turnaround", writes the Merkur, a Munich-based newspaper, today.
It was a pleasure for me to provide some industry insights to the article.

Some take-aways:
* Taiwan is the world's leading producer of advanced microchips, but Europe and the United States are working to reduce their dependence on the island nation.

* The worldwide shortage of #semiconductors , known as the "chip crisis," caused by the Corona pandemic has eased for some chip categories (even causing short term oversupply). But the Taiwan conflict and the Ukraine war are putting long term recovery at risk.

* Especially the tensions between China and Taiwan are fueling concerns that the semiconductor shortage could worsen again. Some indicators of Chinese politics and a slow-down in economic growth in mainland China might lead to elevated conflict with the island.

* The U.S. has moved the issue high up on its political agenda, with President Joe Biden giving his blessing to the "US Chips and Science Act," under which semiconductor production in the United States is boosted to the tune of $52 billion. Further acts, like the Inflation Reduction Act, aim at boosting domestic production of various high-technology products, or technology ban's aiming at China's capability to design, manufacture and access advanced semiconductor technology, further impact the global markets.

* The EU launched the "European Chips Act" at the beginning of 2022 with a goal of doubling Europe's market share of global chip production to 20 percent by 2030 - in an overall growing market.

* On a local level, Bavaria's Minister of Economic Affairs Hubert Aiwanger has launched a Bavarian "semiconductor alliance" in an effort to strengthen chip sovereignty in Bavaria. 

For further details I invite you to have a look at the article in the Merkur (link in the comments).

If you are interested in where Germany's "silicon heart" beats - please look at the great overview from Silicon Saxony e. V. / Frank Bösenberg, link below.

title: "Chip shortage sends auto production sputtering": in an interview today with Janelle Dumalaon of Deutsche Welle Business
text:
"Chip shortage sends auto production sputtering": in an interview today with Janelle Dumalaon of Deutsche Welle Business TV we discussed how the #chipshortage affects the #automotiveindustry, which mitigation measures have been taken to ensure the #semiconductor supply and how these events will shape the #future #automotive #valuechain and #innovation.

* Global light-vehicle production likely to drop to around 76m vehicles in 2021. Over all vehicle classes, 7 to 10m cars will stay unbuilt due to #chipshortage.

* Global semiconductor market >500bn USD in 2021. Auto Industry share is just around 10%. 

* In the short term, OEM's act in taskforce mode to ensure availability of chips to re-assign them to the most profitable models in their portfolio. This mitigates the impact to the bottom-line quite well as we have seen with premium-brands in Europe or with high-end pick-up trucks and SUV's in the US. Also, the OEM have directly established connections to the semiconductor manufacturers, securing manufacturing capacity.

* More flexible handling of the #justintime paradigm required to increase resilience to upcoming supply chain disruptions in 2022 and beyond.

* OEM need to start initiatives to flexibilize their #electronics  #hardware / #software platforms to accept wider range of #silicon and to drive function integration of their #embeddedsystems, reducing the number of required critical #CPU's and other chips.

* In the medium term, the chip crisis acts as a catalyst for the industry, accellerating the process of OEM's to occupy new positions on the automotive value chain.

* Computing, control and connectivity hardware as well as the related vehicle software stack becoming a core competence of the automotive industry.

* Established Tier need to (partly) find new value propositions and excellence in those technological areas to stay relevant.

* Frameworks and open standards, like #SOAFEE of chip-powerhouse Arm or open-source working group of Eclipse, ETAS, Bosch, Microsoft and Red Hat, to play an important role in the future automotive landscape. 

You want to discuss more about this topic? Reach out to us! My colleagues Shiv Tasker, Scott Runner and I are happy to talk to you about how you can make this transformation happen.

Link to the interview: https://p.dw.com/p/42IHX

title: Is the AI boom creating a dangerous "cluster risk" for the chip industry?
text:
🤖 Is the AI boom creating a dangerous "cluster risk" for the chip industry? 
🏭 On the surface, the semiconductor industry appears to be sprinting from one record to the next, with revenues projected to hit $797 billion this year. But the headline numbers hide a fractured reality.

📰 As analyzed in today’s insightful Handelsblatt feature by Joachim Hofer, the market is currently deeply divided. While Nvidia and memory manufacturers are riding the AI gold rush, large parts of the industry are grappling with overcapacity and weak demand.

✳️ I was pleased to contribute my perspective to this analysis alongside other industry leaders: "AI is a cluster risk."

🔂 The industry's heavy reliance on a single technology trend and a few dominant players is distorting competition. For the broader ecosystem—especially in the automotive and industrial sectors—the necessary growth drivers are currently missing.

My concern is echoed by Boris Metodiev from TechInsights, who notes in the article that "only very few companies profit from the boom". He warns that for companies already facing headwinds, this consolidation "will be really painful".

Even major players in the traditional sectors are feeling this shift. As Infineon Technologies CFO Dr. Sven Schneider candidly admits in the piece: "Two years ago, we were more optimistic about growth expectations".

In my view, we won't see the full, balanced potential of the semiconductor industry until AI successfully bridges the gap from the data center to "Edge AI" applications in vehicles and end-user devices.

Thank you to the Handelsblatt team for shedding light on these critical market dynamics. 

👉 Link to the article with all details in the comments!

📊 Do you see the current market concentration as a short-term anomaly or a long-term threat to supply chain resilience?

Christoph Jungnickl Dr. Nico Wolf 

#Semiconductors #Automotive #AI #Strategy #innovation

title: China's Semiconductor Rise: Defying Sanctions and Reshaping the Global Tech Landscape 💡
text:
🌐 China's Semiconductor Rise: Defying Sanctions and Reshaping the Global Tech Landscape 💡

I had the privilege of speaking with Stern, the influential German news magazine, about China's remarkable push into the semiconductor industry. Despite facing increasing export restrictions from the West, China is rapidly advancing in this critical sector, with AI chips being a primary focus. 💻

Here are some key takeaways from our discussion:

1️⃣ €100 billion investment: China has committed more than €100 billion to develop an autonomous semiconductor ecosystem. While the media often focuses on cutting-edge "super chips", much of this investment is also going into mature nodes - the standard technologies that drive industrial semiconductors and make up the bulk of the market. They power industries from telecommunications to automotive, and cover technologies from processors and system-on-a-chip components to power electronics. 🚗📞

2️⃣ Building a self-sufficient value chain: What's truly remarkable is how China is attempting to build an almost complete value chain within its borders. From raw material extraction to finished goods. With $26 billion worth of semiconductor manufacturing equipment imported from Jan to Aug 2024 - a record high - China is a premium market for global suppliers.

3️⃣ AI chips and global competition: With the US restricting the export of its top AI chips, companies like Huawei are stepping up to the challenge. Huawei's Ascend 910C AI chip, which is roughly comparable to Nvidia's H100, is expected to reach €2 billion in annual sales, according to the manufacturer's estimates. While these chips may experience initial growing pains (similar to what we've seen with previous iterations), they are clear indicators of China's commitment to mastering AI technologies.

4️⃣ A shifting balance of power: China is increasingly setting its own norms/specifications and requiring companies to comply if they want to continue selling in the country. This is not just about technology - it is a geopolitical move. For example, standards for online access/monitoring of fab equipment, giving government bodies a near real-time view of the situation.

5️⃣ Challenges ahead: Despite the progress, there are hurdles. China's model is under pressure from overcapacity, intense domestic competition and the need for consolidation in various industries. Entrepreneurial spirit - a key driver of China's success - is also at a crossroads, raising questions about the long-term viability of the proven model established in the early 1980s.

As these dynamics evolve, the West needs to navigate this transformation with care. Europe, in particular, needs robust industrial policies to remain competitive in this rapidly changing landscape.

Read the full article here: https://lnkd.in/eY-H78az

#Semiconductors #Innovation #AI #China #Technology
author: Peter Fintl

title: The current Auto Shanghai Show has proved to be a festival for Chinese car manufacturers. With a fireworks display of ne
text:
The current Auto Shanghai Show has proved to be a festival for Chinese car manufacturers. With a fireworks display of new #electricvehicles, the domestic suppliers have caused unrest among Western and Japanese manufacturers, who are rightfully worried about their position in the important Chinese market.

It is now time to see what plans the Chinese manufacturers have for exporting their promising models. The leading trade journal »kfz-betrieb« researched in a piece from Andreas Wehner the plans of the Chinese suppliers, including the "early starters" BYD, ORA and WEY (Great Wall Motor Deutschland GmbH). 

Together with colleagues such as Jens Schulz from ORA Importer Emil Frey Gruppe Schweiz, Thiemo Jahnke from Wey and Matthias von Alten / Publicis Sapient, I was able to shed light on the opportunities and challenges of the "newcomers" on the German market. It was a pleasure for me to contribute technical analyses here.

* In times of the agency distribution model (which is a sour point for many dealers), the Chinese are (currently) trying a more cooperative model.

* The majority of German car dealerships and independent workshops are positive about cooperating with Chinese brands.

* However, there is still a long way to go to win the hearts of customers. As a survey by "KFZ-Betrieb" shows, many buyers are still sceptical. But if price and performance are right....

* It is often said that international imponderables make business with China a risk. However, past experience (and economic reality) shows that far-reaching decisions are made rationally in China. Therefore, from the traders' point of view, I would say that entrepreneurs can look to the future with optimism and continue to hope for prosperous cooperation with the Middle Kingdom.

The exact analyses as well as figures, data and facts here in the article: https://lnkd.in/e45wWdpZ

title: The IAA TRANSPORTATION has started this morning. The all-new format promised to be a platform for discussions around fut
text:
The IAA TRANSPORTATION has started this morning. The all-new format promised to be a platform for discussions around future #transportation - beyond  the #commercialvehicle. And it seems they delivered. 

The trends #sustainability, #automateddriving and #digital #innovation are some of the overarching  topics. 

At the press conferences this morning it was clear: the most efficient diesel-powered trucks of today will soon be accompanied by #electricvehicles for medium- and maybe - even long haul (Daimler eActros: up to 500km, megawatt charger). The upcoming models from Daimler, MAN or Volvo Trucks look very promising and will be on the roads soon - at competitive TCO rates. 

But also the #hydrogen #fuelcell option is alive: the Nikola or Daimler trucks are here as well as demonstrators from BOSCH or Cummins Inc.   

And, clearly, the fraction for automated driving has a strong presence here: ZF Group or Luminar Technologies are demonstrating their technologies. Same like Einride, who brought their autonomous truck and tele operation center. 

Such a safe, trustable and automated logistic system is also a topic Capgemini is talking about during the next days. We‘ll be around Hannover with our driving validation demonstration truck. 

Also digital services are everywhere. For instance, BOSCH announced it‘s partnership with Amazon Web Services (AWS) this morning providing digital operation and logistic services. 

See you at the IAA to discuss all future trends with the Capgemini team:

Ralf Blessmann Dr. Rainer Mehl Sebastian Tschödrich Markus S. Klaus Feldmann

title: “Exceptional, worth a special journey", these words from The MICHELIN Guide rankings I would like to borrow to describe
text:
“Exceptional, worth a special journey", these words from The MICHELIN Guide rankings I would like to borrow to describe “Motion. Autos, Art, Architecture” - the current special program at the Museo Guggenheim Bilbao, which I visited recently. 

In cooperation with the Sir Norman Foster Foundation, supported by Volkswagen AG and Iberdrola, a truly unique exhibition has been assembled.  

“The exhibition brings together nearly forty automobiles-each the best of its kind in such terms as beauty, rarity, technical progress and a vision of the future. These are placed centre stage in the galleries and surrounded
by significant works of art and architecture. Many of these have never before left their homes in private collections and public institutions, so
they are being opened up to a wider audience for the first time.”, says the invitation to the exhibition. 

If you are interested in the intersection of technology and art and want to see the works of Marcello Gandini, Flaminio Bertoni, Harley Earl, … as well as art works in the league of Andy Warhol or David Hockney - then you should come to #Bilbao.  

„Unlike any other single invention, the automobile has completely transformed the urban and rural landscape of our planet and in turn our lifestyle. We are on the edge of a new revolution of electric power, so this exhibition could be seen as a requiem for the last days of combustion.” 

Further readings here: https://lnkd.in/drH256EG

title: „The challenge of new fuels“, is the topic of the current #MTZ. Here are some thoughts about this edition of the most re
text:
„The challenge of new fuels“, is the topic of the current #MTZ. Here are some thoughts about this edition of the most reputable magazine for propulsion technology… for which I had the honor to contribute a guest commentary. 

👉 We need to manage a swift #transition to #carbonneutral #mobility. Everyone in the #automotiveindustry agrees. 

👉 While #batteryelectric propulsion is becoming mainstream (and the best solution) for many applications, it is clear that #BEV can and will not fit all use cases and energy supply systems. 

👉 The same is true for #hydrogen . We will enter a #hydrogeneconomy . But research is becoming more clearer that using #greenhydrogen for #fuelcell cars, urban/regional delivery #trucks as well as domestic heating is a sub-optimal use of this scarce resource. We are much better off prioritizing it’s use in the chemical industry, for steel manufacturing, off-grid machinery or long-term #energystorage. 

👉 For #shipping or #longhaul #aviation, we need green hydrogen most probably in the form of #ammonia or as #efuels.  

👉 and those efuels, as well as their use in internal combustion engines, are a part of our #sustainabledevelopment 

👉 so we will go for #electricmobility (mostly), but killing off now all #ICE development and the respective work for efuels and breaking this lever for #decarbonization is not wise. Especially for countries who could soon become a vital exporter of energy. 

👉 so the often cited “technology openness” does not mean to cling to polluting engines forever. It means to explore all technological alternatives to manage the #energytransition as smooth as possible and to meet the #climategoals as desperately needed. 

More about this in my commentary at https://lnkd.in/efKXZ3xV

And if you need even more reasons to regularly read the MTZ: this edition features a nice article from Richard Backhaus about “#battery raw materials-where from and where to?” as well as an interesting interview with Prof. Frank Atzler , holder of the chair of combustion engineering and #powertrain  systems at Technische Universität Dresden about #syntheticfuels and hydrogen. 

Capgemini #consulting Capgemini Engineering #automotive #renewables #sustainability #development #engineering

title: Historical Insights, Modern Challenges: What a 1936 novel can teach us about navigating the future of AI
text:
🤖 Historical Insights, Modern Challenges: What a 1936 novel can teach us about navigating the future of AI

As AI continues to soar towards the peak of the hype cycle, we find ourselves at a crossroads: Will AI remain a sophisticated "stochastic parrot", or are we on the cusp of true artificial general intelligence?

🦾 Another strong impetus for me to think about "what if" was the World Robot Conference in Beijing this week. Chinese companies showed off humanoid robots designed to work in factories and warehouses, and the made-in-China precision parts needed to build them. See the booth of the Chinese Academy of Sciences. 

🇨🇳This is underpinned by the industry policy of developing “new productive forces”, with mass production of humanoid robots to begin as early as 2025 - a topic I've covered before.

📕 During my recent holiday, I picked up Karel Čapek's “War with the Newts”, a novel written in 1936.

🇨🇿 Fun fact: Karel Čapek introduced the word "robot" to the English language in his 1920 play “R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)”. In many ways, he was ahead of his time in thinking about the relationship between humans and intelligent machines.

📰 Čapek's story of humanity discovering and exploiting a new species (“Newts”) - only to face unforeseen and uncontrollable consequences - offers profound parallels to how we might engage with AI.

SPOILER ALERT: if you intent to read the book, skip the following part

The novel highlights five key themes that resonate deeply with our current AI journey:

1. Exploitation and commodification: Just as humans initially view newts as a resource, advanced AI is likely developed with a focus on commercial gain, potentially at the expense of ethical considerations.

2. Underestimation of intelligence: The newts were underestimated, just as we may underestimate the potential of AI in the long run, particularly the risks of it evolving beyond our control.

3. Loss of Control: The newts eventually rebel against their human oppressors, reflecting fears that AI might act autonomously and unpredictably if it becomes truly intelligent.

4. Moral and Ethical Dilemmas: The treatment of the newts raises questions about the ethics of exploiting sentient beings, a debate that will become increasingly relevant as AI becomes more sophisticated.

5. Global Consequences: The novel depicts a global crisis resulting from humanity's actions - an outcome that parallels the potential for AI to cause widespread disruption if not managed responsibly.

As we move forward in our pursuit of advanced AI: will we handle this transformative technology with the wisdom it requires, or will we, like the characters in Čapek's novel, overlook the potential dangers until it's too late?

I highly recommend this book as an entertaining way to think about the future of AI and the ethical challenges it poses.

Find it here: https://amzn.eu/d/hiYiQRb

Cc: Daniela Rittmeier Dr. Cara Antoine 

#AI #Innovation Capgemini Engineering

title: On Labor Day: Honoring Work — and Rethinking Its Future
text:
On Labor Day: Honoring Work — and Rethinking Its Future

Every year on May 1st, much of the world pauses to reflect on the value of labor. The day traces back to the labor movements of the late 19th century, which fought for fair hours, safe conditions, and human dignity. It reminds us that work is more than productivity. It is identity, contribution, and connection.

But today, the definition of work is evolving—faster than ever before.

At this year’s HANNOVER MESSE, and just days ago at the Auto Shanghai Show, it became vividly clear to me:
We are witnessing the next industrial revolution, driven by #AI, #robotics, and digital ecosystems.

In Shanghai, robots were highly visible—
🤖 As agile humanoids walking factory floors (e.g. Unitree Robotics),
🤝 As service assistants in restaurants (e.g. Pudu Robotics)
🚗 As autonomous vehicles soon navigating Chinese traffic. (e.g. Horizon Robotics, Huawei)

I believe these aren’t gimmicks—they are signals.
Signals of a new kind of labor emerging across all domains.

And it’s not just robotics. AI—particularly Generative AI—has already reshaped the workplace.
From coding copilots to automated test suites, GenAI enables engineers and software developers to focus on higher-value thinking. 
It augments talent, rather than replacing it. 

As I recently shared in an interview with Süddeutsche Zeitung on the new wave of AI-powered robotic systems:

“Fabriken, in denen vorwiegend nur noch Roboter arbeiten, halte ich nicht bloß für unvermeidlich, sondern sogar für erstrebenswert.”

But this vision is not just about automation—it’s about redefinition.
Work in the future won’t vanish. It will shift:

🏭 In factories, from manual repetition to high-value oversight.
🧠 In services, from transactions to truly human-centered experiences.
🏡 In our private lives, digital agents may become everyday companions—handling tasks, learning with us, and even co-creating.

Yet, it is not only about technology that changes work. Speed matters.

Last week at the China Car Symposium, I had the privilege of decoding what “China Speed” really means—together with Lijun(Jerry) Zhang from Horizon Robotics and Wilson Yan Chu from Capgemini.
It’s a powerful mix of mindset, vertical integration, trust, and uncompromising customer focus. And it shows us what’s possible. (Thank you Prof. Dr. Ferdinand Dudenhoeffer , Dr. Rainer Mehl for the format)

💡 The pressure for change is real—but so is the opportunity.
The future of work is not just about technology—it’s about vision, values, and collaboration.

Today, on International Labor Day, let’s honor the past—but also get excited about what’s next.
Because work is changing.
And being part of that change is one of the most meaningful things we can do.

#LaborDay #FutureOfWork #AI #Robotics #ChinaSpeed #GenAI Capgemini Automotive #IndustrialInnovation #WorkplaceTransformation #TechnologyLeadership #ShanghaiAutoShow #Freundschaft

text:
**𝐂𝐄𝐒 2024: 𝐀𝐈 𝐓𝐚𝐤𝐞𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐖𝐡𝐞𝐞𝐥, 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐈𝐭'𝐬 𝐍𝐨𝐭 𝐀𝐮𝐭𝐨𝐩𝐢𝐥𝐨𝐭**
 
As the anticipation for CES 2024 builds, it's not just the tech community buzzing with predictions. The venerable newspaper Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung FAZ has chimed in too, offering an insightful outlook on the event where AI promises to shine – and where I had the privilege of contributing insights.
 
You can find the article right here https://lnkd.in/dEMvTh8N
 
or have a look at the key take-aways from my point of view:
 
🤖 AI's transition from a burgeoning tech segment to a mainstream powerhouse is the hallmark of CES 2024, according to Gary Shapiro, President of Consumer Technology Association. It's no longer a futuristic concept but a present-day business imperative, driving innovation across diverse sectors.
 
🌐 CES has evolved into a confluence of technological advancement, where traditional and cutting-edge innovations merge. It's a platform where industry leaders and visionaries chart the course for the next tech frontier. It is estimated to attract around 130k visitors this year and 3500 exhibitors.
 
🚗 The automotive sector at CES is poised for a pragmatic shift. The focus is on real-world applications of technology, reflecting a matured approach to product unveilings and industry trends. The number of vehicle premiers is expected to be lower (rumors suggest to look at Honda, Hyundai). Also in the much looked-at keynotes prominent automotive brand names will be missed.
 
🏭 Instead, the industrial players will take a stage. Siemens CEO Roland Busch and its Head of Digital Business, Cedrik Neike, will showcase the companies take on the 'industrial metaverse' at CES that shall exemplify the fusion of AI with industrial prowess. This underlines the industries ambitions to reimagining industrial operations, highlighting the potential of digital twins and AI-driven simulations.
 
🌎  With CES unfolding in an US election year, the event underscores the intricate interplay between technology innovation and regulatory frameworks. The CES is expected to also be a place of dialogue, which is crucial for shaping a conducive environment for technological advancements.
 
If you want to learn more about what is “beyond (artificial) intelligence” or how to navigate your business successfully through this transformative times, I cordially invite you to give a visit to the Capgemini Executive Lounge at The Venetian, Titian Ballroom. 

Capgemini presents a curated series of keynotes, future-oriented workshops, and innovative demos on some of the leading technologies and applications driving the transformation across key industries.

title: "Wolfspeed and ZF Group plan €2bn chip factory for SiC in Saarland," reports Handelsblatt . For this article I was allow
text:
"Wolfspeed and ZF Group plan €2bn chip factory for SiC in Saarland," reports Handelsblatt . For this article I was allowed to shed a little light on the power semiconductor industry.

* The accelerating shift to electric propulsion for passenger cars and commercial vehicles, as well as the need for industry and consumers to use electrical energy in a low-loss manner, is driving global demand for highly efficient power semiconductors made from materials such as SiC and GaN. The market will develop into a >6bn USD Segment until 2027, according to most forecasts. 

* Wolfspeed  along with Infineon Technologies , STMicroelectronics , ROHM Co., Ltd. , one of the major suppliers of these special components is now probably planning to invest heavily in Germany after opening its "mega factory" in the USA.

* as a lesson from the chip crisis, more and more OEM's and Tier-1's are now participating directly in such factories or are making advance payments to obtain fixed quotas (e.g. Borg-Warner).

* many consider this planned investment by Wolfspeed as a direct challenge to the competitor Bosch  which has just invested heavily in this area.

* however, one must not forget the worldwide competition. Also in response to the chip ban, China has begun to significantly realign its semiconductor strategy. Chinese #automotive OEM’s, for instance are pushing hard to use chips from local sources like BYD Semiconductor, Hangzhou Silan Microelectronics, Starpower Semiconductor, Zhuzhou CRRC Times Electric, China Resources Micro. 

* High investments are now flowing in order to enforce "Made in China" also in the area of power electronics with wide bandgap materials and to achieve a leading position on the world market. Geely, for example, has recently invested directly in the Chinese SiC startup AccoPower (accompanied by political goodwill).

title: Tesla beats all expectations. Find below all the important points from the earnings call last night. In an interview wit
text:
Tesla beats all expectations. Find below all the important points from the earnings call last night. In an interview with the Handelsblatt, we discussed and evaluated the key points right during the night.

In a nutshell:
* Revenue 53,8 bn USD in 2021 (+71% YOY)
* Earnings 5,5 bn USD net in 2021 (+665% YOY)
* Operating margin 12,1% (beating most other #automotive  OEM's, on par with German luxury brands)
* total 936k vehicles delivered (+90% YOY)
* production network under utilized due to #chipcrisis and other #supplychain issues, to remain throughout 2022
* production ramp up in plan (Austin, Berlin), 4680 cell production, structural #battery integration, #vehicle assembly
* efficient #manufacturing in #Giga #shanghai is boosting margins, as well as operation optimizations and increased prices across many markets and models
* vehicle deliveries can grow to 1,5m in 2022 (if supply chain issues permit), 3m in 2024
* since #semiconductor supply issues will stay, no new model will be introduced in 2022 (#cybertruck, Roadster, Semi, small vehicle). Simply focus on existing portfolio
* Cybertruck faces challenges in production cost optimization
* Elon still super bullish on #fsd. Expects automated driving "safer than human" within 2022. Based on current FSD beta technology. Progress of #DOJO project not a limiter for FSD. It is a cost optimization and acceleration of training time.
* Tesla assumes that FSD enabled #robotaxis will supercharge software revenues and disrupt vehicle sales (car "appreciating asset"). Again stated: all cars produced in the last 3 to 4 years FSD capable (with mods maybe).
* Elon says, the tesla robot (#optimus "Subprime") expected to become bigger than car business in the long run. First applications of the humanoid #Teslabot production robot for logistic applications to be expected in 2022 in Tesla own factories
* Energy business to regain focus in 2022. Might grow by 50%. All stationary storage to switch to #lithium #Iron / #manganese  based chemistry in the medium term
* Cross-industrial exploitation of Tesla HVAC know-how not a priority
* Tesla insurance to be rolled out US wide in 2022: starting to work on Europe in late 2022. Live feedback to drivers about driving style and insurance costs makes traffic safer ( based on data from Texas)

See the excellent Handelsblatt piece of Felix Holtermann (Handelsblatt correspondent New York) for a comprehensive assessement of the facts above: https://lnkd.in/eSRUauMZ

title: "Are lavish subsidies for the semiconductior industry worth paying for? - The Taiwanese chip manufacturer TSMC is settin
text:
"Are lavish subsidies for the semiconductior industry worth paying for? - The Taiwanese chip manufacturer TSMC is setting up shop in Dresden - in partnership with Bosch, Infineon and NXP. High subsidies also made the deal possible. In an interview with n-tv, I talk about the opportunities and challenges of Germany as a location for semiconductor research and production, why subsidies and an intelligent industrial policy are necessary for Europe, the possibilities of the EU Chips Act and what still needs to be done.


Some key facts about the new fab:

TSMC said it would invest up to €3.499 billion in a subsidiary, European Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (ESMC), of which it will own 70%.

German industrial giant Bosch, semiconductor specialist Infineon and Dutch chip giant NXP will each own 10% of the plant, which will produce up to 40,000 300mm (12 inch) wafers a month for cars and industrial and consumer products when it opens in 2027. 

The future Dresden facility will be operated by TSMC and will be based on the company's established and performant 28/22-nanometer planar CMOS and 16/12-nanometer FinFET process technology.

Germany will contribute up to 5 billion euros to the new Dresden plant. The factory will cost around €10 billion in total.


You can read the full interview here: https://lnkd.in/dBYhMM6N

title: climateneutral #vehicle powertrains can be realized on several technology paths. In addition to #batteries , climate-neu
text:
#climateneutral #vehicle powertrains can be realized on several technology paths. In addition to #batteries , climate-neutral #greenhydrogen is a suitable #energystorage medium. In an interview with the Automobil Industrie, we looked at the opportunities offered by the use of #hydrogen in internal combustion engines. 
* In terms of overall efficiency, the hydrogen ICE is within striking distance of #fuelcell solutions. 
* However, hydrogen will play a key role as an energy carrier for the #decarbonization of many sectors. 
* For example, the IEA Energy Outlook 2021 sees the demand for 2030 at over 200Mt (Net Zero scenario). A large part of this will have to be used for industrial applications.
* This means that green hydrogen will remain a scarce resource without accelerated efforts to build an international production-, trading- and distribution infrastructure.
* For mainstream passenger vehicles in many markets hydrogen will likely not be the first choice.
* But the real demand for #efuels will be exciting. Even if the production of these synthetic fuels requires a high energy input (and overall efficiency is poor compared to #electricvehicles), an answer can and must be found to the decarbonization of the current stock of combustion-engine vehicles of over 1.4 billion units worldwide. 

Read more about hydrogen in vehicles in the article:
https://lnkd.in/eeczv4ab

title: "Real men have fabs." Elon Musk thinks Silicon Valley Legend Jerry Sanders was right all along. When AMD still owned its
text:
"Real men have fabs." Elon Musk thinks Silicon Valley Legend Jerry Sanders was right all along. When AMD still owned its fabs in the 1980s, Sanders coined that line to defend vertical integration against an industry that was about to bet everything on the opposite. Four decades later, Elon Musk is reviving the argument at "tera" scale.

In today's Handelsblatt feature by Joachim Hofer, I shared my thoughts on Musk's Terafab plan with Intel, possibly the most expensive and ambitious semiconductor project ever announced. A Texas megafab, integrating chip design, fabrication, packaging, and test on one site, sized to deliver one terawatt of compute for Tesla vehicles, Optimus robots, SpaceX comms, and orbital AI data centers.

Musk's playbook is consistent: identify a systemic bottleneck, deconstruct the established supply chain, bet on radical vertical integration. It worked with cars, space launchers, and satellite comms. With Optimus, the jury is still out. 

With Terafab, he attempts reversing 25 years of industry specialization in a single move.

Which is also why I doubt he'll build it exactly the way he announced it. 
The hurdles are real:
* Lip-Bu Tan (Intel) frames it as filling a global supply chain gap, but Intel Foundry has also been trying hard to land external customers for years.
* Tanjeff Schadt (Strategy&) reminds us that there are reasons only three firms worldwide can manufacture the most advanced nodes.
* TechInsights estimates years until installation, plus another year of yield ramp before the fab is competitive.
* Jensen Huang (Nvidia) recently noted that TSMC's real moat isn't technology. It's responsiveness to customer demand.

And the strategic imperatives from Beijing add another layer.

I'm in China this week for Auto China 2026, and the contrast is sharp. What Musk is attempting privately, the People's Republic is executing as state industrial policy. The 15th Five Year Plan elevates intelligent connected NEVs and semiconductor independence to the same strategic tier. Beijing is reportedly guiding domestic automakers toward roughly 100% local chip sourcing by 2027 in select segments, a timeline that would have looked like fantasy not long ago.

Two parallel bets on vertical integration: one billionaire in Texas, one nation across the Pacific. Same diagnosis: the most advanced manufacturing has become a geopolitical chokepoint. Very different toolkits.

My base case: Musk won't build the world's largest integrated fab the way he sketched it. But he doesn't have to. The announcement alone resets expectations.

The established semicon players won't be displaced. They will be forced to become faster, better, and different.

Full Handelsblatt article with all the details linked in the comments.

#Semiconductors #Terafab #Intel #ChipIndependence #innovation

title: 𝗛𝗮𝗿𝗱𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗳𝘁𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘀𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝗰𝗸𝘀: 𝗔 𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗸 𝗮𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗱𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗱𝗶𝘀𝗿𝘂𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗼�
text:
𝗛𝗮𝗿𝗱𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗳𝘁𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘀𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝗰𝗸𝘀: 𝗔 𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗸 𝗮𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗱𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗱𝗶𝘀𝗿𝘂𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗵𝗮𝗽𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗜 𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝘆. A fascinating read in today's Handelsblatt on the shifting power dynamics in the AI landscape. I had the pleasure of weighing in on Meta's massive, up-to-$60 billion custom chip agreement with AMD.

As I noted to the editors: "Nvidia customers are desperately seeking alternatives." The math behind this is simple. When the dominant AI chipmaker operates at a significantly higher profit margin than its biggest buyers, those tech giants will inevitably engineer their way out of the squeeze.

We are seeing this live. 

As Meta's infrastructure chief Santosh Janardhan confirmed in the piece, their strategy relies heavily on mixing multi-vendor processors with their own proprietary designs. They are aggressively diversifying their infrastructure toward bespoke silicon, AMD, and alternatives like Google’s TPUs.

But these structural shifts don't stop at the hardware layer. We are seeing a parallel, real-time disruption in enterprise software.

Look at Anthropic's recent rollout of a new Claude Code, designed to automate the modernization of legacy COBOL systems. The market reaction was swift and decisive, with IBM's stock plunging over 13%. Why? Because an incredibly complex migration process that historically required whole armies of consultants can now potentially be tackled in a matter of quarters instead of years.

This dynamic perfectly illustrates the intense pressure currently weighing on traditional SaaS and tech stocks. AI is actively commoditizing code generation and collapsing the defensive moats of legacy software and service models.

However, there is a crucial caveat here: AI will massively change our economic system, but it will not implement itself.

The technology provides the foundation, but making a real impact requires workflow redesign, deep integration across data ecosystems, and strategic change management. This is exactly why OpenAI's recent announcement of their "Frontier Alliances" is such a massive market signal. They have recognized that to scale enterprise AI beyond isolated pilots, they need full-service end-to-end transformation partners.

I am incredibly proud that Capgemini is one of the foundational partners in this alliance. We are moving from the era of AI experimentation into the era of industrialization, helping to wire these frontier models securely and reliably into the core systems that enterprises actually run on.

We are witnessing a complete top-to-bottom rewiring of the tech stack. From the physical data center all the way up to the oldest enterprise mainframes.

🔗 You can find the link to the full Handelsblatt article in the comments below.

Francois Babin Luz G. Mauch Christoph Jungnickl Dr. Nico Wolf 

#AI #Semiconductors #Innovation #technology

title: Hydrogen is dead. Long live Hydrogen! 🔋💧
text:
Hydrogen is dead. Long live Hydrogen! 🔋💧
Excited to contribute to the current debate on the future of hydrogen in mobility in the latest WirtschaftsWoche article by stefan hajek.

The German government has announced a further €220 million funding package for hydrogen infrastructure. This sparks a crucial discussion: Are we strategically building the future, or are we subsidizing past the market ("am Markt vorbei")?  

As I mentioned in the article, the industry reality is becoming undeniable: "The hydrogen drive in passenger cars is as good as dead.". The long-held hope that a hydrogen network for trucks would be cross-financed by wealthy passenger car users is no longer realistic.  

The data supports this shift:
💹The Market: While we have over 2 million BEVs on German roads, new registrations for fuel cell cars dropped to just 49 in 2025.  

💶The Cost: As Florian Hacker from the Oeko-Institut e.V. points out, the Total Cost of Ownership speaks a clear language. Projected costs for 2030 show battery-electric trucks at ~€0.90/km versus hydrogen trucks at ~€1.50/km.  

🚛The Fleet: We already see ~93,000 electric trucks registered in Germany compared to roughly 300 hydrogen trucks. 

🙋Does this mean Hydrogen is out? Absolutely not.

Hydrogen remains an indispensable pillar of our future energy architecture. It is vital for resilience, energy storage, and decarbonizing hard-to-abate industrial sectors. However, in road mobility—where efficiency and cost per mile are king—the battery has taken the lead.

Innovation is about placing the right technology in the right use case. Let’s focus our hydrogen efforts where they yield the highest systemic value, rather than trying to revive a use case the market has already decided on.

Thank you to the authors for the deep dive and to the fellow experts for the fact-based analysis.

Link to the article in the comments!

Oliver Hegen Alexander Sorgenicht Dr. Dorothea Pohlmann Jens Radtke 

#Hydrogen #Mobility #EnergyTransition #Capgemini #Innovation #FutureOfMobility #Automotive #battery

title: Chips, AI, and Data have already become battlegrounds in the race for global technological dominance. Will robots and ad
text:
Chips, AI, and Data have already become battlegrounds in the race for global technological dominance. Will robots and advanced machine tools be next?

The U.S. government has launched a Section 232 investigation to determine whether robot and machine tool imports pose a national security risk. The potential outcome: tariffs that could disrupt global value chains and industrial competitiveness.

Martin Ciupek investigated this situation with an article for Ingenieur.de/VDI nachrichten, leading experts weighed in:
* Thilo Brodtmann (VDMA) warned of a fundamental breach of the transatlantic “tariff deal,” insisting: “European mechanical engineering is not a security risk!”

* Legal experts from White & Case LLP pointed to the dynamics of the process and the possibility of swift decisions.

* Thomas Dr. Klindt , partner at the law firm Noerr, sees the national security aspect a “disguise” for erecting trade barriers. 

* I looked at the technology aspect and stressed that such measures could hold back U.S. re-industrialization rather than accelerate it – particularly where robotics are essential to overcome labor shortages and productivity gaps. Also, it may lead to further trade barriers for American Chips and AI technologies. 

I am privileged to see every day how software, semiconductors, and data-driven intelligence are converging with advanced hardware to shape the future of industries. Restricting access to robotics could slow down precisely the transformation needed to stay competitive.

For Germany and Europe, the stakes are equally high: our strength in machine tools and advanced manufacturing, showcased most recently at the EMO Hannover, makes the continent both a leader and a potential target in this evolving debate. Any escalation risks undermining innovation, competitiveness, and transatlantic industrial cooperation.

👉 I invite you to read the full article here: https://lnkd.in/dDZXz-6t

👉 What’s your view: do trade barriers ensure security or do they stifle the very innovation that drives progress?

#innovation #robotics Jochen Bechtold Christoph Jungnickl

title: Battery-electric drivetrains are king in terms of overall efficiency. But are they (already) fit for all use-cases? Let‘
text:
Battery-electric drivetrains are king in terms of overall efficiency. But are they (already) fit for all use-cases? Let‘s have a look at the specific requirements of future sustainable, electrically driven aviation.

🏋️‍♀️ Weight: every kilo saved counts for aircrafts. Despite promising development paths for improved energy density of batteries, from todays perspective fuel cell systems using hydrogen as energy carrier have significant benefits in terms of system weight.

🌏 Range: Fuel-cell systems for aircraft propulsion show potential advantages in terms of achievable flight range compared to purely battery powered aircraft’s.

⏱️ Downtime: a fast turn-around time is a major factor for the profitable operation of aircraft’s. Despite improving fast-charging capabilities of batteries (where colleagues like Christoph Weissinger  and Tobias Titze are working on) refuelling hydrogen is still faster from todays point of view.

💴 Cost: Battery technology is improving at incredibly speed. And with growing adoption, scale effects in production are driving down price (yes, my colleague Michael Müller and his teams are on this challenge).  Still, for heavy-duty applications, many studies show that fuel-cells are the cheapest way to achieve decarbonisation.

🏭 Infrastructure: For battery-powered systems we need to build a powerful fast-charging system that is capable of handling peak demand. This is expensive. For hydrogen powered fuel-cells you simply need to build a bigger tank (yes, oversimplified) on site to handle such demands. This storage advantage of hydrogen makes fuel-cell systems attractive for some applications. 

Overall, those advantages make fuel-cell powered aircraft’s a viable option, like pictured below in the concept from Airbus.

In order to drive progress in fuel cell technology, we need to better understand physical and chemical mechanisms to optimise the chemical compositions of the electrodes and electrolytes.

With classical computation methods building simulation models to this fine detail and highest fidelity is next to impossible.

With our partners in the EQUALITY Project we try to use quantum computing to create new solvers for differential equations to make such simulations feasible.

I‘m always impressed what my colleagues around Wael Yahyaoui  and the many industry partners create in this exciting project.

Have a look at how EQUALITY will contribute to advance fuel cell technology here:

⚡ Problem specification sheets (energy storage): https://lnkd.in/epeh4_Z7  

💹 Market analysis, business model and upscaling: https://lnkd.in/erjb8Si6  

Learn more about EQUALITY: http://equality-quantum.eu

title: The European debut of the Tesla Semi: A turning point in electric trucking or "cool is not enough"?
text:
The European debut of the Tesla Semi: A turning point in electric trucking or "cool is not enough"?

🌟 The Tesla Semi made its long-awaited European debut at the IAA TRANSPORTATION in Hanover this week and quickly became one of the highlights of the show. Its sleek, minimalist design and innovative features captivated visitors and industry competitors alike. This is why the leading european news magazine DER SPIEGEL has taken a closer look and also spoke with Prof. Ferdinand Dudenhöffer and me. 

⌛ The first prototypes of the electric truck were unveiled to the public seven years ago. It was announced that the Semi would eventually be equipped with the Plaid drivetrain familiar from the Model S, with an output of 1020 hp, capable of accelerating the truck from 0 to 60 mph in 5 seconds. That got people's attention. Tesla boss Elon Musk wanted the truck to go into production in 2019. Now it seems that large-scale production is planned for 2025/2026. A considerable delay. And time waits for no one!

⚡ Traditional manufacturers such as Volvo Trucks, MAN Truck & Bus SE, DAF Trucks NV/PACCAR, Mercedes-Benz Trucks (the eActros 600 has just been named Truck of the Year), Scania Group and Iveco Group also presented their own electric trucks at the show, many of which are already in production or will be soon. As a result, I was quoted in the article as saying that the event was a "show without question marks".

🚨  For example, fellow industry expert Prof. Dr. Ferdinand Dudenhoeffer is critical of the semi-truck. He puts it bluntly: "The chances that Tesla will 'take-off' with the Semi truck in Europe are close to zero".

And from my point of view, there are good reasons why success is not guaranteed: besides strong competition, the innovative concept needs work to meet local requirements, a service network must be built. And that takes time and money.

However, it's important to consider Tesla's strengths:

🔋 Expertise in battery management and vehicle efficiency: Tesla's extensive experience in optimising battery performance and vehicle efficiency could give it a competitive edge. In addition to mastering NCM battery chemistry, Tesla is making the most out of LFP cells for passenger car applications. 

🔌 Charging network leadership: With one of the most robust global charging infrastructures, Tesla offers convenience and reliability that few can match. So why not be bold in CV charging too?

🤖 Ambitious fully self-driving system: Tesla's big bet on autonomous driving technology could revolutionise freight transport in the future. Once FSD is mature and ready for non-US markets, it would certainly be applicable to commercial applications, too.

Find the article here: https://lnkd.in/duiQfSxb

Have you visited the Semi Truck in Hanover? What is your take away?

Capgemini Automotive #innovation Capgemini Engineering #consulting

title: „BMW will end #i3 production in July as buyers shun once ground-breaking EV“, reports Automotive News Europe. It was a p
text:
„BMW will end #i3 production in July as buyers shun once ground-breaking EV“, reports Automotive News Europe. It was a pleasure for me to provide some insights to this article, as the i3 idea as well as the unique technology utilized is still really remarkable on many levels - even a decade after it‘s inception. The „project i“ team under Ulrich Kranz pioneered innovations in this vehicle class like: 
* Aluminum chassis
* Carbon frame
* Plastic body
* Rear-wheel drive
* Portal doors
BMW Group  also spent significant efforts on #sustainability, considering the whole lifecycle of the vehicle. Recyclates and natural fibers have been used extensively.

This all brought a unique „Mega City Vehicle“ to the market with then excellent figures for weight (1,345kg for the i3 120Ah version) and efficiency (test mix ~17.9 kWh/100km incl. charging loss in ADAC  EcoTest) and even a range-extender option. 

However, it also showed the facts that the success of an electric vehicle not only depends on affordable driving range (battery capacity, energy consumption) but also on an available charging infrastructure. And as those factors improved over the life cycle (two capacity upgrades of the Samsung SDI batteries, overall improvement in charging infrastructure) so did the sales numbers increase year on year to a total close to 250k.

But as the proverb says: „Better is the enemy of good“. So BMW has already prepared worthy electric successors for the i3, catering to various customer needs. Many experiences and technologies of the i3 have inspired those new vehicles. 

The full article can be found here: https://lnkd.in/eYS76PV4 (paywall)

Please find below a BMW corporate video I found which outlines the interesting features of the i3. 

https://lnkd.in/efAGS4nX

title: Megawatt Charging: A Bold Signal from China – but is it really a Wake-up Call for the Industry? Check out the expert dis
text:
🔋 Megawatt Charging: A Bold Signal from China – but is it really a Wake-up Call for the Industry? Check out the expert discussions!

The new print edition of Automobilwoche just came hot off the press—and it’s packed with stories that show one thing: the automotive industry is wild right now.

One article from Micha Gebhardt stood out: BYD’s promise of 1000 kW fast charging for EVs. A charging process that takes just 5 minutes for 400 km? It's a bold claim—and possibly a bold move to reset industry expectations.

I had the pleasure of sharing my perspective alongside Prof. Martin Doppelbauer (Karlsruhe Institute of Technology (KIT)), Dr. Philipp Seidel, LL.M. (Arthur D. Little), and Matthias Vogt (ADAC) in two feature articles exploring the technical and strategic implications.

In my view: 
⚡ It’s not just about the battery—power electronics and high-efficiency semiconductors (Silicon Carbide technology) are make-or-break elements on both vehicle and infrastructure side.

⚙️ BYD’s edge today lies in full-stack integration: vehicles, batteries, chargers, and SiC chips—all in-house.

🔋 Thermal management, voltage levels beyond 800V, and infrastructure with buffer batteries are part of the puzzle. Chinese players try to master all elements of the value chain. Western players usually try to solve it via eco-system play.

But the real question is: 
🧭 Do we need megawatt charging—now? For most drivers, likely not. For selected fleet or urban use cases, absolutely. And for commercial vehicle use-cases it makes certainly sense. And highest peak charging power gives owners bragging rights. With tongue-in-cheek: are peak kw-charging the new horse-power? 

💸 And let’s be honest: kilowatt-hour pricing at this speed will be a key differentiator for adoption.

Ultimately, this isn’t just about charging speed. It’s about signaling capability, ambition, and readiness to lead. Whether or not it scales, BYD is making a statement—and the rest of the industry is watching. For instance last week at the HANNOVER MESSE, it was next to impossible to find one single plug of the MCS Megawatt Charging system on display. But at least, I found a nice 500kW charging station from Delta Electronics. And a high power, battery buffered charger from ADS-TEC Energy (max 300kW, as far as I have seen). So, kudos!

🔍 Thoughts? Are we looking at a turning point—or a tactical distraction?

Please find the articles with the full information and technical data here: https://lnkd.in/dwHFqwbi or in the print edition of Automobilwoche.

More information on the (Western) Megawatt Charging initiative #MCS (from CharIN e.V.) can be found in the comments!
