title: The joy of choice - not only for China. In the slipstream of the big chip architectures from Intel Corporation and Arm,
text:
The joy of choice - not only for China. In the slipstream of the big chip architectures from Intel Corporation and Arm, the open standard RISC-V International has evolved from an academic exercise into a thoroughly serious - and attractive - alternative.

As part of its "Insight Innovation" series, the Handelsblatt investigated which recent developments are giving the RISC-V standard a tailwind and what new market is emerging as a result - also for German companies.

The article features experts like Lars Reger, industry veteran and CTO of NXP Semiconductors, Stefan Wallentowitz of Hochschule München University of Applied Sciences, Georg Sigl of Fraunhofer-Gesellschaft Fraunhofer AISEC Angewandte und integrierte Sicherheit. I was pleased to be able to contribute to this piece with analyses on the use of RISC-V in China. 

Some key points from the article:
📣 ARM is - for very good reasons - the undisputed market leader when it comes to IP for processors: over 200 billion chips have been produced with ARM technology so far. ARM offers superior technology and performance for a wide area of applications - from ultra-low-power to data center.

🚨  At least since the (for now) failed takeover attempt by nVidia, many customers have become aware of their dependence on ARM.

💲  At the same time, ARM is currently trying to change its licensing model and to participate in the "total value" of the end product - i.e. to tap into the value added by ARM's IP. The (rumored) plans of ARM to design a complete own SoC has also caused discussions in the industry press..

🛑  The current challenging relations between the USA and China make alternative chip architectures, which will not be affected by export restrictions, interesting for players worldwide.

💡  China in particular has a strong interest in becoming more resilient and building its own local chip industry, including for processors. And is accordingly promoting the development and use of RISC-V.

🚀  But there are also great opportunities for Europe. From increased resilience (Chipcrisis, Chips Act) to newly created markets. For example, the EU has launched funding programmes for RISC-V, for example for the use of the architecture in HPC applications. The EuroHPC Joint Undertaking (EuroHPC JU) has published an upcoming call for proposals to fund a project targeted at building high-performance computers based on RISC-V hardware and software. The total volume will initially be €270M.

Or as Lars Reger says: "We discuss for each product whether it makes sense to use RISC-V".

Read more in the article here https://lnkd.in/d6QYZqMp or in print!

For more information on #semiconductors feel free to reach out to us!

Jonathan Nussbaumer Shiv Tasker Scott Runner Loïc Hamon Christoph Jungnickl

Capgemini Engineering #innovation #riscv #china #chipsact

title: The energy issue is sparking business. High-efficiency power #semiconductors made of silicon carbide are suddenly in sho
text:
The energy issue is sparking business. High-efficiency power #semiconductors made of silicon carbide are suddenly in short supply and in hot demand. Now customers are signing long-term contracts - and even financing manufacturers' plants. Handelsblatt has shed light on this situation and it was a great pleasure for me to assess the situation together with industry leaders such as Gregg Lowe (CEO Wolfspeed), Isao Matsumoto (CEO ROHM Co., Ltd.), Peter S. (President & CEO Automotive at Infineon Technologies).
 
In a nutshell:
* Power semiconductors made of silicon carbide (SiC) are in sudden high demand due to their high efficiency.

* This means not only better energy utilization but also lower waste heat and reduced cooling requirements. Components can therefore be made smaller, lighter and more reliable. This outweighs the (currently) higher prices of the SiC chips for many use cases.

* Electric vehicles, for example, can achieve around 8% more range (compared to conventional technology) through the use of SiC semiconductors. It also allows vehicles to be charged potentially faster and also reduces operating costs through overall lower energy consumption.

* The leading manufacturers in this field are Wolfspeed, Rohm, Infineon, STMicroelectronics and onsemi.

* Wolfspeed has developed some lead with the opening of its new 200mm manufacturing line (Mohawk Valley, NY, SUA). It can produce about 1.9 times more chips per wafer (compared to a 150mm line). 

* Customers are signing long-term contracts and financing manufacturers' plants to ensure they always have access to the chips.

* Stellantis, which owns Peugeot and Opel, recently ordered SiC chips from Infineon for one billion euros. Jaguar Land Rover has just agreed a long-term contract with Wolfspeed, with deliveries starting in 2024. Lucid, the "efficiency world champion," also relies on Wolfspeed technology. BorgWarner recently took a stake in Wolfspeed to the tune of about $500M. In return, Borg-Warner gets guaranteed access to SiC chips worth $650 million per year.

* The market for SiC chips (in 2021 around 1bn USD) is expected to really take off from 2025 onwards and will expand to roughly 6.3bn USD yearly in 2027, according to the colleagues of Yole Group research.
 
Check out the Handelsblatt to find more details. Link in the comments.

Capgemini Automotive #innovation Capgemini Engineering #consulting #electricvehicles #automotiveindustry

title: Defend at home. Win outside.
text:
Defend at home. Win outside.
You've seen the Beijing Auto Show posts. SDV's are real. Chinese products are competitive. The design has matured. Impressive. But that's not the real story.

The real story is how Chinese automakers are escaping the domestic downturn and conquering export markets. And we're all looking in the wrong place.

Everyone watches Europe. Yes, the Chinese are coming. Yes, they're building factories, tuning pricing, assembling sales and service networks. But Europe is just one front. The wider war is happening in markets Western conversation overlooks. Markets that are vital profit pools for Western manufacturers themselves.

In Thailand, Chinese brands hit ~47% market share in January 2026. BYD alone took 14.2% of total sales that month and ~37% of pure EVs. Japanese dominance, with 80-90% market share for decades, is down to 68.5% in 2025.  

In Indonesia, four Chinese brands broke into the top 10 in Q1 2026. BYD went from zero to 4.8% market share in under two years. Toyota still leads but is sliding (-9% in January, -8% in February YoY). BYD now leads Indonesia's EV segment, displacing Wuling.

In Brazil, BYD became the first Chinese brand to top monthly retail sales in April 2026 with nearly 13% share, neck-and-neck with Volkswagen. BYD now dominates Brazil's EV market with around 75-80% share. They built their R$5.5B factory on Ford's old Camaçari site.

In Mexico, Chinese brands matched German manufacturers at 11.2% share in Q1 2026 — and overtook them in March. Vehicles manufactured in China account for ~23% of all sales. BYD accounts for roughly 70% of Mexico's EV and plug-in hybrid sales.

In Australia, vehicles sourced from China became the number 1 source of new vehicles in February 2026, surpassing Japan for the first time since 1998. Country-of-origin share hit ~25%; Chinese brands themselves now hold ~20%.

In the Gulf, Chinese OEMs hold 15-16% share in Saudi Arabia and the UAE. The GCC EV market is projected at $11.6 billion in 2026.

These markets matter. Combined, Brazil + Mexico + Thailand + Indonesia + Australia + Saudi Arabia + UAE represent roughly 7.5 million annual vehicle sales. This is larger than Germany, France, and the UK combined (~6.4 million in 2025). Chinese automakers aren't just entering them. They're building factories, dealer networks, and brand recognition at scale, and largely unobserved.

In essence, Japanese dominance in Southeast Asia is cracking. The Germans are under siege in Latin America. The Americans are retreating.

The European discourse only watches two battlefields: China, and Europe itself. 

The real fight is already underway in the corners not many are looking at.

So here's the question for European auto: Do you focus on cost, product, or new business fields? Or something else entirely?

Your views in the comments!

For professional advice, as always, talk with the CG team and me: Laurence Noël Christian Hummel Luciano Bottoni Olaf Pietschner Dr. Rainer Mehl Yan Chu

title: "The battle for Taiwan's microchip treasure". This is how the Münchner Merkur highlighted the situation of Taiwan's semi
text:
"The battle for Taiwan's microchip treasure". This is how the Münchner Merkur highlighted the situation of Taiwan's semiconductor industry in an interesting article today. Together with Julia Hess (stiftung neue verantwortung) and Juergen Matthes (German Economic Institute), I was allowed to contribute to this piece with market assessments and analyses.

🌏 Taiwan currently has just over 20% of the world's semiconductor manufacturing capacity. Converted into 200mm wafer equivalents, that's about 4.5 million wafers that Taiwanese companies process every month.

However, these figures do not reflect the whole picture. 

💪🏼 In the contract manufacturing sector, Taiwanese companies such as TSMC, UMC, PSMC, etc. have a share of over 60%. From a manufacturing technology perspective, Taiwan is even more important: for chips with technologies below 10nm, a share of 90%. Among the customers who have these high-end chips (processors) manufactured in Taiwan are such illustrious names as Apple, AMD, Qualcomm, Broadcom, Nvidia and Sony.

💥The production of semiconductor components is an enormously complex and labour-intensive undertaking. Even minor disruptions (such as energy shortages in the extraction of high-purity silicon or water shortages in cleaning processes in manufacturing) can have significant effects on supply chains - as we have seen in the #chipshortage.

Read more in the article in today's print edition about the dependency of the world from taiwanese technology and which steps Europe can and need to take to achieve more technological sovereignty.

title: While the headlines are dominated by conflicts, energy price spikes, and the risk of renewed supply chain disruption, on
text:
While the headlines are dominated by conflicts, energy price spikes, and the risk of renewed supply chain disruption, one strategic development has almost slipped under the radar: China has released its new 15th Five-Year Plan. And that matters. These plans show where China intends to build capability, reduce dependency, and define its next phase of economic development.

I was pleased to be featured in today’s Table.Briefings China.Table alongside Dr. Philipp Seidel, LL.M. of Arthur D. Little, discussing the implications of the newly released plan, known in China as Shíwǔwǔ (十五五), shorthand for the 15th Five-Year Plan. This is China’s economic blueprint through 2030, with industrial upgrading and technological self-reliance once again at the heart of the agenda.

What stands out is not one isolated priority, but the breadth and clarity of the direction. Beijing wants core digital economy industries to reach 12.5% of GDP by 2030, while national R&D spending is set to grow by around 7% over the next five years. The plan places strong emphasis on AI and digital intelligence, as well as semiconductors, industrial software, 6G, quantum technologies, biotechnology, advanced materials, and brain-computer interfaces. The broader “AI Plus” push is meant to drive deployment across manufacturing, logistics, healthcare, and urbanisation. Simply, to make AI an integral part of society, industry, and government.

At the same time, there is an important open question in this plan. China is clearly betting on technology-led growth. But it remains to be seen whether that alone can solve weaker demand, labour-market pressure, and deeper structural imbalances in the economy. High-tech industries may strengthen long-term competitiveness, but they are capital-intensive and cannot absorb the sheer number of graduates entering the workforce, while the traditional engines of employment are no longer growing at the same pace. The plan also speaks of raising household consumption, but without clear targets, while the property downturn and weaker consumer confidence continue to weigh on the economy.

That is exactly why this plan deserves far more international attention than it has received so far. Not because every target will be met exactly as written, but because the priorities themselves tell us a great deal about how China sees the world, where it expects future vulnerabilities, and where it intends to move next.

Read the full feature with an analysis here: https://lnkd.in/dMN-ei6M (paywall)

Feel free to share your take on the plan in the comments or reach out for further information.

Luciano Bottoni Bruno Casadinho Rodrigo Maia

#china #innovation

title: Wrapping up an extraordinary week at HANNOVER MESSE, I’m both inspired and energized by the groundbreaking innovations o
text:
Wrapping up an extraordinary week at HANNOVER MESSE, I’m both inspired and energized by the groundbreaking innovations on display. 

🦾One trend stood out in particular: #AI and #robotics were front and center, with especially strong momentum coming from Chinese players—demonstrating how these technologies are rapidly becoming enablers for enterprises to become more innovative, agile, and efficient.

📰In its in-depth coverage titled „Der Schlüssel für autonome Roboter“, Handelsblatt delves into what is required to make robots truly autonomous. The real breakthrough lies in combining AI with the ability to safely interact with the physical world—to perceive, navigate, manipulate, and adapt in real time. From football-playing humanoids to collaborative industrial arms, the examples at the fair showed how far we’ve come in bridging the gap between digital intelligence and mechanical capability.

👨‍🔬Experts like Prof. Sami Haddadin (Munich Institute of Robotics and Machine Intelligence) and Dr. Bernd Liepert  (Deutsches Forschungszentrum für Künstliche Intelligenz (DFKI) , Svenja Böttger  emphasize the critical role of data, simulation environments, and closed feedback loops to create safe learning cycles for autonomous systems. As Susanne Bieller of International Federation of Robotics  points out, 
the ability to train and validate robotic behavior in safe virtual environments is essential for real-world deployment.

I was honored to be featured among the experts interviewed and to share this perspective: “GenAI is not enough to conquer the world of robotics.” More on my views and Capgemini research in the newspaper article!

We are entering an era where cyber-physical intelligence will define competitive advantage—not just in manufacturing, but across industries. The fusion of machine learning, edge computing, high-performance mechatronics, and robust safety frameworks is enabling the leap from automation to autonomy.

And beyond the factory floor, a more philosophical question emerges:
How will the future of humanoid robotics look like? Will it take on a friendly, futuristic shape like the Unitree Robotics machine —or a more serious “strict nanny” design like the NEURA Robotics device (which is actually quite friendly)?

As the final day of Hannover Messe draws to a close, the message is clear: The future is being built now—through collaboration, ingenuity, and bold experimentation. A glimpse of that was on exhibition at the Capgemini booth. Let’s shape the future wisely.

Find the article at Handelsblatt newspaper or online: https://lnkd.in/dXyQdcVK


Capgemini #innovation #intelligentindustry

title: What contribution can decarbonization of the existing vehicle fleet make to achieving #CO2 targets? This question was di
text:
What contribution can decarbonization of the existing vehicle fleet make to achieving #CO2 targets? This question was discussed in an article in Springer Professional Automobil + Motoren.
* A recent FVV study (conducted by Frontier Economics and the (ifeu - Institut für Energie- und Umweltforschung Heidelberg gGmbH)) shows that compliance with the Paris #climate targets can only succeed if we phase out the use of #fossilfuels as quickly as possible.
* Even if we only allow more vehicles to be operated in a climate-neutral manner (e.g. #batteryelectric, #electricvehicles ) as soon as possible, the "emissions backpack" of the existing vehicle fleet is significant.
* The study concludes that the use of #eFuels in the vehicle fleet is essential.
* This does not mean an eFuels monoculture, but as FVV Managing Director Dietmar Göricke says: "Rather, it is important to pursue several technology paths in parallel. Then we have a chance to achieve climate goals while keeping individual mobility affordable."

I have addressed the importance of eFuels as an opportunity in a separate article for #MTZ. Success depends in particular on whether we succeed in importing sustainably produced eFuels or #hydrogen on a large scale. After all, the immanent efficiency disadvantage of thermal engines compared to electric drives is not going away. The primary energy demand for the operation of "old" combustion engines is significantly higher. This means, how do we succeed in using the scarce resource of green energy sources in the most sensible way? Which political measuers can we apply to change individual mobility systems or renew the fleet? Because other sectors desperately need a #decarbonization , too. And there are less alternatives to Hydrogen and synthetic fuels.

Read the discussion here: https://lnkd.in/eFQydnWi

Capgemini #Consulting Capgemini Engineering #automotive #sustainablefuture Capgemini Automotive #innovation

title: Efficiency is king in #electricmobility. And #Silicon (Si) has long been the dominant material for #powerelectronics due
text:
#Efficiency is king in #electricmobility. And #Silicon (Si) has long been the dominant material for #powerelectronics due to it’s technical maturity and availability. 

However, in recent years #siliconcarbide (SiC) substrates have become the engineer’s choice for applications like high-power on-board chargers or traction inverters of #electricvehicles. This wide-bandgap material can handle higher energy densities, offers better conversion efficiency and better thermal properties than Si alternatives. This all translates simply into more driving range from the same battery.

The Handelsblatt has taken a look at these developments and how one of the leading companies for this technology, Wolfspeed (former #Cree), is not only supplying base materials to other #semiconductor manufacturers but also delivering MOSFET’s, Schottky diodes and power modules to clients like ZF Group and General Motors.

It was my pleasure to contribute some insights to this article about SiC, it’s applications in #electricandhybridvehicles as well as the supplier landscape of innovators like Infineon Technologies, STMicroelectronics, ROHM Semiconductor Europe or onsemi. 

Read the article here: https://lnkd.in/eyPpdwKf

title: Autonomous Driving - Made in China. A future Export Hit? 🤖🚗🔋
text:
Autonomous Driving - Made in China. A future Export Hit? 🤖🚗🔋

I recently had the honor of publishing a guest commentary in ATZ electronics magazine, highlighting China's remarkable transformation in the automotive industry and a potential outlook on future Sino-global cooperation . 

Not long ago, China was seen as a receptive market for Western automotive technology. Today, it's a powerhouse driving global advancements in e-mobility and autonomous driving.

Western OEM’s, once dominant with their combustion engine expertise, are now facing a transformed market. Chinese brands have taken the leadership for sales of New-energy vehicles (PHEV, BEV). 

Supported by Beijing’s ambitious industrial policies, Chinese manufacturers are not just integrating Western tech but also developing competitive homegrown solutions—from sensors to microprocessors to control software.

The strategy is dual: tech giants are pushing the famed Robotaxi, while local automakers aim for situational hands-free driving. Trials in cities like Beijing and Shenzhen showcase China’s competitive edge against US rivals. Recent L3 pilot licenses to nine local manufacturers mark significant progress toward widespread public road testing.

For Western manufacturers, this means increased competition but also new opportunities for collaboration. Chinese suppliers are emerging as attractive partners for hardware and software components essential for automated driving. Some Chinese companies are now leaders in areas like components for advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS) and novel electric vehicle architectures, becoming go-to partners for Western firms.

This competitive environment accelerates innovation and reduces costs, potentially benefiting global markets. However, success hinges on more than just functionality and safety—customer trust is paramount. Chinese providers must emphasize transparency and build trust through collaboration with Western partners and authorities.

Considering the rising tensions between the large power blocs, leading to heightened trade barriers, increased collaboration in fields like automated driving and high-tech components might not seem viable at the moment. 

However, the question remains: how long can global companies and policy makers afford to keep competitive Sino technology limited to“from China for China” products and out of their global products?

Capgemini Engineering Capgemini Automotive 
Springer Professional

title: "Attack of the production machine. Foxconn puts automotive contract manufacturers on the spot". The leading industry new
text:
"Attack of the production machine. Foxconn puts automotive contract manufacturers on the spot". The leading industry newspaper Automobilwoche has shed light on the plans of electronics giant Foxconn for its powerful entry into the automotive world. It was a great pleasure to contribute to this article with industry analysis.

* The Taiwanese Hon Hai Technology Group (鴻海科技集團) / Foxconn (富士康) is hardly known to the general public. Yet the company is a giant in the contract manufacturing of information technology and consumer electronics products, with over US$200 billion in sales in 2021 and more than 1.2 million employees. Many of us will hold a product manufactured by Foxconn - such as an Apple smartphone - in our hands every day.

 * However, the group is looking for new growth markets in other industries outside its core area. In this context, Hon Hai Technology has identified the mobility industry and everything based around the classic vehicle system as an extremely promising market. According to its own information, Foxconn would like to generate around USD 35 billion in sales with automotive activities in a few years.

* Foxconn doesn't just want to build a new car, though. They want to develop a flexible platform and modular solutions to supply other market players. They are thinking not only of vehicle technology, but also of production technology/facilites in all major markets. Foxconn is uniquely addressing this issue in this breadth.

* Foxconn has not only forged close links with suppliers, for example by acquiring stakes in manufacturers of electric motors or power semiconductors, but has also invested in its own vehicle software (e.g. the company Sonatus, based in the USA). Also, cooperation with Stellantis have been forged to provide them with #semiconductors  technology across Stellantis’ STLA Brain architecture, to debut in vehicles latest 2024.

* In Ohio, Foxconn also acquired the Lordstown Motors plant, a former GM plant, in 2021 and also acquired shares in Lordstown Motors Corporation (creator of the e-SUV "Endurance"). In 2023, Foxconn plans to produce the Pear model there for e-vehicle manufacturer Fisker Inc.

* In October 2022, Foxconn unveiled two new e-cars at its Hon Hai Tech Day, a pickup truck and a cross-over hatchback. This means that, in addition to SUVs, sedans and city buses, the group has now presented a total of five models under the Foxtron brand, based on the company's open MIH platform. Taiwanese suppliers are mainly involved in this.

For more details and how this initiative by Hon Hai - such as the possible cooperation with Volkswagen AG for the production of Scout Motors Inc. brand vehicles - may put established players such as Magna International or Valmet Automotive under pressure, read the full article in Automobilwoche.

Link in the comments.

title: China's Automotive Success: Key lessons for (German) industry leaders 🇨🇳🚗
text:
China's Automotive Success: Key lessons for (German) industry leaders 🇨🇳🚗 

The transformational pressures facing the German automotive industry are undeniable. Many discussions of the past days at the Technischer Kongress 2024 of the German Association of the Automotive Industry (VDA) highlighted the urgent need to adapt. 

China's astonishing automotive rise offers valuable, if sobering, lessons. Even if the "tsunami of Chinese vehicles" has not yet reached European shores (as Christoph Seyerlein recently described in an interesting article for manager magazin), we should not overlook the export success of the Chinese automotive industry in markets of the global south - or the recent move into Russia, for example. 

My recent contribution to an in-depth analysis for the venerable MTZ Motorentechnische Zeitschrift (the leading international technical and scientific journal for engineers in drive development), together with Deloitte 's China Automotive Leader, Andreas Maennel , revealed these key success factors:

Pragmatic strategy: China doesn't focus on short-term trends, but on long-term market dominance driven by meticulous planning on macro level and extreme competition on micro-level. 📈

Tech Fluidity: A willingness to embrace competing technologies (electrification, hydrogen, etc.) allows for rapid adaptation and avoids dead ends. ⚡⚙️

Speed and scale: Decisive action backed by massive investment in manufacturing capacity creates a competitive advantage that's hard to beat. 🚀

These factors have propelled Chinese automakers to the forefront in areas such as EV dominance and battery technology. Their growing success in global markets highlights the need for introspection.

Find the article here for more details and also the challenges the Chinese industry is facing (Springer Nature account/subscription required)): https://lnkd.in/d-naVRjc

title: "Almost every week there is a minor or major crisis somewhere," this is how Rutger Wijburg, Board Member and COO of Muni
text:
"Almost every week there is a minor or major crisis somewhere," this is how Rutger Wijburg, Board Member and COO of Munich-based semiconductor manufacturer Infineon Technologies recently describes the current situation on the #semiconductor market. 

The Handelsblatt  reported in detail about the effects of the worldwide #chipshortage in its weekend edition.

Often we only look in this context at the car manufacturers, which have to react to the component shortage with production interruptions. Michael Adam of BMW Group  and Matthias Zink, CEO Automotive Technologies of Schaeffler, describe in the piece the drastic effects of the current situation.

However, the article also takes a look at how the "hidden champions" that are so important for Germany, such as GEZE GmbH , Vorwerk Group  or ebm-papst Group are coping with the crisis.

One factor in the bottleneck is the run on electromobility, connectivity and driving automation. According to BMW, the value of the chips installed in the vehicle has risen from €600 to €2,500. And for power electronic components and certain micro-controllers, machinery and equipment industry is fighting with the automotive sector for much needed components. 

Even though the global semiconductor industry is responding to the crisis with gigantic investments - market estimates put the figure at around $190 billion this year - the long delivery times for equipment mean that it will probably take until 2024/2025 before the supply gap is completely closed.

I was very pleased to be able to contribute to this article alongside Marc Alber Managing Director / CTO of GEZE,  Dr. Thomas Stoffmehl CEO of Vorwerk and Klaus Geissdoerfer CEO of EBM Papst.

Read more in the print edition or here: Handelsblatt: Autos, Thermomix und Ventilatoren: Der Chipmangel findet kein Ende - https://lnkd.in/g3gcVQXq

title: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝗰𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸 – 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘀𝗽𝗼𝗶𝗹𝗲𝗿: 𝗶𝘁’𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗜 𝗯𝗼𝗼𝗺.
text:
🚨 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝗰𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸 – 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘀𝗽𝗼𝗶𝗹𝗲𝗿: 𝗶𝘁’𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗜 𝗯𝗼𝗼𝗺.
Lead times stretching to 50 weeks. Prices multiplying by four. If you thought our semiconductor supply chains had finally stabilized, the latest market realities are a harsh wake-up call, especially for automotive and industrial sectors.

Handelsblatt and Joachim Hofer are, as usual, right at the pulse of this disruption and have a great piece in today's print edition. It was a joy to contribute to the text! 

The nerve we hit is global: the story has also been picked up by outlets like CNA in Taiwan, the absolute epicenter of advanced semiconductor manufacturing.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗜𝗺𝗺𝗲𝗱𝗶𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁 𝗦𝗵𝗼𝗰𝗸: 
We see extreme dynamics at play. My industry peers accurately highlight the immediate pain points: 
Bo Lybaek (GPV) points to the raw pull of AI demand, 
Noureddine Seddiki (SAND & SILICON GmbH) notes 4x price multipliers just to secure inventory, and 
Tanjeff Schadt (Strategy&) warns that pandemic-era emergency purchasing task forces are returning.

𝗕𝗲𝘆𝗼𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗪𝗮𝗳𝗲𝗿 - 𝗠𝘆 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗼𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝗲𝗽𝗲𝗿 𝗯𝗼𝘁𝘁𝗹𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗰𝗸𝘀:
As I emphasized in the piece, a quick recovery is highly unlikely. While headlines focus on leading-edge chips, we are seeing this boom severely strain the supply of "inconspicuous" but critical components like microcontrollers, power electronics, and PCBs.

I pointed out that the true choke point isn't just wafer fabrication—it is fundamentally a packaging issue:
* The Substrate Squeeze: Since November 2025, demand from AI, 5G, and high-performance computing is structurally outpacing substrate supply.
* System-Critical Dependencies: There is simply no way around key players like Nittobo, the market leader in substrate materials.
* Cost Pass-Throughs: Standard component providers are currently riding this momentum to pass cost increases directly down the line.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗶𝗰 𝗢𝘂𝘁𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗸: 
Chancellor Friedrich Merz’s state visit to China underscores a critical balancing act: maintaining robust relations with China, both as a massive buyer and as an indispensable supplier. Past supply chain shocks (like the Sino-Dutch challenges in power electronics) are still heavily priced into the market's risk models.
But as I noted in the article, this structural squeeze presents a massive strategic window. It’s a prime opportunity for manufacturers from Mainland China and Taiwan to step up. Now that they can deliver, they are highly viable partners ready to capture significant market share in these mature process segments.

I'll be at embedded world Exhibition&Conference in Nuremberg soon. Let's meet there to discuss these topics, and much more! 

Link to the Handelsblatt article in the comments 👇

Luz G. Mauch Clément Charruel Christoph Jungnickl Dr. Nico Wolf Sascha Kaus Claudia Hytrek

#innovation #semiconductor

title: What technologies will shape 2025? AI, Robotics, and a World of Possibilities
text:
What technologies will shape 2025? AI, Robotics, and a World of Possibilities

🚀  As we head into the new year, artificial intelligence continues to dominate the conversation and drive innovation across industries. Nowhere is this more evident than at #CES2025, which kicks off tomorrow in Las Vegas, where AI will take center stage as a cornerstone of technological advancement.

🤖  One particularly fascinating application of AI is in #robotics. This was the focus of my recent contribution to Euro magazine's (in cooperation with Financial Times) story, "Revolution of the Machines”. In the article, we explored how AI-powered robotics might likely transform industries and bring us closer to solutions that were once only “science-fiction”.

🛠️  Humanoid robots vividly illustrate this progress. In both the U.S. and China, innovators have tackled complex challenges in mechanics, control systems, and power management. Today, thanks to advances in AI, robots are beginning to understand basic environmental and contextual cues. While we're still a long way from an "android army," concrete applications are already emerging - from robots assisting in factory automation (think the promises of Tesla's Optimus or its Chinese counterparts) to Intuitive Surgical's precision breakthroughs in healthcare.

🔍  And as Angus Muirhead of UBS points out in the piece: "KI-powered automation offers a wide range of opportunities for persistent investors, even years down the road."

📰  For the full conversation please refer to the last edition of “Euro am Sonntag” in print or online.

As CES kicks off and HANNOVER MESSE approaches, I'm excited to see how these trends play out in the coming year. 

If you're at CES, stop by the Capgemini booth in The Venetian and have a conversation about how to put the many faces of AI to work now!

#AI #Robotics #TechnologyTrends #CES2025 #HannoverMesse2025 #Innovation

👉 this translates to a loss of revenue of up to 220bn USD

👉 many #OEM and #tier1 established daily sourcing calls for chips

👉 manufacturing capacity in the #semiconductorindustry  is fully booked for high-end processes (sub 10nm) as well as with typical lines for automotive/medical products (28nm and above)

👉 despite many measures to improve the #supplychain and increase #productioncapacity the crisis will last very likely throughout the year 2022

👉 for #mitigationstrategies how to master the chip-crisis successfully and how to make your technology developments more #resilient towards scarcity of semiconductors you should have a talk with my esteemed colleagues  Shiv Tasker and Scott Runner. They know how!

title: „Sustainability by Design“. Werner Ferreira da Silva in his keynote today at Zinnov Confluence in Bangalore about India‘
text:
„Sustainability by Design“. Werner Ferreira da Silva in his keynote today at Zinnov Confluence in Bangalore about India‘s role in a better future.

Key points of this tangible speech about sustainability:
🔬 we as engineers and scientists have a joint responsibility to solve our societal challenges with technology and innovation

♻️ the switch towards a circular economy is inevitable. Our current trajectory is clearly not sustainable (enough)

💪 Capgemini has committed itself to very strong Sustainability goals

🏭 Every industrial sector is impacted by sustainability actions and this presents a huge market opportunity for 🇮🇳 

🪫 to become a sustainable society, we need to deal with the world’s energy hunger and de-carbonize our energy supply. India’s energy consumption increased by 7.3% last year alone due to strong economic growth. The engineering powerhouse India has the capabilities to make the industry much more efficient

Werner showed client examples of how to address sustainability and energy efficiency in various sectors:

📱The Telecom sector uses energy on a level as the Aviation industry. New, open telco standards like OpenRAN bring many benefits in innovation and resilience. But up to 40% of the network OpEx is energy. So without energy efficiency, OpenRAN can not make it. Capgemini India is working with partners on this. 

🏡Energy for Buildings account for roughly 20% of green house gas emissions. Digitalization and digital twins are helping substantially to bring down energy consumption, as demonstrated in the Energy Control Center of Capgemini and Schneider Electric. 

🔋The BEV revolution requires a dramatic build up of cell manufacturing capacity in Europe. To keep costs under control, dramatic efficiency gains in energy consumption and productivity are required. Capgemini works together with Stellantis/ACC to make this happen

🫧Hydrogen is a key element for the future energy system. Made with renewable energy, it is indispensable for carbon-free high-power, high-temperature processes and raw material production (eg fertilizers). Capgemini is part of delivering core innovations along the whole process chain

Conclusion:

👩🏽‍🏫🧑🏽‍🏫India as an engineering powerhouse is ideally positioned to provide answers to those looming societal questions

➡️Addressing climate change, achieving sustainability, and earning healthy profits - the winning formula for 🇮🇳 engineering


Cc: Dr. Dorothea Pohlmann Corinne Jouanny Lukas Birn Jochen Bechtold Sascha Kaus Andreas Kötter Alexander Sorgenicht Dr. Luis Prado Dr. Selim COSKUN Konstantina Gkika  Frédéric Arquier Mariana Pereira Coutinho Latha Anand Prashanth Chetty  and many more #sustainability champions at Capgemini.

title: 𝐒𝐢𝐂 𝐬𝐞𝐦𝐢𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐬 𝐚𝐬 𝐚𝐭𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐚𝐬 𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫 - 𝐁𝐎𝐒𝐂𝐇 𝐢𝐧𝐯𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐬 𝐔𝐒𝐃 1.5 𝐛𝐧
text:
𝐒𝐢𝐂 𝐬𝐞𝐦𝐢𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐬 𝐚𝐬 𝐚𝐭𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐚𝐬 𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫 - 𝐁𝐎𝐒𝐂𝐇 𝐢𝐧𝐯𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐬 𝐔𝐒𝐃 1.5 𝐛𝐧 𝐢𝐧 𝐂𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐧𝐢𝐚
🚘 Tesla's announcement that it would dispense with expensive Silicon carbide (#SiC) components in its future entry-level vehicles caused a great deal of discussion a few weeks ago. It also sent the share prices of many manufacturers of power semiconductors plummeting for a short time. But as we said at the time: Tesla is not putting an end to the industry-wide trend towards SiC components. On the way to the "electric society", highly efficient power electronics are indispensable! No matter whether in industrial or automotive applications!

⚡ In this respect, it fits into the global picture that the BOSCH Group is now investing 1.5 billion USD in the purchase and expansion of a site for SiC production in the USA. The takeover of the former TSI Semiconductors (ex NEC / Renesas, ex TEMIC Telefunken Microelectronic) plant is intended to create a manufacturing centre for this technology and to complement the existing plants in Europe accordingly. TSI currently manufactures Si-based semiconductors on a 200mm line at its Roseville/CA site. This should result in synergies with the BOSCH line in Reutlingen, where comparable products are manufactured. 

🚨 At the same time, in my view, it also shows how attractive the Chips Act, Inflation Reduction Act, the prospect of favourable electricity prices and rapid approval processes have made the USA a manufacturing location. This should be a wake-up call for Europe and especially Germany! Even if the EU has now passed its own Chips Act - that is not the end of the story.

The Handelsblatt reported on this in detail and it was a pleasure for me to be able to contribute to this with analyses.

All in all, BOSCH will thus significantly increase it's position in SiC chips. Even though the turnover in the industry is expected to increase sevenfold to almost 7 billion USD by 2027, the competitive pressure is increasing. In addition to the top dogs ST Micro, Infineon, Wolfspeed, Onsemi and Rohm, Chinese manufacturers in particular will also enter the market in the next few years.

Read more in the article here: https://lnkd.in/d44ef5DS

title: Chinese car manufacturers are on the move. Next week, Auto Shanghai opens its doors. From April 18-27, China's leading a
text:
Chinese car manufacturers are on the move. Next week, Auto Shanghai opens its doors. From April 18-27, China's leading automotive trade fair will showcase the highlights of domestic manufacturers. 

Once widely ridiculed, the Chinese auto industry - supported by smart industrial policies - has carved out a strong position in the field of battery electric vehicles. At the same time, China is increasingly becoming a sought-after production location for exports - and not just for BEVs. 

According to the China Automobile Association (CAAM), Chinese car exports have tripled to around 2.5 million vehicles annually since 2020 alone.

In the world ranking of exporters, China has thus now moved up to third place. Just ahead of it is only Germany, with Japan at the top. The German Association of the Automotive Industry (VDA) reported 2.61 million passenger cars exported from Germany last year. Japan exported around three million passenger cars.

There has been much discussion about the powerful entry of Chinese OEMs into Europe. Even if the "red wave" does not roll into Germany so quickly, even double-digit market shares are conceivable in the medium term. Largely unnoticed, however, Chinese brands have begun to establish themselves in markets outside Europe. Particularly in markets where traditional Western manufacturers have withdrawn, China's automakers are rapidly filling the gaps that have arisen.

WirtschaftsWoche has examined the situation and started a series of articles around these developments. It was a pleasure for me to contribute with analyses to them.

Find more here: https://lnkd.in/dZdnTZJh

title: "The savior comes too late" - can the #combustion engine be made #sustainable by switching to "green #hydrogen"? In an i
text:
"The savior comes too late" - can the #combustion engine be made #sustainable by switching to "green #hydrogen"? In an interview with »kfz-betrieb« magazine I recently discussed the technical issues surrounding the use of hydrogen in internal combustion engines ("largely solved"), explained the efficiency of different drive variants ("H2 #ice cars nearly as efficient as #FCEV"), and explained the (likely ongoing) problem of the #scarcity of #greenhydrogen  ("#automotive  vs. industrial use and other high-energy demand #mobility applications"). 

Read more in the print edition of the KFZ-Betrieb Magazin edition 1-2/2022.

Sounds disappointing for ICE fans in the #automotiveindustry ? While #electricvehicles will win in many markets for light-duty applications, for #commercialvehicle use the H2 engine is definitely a solution to have on the radar.

title: How Amazon attacks the #chip giants, explains the #Handelsblatt. In the past, most #CPU’s for desktop computing and serv
text:
How Amazon attacks the #chip giants, explains the #Handelsblatt. In the past, most #CPU’s for desktop computing and server/data center applications came from the big names in the industry: Intel Corporation, and AMD. In #supercomputing, also IBM had it’s say. 

But now, with the advent of new computing applications like #machinelearning / training of #artificialintelligence and the explosion of #edge/ #cloud based services at (hyper)scale it is becoming even more attractive for large users (like Amazon Web Services (AWS)) to invest in it’s own #silicon. 

My colleague Shiv Tasker and I had the pleasure to conduct some talks with various German press houses to explain the context of those undertakings, the trends in #semiconductor  #technology and #manufacturing, development cycles for chips and how industry giants (also in the #automotiveindustry ) can create unique value propositons and competitve advantages by utilizing customized semiconductors (mostly built on Arm technology). And also on how to mitigate the impact of the current #semiconductorshortage. 

For more information on how to make such a semiconductor project happen - just reach out!

Links in the comments.

title: Motorsport isn’t just competition—it’s a feeling. The roar of the engines, the split-second decisions, the chase for the
text:
🏁 Motorsport isn’t just competition—it’s a feeling. The roar of the engines, the split-second decisions, the chase for the perfect lap—it sends a tickle up your spine that only racing can. 

It’s a place where people and technology are pushed to their absolute limits, and where innovation is forged under the intense pressure of competition.

That’s why I’m thrilled to see Capgemini  and Peugeot Sport renewing their partnership, continuing our journey to enhance performance, accelerate decision-making with AI, and drive sustainability in motorsport. Over the past two years, we’ve developed an AI-powered data engineering platform that processes real and simulated race data in near real-time, optimizing the Peugeot 9X8 Hypercar’s behavior on the track.

💡 Just how fast is this tech? What used to take an entire day to analyze now takes just 10 minutes.
🧠 Next step? Generative AI will identify anomalies over long races and structure driver-engineer interactions, turning instinct into intelligence.

And what a machine we’re working with:
🔹 Peugeot 9X8 Hypercar – competing in the FIA World Endurance Championship
🔹 Hybrid Power! 520 kW Petrol ICE 2.6L 90° V6 (Bi-Turbo) & 200 kW electric motor/generator unit (MGU)
🔹 900V battery system, developed with cutting-edge efficiency
🔹 1030 kg dry weight – all built for speed, agility, and endurance

Beyond performance, this collaboration is also about pioneering sustainability in motorsport. Capgemini and Peugeot Sport have assessed and optimized the entire carbon footprint of the racing ecosystem, implementing over 30 initiatives to reduce emissions by 2030. From eco-design and biofuels to supplier engagement and optimized logistics, we are driving racing toward a more sustainable future.

This partnership is proof that motorsport is more than just racing—it’s a testbed for the future of mobility. Every challenge on the track fuels innovation for the road. Every second counts. Every decision matters. And just like a racing team, our collaboration continues to push boundaries, setting new benchmarks for AI-powered performance and sustainable racing.

💙🏎️ Proud to be part of this journey. The best is yet to come 🚀

A big thank you to all leaders and collagues who are passionate “auto guys” for making this happen! Andrea Falleni William Rozé Laurence Noël Christian Hummel Alexandre Audoin Fabienne LEFEVER Werner Ferreira da Silva Giles Guidicelli  Capgemini Automotive #innovation #motorsport FIA World Endurance Championship

title: "Even After $100 Billion, Self-Driving Cars Are Going Nowhere" - that's how Bloomberg Businessweek recently reported on
text:
"Even After $100 Billion, Self-Driving Cars Are Going Nowhere" - that's how Bloomberg Businessweek recently reported on the state of #autonomousdriving. Even though the report is unusually pointed (and some of the content is definitely worth discussing, recommend to have a look at it for yourself), it shows the increasingly critical sentiment towards the topic (hello, hype-cycle...). 

In an interview with Stern magazine, I was allowed to classify the state of affairs - also with a view to #China - and give an outlook on the topic of (highly) automated driving.

* The fully autonomous car will not come with a big bang, but in small steps. The presentation of the "Google Car" as well as the full-bodied promises of Tesla have spurred a revolution. Even though the project has now turned out to be much more complex, the first production-ready products are currently beginning to reach customer hands. The much-cited Autobahn Pilot is an example here.

* The legislative complex was initially underestimated. Building a safe and reliable legal framework has taken years. However, Germany is a pioneer in this area and began introducing appropriate legislation last year.

* China is vigorously pursuing automated driving technology. There are a handful of companies that have appropriate self-driving technology and have applied for or redeemed licenses for commercial passenger transportation. Until recently, most of these automated vehicles were on the road in pilot applications with a safety driver on board - especially in metropolitan areas such as Wuhan, Shenzhen, Shanghai and even Beijing. The technology has now been deemed so mature by government agencies that in many cases a safety driver is no longer required. In addition, commercial pilot operation has been approved in some zones - in regular Chinese road traffic. 

* Chinese manufacturers expect to be able to roll out commercial cab operations on a large scale in megacities in the next two to three years. That would mean moving into mass production. The Chinese government attaches great importance to this entire "automated mobility" sector. For example, it is firmly anchored in the "Made in China 2025" industrial strategy, meaning that not only political goodwill is flowing into the planning, but also tangible investments. This means not only developing the software, but also mastering and manufacturing sensor technology in China, for example.

Further aspects such as automated freight traffic, payment models or technical challenges can be found here in the article: 
https://lnkd.in/dD3rF2-4

title: The Hidden Cost of the AI Boom: In a recent discussion with Jerrit Schloßer at Sueddeutsche Zeitung, I explored the reas
text:
The Hidden Cost of the AI Boom: In a recent discussion with Jerrit Schloßer at Sueddeutsche Zeitung, I explored the reasons behind the recent surge in prices for laptops, smartphones, and graphics cards. In summary, the rapid growth of AI is driving increased demand and consuming the global memory supply.

The "Pig Cycle" (Schweinezyklus)—a recurring pattern of boom and bust in supply and demand—in the semiconductor industry is nothing new, but this time, the dynamics are different.

Key takeaways from the interview:
🤖 The HBM Effect: Manufacturers are aggressively shifting capacity to High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) to power AI data centers. This cannibalizes the production lines needed for standard DRAM used in consumer devices.

🏭 Sold Out: Major players like Samsung and SK Hynix report that 2026 production runs are already effectively oversubscribed.

📈 Price Impact: We are considering price hikes of up to 15% for PCs and laptops, with even sharper increases in the high-end gaming sector.
As I mentioned in the article, we are entering "golden times" for chip manufacturers, but for IT procurement and private consumers, these high prices will persist for quite some time. Building new semiconductor fab capacity takes years, not months. And the AI bubble might not bust anytime soon.

Read the full analysis here (German) - who will benefit (Chinese memory makers) and who is investing (Micron): https://lnkd.in/dng-ZNxz
...and in the print edition at your kiosk.

Attached is a collage from the Sueddeutsche Zeitung print edition, visualized with GenAI assistance - using plenty of memory 😉 .

title: 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗯𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗕𝗮𝗻, 𝗘-𝗙𝘂𝗲𝗹𝘀 & 𝗖𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗮: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗜𝗻𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘃𝗲𝗻𝗶𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗧𝗿𝘂𝘁𝗵
text:
𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗯𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗕𝗮𝗻, 𝗘-𝗙𝘂𝗲𝗹𝘀 & 𝗖𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗮: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗜𝗻𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘃𝗲𝗻𝗶𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗧𝗿𝘂𝘁𝗵
While we are busy celebrating "technology openness" for combustion engines, we are losing the real battle elsewhere. Physics doesn't negotiate.

I was honored to contribute to current article in WirtschaftsWoche alongside a "Who's Who" of  experts—including automotive legends Stefan Bratzel, Prof. Dr., Prof. Dr. Ferdinand Dudenhoeffer, and Prof. Monika Schnitzer (Chairwoman of the German Council of Economic Experts). Editor stefan hajek did an excellent job in bringing these voices together and writing a compelling analysis.

The result is a clear rejection of political wishful thinking.

While leaders like Manfred Weber (President EPP) and esteemed German Association of the Automotive Industry (VDA) President Hildegard Müller celebrate the softened combustion ban as a win for the "free market" and "technology openness", we paint a different, much sharper picture of the situation in the article.


The key takeaways from the expert panel:
📉 The Real Problem: Economic expert Monika Schnitzer puts it dryly: "Too high costs and technical backwardness... are the problem, not CO2 rules." 

🛑 Poison for Investment: Ferdinand Dudenhöffer urgently warns against political flip-flopping: "This is absolute poison for the industry. Politics is damaging investor confidence." 

🌏 The Market Illusion: Stefan Bratzel reminds us of the reality beyond EU borders: "The often-demanded free market... does not actually exist"—a crucial reality check given the massive subsidies in China. 

My conclusion in the discussion?
We must stop hiding behind phantom solutions. I consider e-fuels as mainstream for passenger cars to be physically and economically a "dangerous illusion" - and same goes for hydrogen in individual mobility.  But what is even more fatal is what we are not talking about while we emotionally cling to the combustion engine:

"The extreme dependency on China is the industry's real problem, not the combustion ban."

The future of the European car industry will not be decided at the gas pump, but in securing value chains, and creating a positive China strategy—steps like VW's cell production in Salzgitter are the only right path forward.

Thank you to Stefan Hajek for this excellent overview!

Much more details are available in the article here: https://lnkd.in/e2tk8-49

👇 What’s your take: Are we fighting mock battles over engines while losing the trade war for raw materials, market access, competitiveness and technology?

#Automotive #Strategy #China #SupplyChain #WirtschaftsWoche #Mobility #Capgemini

Dr. Rainer Mehl, Christian Hummel, Marc Matthies, Laurence Noël, Christoph Jungnickl Capgemini Invent
author: Peter Fintl

title: The AI Chip Showdown: Who will take the crown in the future?🤖👑
text:
The AI Chip Showdown: Who will take the crown in the future?🤖👑

Nvidia's H100 chip reigns supreme in the AI realm, but contenders are rising.  I was honored to contribute to a recent Handelsblatt  analysis (link below) alongside my colleague Alan Priestley  of Gartner exploring this dynamic landscape.

Here are a few key points from the article: 

* H100's Success Drivers: Unparalleled processing power for AI, plus Nvidia's powerful software suite, fueling explosive demand.

* Competition Heats Up: AMD's "chiplet" based designs for it’s MI300 series of AI accelerators offer potential cost advantages (some industry estimations say that the H100 is at USD3100 cost for the huge 814mm2 die size chip with an average sales price of USD20k). Meanwhile, tech giants explore custom silicon for tailored AI acceleration. I've attached a few examples of these custom-designed AI accelerators, which I recently discussed at the CAR Center Automotive Research IT Symposium in Stuttgart. 

* Staying Ahead:  Nvidia's H100 is a profit powerhouse, and even more powerful successors are on the horizon.  But the AI chip race is far from over.  

Is the upcoming H200 the answer, or will emerging solutions reshape the playing field? 

Read the Handelsblatt article for more insights: https://lnkd.in/dkGgs49B

Capgemini Engineering  #innovation #semiconductorindustry #ai Capgemini High Tech #consulting

title: "The chip paradox: Why business is booming and share prices are suffering". I recently had the pleasure of talking to Ha
text:
"The chip paradox: Why business is booming and share prices are suffering". I recently had the pleasure of talking to Handelsblatt about the reasons why chip manufacturers have lost massive amounts of value on the stock market recently. 

Among other things, investors fear an end to the unprecedented order boom and falling prices for some #semiconductors in the foreseeable future due to enormous investments in building up production capacities. 

Even if the Russian crisis, China's no-covid strategy and fragile supply chains are causing increased uncertainties - this alone cannot explain the decline in valuations since the highs at the beginning of the year.

* In the past three years, the SOX (Philadelphia Semiconductor Index) has grown 40% yoy
* For instance, NVIDIA value has gone 10x from 2019 to 2021
* The manufacturing capacities for semiconductors are consistently utilized and the delivery times are correspondingly long
* 6 -12 months delivery time has become quite common in the industry
* TSMC, for example, expects a revenue growth of 30% compared to last year, profits may grow by more than 20%.

This has led to quite "sporty" valuations of the stocks on the stock exchanges.

But in addition to the geopolitical challenges, a number of home-made problems have also put pressure on the stocks:

* The long-standing world market leader Intel Corporation has started a technological race to catch up and is investing double-digit billions of euros
* nvidia has to reinvent itself to some extent after the failed takeover of Arm
* TSMC is investing USD 40 billion in new plants and machinery this year

In addition, there are some developments on the demand side such as:
* Hopefuls such as #NFT or #metaverse not taking off as quickly as hoped
* Tech companies are starting to develop their own semiconductors
* The supply gap is narrowing steadily, although further significant bottlenecks will occur in 2022

Overall, this has led to a reduction in high prices.

What is the outlook?
* the current corrections have led to more realistic valuations
* chip equipment suppliers like ASML, Lam Research and Applied Materials are enjoying bulging order books
* "new" markets like the #automotiveindustry will massively increase their share of the global chip market. (Today just under 10%, in a few years around 16% sales share for the automotive industry)
* For instance, the massive electrification of vehicles will power the business of the #SiC champions like Wolfspeed, STMicroelectronics, Infineon Technologies or ROHM Co., Ltd. / SiCrystal GmbH 

More about this in the Handelsblatt print and online: https://lnkd.in/ew_dN9hp

title: "Expensive catch-up": Handelsblatt reports today how Intel Corporation's new CEO Pat Gelsinger wants to invest massively
text:
"Expensive catch-up": Handelsblatt reports today how Intel Corporation's new CEO Pat Gelsinger wants to invest massively in factories and #technology. 

My colleague Shiv Tasker and I had the pleasure of contributing some insights to this article about #technologyleadership in the #semiconductorindustry, the role of architectures for #chipdesign and custom silicon, and the opportunities for fabs during the current #chipshortage.

Highlights from the piece:
* Pat Gelsinger also plans to build new plants in Europe. He hopes to break ground next year, the new Intel boss said on Thursday evening at a virtual discussion with EU Commissioner Thierry Breton. He also plans sites for chip packaging and #researchanddevelopement on the continent. 

* Member countries must fully commit to the #europeanunion's plan to increase its share of global #semiconductors production to 20% by 2030, Italy's Prime Minister Mario Draghi said this week. France's President Emmanuel Macron also wants to support the industry.

For further details and how those plans are received by the #capitalmarket check out the article here: https://lnkd.in/edbWm5P3

title: The IAA has opened it’s doors today. And it is all about #change. All #keynotes from Daimler AG, Volkswagen Passenger Ca
text: The IAA has opened it’s doors today. And it is all about #change. All #keynotes from Daimler AG, Volkswagen Passenger Cars, Porsche AG, AUDI AG, etc. are about #electrification, zero-impact / #circular #economy and #intelligentmobility. Unlike the previous show, the #iaa19 features production-ready, (mostly) affordable #electric vehicles from old and new OEM’s like BYTON. And it seems now all manufacturers have understood how important it is to provide an extensive, easy to use #charging #network, too. At the same time we see the #convergence of #automotive #engineering and #IT accelerating at all steps of the value chain. While the big #hype about #autonomous is not at the front page of this years show, the systems are marching steadily towards #L3  functionality and beyond. What’s also not spoken out is, that further partnerships between industry players are inevitable as well as that the #ecosystem between #OEM’s, #Tiers and #engineering / #RnD providers is in flux as well. Many say that the #IAA19 is at crossroads. I can’t speak for the event, but the product pipeline from the OEM’s is now much better prepared to #master the #transformation of the #automotive #industry.

title: ️Cambricon & Co: How China Plans to Win the AI Race
text:
🎖️Cambricon & Co: How China Plans to Win the AI Race

In an article for the venerable WirtschaftsWoche, I was invited by Jörn Petring to comment on the rise of Cambricon, a company some already call the “NVIDIA of China.” While the hype is striking, the bigger picture is even more telling: China pursues a multi-pillar strategy to secure technological sovereignty and leadership in AI.

🔹 Talent & research power – According to a UNIDO-linked study, 65 of the world’s top 100 AI researchers are of Chinese origin, and half of them are already working in China. This is not just “AI” – some call it jokingly “Asian Intelligence.”

🔹 Standard setting – Whether through pushing of formats like FP8 in DeepSeek models, APIs compatible with NVIDIA’s CUDA development environment, or Huawei’s open-source initiatives, China is seeking to write the rules others must follow.

🔹 Hardware & software co-design – With companies like Cambricon, Huawei, Moore Threads, and Enflame, hardware is being optimized for local models such as DeepSeek’s latest V3.1. This creates a tight integration between chips and AI frameworks – and higher switching costs for outsiders.

🔹 Control of data & business models – In AI, access to data is power. China is ensuring that local champions can build at scale, from cloud infrastructure to application ecosystems. And that the data is in line to guidelines and narratives.

So, is all set for taking the lead at the world stage?

Two critical challenges remain:

1️⃣ Manufacturing gap – China’s most advanced domestic production stands at 7nm (SMIC), while NVIDIA and their manufacturing partner TSMC are already at 4/3/2nm roadmap (today, the current generation NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra is using a customized N4P process). The performance and efficiency delta remains a structural hurdle.

2️⃣ Software ecosystems – NVIDIA CUDA is still the de facto global standard. Building credible alternatives requires not just technology, but also the willingness of developers to switch – something the US hopes to prevent by keeping Chinese teams tied into its toolchains.

➡️ My take: Rather than looking for “the one Chinese NVIDIA,” it is a coordinated, competitive ecosystem that Beijing is fostering – with Cambricon as a symbol of ambition. The outcome will shape not only the semiconductor race, but the future balance of AI innovation globally.

Full article (in German): WirtschaftsWoche – “Cambricon: Sieht so das Nvidia Chinas aus?” https://lnkd.in/dXEkbbSf

Feel free to reach out to for more information on AI, semiconductors in China and beyond.

Big thanks to my awesome colleagues:

 Andy Vickers Mark Roberts Francois Babin Christoph Jungnickl 

#innovation #china #MIC2025 #ai #semiconductors

title: Catalyzing Europe's Next Semiconductor Revolution with Gallium Nitride (GaN)
text:
🚀 Catalyzing Europe's Next Semiconductor Revolution with Gallium Nitride (GaN)

⚡ We enter a "second electric revolution" driven by the urgent need to decarbonize our society, replacing fossil energy carriers with sustainable electric energy solutions. 

🔋  Efficient power electronics plays a key role in this process. Components based on so-called "wide bandgap” semiconductor materials such as GaN or SiC (Silicon Carbide) will be essential to achieve the desired outcomes. GaN's unique properties enable better switching efficiency, increased power density  and higher operating temperatures than traditional silicon-based components, making it a game-changer for applications in electric vehicles, data centers, high-speed chargers and many other applications that require power conversion. 

🏁  The race is on to manufacture GaN components as cheap as possible to match cost of today's dominant silicon-based technology. 

📰  I'm honored to be quoted in Handelsblatt's “Insight Innovation” article, "Gallium Nitride - The Hope for Chips from Europe," discussing the impact of GaN with other industry leaders.

Some facts from the article:

📈  Yole Intelligence's market researchers anticipate significant growth for these GaN power chips. By 2029, global sales are expected to increase tenfold to $2.5 billion. Today, you see GaN components for instance mainly in the ultra-small chargers for your electronics.

🏭 But what about investment needs for new manufacturing equipment? GaN technnology is widely compatible with existing lines for silicon-based chips. 

This is the big difference with another promising new chip material: SiC. To make SiC chips, companies must build entirely new production lines. As a result, such chips are about three times as expensive as GaN semiconductors.

Some statements from the article:
- Jochen Hanebeck, CEO of Infineon Technologies, said "This technological breakthrough will change the industry" after Infineon achieved a major milestone - scaling GaN production to 300mm wafers, reducing costs and increasing efficiency.

- Michael Sorger, GaN project leader at Infineon, noted the extreme technical challenge of combining GaN with silicon, using "pressure like in ten kilometers of ocean depth".

Other key players in GaN innovation:
- STMicroelectronics, Texas Instruments: Investing in GaN R&D.
- Renesas Electronics: Strengthened its capabilities by acquiring Transphorm Inc., a GaN expert.
- Innoscience: A Chinese GaN technology player, operates “the world's largest dedicated 8-inch GaN-on-Si FET fab”.
- TSMC, Samsung Semiconductor are/will be preparing some fabs for GaN production. 

🇪🇺 Materials like GaN offer a huge chance for Europe to up its game in power electronics.

Read more in the article here: https://lnkd.in/dba_EmGQ

Christoph Jungnickl

#Innovation #Semiconductors #Sustainability

title: After a four years break, the #izb is back to Wolfsburg. And so much has changed in the #automotiveindustry since Octobe
text:
After a four years break, the #izb is back to Wolfsburg. And so much has changed in the #automotiveindustry since October 2018!

* #Software has become a core skill for OEM‘s and Tier‘s alike and is now the major driver of #innovation 

* Electronics and #semiconductors are now competitive advantages for manufacturers to ensure longer range and unique user experiences

* Buyer - Supplier relationships have (often) changed as OEM’s re-define their new core-competencies and are requiring eco-system partners who help them to innovate-fast, scale globally for their world markets, ensure a resilient supply chain and help to keep costs in check

The industry “bible” Automobilwoche has shed some light on those changes in their special edition about the #izb2022. Very interesting to read is an interview with Murat Aksel , CPO of Volkswagen AG / VW brand , about his new purchasing strategy. 

In the articles about software defined vehicle as well as semiconductors it was a pleasure to see Capgemini Engineering citations. 

I am looking forward to meet you at the IZB next week, together with many Capgemini colleagues, to discuss with you the trending topics and demonstrate how Capgemini - with it’s combined capabilities for strategy, IT and engineering - can help you to master that profound transformation the industry is undergoing right now. 

At our booth we show you the Capgemini expertise for innovative vehicle software development at scale, future domain controller / EE concepts, advanced cockpit systems, validation of EE/Infotainment/ADAS systems and much more.

See you in #WOB !

Werner Ferreira da Silva Luciano Bottoni Dr. Rainer Mehl Ralf Blessmann  Daniel Leuchter Jan Schulz Mirco Baran Maria Noemi Giuffrida Juergen Wiemann Giles Guidicelli Christoph Jungnickl

title: "Hidden champion of #semiconductors". This is how the Welt am Sonntag describes the chip manufacturer Arm in a report to
text:
"Hidden champion of #semiconductors". This is how the Welt am Sonntag describes the chip manufacturer Arm in a report today. I had the pleasure to contribute my point of view to this article. 

* With over 200 billion chips shipped containing ARM technology, the company has become a system-relevant player in the last 30 years. That's because ARM is in a unique position in the growing semiconductor market.

* At present, hardly any smartphone, server or car can get by without the designs and patents of the Brits. The company licenses its IP to the world's leading corporations, and its architecture
is the technological basis for the chips
chips from Apple, Qualcomm  , Samsung Electronics or Broadcom Inc.  

* WamS argues that ARM could be the nucleus for a resurgence of the European chip industry.

Have a look at the article to understand the opportunities of the failed takeover by NVIDIA  but also the challenges, such as the necessary investments in future server chips which designs can be of interest for Google , Amazon Web Services (AWS) or Tesla. 

Article can be found (paywall) at Welt am Sonntag Store: https://lnkd.in/evXE8b5B

Welt am Sonntag is the leading quality Sunday newspaper in Germany, known for their excellent authors, exclusive interviews, controversial opinions and a wide range of topics.

title: A glimpse on the #autonomousdriving progress of Waymo , Cruise, Tesla and others:  In an Interview with Handelsblatt, I
text:
A glimpse on the #autonomousdriving progress of Waymo , Cruise, Tesla and others:  In an Interview with Handelsblatt, I had the pleasure to analyze the latest #selfdrivingvehicles testing figures from the California Department of Motor Vehicles. 

From those figures one can see that Waymo and Cruise now report one system disengagement from self-driving mode per roughly 30.000 miles driven. Those figures need to be taken with a grain of salt (test area, disengagement definition, ...) but they indicate the progress. For comparison, Waymo writes in their safety report that they achieved in the Chandler/AZ area a whopping 130.000 miles per “contact” disengagement. 

Since Tesla does not register official data, we can deduct from current performance of the FSD beta about where they stand in relation to the other top players.... 

Find more info at: https://lnkd.in/eCGAA9a

title: "Mittendrin statt nur dabei"* - the decarbonization of aviation or how Capgemini talents are shaping the future of air t
text:
"Mittendrin statt nur dabei"* - the decarbonization of aviation or how Capgemini talents are shaping the future of air transportation. Today, Pascal Brier, our Group Chief Innovation Officer, wrote a very interesting post about the different ways to decarbonize aviation. From sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), to hydrogen, to electric flight, to entirely new aircraft concepts. 

But wait, there is more! 

It makes me immensely proud to see (and drive) how Capgemini Engineering has been involved for years in various research programs (hello CleanSky!) for new aircraft concepts, novel propulsion systems as well as innovative aerostructures. 

I am particularly excited about one of our latest research projects, in which Capgemini and a research consortium will convert an aircraft to use hydrogen as an energy carrier. 

To paraphrase Pascal - and adding a slight twist to his statement: "So if you look up in the sky sometimes after 2035, do not be surprised to see in many airplanes technologies that Capgemini talents have researched and developed in 2025." 

I am proud of my colleagues because "they are making a dent in the universe! Andreas Kötter Oliver Hegen Dr. Luis Prado Dr. Selim COSKUN Rodrigo Maia Anne-Laure CADENE Keith Williams Werner Ferreira da Silva Jochen Bechtold Philipp Prott Capgemini Aerospace & Defense

* "Mittendrin statt nur dabei", one of my favorite german phrases, I would translate it with "at the heart of things"
author: Peter Fintl

title: 𝗡𝗮𝘃𝗶𝗴𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗪𝗮𝘃𝗲𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝗚𝗹𝗼𝗯𝗮𝗹 𝗧𝗲𝗰𝗵𝗻𝗼𝗹𝗼𝗴𝘆 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽: 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁𝘀 𝗳�
text:
𝗡𝗮𝘃𝗶𝗴𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗪𝗮𝘃𝗲𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝗚𝗹𝗼𝗯𝗮𝗹 𝗧𝗲𝗰𝗵𝗻𝗼𝗹𝗼𝗴𝘆 𝗟𝗲𝗮𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽: 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁𝘀 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗺𝘆 𝗿𝗲𝗰𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝘁𝗮𝗹𝗸 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻 𝗼𝗻 𝗖𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗮'𝘀 𝗾𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝘁𝗼 𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗲𝗰𝗵 𝗿𝗮𝗰𝗲. 

As we stand on the cusp of significant global technological shifts, the spotlight is inevitably on China's strategic ambitions. This was a key focus of my recent dialogue with Stern magazine for their article "Chinas Griff zur technologischen Weltherrschaft - so wird Peking den Westen schlagen". (China's Quest for Technological Supremacy - How Beijing Plans to Outpace the West.)

💡China's technological marathon is not just a quest for innovation, but evidence of its carefully planned leap from world factory to knowledge and technology leader. In my discussion, we delved deep into the strategic areas where China is poised to assert its dominance - from solar technology and electric vehicles to cutting-edge automation and AI. The vision is clear: to lead through knowledge, not force, and to embrace a future where smart technology drives global influence.

🗓 Moreover, China's strategic technology push is deeply rooted in a long tradition of state-led initiatives. Forty years ago, programmes such as '863' and '973' were launched, serving as a blueprint for high-tech development and underlining the country's commitment to innovation and scientific exploration. These pioneering programmes have evolved into more recent strategies such as "Made in China 2025", reflecting a seamless transition from ambitious national plans to formidable global benchmarks. 

❗ The recent delineation of key technology areas by the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology earlier this year is a continuation of this legacy - a strategy to build China's technological supremacy.

📢 The upcoming Auto China Show in Beijing and the recent visit of German Chancellor Olaf Scholz in the People's Republic underline the timeliness of this narrative. As China unveils its latest advances, it's an opportune moment to reflect on how the global community can engage with and respond to these emerging tech powerhouses.

🌍 Europe, with its rich cultural, scientific and economic heritage, is well positioned to thrive in the midst of these challenges. The question is how we will use our inherent strengths to not only participate in the next wave of global innovation, but to lead it.

🎙 As we navigate the currents of this global technology race, your insights and engagement are invaluable. I invite you to read more on China's strategic manoeuvres in the full Stern article and join the conversation. How do you see these developments impacting our global landscape? Share your views and let's explore the vast opportunities and challenges ahead.

title: "Decarbonisation can only succeed with efficient power electronics [...]," says Jochen Hanebeck, CEO of the semiconducto
text:
"Decarbonisation can only succeed with efficient power electronics [...]," says Jochen Hanebeck, CEO of the semiconductor specialist Infineon Technologies. Today the Handelsblatt took a comprehensive look at developments in power semiconductors and highlighted Infineon's expansion plans for its factories in Dresden and Kulim, Malaysia.

It was a pleasure to contribute to the article with some industry analysis on technology and application areas.

Here are some key facts from the article:
📈  From Infineon's perspective, customer demand for power electronics continues to grow strongly. 

⚡  The class of so-called "wide bandgap" (WBG) semiconductors is particularly important. Examples include silicon carbide (SiC) and gallium nitride (GaN). They have better properties in the areas of high switching frequency, high switching voltage and high temperature resistance compared to classic silicon (Si) technology.

💎  Applications for these WBG-based components can be found in e-mobility, renewable energy, chargers and, of course, data centres.

🚘  There is a strong trend towards SiC in the high efficiency/high power density area. In electric cars, for example, there is no way around SiC for optimum range. 

🛵  In cost-sensitive applications, such as entry-level electric vehicles, combinations of both technologies are foreseeable (SiC+Si).

💻  In charging electronics (DCDC HV-HV, DCDC HV-LV and on-board chargers), GaN technology is becoming increasingly established. It is already being applied more frequently in ultra-compact high-end chargers for laptops.

📢  Numerous customers have approached us and said: 'We need higher capacities'," said Jochen Hanebeck of Infineon in a conference call with analysts. For the Malaysian plant, he said, customers had paid more than a billion euros in advance. In addition, the group has orders worth five billion euros on its books.

💶  In total, Infineon plans to invest around 10 billion euros in the Dresden (Germany) and Kulim (Malaysia) plants.

🔝  In the coming years, the production lines for SiC components will be converted to 200mm wafers. Wolfspeed has pioneered this trend with its new fab in the US. This will lead to significant efficiency gains. Today, SiC components cost 3 to 4 times as much as Si components due to the complex, energy-intensive manufacturing process.

🗺  STMicroelectronics is the world's largest supplier of SiC chips. Analysts at Yole put its market share at 37 per cent. Infineon is in second place with 19 per cent, followed by Wolfspeed with 16 per cent.

Read more in today's Handelsblatt!

And as always: for all kind of "semi-know-how" reach out to us...
Jonathan Nussbaumer  Shiv Tasker Loïc Hamon Christoph Jungnickl


Capgemini Engineering #innovation #semiconductorindustry  Capgemini Automotive #electricvehicles #semiconductors 

Pictures: Peter Fintl, Handelsblatt, Infineon
