title: "Supply shortages: India courts chip industry with billions". In a recent article, Handelsblatt highlighted how the Indi
text:
"Supply shortages: India courts chip industry with billions". In a recent article, Handelsblatt highlighted how the Indian government wants to counter the #semiconductor shortage with the help of its own factories and is already achieving initial success. It was a pleasure for me to contribute to this piece with industry insights.

Companies are answering the call of government leader Narendra Modi, who wants to lure the #semiconductorindustry with billions in subsidies. Although India is a giant around IT and also a powerhouse in HW/electronics development, the country plays virtually no role in chip production. 

Here are some projects for planned fabs in India:
* ISMC (a JV of Next Orbit Ventures Fund and Tower Semiconductor of Israel->currently being acquired by Intel Corporation) wants to invest $3 billion in Karnataka to build a chip fab. Approximately 1,500 jobs are to be created.

* Vedanta has signed a memorandum of understanding with Foxconn to jointly produce semiconductors in India. Up to $20 billions are planned for factories that will produce smartphone screens as well as chips.

But India must reckon with strong competition here, despite the most generous support for settlements. The number two in the chip industry, Intel, has already launched publicly funded investments worth billions in the EU and the USA.

According to the industry association SEMI, the global manufacturers want to invest 446 billion dollars in the construction of new plants and additional production lines.

Can India repeat the success of high-tech settlements like in the mobile phone sector?

Prof. Rakesh Kumar (Electrical and Computer Engineering Department at the University of Illinois at Urbana Champaign), is skeptical. "India lacks the specialized hardware ecosystems and skills that chip manufacturing requires," he commented on the effort.

However, from my own experience with the great and talented colleagues in India, as well as the Indian government's ability to drive strategic developments in a sustainable way, I am positive about the chances of building an Indian chip manufacturing ecosystem.

Read the article here: https://lnkd.in/gVnxevXR (Paywall)

What do my colleagues Shiv Tasker and Scott Runner think about this?

title: AI is hungry, and it’s eating the automotive industry’s silicon.
text:
AI is hungry, and it’s eating the automotive industry’s silicon.

Today’s feature in the Handelsblatt  highlights a massive structural shift: the AI boom is vacuuming up the global memory chip (DRAM) market. For the automotive industry, this means higher procurement costs, intense margin pressure, and a forced departure from old supply chain patterns.  

To be fair, we are seeing brilliant software optimizations emerge. Techniques like Google's Turbo Quant and other advanced quantization methods significantly reduce the memory footprint of AI models. 

But let's be realistic: while these innovations help soften the blow per model, the sheer, explosive scale of global AI deployment means they don't change the underlying market dynamics.

The expert consensus in the article paints a stark picture of the road ahead:

Pedro Pacheco (Gartner) forecasts a well-equipped vehicle will become about $150 more expensive this year, warning that supply disruptions could hit as early as Q2 2026.  

Tanjeff Schadt (Strategy&) notes that individual, highly specialized memory modules have already seen price jumps of 300% to 400%.  

Martin Geißler, MBA, CFM  (Argon & Co) points out that even for volume manufacturers, extra costs of $20 to $30 per vehicle quickly cascade into high three-digit million burdens. He expects this pressure to persist at least until the end of 2026, as chipmakers naturally focus on the high-margin AI business.  

SK Group's Tae-won (Tony) Chey expects memory chips to remain scarce for another four to five years.  

Samsung Electronics Co-Chief Jun Young-hyun anticipates that long-term, 3 to 5-year purchasing contracts will become the new industry standard.  

As I shared in the piece, this insatiable hyperscaler demand is tying up massive production capacities because manufacturers prioritize these lucrative components. This cost pressure is forcing a tough strategic pivot. Over the last few years, the trend was to install unified, high-performance hardware across all vehicle tiers to monetize them later via software subscriptions. 

However, escalating memory costs make compromises highly likely. We may soon see less RAM in entry-level models or a return to paid hardware packages for premium features.  
Navigating this dynamic environment requires more than just reactive purchasing. In our current client discussions, we are tackling this on three strategic fronts:

1. Short-term Supply Chain Agility: Moving away from quarter-to-quarter haggling and adapting to long-term volume commitments.

2. Mid-term Product Architectures: Redesigning EE-architectures to optimize compute and memory efficiency, ensuring we don't over-spec hardware without ROI.

3. Long-term Silicon Engineering: Taking greater control of the silicon lifecycle. It is no longer just an innovation play; it's a requirement for technological sovereignty, cost control, and securing supply.

See comments!

Clément Charruel Luz G. Mauch Francois Babin Dr. Nico Wolf
author: Peter Fintl

title: 𝐍𝐚𝐯𝐢𝐠𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐀𝐈 𝐑𝐞𝐯𝐨𝐥𝐮𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧: 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐂𝐡𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐞𝐧𝐠𝐞 𝐭𝐨 𝐍𝐯𝐢𝐝𝐢𝐚'𝐬 𝐓𝐡𝐫𝐨𝐧𝐞
text:
𝐍𝐚𝐯𝐢𝐠𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐀𝐈 𝐑𝐞𝐯𝐨𝐥𝐮𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧: 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐂𝐡𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐞𝐧𝐠𝐞 𝐭𝐨 𝐍𝐯𝐢𝐝𝐢𝐚'𝐬 𝐓𝐡𝐫𝐨𝐧𝐞

In an era where AI technology is not just evolving but exploding in capabilities, the demand for computing power has skyrocketed. Nvidia, long regarded as the linchpin in the AI accelerator chip market due to excellent products, has witnessed unparalleled growth thanks to this AI boom. 

A recent insightful article from Handelsblatt, "𝘞𝘦𝘳 𝘕𝘷𝘪𝘥𝘪𝘢 𝘫𝘦𝘵𝘻𝘵 𝘨𝘦𝘧ä𝘩𝘳𝘭𝘪𝘤𝘩 𝘸𝘦𝘳𝘥𝘦𝘯 𝘬ö𝘯𝘯𝘵𝘦," unveils a fascinating turn in the narrative.

The surge in AI has not only benefited Nvidia but has also painted a target on its back, attracting a diverse array of competitors keen on carving out their share of the lucrative AI chip market. This competitive push comes from several fronts:

𝐓𝐞𝐜𝐡 𝐓𝐢𝐭𝐚𝐧𝐬 𝐃𝐢𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐢𝐟𝐲: Giants such as Google, Microsoft, AWS, and Meta are not just relying on Nvidia and AMD but are also developing their own AI chips. This move towards self-reliance underscores the strategic importance of in-house capabilities in driving AI innovations forward.

𝐈𝐧𝐧𝐨𝐯𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐒𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐮𝐩𝐬 𝐄𝐦𝐞𝐫𝐠𝐞: Companies like Groq are at the forefront of innovation, offering chips that are custom-designed for specific AI tasks. This level of specialization could redefine efficiency and performance standards in the AI chip industry.

𝐄𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐛𝐥𝐢𝐬𝐡𝐞𝐝 𝐑𝐢𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐬 𝐈𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐬𝐢𝐟𝐲 𝐂𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐞𝐭𝐢𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧: Intel and AMD are not standing still. They are ramping up their offerings, aiming to challenge Nvidia's dominance directly.

And, we also need to see the China-angle. Domestic tech players like Biren or Huawei are introducing new chips, some of them even manufactured in China with advanced processes.

Collaborating with industry experts such as Glenn O'Donnell from Forrester , Jonathan Curtis from Franklin Templeton, and Gartner's Alan Priestley and myself, this article sheds light on the dynamic shifts within the AI chip market. As we navigate this transformative period, it’s clear that the race is not just about who can provide the most powerful chip but who can drive innovation to meet the rapidly evolving demands of AI applications.

As executives, our challenge is to stay ahead of these shifts, ensuring our strategies are as adaptive and forward-thinking as the technologies we depend on. The emergence of new players and technologies in the AI chip market is not just a challenge for Nvidia but a testament to the vibrant innovation shaping our future.

Let's engage in a dialogue: How is your organization adapting to these technological advancements in AI? Are you considering diversifying your AI chip portfolio, or is the focus on fostering innovation through strategic partnerships?

Link to the Handelsblatt article: https://lnkd.in/eVuJg4Dk

title: The goal is clear: the German car industry is driving towards #electromobility. The renowned Hans-Böckler-Stiftung has e
text:
The goal is clear: the German car industry is driving towards #electromobility. The renowned Hans-Böckler-Stiftung has examined the "potholes" on the path of Germany's flagship industry towards sustainable mobility - especially from the perspective of employees.

It was a pleasure to contribute together with Alexander Timmer, Partner at Berylls Strategy Advisors, to this article with some industry and technology analyses, for example on the opportunities for Chinese manufacturers.

Some key messages of the article:

The preservation of the approximately 800,000 German jobs in the car industry is of great importance.
 
The "Initiative Agora Energiewende" assumes that 20,000 new jobs will be created in this young industry in Germany by 2030.

The industry is facing what is probably the biggest wave of qualification in its history. Electrical, electronic and software skills are in demand.

The investment plans of the manufacturers suggest that a considerable share of the development work would be transferred from suppliers to the car companies.

Without further measures and proper startegies, around 100.000 jobs could be at risk in the German auto industry.

Read more here in the article: https://lnkd.in/dwNhrdiT

title: "Intel is pushing into the software business" - this is what Intel Corporation's head of technology Greg Lavender explai
text:
"Intel is pushing into the software business" - this is what Intel Corporation's head of technology Greg Lavender explains in an interview with Handelsblatt today. The change of heart at Intel, "Project Amber" and the software strategies of competitors AMD and NVIDIA are examined in this article. It was a pleasure to contribute to this piece with some industry analyses.

Some facts and figures from the article and the industry:

👉 Intel employs about 20,000 software developers - that is about the number of employees of nVidia as a whole (about 22,000 worldwide).

👉 Up to now, most of Intel's powerful software programs and libraries have been "sales promotions" for its processors, to put it bluntly.

👉 On the processor side, competitors like AMD or nVidia have certainly put pressure on the #semiconductors giant and long-time technology leader. However, at the Intel #innovation event the day before yesterday in San Jose, Intel showed that it has strong plans for desktop market by presenting the i9-13900KS (24 cores, max. clock 6 GHz, limited release).

👉 At the same time, it has become necessary for the semiconductor manufacturers to build up a functioning ecosystem of "metal" and "software". nVidia is pushing strongly into the application stack here - be it in the area of #autonomousdriving  / #ai applications, virtual development/digital twins or the entire topic of "metaverse". AMD also offers a substantial number of optimised libraries around its AI accelerator family "Instinct" - but these are largely free of charge.

👉 Intel is now getting involved and is not only trying to monetise existing software, for example for the trustworthy operation of data centres, or the optimised use of CPU resources in cloud applications. 

👉 The prominent acquisition of Mobileye - for which there are now actions to take it public again - is an example of how intel has acted here so far.

Read more in the article in today's issue.

Images: Handelsblatt, nVidia/BMW AG, AMD

title: "𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐛𝐚𝐭𝐭𝐥𝐞 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐛𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐨𝐟 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐟𝐮𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞". Especially in the field of #automateddriv
text:
"𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐛𝐚𝐭𝐭𝐥𝐞 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐛𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐨𝐟 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐟𝐮𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞". Especially in the field of #automateddriving, tech corporations are trying to penetrate the #automotiveindustry with all their might. In today's issue, Handelsblatt reports on how leading suppliers Bosch, Continental and ZF Group are dealing with this challenge. It was a pleasure for me to contribute to this piece with a general industry analysis.

Here's the gist of this interesting article:

* With components such as engine management systems, ABS, ESP or lane departure warning systems, the major Tier-1 suppliers have long played an important role in the #automotive ecosystem.

* However, in the area of driving automation, an application that requires the highest computing power, chip manufacturers such as NVIDIA, Qualcomm and Intel Corporation are now making increasing inroads directly into the OEMs.

* However, the chip giants are not only supplying the computing power. Basic software, middleware and function development are now also part of the portfolio. 

* This puts the established suppliers in a new position. New, solvent players are entering the market (EBIT margin nVidia 36.1%, Qualcomm 29.8%, intel 22.4%). In some cases, they are also able to implement new revenue models (revenue sharing) with the OEM's.

* Have the established business models of the suppliers now literally reached the end of the road? I don't think so! A look at the different strategies of the big houses shows why.

* Volkswagen AG: goes "all-in" with CARIAD. Wants to bring comprehensive value creation in-house. For this purpose, a cooperation with BOSCH was founded.

* ZF: cooperates with Nvidia and supplies the hardware for autonomous vehicles with the ZF Pro Ai. For software, the focus is presumably on integration services.

* Continental: supplies hardware and software together. Tends to be chip HW agnostic and aims to develop its own platform-independent software.

* BOSCH: a heavyweight with over 30,000 software developers. Will leverage and defend its position and excellent relationships in the industry. Also in cooperation with Volkswagen.

* And many companies are looking closely at the area of customized or self-developed semiconductors. True to the Tesla model. 

In many cases, new ecosystems will be built here. Because it is precisely this area that needs new service providers and development partners.

More about this in today's print edition of Handelsblatt or online.

title: Trump is reshaping the American auto industry. Instead of small electric cars, large combustion engines are now in deman
text:
Trump is reshaping the American auto industry. Instead of small electric cars, large combustion engines are now in demand again. Here is what it really means for German OEMs.

I was pleased to contribute as an expert to a recent Handelsblatt analysis exploring how Donald Trump’s plans for a comeback of the internal combustion engine could reshape the U.S. automotive market – and how German manufacturers might benefit from this shift.

The article highlights that the potential rollback of EV incentives could fundamentally alter market dynamics. Instead of direct subsidies, Washington now favors a tax-deduction model for car loans of up to $10,000 per year until 2028, as explained by Kem Washington (Bankrate). This policy clearly favors higher-priced vehicles and, crucially, those assembled in the United States.

This could make German premium brands the unexpected winners. Models like the BMW X3 to X7, the Mercedes GLE and GLS, and the VW Atlas are all built locally and would thus qualify for the new incentives. In contrast, Audiand Porsche – without U.S. assembly – could face competitive disadvantages, as Porsche North America CEO Timo Resch pointed out.

Yet, not everything is bright. Guillaume Dejean (Allianz Trade) estimates that U.S. tariffs could still cause around $4 billion in revenue losses for German OEMs in 2025, squeezing margins just when the industry needs to invest heavily in software, automation, and electrification.

At the same time, Erin Keating (Cox Automotive) notes that the average transaction price of a new car in the U.S. has surpassed $50,000. The American market is becoming increasingly premium-oriented – but success depends on quality, product-market fit, and design relevance.

In my contribution to the article, I emphasized that policy alone will not decide the winners. Manufacturers must address portfolio fit, quality issues, and design appeal to reconnect with U.S. consumers. Where German brands align with American tastes – for example, through robust, high-performance SUVs – they continue to perform strongly. But we should not mistake short-term political momentum for structural change: trying to reverse electrification is like trying to stop the hands of a clock.

My thanks to all experts featured in the article – including Hendrik Lang (BMW) on technology openness, Kem Washington (Bankrate) on fiscal mechanics, Erin Keating (Cox Automotive) on demand dynamics, Guillaume Dejean (Allianz Trade) on trade impacts, and Timo Resch (Porsche NA) on production realities.

👉 You can find the full Handelsblatt article in the comments below.

Capgemini Automotive #innovation Christian Hummel Dr. Rainer Mehl Jens Sulek
author: Peter Fintl

title: "In the short term, this is dramatic". writes Handelsblatt today. In the past week, the U.S. government has imposed far-
text:
"In the short term, this is dramatic". writes Handelsblatt today. In the past week, the U.S. government has imposed far-reaching export control measures on China in the #semiconductorindustry sector. 

Although China has not officially reacted, presumably due to the ongoing party congress, the consequences are severe and have the potential not only to weaken chip development in China for years but also to severely impact the business of European suppliers of semiconductor technology. 

The Americans want to prevent China from developing advanced semiconductors, build modern manufacturing lines, and also increase its capacities of data centers and supercomputers and thus further expand its military capabilities with the help of Western technology.

The new measures include not only strict export controls on advanced processors and computing accelerators, such as for AI applications, but also comprehensive measures to virtually cut China off from development and manufacturing technology for chips that have 3D transistor structures (such as FinFETs or GAAFETs) and a structure width of 16 nanometers or less, as well as DRAM chips with a structure width of 18 nanometers or finer and NAND flash memory with 128 layers or more. See document in comments.

Due to the global interconnectedness of the worldwide semiconductor industry, as well as the fact that U.S. technology is used virtually everywhere in some form, European companies are also well advised to comply with the sanctions.

Not only have the first orders been lost by large Chinese chip manufacturers, but also the large foundries such as TSMC have had to stop supplying components to the People's Republic.

Read more about the challenges of the Chinese semiconductor industry and what measures China might take to "decouple" itself from the rest of the semiconductor world in today's print edition of the Handelsblatt or check it out online (link in comments).

Capgemini #consulting Capgemini Engineering #innovation  #semiconductors #manufacturing

title: "Metamorphoses (of mobility)": this is how poetically - and perhaps also inspired by Ovid's magnum opus - Automobilwoche
text:
"Metamorphoses (of mobility)": this is how poetically - and perhaps also inspired by Ovid's magnum opus - Automobilwoche chose the title to report in today's issue on the status and challenges of digital transformation in the #automotiveindustry.

If you follow the recent press reports, you might be worried whether the industry will manage the transformation to a "software-defined vehicle" in the foreseeable future and in a profitable way.

It is important here (once again) to take a balanced view of the situation.
The automotive industry is a leader when it comes to the digitalisation of value chains. According to the study "Embracing Industry 4.0" by ABI Research, the automotive industry will have invested around 100 billion dollars in this field in 2022, and this amount will increase to a good 238 billion dollars by 2030. 

BMW, for example, is investing in the development of a digital factory in cooperation with the chip manufacturer Nvidia - the new generation of e-cars is to be built as cost-efficiently and smoothly as possible. The carmaker Renault is trying to digitally map every process at the heart of its #circulareconomy, in the "re-factory" in Flins. The data exchange consortium Catena-X Automotive Network plans to present the results achieved so far at the Hannover Messe in April and to go live with the first applications.

We have to distinguish between the high level of digitalisation in production and the new digital processes in sales, aftersales and customer management. The automotive industry has always been a driving force in increasing efficiency in manufacturing and in many cases also in traditional product development. But what is new now is the often software-driven transformation in all business areas.

There is a great shortage of skilled workers in many areas, which has so far significantly inhibited the large-scale scaling of software organisations.

My colleague Ralf Blessmann gave a well-received talk about this (and ways out of the skills crisis) at the CAR Center Automotive Research IT Symposium Software-defined car in Munich last week. 

More cooperation and earlier collaboration with partners are the key to a successful digital organisation. This is clearly not yet the strength of many companies in the sector. 

But we see from our consulting practice how many initiatives are being launched here in the industry. My colleague Christian Hummel and his teams are helping to successfully implement the transformation of the industry.

Read more in today's issue https://lnkd.in/dWEHi63P

Capgemini Engineering #innovation #software Capgemini Automotive

title: Is "decoupling" in the semiconductor industry an illusion?📢
text:
📢Is "decoupling" in the semiconductor industry an illusion?📢

Recent geopolitical tensions have spurred Taiwanese chip giants such as Vanguard International Semiconductor Company and TSMC to diversify their manufacturing bases overseas. As a result, Vanguard will invest $7.8 billion in a new chip plant in Singapore, and TSMC is expanding its presence in the US, Japan and Germany with multi-billion dollar projects. For example, TSMC is receiving up to $11.6 billion in US government subsidies to build three facilities in Arizona, part of a $60 billion investment.

In a recent article, Handelsblatt, one of Europe's leading business newspapers, discusses these pivotal shifts in the semiconductor industry. 

These strategic moves are critical to ensuring a resilient global supply chain amid growing uncertainties. However, reliance on Taiwan for advanced semiconductor manufacturing remains significant. Even with the new facilities, Trendforce estimates that 87% of TSMC's production will still be in Taiwan by 2027, underscoring the continued dependence on the island.

The wider industry is also reacting. Kurt Sievers, President and CEO of NXP Semiconductors, a major player that partners with Vanguard, highlights the importance of geographic diversification for a resilient supply chain. This comes as the US imposes 50% tariffs on Chinese semiconductors from January 2025, prompting Western companies to shift orders away from China.

At Capgemini, we are closely monitoring these developments. Despite global diversification, the backbone of the semiconductor supply chain is still heavily reliant on Taiwan. This underscores the need for robust strategies to mitigate risk and ensure continuity - this is what we help our clients to design and implement.

According to McKinsey, the semiconductor market is expected to more than double to $1.3 trillion by 2030. This growth trajectory underscores the critical need for strategic investment and innovative solutions in the industry.

I would like to thank Handelsblatt for sharing my views on this critical issue. As the industry evolves, it is vital for companies to stay informed and agile to adapt to these changes.

Link to the article in the comments.

Capgemini Engineering #consulting #innovation #semiconductor

title: 𝐋𝐨𝐨𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐰𝐚𝐫𝐝 𝐭𝐨 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟰 𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗼 𝗖𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗮 𝐒𝐡𝐨𝐰: 𝐀 𝐒𝐡𝐨𝐰𝐜𝐚𝐬𝐞 𝐨𝐟 𝐆𝐥𝐨𝐛𝐚𝐥 �
text:
𝐋𝐨𝐨𝐤𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐰𝐚𝐫𝐝 𝐭𝐨 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟰 𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗼 𝗖𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗮 𝐒𝐡𝐨𝐰: 𝐀 𝐒𝐡𝐨𝐰𝐜𝐚𝐬𝐞 𝐨𝐟 𝐆𝐥𝐨𝐛𝐚𝐥 𝐀𝐮𝐭𝐨𝐦𝐨𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐈𝐧𝐧𝐨𝐯𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧

As the Beijing Motor Show approaches next week, I'm excited to share insights from the recent Automobilwoche special report, where I was honoured to be quoted alongside esteemed industry experts.

This year's show marks a pivotal moment in automotive history, vividly illustrating the shifting power dynamics as Chinese manufacturers now command over 50% of the local market. The evolution from four years ago is striking, with domestic brands not only participating but leading the way with innovative designs and technologies.

I'm particularly fascinated by the intense competition that is pushing both Chinese and international manufacturers to excel. With a home market characterised by intense competition and plenty of production capacity, Chinese brands are looking to new export markets

🌏 The Middle Kingdom is poised to remain the world's largest vehicle exporter in 2024 (up to 5 million vehicles, 30% of which are estimated to be electric). 

🎢 As the report notes, this year's show will be a battleground for attention, with over 150 brands showcasing the latest models and technologies. This is not just about market share, but a deeper demonstration of strategic shifts and technological advances in the automotive industry. 

A few examples: 
🤖  DiDi Automomous Driving and GAC recently formed a JV with the AION brand to commercially build L4 robo-taxis (pic top right), 
🚘  BYD will unveil a brand new 4.8m mid-size vehicle Qin L (hybrid, up to 16kWh battery, picture bottom left), 
🤑  GAC has slashed the entry price of the AION Y all-electric range by 10,000RMB - now starting at 130,000RMB (up to 610km range in Chinese cycle, picture bottom right).

I was joined by Dr. Klaus Schmitz of Arthur D. Little and several other leading voices in the industry. Klaus highlighted the ongoing race for automated driving technologies, noting that despite regulatory advantages in China, challenges such as chip availability might impact the sector.

For this - and much more - look into the current edition of Automobilwoche!

Let's discuss what these changes mean for global markets and how we can navigate the evolving landscape of automotive technology together. For any questions, reach out to Capgemini, in China and beyond!

Capgemini Automotive #innovation Capgemini Engineering #automotiveindustry #automateddriving Burkhard Riering

title: 𝐖𝐢𝐥𝐥 2024 𝐛𝐞 𝐚𝐧 𝐚𝐧𝐧𝐮𝐬 𝐦𝐢𝐫𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐬 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐠𝐥𝐨𝐛𝐚𝐥 𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐩 𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐲? After a mixe
text:
𝐖𝐢𝐥𝐥 2024 𝐛𝐞 𝐚𝐧 𝐚𝐧𝐧𝐮𝐬 𝐦𝐢𝐫𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐬 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐠𝐥𝐨𝐛𝐚𝐥 𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐩 𝐢𝐧𝐝𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐲? After a mixed year in 2023, the semiconductor industry can expect another record year, as many early indicators are pointing in the right direction. Today's issue of Handelsblatt looks at the opportunities the new year will bring for chipmakers and the uncertainties that still remain.

Together with experts such as Ondrej Burkacky (McKinsey & Company), Alan Priestley (Gartner), John Neuffer (Semiconductor Industry Association) and Dr.-Ing. Gunther Kegel (ZVEI e. V. / CEO Pepperl+Fuchs Group), I was pleased to paint a picture of the situation.

The key facts are:
* Many memory chip and processor manufacturers were upbeat about Q4/23 revenues.

* Analysts are already predicting similar or even higher sales in 2024 than in the boom years of 21/22, despite higher inventories at present.

* Gartner expects chip industry revenues to grow by 17% to US$624 billion in 2024 - a new record.

* After the boom years of the pandemic, companies and consumers have been tightening their belts. Various crises and high inflation have further dampened consumer spending. Gartner estimates: revenues will decline by 11%, around USD 534 billion by 2023.

* Given the correlation between the global economy and chip sales, as well as the lead times in semiconductor manufacturing, a global upturn is expected in the autumn of 2024.

* "Global semiconductor market grew for the eighth consecutive month in October, showing clear positive momentum for chip demand through the end of 2023," said SIA CEO John Neuffer.

* A McKinsey survey of chip industry executives: > 40% expect the Q2 of 2024 to be better than last year. ~25% expect the recovery to start later. Nearly 2/3 expect revenue growth of 4-8% in 2024, slightly less optimistic than Gartner.

* A key driver for the long-term growth of the chip industry will be the fact that AI is making its way into literally every area of our lives. In addition to large-scale applications in data centres, think GPT-4 or Gemini, EDGE AI will drive the demand for energy-efficient computing capacity. "In ten years' time, AI will be in every electronic device, no matter how simple," predicts Gunther Kegel, President of the German Electrical and Electronic Manufacturers' Association (ZVEI).

* China is playing a major role in the expected upturn. Will the Chinese government be able to stimulate domestic demand and ensure strong  growth of GDP in 2024? Who will win the Taiwanese presidential election and how will the situation in the Taiwan Strait develop? Will we see an easing of tensions or will new sabre-rattling increase the pressure? And will the production of advanced semiconductors in the Middle Kingdom be accelerated in order to erode Taiwan's "silicon shield" in the medium term?

Read more in the Handelsblatt or here in other articles! 

Capgemini Engineering #consulting #innovation #semiconductors #ai #semiconductorindustry

title: 𝐅𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝐚𝐬𝐬𝐞𝐭 𝐭𝐨 𝐥𝐢𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲? The semiconductor industry's most important market - China - is threatened
text:
𝐅𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝐚𝐬𝐬𝐞𝐭 𝐭𝐨 𝐥𝐢𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲? The semiconductor industry's most important market - China - is threatened by the economic downturn and the dispute with the US. For many years, flourishing business with the Middle Kingdom was seen by many companies as a sign of prudent business policy and a symbol of taking advantage of global market opportunities. The semiconductor industry, in particular, has steadily built up China not only as a sales market (both for local needs and especially for re-export of finished goods) but also as a supplier of important raw materials and precursors. But these "golden times" seem to have come to an end for the time being. In today's article, the Handelsblatt provocatively poses the question of a "China risk" for the global semiconductor industry. 

It was a great pleasure to contribute to this article with industry analyses together with Thibault Morel of Bryan, Garnier & Co.

Here are some key points from the piece:

* China's uncertain economic climate is becoming a growing risk factor for the chip industry, with increasing scrutiny from both Beijing and Washington.
* Companies such as Nvidia have warned of the consequences of US government export restrictions, potentially costing billions in lost sales.
* Arm's IPO prospectus emphasises the potential dangers posed by the market situation in the People's Republic.
* Qualcomm is one of the companies most vulnerable to political and economic developments in China, with 60 per cent of its total revenue coming from the country.
* Infineon and Arm also derive a quarter of their revenues from China, representing a significant share of the industry's global sales.
* Economic stagnation, US sanctions, Chinese competition and export restrictions are making it increasingly difficult for companies to compete in the Chinese market. 
* This is particularly challenging for European chip companies as Chinese competitors are building up R&D and manufacturing capabilities - and are already head to head within some of their products against their foreign competitors.

For more details, see the article in today's Handelsblatt print edition or online: https://lnkd.in/d-DWGZCk

Capgemini Engineering #consulting #semiconductors #innovation

title: "Strong words from Korea: Can Hyundai become dangerous to VW?" asks WirtschaftsWoche in an interesting article. This was
text:
"Strong words from Korea: Can Hyundai become dangerous to VW?" asks WirtschaftsWoche in an interesting article. This was prompted by announcements at the Busan International Motor Show, which ended yesterday. 

At its "in-house exhibition", the Hyundai Motor Group not only presented its new model Ioniq 6. CEO Jae Hoon Chang also used the occasion to reaffirm Hyundai's strategy and to attack the world's top automakers.

It was a pleasure to contribute to the article with somre analysis on technology as well as the automotive industry. 

Please find the link to the article in the comments.

* Since Hyundai Motor was founded in 1967, the company has seen tremendous growth. In the beginning, it relied heavily on licensed models and technology from foreign manufacturers, such as Ford and Mitsubishi, but Hyundai has always had the goal of building its own expertise in the core areas of automotive manufacturing.

* In the 1990s, Hyundai reached a milestone and was able to introduce its own designs of engine, transmission and body into the global markets. Of course, the low price was a decisive argument. However, the group also set standards in Western markets with long-term warranties.

* Unusually for Asian companies, Hyundai did not shy away from hiring top Western employees and letting them do so. This was evident, for example, in the progress made in Hyundai/Kia/Genesis design or in the significantly improved driving dynamics.

* Early on, Hyundai demonstrated its expertise in electrification. The Hyundai Ioniq Electric has been among the most efficient electric vehicles on the market.

* With the current E-GMP platform, the Group has a powerful foundation  for electric vehicles and is a global pacesetter with the introduction of 800-Volt technology in the mid-size segment.

* The just introduced Ioniq 6 sets standards in terms of aerodynamics, efficiency as well as actual achievable charging speed.

* With the development of the new eM / eS platforms as well as the Integrated Modular Architecture for batteries, Hyundai has charted a path to make its electric vehicles more cost-effective and powerful.

* By 2025, the Group wants to play a leading role in electric vehicles, it wants to become a provider of mobility systems, and it also wants to be at the forefront of the hydrogen and fuel cell systems business.

* Hyundai plans to invest over USD 10 billion in the USA by 2025, including the construction of a vehicle and battery factory. 

* Strategic investments also include automated driving. Here Hyundai - in collaboration with Aptiv - is active in the joint company Motional. For example, it plans to start passenger services from 2023. 

* Hyundai has recognized all the determining technology trends and plans to be involved in them. The acquisition of Boston Dynamics, known for its robot dog Spot, by Hyundai in 2020 is still well remembered. 

Capgemini #Innovation Capgemini Engineering #autonomousdriving Capgemini Automotive #automotiveindustry

title: "Dozens of new plants," headlines Handelsblatt. The #semiconductor industry is trying to cope with supply bottlenecks by
text:
"Dozens of new plants," headlines Handelsblatt. The #semiconductor industry is trying to cope with supply bottlenecks by investing billions. But while the Americas and Asia, already dominant in semiconductor manufacturing, are attracting much of the investment, Europe is in danger of missing its targets. The Handelsblatt has done research on this and it was a great pleasure for me to be able to contribute with industry analyses. 

* the #chipshortage continues to have a firm grip on the global economy. The #automotiveindustry in particular is having to cope with scarcity of electronic components and, in some cases, production interruptions. 

* In the next few years, the semiconductor industry plans to invest USD 446 billion worldwide, according to recent Semi figures. 

* For example, the capacity of 200mm wafers will increase to around 6.9 million units by 2024. However, the majority of these fabs will be located in China. 

* According to Semi, additional production capacities for around 56 billion dollars will be created in Europe. However, this corresponds to only 13 percent of global investments - and is thus less than the EU is targeting. Commission President Ursula von der Leyen  wants to double the EU's share of global production from less than ten percent to 20 percent by the end of the decade.

* In the USA, President Biden has introduced legislation to provide $52 billion in support for the industry (CHIPS for America Act & FABS Act). The EU has earmarked 43 billion euros in subsidies. Individual member states are also putting up money. In Germany, for example, Intel's relocation to Magdeburg will probably be supported by the state to the tune of a good five billion euros.

* European chip powerhouses like Infineon Technologies or STMicroelectronics have announced to increase their investments (by 40% for Infineon and “double then” by STM). 

* Even though the sums earmarked by the EU are huge, the question is whether they will be enough to make up for the shortfall.

For further details check out the Handelsblatt print edition

Cc to my “semi-colleagues”: Shiv Tasker , Christoph Jungnickl , Scott Runner

title: Billions in subsidies for the chip industry are unnecessary - right? During the budget discussions of the last few weeks
text:
Billions in subsidies for the chip industry are unnecessary - right? During the budget discussions of the last few weeks, there has been much talk in Germany about whether the promises of subsidies for the establishment of chip manufacturing plants can be kept, and what the consequences would be if measures to improve the attractiveness of the location were not implemented to the agreed extent.

In today's editon of the Handelsblatt, Dr. Wolfgang Buechele, CEO of Exyte - a major German equipment manufacturer for controlled and regulated environments like in the semiconductor industry - took a clear - and well argumented - stand. The headline indicates the direction: "If support is not forthcoming, manufacturers will go elsewhere".  

It was a pleasure to contribute to the article with some industry analysis with James Hanford, CFA, chip expert at asset manager COMGEST.

Some thoughts about it:

While no one wants to talk about a subsidy arms race, the facts suggest that the US Inflation Reduction Act, the EU Chips Act and Japan's measures to revive the semiconductor and digital industries have triggered fierce competition between locations. While the individual measures differ, they all create a positive environment for the capital-intensive semiconductor industry. The global plans for new fabs in the US, Europe and Japan are impressive evidence of this.

With the current plans for new semiconductor factories, Germany will gain significant production capacity in important key technologies. From high-end logic chips for computing and AI applications, to components for industrial and automotive use, to the power semiconductors that are so important for a successful energy transition. As well as directly creating high-quality jobs, the complex manufacturing processes depend on a network of facility designers, machine suppliers and service providers. The same goes for the supply of high-purity raw materials. Although the semiconductor value chain is highly globalised today, German companies are represented in many of these areas with competitive products - these segments will therefore benefit from a strengthening of domestic production capacities in the future. This includes machines and systems for the increasingly important processes downstream of wafer production: packaging and testing. The establishment of new production lines for semiconductors will also strengthen the existing German research and education networks. Both through the increasing demand for graduates and through new joint research projects with industry.

Read more here: https://lnkd.in/dugUesj4

Capgemini Engineering #consulting #innovation #semiconductorindustry #semiconductors

title: "The signs point to recession. Are the gloomy prospects for the semiconductor industry an indicator for the entire globa
text:
"The signs point to recession. Are the gloomy prospects for the semiconductor industry an indicator for the entire global economy?" asks Handelsblatt today.  It was a pleasure to contribute to this analysis together with other experts such as Ondrej Burkacky from McKinsey & Company, Peter Schiefer from Infineon Technologies, Dr. Klaus Schmitz from Arthur D. Little and Vincent Roche from Analog Devices.

* for many years (pre Covid), the three-month moving average of global chip sales has correlated quite passably with the trend in global gross domestic product.

* during Covid, the semiconductor market has decoupled sharply. The #digitization push and global trend towards home offices has given the industry a substantial boom.

* some major semiconductor houses had missed their own forecasts in the last quarter. Weak order intake has been reported in recent weeks.

* however, while for instance the demand for memory chips was recently reduced, other component groups, like power electronics, are still in relative short supply. 

* before Corona, the chip industry had been running about six weeks ahead of the global economy, according to the industry association ZVEI e. V.  It is unclear whether this is now true again. However, there are indications that the chip industry is again anticipating the development of the global economy - possibly even more than before.

More details in the article - view it in print or online. Link in the comments.

text: #Thoughts from #industry #leaders about #future #mobility. Excellent #opening of the #iaaconference today by Bernhard Mattes of Verband der Automobilindustrie (VDA) e.V., Virginia Rometty of IBM and Ola Källenius of Daimler AG. Interesting #insights how IBM had to #reinvent itself over it’s history of over 100 years, how Daimler needed to change it’s #software skills from #indirect management to #direct doing (re assessing core/context in Altran language, or an #efficient #engineering concept as Werner Ferreira da Silva described it in #press talks today at #iaa19), how a #compete / #cooperate #model on topics like #autonomousdriving works between Daimler AG and BMW Group and how the worlds of #IT and #automotive converge. We also learned about #life #lessons. Guess what: Virginia Rometty drew her life lessons from her mother (never let someone else define who you are), her husband (when she was in doubt earlier in her career about taking an opportunity, he asked “do you think a man would act like that?”) and from Napoleon (“growth and comfort can never co-exist”). As Ola Källenius said: “that’s hard to beat”.

title: Hydrogen plays a key role in #decarbonization. But is it the „unavoidable“ choice for all #energy needs to replace #foss
text:
#Hydrogen plays a key role in #decarbonization. But is it the „unavoidable“ choice for all #energy needs to replace #fossilfuels ? I believe the #hydrogeneconomy needs to and will become a reality. But we need much more thinking about where to put our bets most efficient for the extremely versatile - but scarce and valuable - resource #greenhydrogen. And individual #mobility and short-distance #logistics are maybe not one of them. No matter if #fuelcell , #PtL and #eFuels or #H2ICE - we need to review the #end2end impact and where it helps most to cut on #co2emissions.

title: What's driving the mobility industry? Yesterday, we were able to experience an extremely exciting and insightful evening
text:
What's driving the mobility industry? Yesterday, we were able to experience an extremely exciting and insightful evening. The wonderful host Dr. Wolfgang K. Eckelt had put together a top-class lecture program with top leaders of the mobility industry.

* Martin Daum / CEO Daimler Truck AG Trucks with 5 enormously opinionated theses on the (automotive) future. Most refreshing to see a CEO of a major stock-listed company to speak so freely and clear

* Jens Thiemer / SVP Marketing BMW (prominently placed on the Forbes list of the world's most influential CMO's) with an outlook on the customer experience of the future - not only for Gen Z and Gen Alpha

* Steve Downing / President and CEO of Gentex Corporation talked about the successful transformation of the company - from king of the rear view mirrors to become technology partner for the industry - and how successful niches in the market were identified- Most insightful how Gentex approached the automated driving challenge!

* Maria Anhalt / CEO of Elektrobit gave an extremely exciting presentation on how, with the help of new technological and methodological approaches - as well as a large portion of courage and trust on the part of the partners, the groundbreaking and multi-award-winning SONY Vision S vehicle could be realized within a very short time

* Lars Reger / CTO of NXP Semiconductors picked up the ball ('the chip industry is responsible for the chip crisis') in a witty and substantive presentation and gave us an impresion how NXP is 'firing on all cylinders' to crank out an all-time high of chips but also provided an outlook on the developments in #semiconductors for connected, automated, electric vehicles.

It was especially nice to be able to meet many of the industry's greats (Cosimo De Carlo, Dirk Wollschläger, ...) once again in person, in addition to the high-level discussions. 

Thank you, Dr. Eckelt, for a successful event. 

Werner Ferreira da Silva, Ralf Blessmann, Michael Hoffmann, M.A.

title: "Nvidia fails in Arm takeover" is the headline in the #technology sector today. In an interview with Handelsblatt, I was
text:
"Nvidia fails in Arm takeover" is the headline in the #technology sector today. In an interview with Handelsblatt, I was allowed to shed light on some of the reasons for this as well as the effects on the chip market and the wider industry. 
* Over 200 billion chips with Arm technology have been shipped in the last 30 years.
* The planned takeover of Arm by NVIDIA would be comparable to Intel Corporation buying AMD or Qualcomm buying MediaTek. There would be serious concerns about ensuring free and fair competition.
* Ultimately, concerns around competition and ensuring accessibility (China; key customers like Google, Amazon, Meta and Apple) and sovereignty (EU/UK) of essential ARM technology thwarted the deal.

Now many newspapers ask: is Arm's ecosystem and business model now secure as a likely (re)listed company? Although many observers express relief, certain challenges must be overcome. 

* arm's technology roadmap is incredibly strong and the platform extremely attractive for a variety of applications. 
* Technology trends such as #ai , #5g and #iot continue to augur bright prospects. Advanced #robotics,  #automateddriving and #connectedvehicles fuel the demand further. There are also new opportunities in the #cloudcomputing space.
* However, Arm's move into the world's data centers, for example, is not a foregone conclusion. Arm itself has described the need for significant investment in this field to become a leader.
* The strengthening competition from RISC-V architectures must also be kept in mind.
* Nor will the classic #cpu giants like intel and AMD leave the field without a fight. The latest developments of the big players show where the journey is heading.
* Also, there is the question how Arm can improve it's position 
* There is hope and a good chance that a future group of significant anchor shareholders will participate in a listed arm group and continue to ensure an excellent future. A look towards The Netherlands (to other "systemically important" companies) gives an outlook on this.

More here: https://lnkd.in/e7qJsKks

title: “A bumpy road to #visionzero...” Since #humanerror is a significant factor in the majority of #vehicle accidents, the in
text: “A bumpy road to #visionzero...” Since #humanerror is a significant factor in the majority of #vehicle accidents, the introduction of #automateddriving  technology looks like a promising way to reduce fatalities and injuries. The Insurance Institute for Highway Safety / Highway Loss Data Institute has taken a closer look and published a new #study about #accident #scenarios and the potential positive impact of #selfdriving technology in the examined cases. title: "#autonomousdriving for goods transportation  picks up speed again". In an interview with the trade magazines "Trans akt
text:
"#autonomousdriving for goods transportation  picks up speed again". In an interview with the trade magazines "Trans aktuell / eurotransport", I had the pleasure of classifying the developments, framework conditions as well as future prospects of driving automation for the freight service industry. 

After initial enthusiasm around 2014, things got a little quieter in public perception around self-driving commercial vehicles. But now the topic is picking up speed again. 

On the one hand, the technology has now proven what it can do and is even driving safely in city traffic in pilot tests.

On the other hand, it has become clear that it is possible to create the right legal conditions.

A huge milestone for automated driving on public roads has been achieved in 2021, where the German government under Minister of Transport and Digital Infrastructure Andreas Scheuer has released the first usable legal framework - which now get’s continually refined.

So will the #logistics industry soon experience an "autonomous boom"? 

Rising fuel prices and an increasing shortage of truck drivers in most markets emphasize the need for automation. 

To hear how we move from the "electronic drawbar" to #selfdriving  trucks and what roles #5G, #V2X, #unece  #R155 #R156, teleoperation centres, as well as #hdmaps play in this, read on here: https://lnkd.in/eeF7ckAi
or the print edition of trans-aktuell. 

#interview eurotransport.de  Motor Presse Stuttgart GmbH & Co. KG

title: "The world's most coveted chip company," writes Handelsblatt today in an "Insight story" about NVIDIA. For example, flag
text:
"The world's most coveted chip company," writes Handelsblatt today in an "Insight story" about NVIDIA. For example, flagship German companies such as Mercedes-Benz AG, Siemens and Bosch have recently placed major orders with the Santa Clara-based processor giant.

The company was founded in 1993 by three engineers who believed in a market for graphics accelerators and special-purpose processors.

Driven by the video game industry, nvidia has today become a leading supplier of GPUs and accelerator cards, found in many applications. In 2022, nvidia is expected to have revenues of $26.9 billion. 

The data center business will account for around USD 10.6 billion.

Although still small at around USD 600 million, the business with the #automotiveindustry is of immense strategic importance. 

The article highlights the secrets of nvidia's success and how its chips help to improve AI applications in medicine, for example.

It was a great pleasure to contribute to this interesting article with an industry perspective.

Have a look at it in the print edition or online: https://lnkd.in/dgupcntq

text:

title: The AI Memory Squeeze: Prices are "Exploding" and the Geopolitical Floodgates Just Opened as the US allow new GPU export
text:
🤯 The AI Memory Squeeze: Prices are "Exploding" and the Geopolitical Floodgates Just Opened as the US allow new GPU exports to China.

We are facing a memory supply shock that is no longer just a forecast—it is here, and the numbers are unprecedented.

In my latest talk with Handelsblatt, I warned that the massive capacity shift toward AI-critical High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) is draining the supply for everything else (DRAM, NAND). But the situation is escalating faster than many realize.

The Data is Alarming: According to analysts at TrendForce Corp., we are seeing price volatility rarely witnessed in this sector:

📈 DRAM prices are projected to surge by nearly 50% in the current quarter alone, following a 30% jump in Q3.

🕹️ Production costs for gaming consoles like the PlayStation 5 Pro and the next Switch could rise by 15-17% through 2026 due to memory costs.

💻 Notebooks are expected to see price hikes of up to 15% next year.

🛎️ Industry Leaders are Sounding the Alarm: It’s not just analysts. Industry executives featured in the article are already reacting.

Jeff Clarke, COO of Dell Technologies, noted in the Handelsblatt article that we have "never seen cost increases at this speed".

Enrique Lores, CEO of HP, is already preparing "aggressive measures," including potential price increases or reducing memory content in devices.

The Geopolitical Accelerant: While the industry grapples with this scarcity, news just broke that the U.S. administration plans to allow Nvidia to ship its powerful H200 AI chips to China. Against a small 25% "EXPORT FEE" (as can be seen in the nice Nano Banana generated image I have attached).

This is a critical turning point. The H200 relies on HBM3e—the exact same advanced memory that is already arguably the "most valuable and high-margin product the memory industry has ever had". By reopening the Chinese market for these chips, we are effectively unlocking a massive new tranche of demand for an already scarce resource.

My Take: As I stated in the article, demand remains high and no new memory fabs are coming online in the near term. We are entering a period where the "AI tax" will be paid by every other sector—from automotive to consumer electronics.

At Capgemini, we see that static supply chains can no longer withstand these combined geopolitical and technological shocks. We are helping our clients move beyond reactive procurement to build Intelligent Supply Chains—leveraging data and AI to predict scarcity and secure resilience before the market tightens further.

If you are concerned about your component strategy for 2026, let's connect.

📖 Find the link to the Handelsblatt article in the comments. 

Christoph Jungnickl Dr. Nico Wolf Christian Hummel

#Semiconductors #SupplyChain #AI #HBM #TechTrends  #Automotive

title: "A record year for Infineon". The Handelsblatt has written an analysis of the company’s results and strategy on the occa
text:
"A record year for Infineon". The Handelsblatt has written an analysis of the company’s results and strategy on the occasion of yesterday's Annual General Meeting. It was a pleasure to contribute to this report with technical assessments. The financial  analysis was done by Markus Golinski (Union Investment).
 
The key facts from the article:
 
* Sales climbed for the second year in a row by 29 percent, to around 14 billion euros.
 
* The automotive division at Infineon Technologies  accounts for around 45% of sales - and is therefore enormously important. Due to strong demand for car chips, revenues have risen to almost €1.5 billion - but margins are still below those of other industries.
 
* The investment ratio climbed by almost three percentage points to 16.2 percent. This is significantly higher than the 13 percent target previously set by the Group (e.g. new manufacturing capacity in Dresden (300mm) for analog/mixed signal/power).
 
* Infineon sees good business for 2023. Sales are expected to increase by around nine percent to 15.5 billion euros in the new fiscal year.
 
What are the challenges on the market?
 
* While automotive and industrial chips continue to be "allocated" to customers, demand has not been exuberant, especially in the server, PC and mobile sectors. Many lines in this area are underutilized.
 
* China is an extremely important region. Not only as a sales market for purely "domestic" demand, but also as a market for products that are later integrated into products and re-exported.
 
* The chip crisis between the USA and China also remains a future risk for semiconductor manufacturers. Under the impact of the sanctions, the Chinese semiconductor industry is currently reorganizing itself.
 
* Enormous investments are now flowing into areas such as advanced power semiconductors made of materials like SiC and GaN. Companies such as Silan Microelectronic, CRRC Times Electric and Sanan Semiconductor are significantly expanding their capacities. In addition, manufacturers such as BYD Semiconductor (a subsidiary of the BYD car group) are entering the segment with power, along with Chinese Auto players. 
 
* Stronger competition from China can also be expected in the area of microcontrollers.  Companies like GigaDevice, Fudan Micro, ChipON or (again) BYD Semiconductor have introduced microcontrollers for industrial use. One reason for this is that they are under strong margin pressure in their traditional segments such as consumer electronics and are now seeking their salvation in more demanding applications such as automotive. In this, the chip companies are well supported by the government, whose clear goal is to make Chinese cars as independent as possible from foreign chips.

For the full article go to https://lnkd.in/dN8gbXMg (paywall)

Capgemini Engineering #consulting #automotiveindustry #semiconductorindustry #chinatech #innovation Capgemini Automotive
author: Peter Fintl

title: Europe is to catch up with the world leaders in advanced #semiconductor production and at the same time better cope with
text:
Europe is to catch up with the world leaders in advanced #semiconductor production and at the same time better cope with - or avoid - future chip crises. The EU alone is planning to provide over 40 billion euros in funding for new production processes and manufacturing facilities. 

Thus, the European share of global semiconductor production should double to 20% by the end of the decade. 

The ambitious plan, introduced by President Ursula von der Leyen earlier, is all great news. But the conditions for the subsidies set out in the EU Chips Act are the subject of controversial debate among experts.

In an article today, Handelsblatt  has shed light on the details of how far the EU intends to go here, not only to gather information from the manufacturers in order to be able to identify disruptions in the supply chain at an early stage, but also to take action with the help of manufacturing allocation and tariff measures in order to prevent further chip crises.

Some market participants now see this as the first steps toward a new "planned economy".

Take a look at the article and read the voices of experts like Jan-Peter Kleinhans (stiftung neue verantwortung ),  EU Deputy Director Général Thomas Skordas  and me. It was a pleasure to contribute to this important topic.

See also online: Handelsblatt - Die Schattenseite der Subventionen: Chipindustrie fürchtet Planwirtschaft - https://lnkd.in/gdwrcCP7

Capgemini  #innovation Capgemini Engineering #Consulting Capgemini Automotive #europe #automotive #chipshortage

title: In a surprising move yesterday, the European Automobile Manufacturers'​ Association (ACEA) proposed to the European Comm
text:
In a surprising move yesterday, the European Automobile Manufacturers'​ Association (ACEA) proposed to the European Commission a deal for a faster #transition to #co2neutral #mobility. https://lnkd.in/e6td5Kn This means effectively a much faster ramp-up of #electricmobility and #charginginfrastructure. 

But wait, what will happen with the 1.2bn #passengervehicles already on the road today? They will be around for many more years and continue to guzzle #fossilfuels. This is a major factor influencing the speed of achieving #carbonneutrality.

In an interesting #debate article, Automobil Industrie discussed if #efuels are a viable #technology to enable the use of #combustionengines beyond 2030 and if they are a lever for #decarbonization. 

I had the pleasure to contribute among experts like Prof. Fritz Indra, formerly General Motors., Benjamin Stephan of Greenpeace and Stefan Wolf of ElringKlinger. Interesting read with (some provocative) statements.

title: Auto China opens in Beijing in 12 days. Read what my friend and colleague Oussama wrote before you go — or before you dr
text:
🔁 Auto China opens in Beijing in 12 days. Read what my friend and colleague Oussama wrote before you go — or before you draw any conclusions from what you’ll see there.

The show will be read as a speed story. Chinese OEMs shipping faster, more features, lower price points. Impressive. 

But Oussama’s framing is the right one: it’s a balance problem, not a productivity race. That distinction changes what you actually need to do about it.

I spent many years building automotive programs on the ground in Beijing. The advantage isn’t velocity for its own sake — it’s parallel engineering capacity, ecosystem coordination, and a market that absorbs complexity at a different rate. You don’t close that gap by asking your engineers to go faster.

This is where Capgemini’s chip-to-cloud depth stops being a capability slide and becomes a structural answer. Ten years building automated driving together with Oussama and the global AD team has shown me what that looks like in practice.

His closing line is the one to keep: those who orchestrate complexity together restore equilibrium — those who chase productivity in isolation amplify fragmentation.

The show will confirm the problem. The question is what you do on the flight home.

👉 If you’re heading to Beijing — let’s talk. I will be there. Find me. 🤝

#AutoChina2026 #AutonomousDriving #SDV #CapgeminiEngineering #FutureOfMobility

Oussama Laurence Jens Sebastian Dr. Rainer Luz Ulrich
author: Peter Fintl

title: "Made abroad" headlines the venerable WirtschaftsWoche today. The catchphrase "deindustrialisation of Germany" has been
text:
"Made abroad" headlines the venerable WirtschaftsWoche today. The catchphrase "deindustrialisation of Germany" has been heard more often lately. The title story comprehensively examines whether and how expensive energy, a lack of skilled workers and bureaucracy are driving German companies to foreign locations.

BDI - Bundesverband der Deutschen Industrie e.V. head Siegfried Russwurm, for example, warns of "fundamental problems" in industry - and calls for an aid programme for the economy "as soon as possible" "to prevent insolvencies and further economic and social upheavals".

It was a pleasure for me to contribute to this worth reading article with analyses of the automotive industry and its distributed value creation networks - not only in Eastern Europe.

For it is not only in the #manufacturing  sector - also in the areas of #research and development - that an international division of tasks is necessary in order to remain competitive. 

However, this does not automatically mean a weakening of the domestic location in Germany. In many cases, it can enable a necessary transformation of the value chain and thus secure the future of German companies.

Read more about this and how Bayer, Continental, DRÄXLMAIER Group, HARTMANN GROUP or Viessmann are strategically positioning themselves here in the current issue of WiWo.

title: Automotive #semiconductors: "Customers order two years in advance" is how the Handelsblatt quotes Peter Schiefer of Infi
text:
#Automotive #semiconductors: "Customers order two years in advance" is how the Handelsblatt quotes Peter Schiefer of Infineon Technologies today. It was a pleasure to be able to contribute to this piece, which sheds light on the market view and prospects for suppliers of (power) semiconductors in vehicles. 

Some facts from the conversation:
* Many suppliers have invested massively in the development of new products and production facilities in recent years. 

Infineon: new 200mm fab in Kulim, Malaysia in order to repurpose the 150/200mm lines in the Villach, Austria fab to produce #SiC / #GaN elements; introduction of second generation trench power MOSFET technology for SiC still in 2022.

Wolfspeed: built a brand new 200mm fab in Marcy/NY, USA which has a good chance of becoming the world's largest and most productive SiC device fab. Customer deliveries are scheduled for 2022. High-profile deal with Lucid Motors to provide power moduls for their ultra-efficient Lucid Air model.

* These high investments have put pressure on margins in the past financial periods.

* The unexpectedly high demand for #electricvehicles (containing semiconductors per vehicle for around 1000USD) and the rapid expansion of the charging infrastructure (ultra fast charging stations can contain semiconductors for up to 3000USD) are now bringing good profits to the suppliers.

* Likewise, the increased expansion of sustainable energies such as wind power or #photovoltaic (solar inverters) will drive the business for power semiconductors.

Further details and figures can be found in today's print edition of the Handelsblatt or online (link in the commentary).

Capgemini #innovation Capgemini Engineering #consulting  Capgemini Automotive

text:
"Vehicle manufacturers from the People's Republic have big plans for Europe. But the big success has so far failed to materialize," reports the Sueddeutsche Zeitung today. 
Even if so far only the Chinese billionaire Li Shufu with GEELY, Lynk & Co and Polestar has managed to achieve significant sales figures in Europe around his Swedish passenger car subsidiary Volvo Cars: Chinese companies are waiting in the wings to take aim at the European car market.

What strategies established car manufacturers from the Middle Kingdom could pursue, what young brands are doing differently and why entering the European Market is challenging for new players - that's what I recently had the pleasure of talking about with Bloomberg Reuters news agency (contents of this talks are used as a source in the article of SZ).

Because the Chinese competition is to be taken seriously. As Prof. Dr. Ferdinand Dudenhoeffer points out in the article, in their home market they have already put BMW, Mercedes and Volkswagen on the spot with their electric models. "The Germans' supremacy in China is crumbling".

title: "Chip manufacturers are caught in the China trap", writes Handelsblatt today. Stricter export controls and the ongoing l
text:
"Chip manufacturers are caught in the China trap", writes Handelsblatt today. Stricter export controls and the ongoing lockdowns in China are causing the chip business of corporations in the "Middle Kingdom" to shrink. It was a pleasure to contribute to this article with analyses together with Dr. Klaus Schmitz, from Arthur D. Little and Gartner analyst Richard Gordon.

* Trade restrictions on companies belonging to the Chinese "military-industrial complex" made headlines a while ago. A prominent example was Huawei, which was deprived of access to SW and HW for its very successful mobile phone business.

* This also includes rules that cover the export of machines for semiconductor production, like lithography systems from the leading manufacturer ASML.

* These measures have definitely hampered the development of the Chinese semiconductor industry. In particular, the industrialization of 7nm technology was to be delayed. However, the Chinese SMIC group, which has long been limited to 14/12nm chips, has recently started producing chips in a 7nm process, albeit with difficulties. This is still an amazing achievement, as SMIC has made the most of its machinery. A large number of Taiwanese specialists, who are being recruited by China to the best of their ability, have certainly also contributed to this.

* In mid-August, the US Department of Commerce issued further measures, which include an export ban on suspensions made of gallium oxide (Ga2O3) and diamond as well as Electronic Computer-Aided Design software specially designed for the development of integrated circuits with Gate-All-Around Field-Effect Transistor (GAAFET) structure. On the one hand, this inhibits the production of extremely high-performance power electronics (gallium oxide GaO, like silicon carbide #SiC or gallium nitride #GaN, is a class of materials that is extremely well suited for low-loss power semiconductors). The export ban on design software for novel GAAFET transistor designs should, from the American point of view, inhibit the path to designs with structure widths below 5nm/3nm. 

* At the beginning of September, the USA imposed a further measure that affects the export of chips specifically for AI applications. This means that NVIDIA A100/H100 chips or AMD MI250 components can no longer be sold to China without further ado. nVidia is talking about an impact of up to USD 400 million in the next quarter.

* China is the largest semiconductor market, accounting for about one third of its turnover. For years, business there has grown more strongly than in the rest of the world. However, due to the measures and the ongoing lockdowns, sales in the region fell 1.8% year-on-year in July, according to the Semiconductor Industry Association.

For more details and how the leading manufacturers can compensate for the restrictions in their global business, read today's Handelsblatt print edition.

Capgemini #consulting Capgemini Engineering #innovation #semiconductors #electronics

title: Breaking News: Intel Corporation agrees $6 billion deal to buy Tower Semiconductor , writes Handelsblatt just now. With
text:
Breaking News: Intel Corporation agrees $6 billion deal to buy Tower Semiconductor , writes Handelsblatt just now. With this, the US #semiconductor giant continues the ambitious expansion course of its production facilities. 

Tower Semi is, with a turnover of 1.3 billion USD and 7 fabs (150, 200 and 300mm), a powerful supplier in the areas of #SiGe, #BiCMOS, #rf  #cmos  (also SOI); CIS; sensors, power management, #mixedsignal CMOS and #MEMS.

Many technologies that are needed by industries like #automotive or #aerospace. 

This acquisition significantly expands Intel's #technology expertise and manufacturing capacity in these areas, following the new intel strategy. Maybe this is why intel-CEO and industry-legend Pat Gelsinger is giving a big smile in the bunny suit, pictured below.

This is definitely good news for Tower partners and customers like Infineon Technologies, NXP Semiconductors  and STMicroelectronics

title: World #EnergyOutlook #2020: How #covid19 is reshaping the #energymarket. Key findings from the International Energy Agen
text:
World #EnergyOutlook #2020: How #covid19 is reshaping the #energymarket. Key findings from the International Energy Agency (IEA) #report today:
1) Due to #coronacrisis, global #co2emissions decline 7% this year
2) The era of #coal is over, demand likely not to recover
3) The #boom of #oil is about to end, latest in 2030 demand will decline
4) #naturalgas has a uncertain future, drivers are China and India
5) #renewableenergy is the winner of the #crisis, #windpower and #solarenergy shall grow 10% yoy, #pv today is in most countries already cheaper than #electricitygeneration from #coal or #gas power plants. Up to 90% of energy demand could come from renewables.
6) #Risk for #investments, if the #pandemic reaches beyond 2021, investments in #greenhydrogen etc might be delayed (Wind, Solar likely stable)
7) The #economiccrisis has caused a #co2reduction. But this is not a #strategy to fight #climatechange

title: "Tesla's trouble with authorities," writes Handelsblatt  today. Is the electric pioneer Tesla being treated unfairly or
text:
"Tesla's trouble with authorities," writes Handelsblatt  today. Is the electric pioneer Tesla being treated unfairly or are these home-made problems? Together with Christian Koenig  and Johnna Crider , I had the pleasure discussing the facts on recalls by the NHTSA, possible investigations by the South Korean competition authority and lawsuits at the Fremont plant.

Indeed, Tesla has experienced quite some recalls in recent months: 
Feb. 8: Windshield Defrosting
Feb. 4: Novelty "Boombox" software
Feb. 1: Seat belt reminders
Jan. 27: Rolling stops
Dec. 21: Rearview camera and hood latch
Nov. 21: Suspension knuckle fractures 
Nov. 9: airbag tears
Oct. 29: false forward-collision warning
Oct. 25: Loose suspension bolts

The Tesla Autopilot is a particular subject of discussion. In contrast to the corresponding systems of the competition, it only works with cameras and for a long time without continuous monitoring of the driver. This had led to Tesla drivers falling asleep at the wheel or being inattentive - even though they should have kept their hands on the wheel according to Tesla's own guidelines. In August, National Highway Traffic Safety Administration NHTSA  had launched an investigation into Autopilot in 765,000 Tesla vehicles after about a dozen accidents occurred.

Much of this can and will be fixed by Tesla "over-the-air" through software updates. One recent issue that will definitely require a visit to the Tesla Service Center is the silent deletion of the redundancy for electric steering for many M3. Which means those vehicles are definitely not suitable for any FSD “out of the box”.

But, on the upside, in areas like passive safety, Tesla vehicles are usually top-notch. 

Also, it must be said, recalls and mandated service measures are nothing new for the entire #automotiveindustry.  In addition to significant cost savings, platform strategies and common parts often result in hundreds of thousands or even millions of vehicles being affected in the event of a fault. 

For German customers, a visit to the website of KBA, the Federal Motor Transport Authority shows the ongoing service measures. 

More details on the severity and the impacts of the recalls in the print article as exciting weekend reading or online: Handelsblatt: US-Verkehrsbehörde leitet erneut Untersuchungen gegen Tesla ein - https://lnkd.in/ejqnU4zf

Capgemini #consulting Capgemini Engineering #innovation Capgemini Automotive #safety

title: 𝗢𝗽𝗲𝗻 𝘀𝗼𝘂𝗿𝗰𝗲 𝘀𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝘀 𝗮 𝗸𝗲𝘆 𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲-𝗱𝗲𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗱 𝘃𝗲𝗵�
text:
𝗢𝗽𝗲𝗻 𝘀𝗼𝘂𝗿𝗰𝗲 𝘀𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝘀 𝗮 𝗸𝗲𝘆 𝗲𝗹𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲-𝗱𝗲𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗱 𝘃𝗲𝗵𝗶𝗰𝗹𝗲: 𝗗𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗺 𝗼𝗿 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆? The automotive industry has recognised that tomorrow's digital mobility system cannot be efficiently developed using traditional industry approaches. 

Connected, automated and sustainable vehicles - equipped with innovative service offerings - require new development, collaboration and IP models. 

In an opinion piece for the Eclipse Software Defined Vehicle  working group, colleagues Max Senges, Michael Plagge, Ansgar Lindwedel, Daniel Lueddecke, Thomas Fleischmann and I describe why we believe #opensourcesoftware  can be a viable foundation for tomorrow's #automotiveindustry  and call for participation in the working group. Have a read!

And if this topic is exactly your cup of tea: join us next week at the Automotive Open Source Summit 2023
Driving Open Source Innovation in Automotive Grade Software
6 June 2023 | Hotel Königshof, Garmisch-Partenkirchen, Germany
More info at:
https://lnkd.in/dms4DDvH

Dr. Rainer Mehl, Jean-Marie Lapeyre, Fergus Duncan, Arnim Buch, Ralf Blessmann Capgemini Automotive #innovation #automotive #opensource

title: Pure #electricvehicles receive #subsidies of up to 9,000 Euros in #Germany. The federal government has thus been able to
text:
Pure #electricvehicles receive #subsidies of up to 9,000 Euros in #Germany. The federal government has thus been able to increase the #sales figures of #BEVs enormously. But how does this "#Innovationsprämie" affect the prices of electric #usedvehicles ? In an interview with ADAC, I examined the reasons for the plunge in residual values (#evehicles have currently a higher #depreciation than #Diesel cars, according to DAT Barometer March 2021). Basically the market has increased (BEV in 2020 ca. 194k new sales, 19.4k used vehice sales). But in addition to the high subsidy for new cars and "young used cars," there is a mistrust among many customers about the actual condition of the #battery, as well as a feeling that they are simply buying outdated #technology in view of the dramatically increased ranges and #innovation in recent years. 

Read more here: https://lnkd.in/esz9WED

For more #insights around automotive market mechanics, sales planning in the #digital and #electricmobility age, please also follow my colleagues from Capgemini Invent , Sebastian Tschödrich and Dr. Philipp M. Haaf .

Capgemini Engineering Capgemini Automotive

title: The #energy markets see rapid changes. Legislators have liberated the trade of electricity and wiped out significant mar
text: The #energy markets see rapid changes. Legislators have liberated the trade of electricity and wiped out significant margins. The sustainable production of energy by wind and solar power became much more competitive and put investments in conventional power stations to a test. Technologies like #Blockchain could potentially create highly efficient, transparent and automated market places, empowering literally every owner of a solar panel to become a independent energy provider. Energy companies need to adapt quickly in order to stay relevant. #Digitalization is also in this sector a key to both make traditional processes more efficient and to enable new, high margin, products and services.  The global energy giant ENGIE is leading this transformation process and is digitizing his operations. ENGIE has selected Altran to help in this exciting project. Check out the article if you are interested to learn how Altran helps ENGIE in creating a digital strategy and also making it happen.
author: Peter Fintl

title: How #clean are #electricvehicles? Automobil Industrie / Christian Otto provided some input for discussion by featuring t
text: How #clean are #electricvehicles? Automobil Industrie / Christian Otto provided some input for discussion by featuring the #Automobilbarometer #2019 of Consors Finanz BNP Paribas: it contains some interesting figures on Well-to-Wheel #WTW #CO2 #emissions for various propulsion systems. So clearly, depending on from which #sources the #electric #energy for #BEV is produced, there are significant CO2 emissions. Does it make sense to wait for #goingelectric in order to be "green"? At least for the #German market this question can be answered. As Prof. Martin Wietschel of Fraunhofer Institute for Systems and Innovation Research ISI showed in a recent study: "An electric battery vehicle (BEV) purchased today in Germany has a significant greenhouse gas (GHG) saving over its average useful life of 13 years. The range of the savings is 28% compared to a luxury class #diesel or up to 42% compared to a small-car #gasoline engine, based on the power mix in Germany. [...] The main environmental advantage of BEV compared to conventional vehicles is that each existing vehicle benefits from the improvement in power generation. By contrast, a conventional vehicle operated with petrol or diesel has the [same] emissions over its entire service life"  Link to the study here: https://bit.ly/2FsjzNs
author: Peter Fintl

title: Highly accurate map data has long been seen as a pre-requisite for safe and reliable #automateddriving. Will technologie
text: Highly accurate map data has long been seen as a pre-requisite for safe and reliable #automateddriving. Will technologies like #machinelearning enable a #autonomousvehicle to become independent of #HDMaps? Meet @Altran experts Francisco Lumbreras Arcos and Christophe Quignon at the #Auto.AI conference in #Berlin and discuss with them how to achieve more robust #online #map #models without relying on #HDMaps.
